topics.title: The ROS Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The ROS Graph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The ROS Graph** is the **peer-to-peer network of ROS processes** (called *Nodes*) that are simultaneously communicating with one another at any given point in time. It is the **runtime computational graph** of a ROS-based robotic system, depicting the data-flow topology between distributed processes, the topics and services used for inter-process communication (IPC), and the centralized coordination services.

According to the KTU 2024 OBE framework, the ROS Graph is *not* a static architectural diagram drawn on paper — it is a **live, dynamic, queryable data structure** that changes as nodes are launched, killed, or restarted. The principal graph entities per the official ROS Wiki specification are:

| Entity | Role in the Graph |
|---|---|
| **Nodes** | Executable processes performing computation |
| **ROS Master** | Name service & centralized registry |
| **Parameter Server** | Multi-variate, globally readable key-value store |
| **Topics** | Named buses carrying `Message` streams (pub/sub) |
| **Services** | Synchronous request/reply RPC endpoints |
| **Bags** | On-disk serialized capture/replay of messages |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Post Office Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a **busy post office** in a city.
> - Each **counter clerk** is a *Node* — a person doing a specific job (stamping, sorting, delivering).
> - The **post office building itself** is the *ROS Master* — every clerk checks in here on arrival so others know they exist.
> - The **notice board** at the entrance is the *Parameter Server* — anyone can read the public notices, and the supervisor can update them.
> - The **mail routes** (Route-1, Route-2) are *Topics* — any clerk can post a letter on Route-1, and any clerk subscribed to Route-1 receives it.
> - The **registered post counter** is a *Service* — one clerk makes a formal request, and another answers it directly, one-to-one.
> - The **CCTV recording** of all mail movements is a *Bag* — it stores the entire communication history on tape for later replay.

In the ROS Graph, **no single node "owns" the graph** — every node is an equal peer, exactly like clerks who are not bosses of one another but coordinate through the Master.

### 1.3 Why the Graph Model Matters in Robotics

A modern autonomous robot is rarely a single monolithic program. It is typically dozens of concurrent processes — camera capture, SLAM, path planning, motor control, sensor fusion, UI, diagnostics — running across multiple CPU cores, and sometimes across multiple machines. The **ROS Graph** is the abstraction that allows these processes to discover each other, exchange typed data, and shut down cleanly, **without hard-coded IP/port bindings**.

### 1.4 Physical / Operational Metrics (KTU High-Yield Highlights)

- **Discovery Latency:** Typical node-to-master handshake ≈ **1–100 ms** over local network.
- **Message Default Transport:** TCPROS (TCP) for reliability; UDPROS for low-latency lossy streams.
- **Standard Tools:** `rosnode`, `rostopic`, `rosservice`, `rosparam`, `rosbag`, `rqt_graph`.
- **Default Master URI:** `http://localhost:11311` (port **11311**).
- **Default Node-Manager Port Range:** **ROS_MASTER_URI** + ephemeral TCP ports negotiated via XMLRPC.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of the ROS Graph

#### 2.1.1 Nodes — The Computational Vertices
A **Node** is a single, purpose-built executable written using a ROS client library (`roscpp`, `rospy`, `roslisp`, `rosjava`). Best practice dictates **one process = one responsibility** (e.g., one node for wheel odometry, one node for laser scan, one for path planning).

**Operational characteristics:**
- Each node has a **unique graph-name** (resolved via remapping arguments).
- Each node initializes a **connection to the Master** at startup via **XMLRPC** on port 11311.
- Each node opens **two XMLRPC APIs** and **one or more TCPROS** data sockets after registration.
- Nodes declare a **publisher**, **subscriber**, **service server**, **service client**, or **action server/client** at runtime.

**Why many small nodes?**
- *Fault isolation* — a crashing camera node does not kill the motion planner.
- *Code reuse* — nodes are language-agnostic; a `roscpp` laser node can serve a `rospy` GUI.
- *Distributed computing* — nodes can transparently run on different machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Node States reported by `rosnode list`:** `Alive` (handshake recent), `Stale` (no ping reply for > registered timeout), `Dead` (URI unreachable).

#### 2.1.2 ROS Master — The Coordination Authority
The **ROS Master** (`rosmaster`) is a special node — but unlike a normal node, it does **not** participate in data transport. It performs four core duties:

1. **Registration of nodes** — accepts XMLRPC registrations.
2. **Topic & Service registration** — tracks the publishers and subscribers of every topic, and the servers/clients of every service.
3. **Parameter Server hosting** — exposes the central key/value store.
4. **Connection Negotiation** — when node A wants to subscribe to a topic that node B publishes, the Master hands B's connection URI to A, and **the two nodes then talk directly peer-to-peer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** The Master is a **rendezvous point only**. Once the connection is established, data flows **directly** between nodes. If the Master dies, **existing peer-to-peer streams keep working** — but no new node discovery or new topic subscriptions can be formed.

The Master is **not** a router, **not** a message broker, and **not** a single point of data flow — it is a **directory service** (analogous to DNS).

#### 2.1.3 Parameter Server — The Global Blackboard
A **shared, multi-variate dictionary** keyed by ASCII strings, with values that may be integers, floats, booleans, strings, ISO-8601 dates, lists, or base64-encoded binary blobs. Typical use:
- Robot physical parameters (wheel radius, PID gains).
- Topic remapping rules.
- Per-node configuration flags.

Accessed via `rosparam` CLI or `getParam/setParam` API calls.

#### 2.1.4 Topics — Asynchronous Many-to-Many Buses
A **Topic** is a named bus over which **anonymous, asynchronous, strongly-typed messages** are passed. Semantics:
- **Anonymous** — publishers do not know subscribers and vice-versa.
- **Asynchronous** — fire-and-forget, no return value, no acknowledgement.
- **Strongly-typed** — every message has a fixed `.msg` schema (e.g., `sensor_msgs/LaserScan`).
- **Many-to-Many** — N publishers and M subscribers may share one topic.

The transport protocol is **TCPROS** by default (reliable, ordered, stream-oriented). **UDPROS** is available for lossy, low-latency sensor streams.

#### 2.1.5 Services — Synchronous One-to-One RPC
A **Service** is a named, strongly-typed request/reply endpoint. Semantics:
- **One-to-one** — exactly one server, any number of clients (but replies go to one).
- **Synchronous** — the client blocks until the server replies or times out.
- **Typed** — defined by a pair of `.srv` files: request message + response message.

Services are ideal for discrete queries (e.g., `get_map`, `toggle_motor`, `reset_odometry`) and for **infrequent control commands** that require acknowledgement.

#### 2.1.6 Bags — Off-Line Time Machine
A **Bag** is a `.bag` file — a binary log of topic messages and their timestamps, produced by `rosbag record` and replayed by `rosbag play`. Bags allow:
- Reproducible debugging.
- Off-line algorithm development without live hardware.
- Dataset creation for machine learning.

> [!CAUTION]
> Bags capture the *stream*, not the *graph* — a bag replay does **not** bring up the original Master; it simply injects recorded messages into whichever topic names the replayer is configured for.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Symbolic / API Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Discovery Latency | $T_{disc} = 2 \cdot T_{xmlrpc} + T_{lookup}$ | typically $\le \mathbf{100\,ms}$ on LAN |
| 2 | Effective Topic Bandwidth | $B_{topic} = \dfrac{N_{msgs} \cdot S_{avg}}{T_{window}}$ | **bytes/sec** |
| 3 | Service Round-Trip Time | $T_{rpc} = T_{req} + T_{exec} + T_{resp}$ | blocking call, in **seconds** |
| 4 | Node-to-Master URI | $URI_{master} = \text{ROS\_MASTER\_URI} = \texttt{http://\textit{host}:11311}$ | port **11311** |
| 5 | Master Failure Behavior | Throughput $= B_{topic}$ (unchanged); new connections $= 0$ | partial-failure tolerant |
| 6 | Publisher–Subscriber Handshake | $N_{peers} = \vert P \vert + \vert S \vert$ | set sizes |
| 7 | TCPROS Message Header Size | $S_{hdr} = 4\,\text{bytes (length)} + \text{connection header}$ | serialized via **CDR** |
| 8 | UDPROS Loss Probability | $P_{loss} = 1 - (1-p)^n$ for $n$ packets | $p$ = per-packet loss |
| 9 | Bag Replay Clock Multiplier | $t_{replay} = \alpha \cdot t_{record}$, $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}^{+}$ | $\alpha=1$ real-time |
| 10 | Parameter Namespace | $\texttt{/<node\_ns>/<param\_name>}$ | global key path |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU ESE, when asked *"What happens if the ROS Master crashes?"*, the model answer is: *"Existing peer-to-peer TCPROS connections continue to stream data normally; however, no new publishers, subscribers, services, or parameter updates can be registered, and node discovery is suspended until a Master is brought back up."*

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ROS Graph abstraction is used across:
- **Autonomous Vehicles** (Apollo, Autoware) — dozens of nodes for perception, planning, control.
- **Industrial Arms** (UR, Franka) — joint-state, trajectory, and I/O nodes.
- **Warehouse AMRs** — fleet manager, map server, localization, motor controller, safety monitor.
- **Aerial Drones** (PX4-ROS) — offboard control, vision, and mission nodes.
- **Research Platforms** — TurtleBot, Husky, Jackal — used as educational testbeds.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations

#### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Publisher–Subscriber Connection Procedure

When a publisher and subscriber are to be linked across the ROS Graph, the Master performs a **connection handshake** that is purely a directory lookup. We derive the logical sequence below.

**Step 1 — Publisher Registration**
The publisher node $P$ opens an XMLRPC connection to the Master and registers:

$$
P \xrightarrow{\text{XMLRPC}} M : \bigl(\text{registerPublisher}, topic, P_{URI}\bigr)
$$

**Step 2 — Subscriber Registration & Callback**

$$
S \xrightarrow{\text{XMLRPC}} M : \bigl(\text{registerSubscriber}, topic, S_{URI}\bigr)
$$

**Step 3 — Master Response to Subscriber**
The Master returns the publisher's URI to the subscriber:

$$
M \rightarrow S : \bigl\{ P_{URI} \bigr\}
$$

**Step 4 — Subscriber Requests TCPROS Connection to Publisher**

$$
S \xrightarrow{\text{TCPROS}} P : \bigl(\text{requestTopic}, topic, protocols=[TCPROS]\bigr)
$$

**Step 5 — Publisher Acknowledges & Opens Data Socket**

$$
P \rightarrow S : \bigl(\text{connection\_header}, md5sum, type, callerid\bigr)
$$

**Step 6 — Data Stream Begins Peer-to-Peer**

$$
P \;\rightleftharpoons\; S \;\; \text{(direct, Master no longer in the data path)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Total Master involvement} = O(1) \text{ per subscription event}\;}
$$

> **Logic Note:** Steps 1–3 use XMLRPC (control plane); Steps 4–6 use TCPROS (data plane). The Master is *never* a packet router — that is the critical property that allows ROS to scale to high-bandwidth sensor streams.

---

#### 3.1.2 Derivation of Effective Topic Bandwidth

Let $N_{msgs}$ be the number of messages published in observation window $T_{window}$ seconds, and $S_{avg}$ the average serialized message size in bytes.

$$
B_{topic} = \frac{N_{msgs} \cdot S_{avg}}{T_{window}} \quad [\text{bytes/sec}]
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A laser scanner publishes `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` messages, $N_{msgs} = 1080$ scans, $S_{avg} = 6\,\text{KB}$, $T_{window} = 60\,\text{s}$:

$$
B_{topic} = \frac{1080 \times 6 \times 1024}{60}
$$

$$
B_{topic} = \frac{6\,635\,520}{60}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,B_{topic} = 110\,592\,\text{bytes/s} \approx 108\,\text{KiB/s}\,}
$$

This bandwidth figure is **independent** of how many subscribers are connected — TCPROS is a unicast per subscriber, so total switch bandwidth is $\vert S \vert \cdot B_{topic}$. Multi-subscriber broadcast is provided only if a `multimaster` or `nodelet` topology is used.

---

#### 3.1.3 Derivation of Service Round-Trip Time

For a synchronous service call, let $T_{req}$ be the request serialization + network transit, $T_{exec}$ the server's handler execution, and $T_{resp}$ the response transit. The total blocking time at the client is:

$$
T_{rpc} = T_{req} + T_{exec} + T_{resp}
$$

Typical LAN values: $T_{req}, T_{resp} \approx 0.5\,\text{ms}$ each, $T_{exec} \approx 1$–$5\,\text{ms}$, so:

$$
T_{rpc} \approx 2 \text{–} 6\,\text{ms}
$$

> **Engineering Implication:** Services are **not** suitable for high-rate control loops ($\ge 100\,\text{Hz}$); topics are the correct primitive. Services are for *configuration* and *discrete event* exchanges.

---

#### 3.1.4 Derivation of Master-Failure Throughput Invariant

Let $B_{topic}(t)$ be the live topic bandwidth at time $t$ and $B_{topic}^{pre}$ the bandwidth immediately before the Master dies at $t = t_f$.

$$
\forall\, t > t_f : \quad B_{topic}(t) = B_{topic}^{pre}
$$

because the **Master is not in the data path** for already-established TCPROS connections. Conversely, for any new subscription attempt at $t > t_f$:

$$
B_{new\,sub}(t) = 0
$$

because XMLRPC registration to the Master is required, and the Master is unreachable.

$$
\boxed{\;B_{existing}(t) = B_{pre},\quad B_{new}(t) = 0 \quad \text{when Master is down}\;}
$$

This is a **partial-failure tolerant** design — graceful degradation, not hard stop.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Graph Entity | Communication Style | Sync/Async | Cardinality | Best Use Case | Survives Master Crash? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Topic** | Anonymous publish/subscribe | Async | Many-to-many | Continuous sensor / actuator streams | **Yes** (existing streams) |
| **Service** | Named request/reply | Sync | One-to-one (multi-client) | Configuration, discrete queries | **No** (new calls) |
| **Action** (extension) | Goal + feedback + result | Async w/ feedback | One-to-one | Long-running preemptible tasks | **No** (new goals) |
| **Parameter** | Read/write key-value | Sync | Many-readers / one-writer | Static configuration | **No** (new reads) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ROS Graph — Full Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below renders the complete **functional architecture** of a canonical ROS Graph containing a camera node, an image-processing node, a motor-control node, a planning service, and the Master + Parameter Server, plus a bag recorder for off-line capture.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL PLANE  XMLRPC on :11311"]
        MASTER["ROS Master  ros_master"]
        PARAM["Parameter Server  /robot/wheel_radius  /planner/max_vel"]
    end

    subgraph CP1["COMPUTATIONAL NODES"]
        CAM["cam_node  roscpp  /usb_cam"]
        IMG["img_proc_node  rospy  /image_proc"]
        MOT["motor_node  roscpp  /cmd_vel"]
        PLAN["plan_server  roscpp  /make_plan"]
    end

    subgraph TOPICS["TOPIC BUSES  TCPROS"]
        T1["/camera/image_raw  sensor_msgs/Image"]
        T2["/cmd_vel  geometry_msgs/Twist"]
        T3["/odom  nav_msgs/Odometry"]
    end

    subgraph SVC["SERVICES  TCPROS RPC"]
        S1["/make_plan  nav_msgs/GetPlan"]
        S2["/reset_odom  std_srvs/Empty"]
    end

    subgraph OFFLINE["OFFLINE STORAGE"]
        BAG["rosbag record  output.bag"]
    end

    CAM -- "PUB" --> T1
    T1 -- "SUB" --> IMG
    IMG -- "PUB" --> T3
    T3 -- "SUB" --> MOT
    MOT -- "PUB" --> T2
    T2 -- "SUB" --> CAM

    PLAN -. "SRV" .-> S1
    MOT  -. "SRV" .-> S2

    MASTER --- CAM
    MASTER --- IMG
    MASTER --- MOT
    MASTER --- PLAN
    PARAM  --- PLAN
    PARAM  --- MOT

    T1 -. "LOG" .-> BAG
    T3 -. "LOG" .-> BAG
    T2 -. "LOG" .-> BAG
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows are **topic data flow** (publisher → topic → subscriber). Dotted arrows are **service calls** or **bag recording taps**. Dashed lines without arrow are **control-plane registrations** to the Master. The Master is connected to *every* node but sits *outside* the data flow.

---

### 4.2 Publisher–Subscriber Handshake Sequence

The following sequence diagram shows the **temporal** order of XMLRPC + TCPROS messages exchanged between a subscriber, the Master, and a publisher.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Subscriber Node
    participant M as ROS Master
    participant P as Publisher Node

    S->>M: registerSubscriber(topic=/scan)
    M-->>S: OK + list of publisher URIs
    P->>M: registerPublisher(topic=/scan)
    M-->>P: OK
    S->>P: requestTopic(/scan, protocols=[TCPROS])
    P-->>S: TCPROS connection header (md5sum, type)
    Note over S,P: DIRECT PEER-TO-PEER STREAM BEGINS
    P-->>S: Message #1
    P-->>S: Message #2
    P-->>S: Message #3
    M-->>M: Master is no longer in the data path
```

> **Key Visual Insight:** After the third line of dialogue, **the Master drops out** of the conversation. The subscriber and publisher carry on a long-lived direct socket — exactly why ROS can sustain high-bandwidth streams.

---

### 4.3 Master-Failure Degradation State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Healthy
    Healthy: Master UP  -  All operations nominal
    Healthy --> Degraded: Master crash  (t = tf)
    Degraded: Master DOWN  -  Existing TCPROS streams continue
    Degraded --> Healthy: Master restart + node re-registration
    Degraded --> [*]: Process exit  (no recovery)
    note right of Degraded
      New topics / services / parameters
      CANNOT be created.
      Pre-existing topic streams
      continue at full bandwidth.
    end note
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define the **ROS Graph**. List its principal components and state the role of the ROS Master within it. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The ROS Graph is the **peer-to-peer network of ROS processes (nodes)** that are concurrently running and communicating via topics, services, and the parameter server at any given moment.

Principal components:
1. **Nodes** — executable processes.
2. **ROS Master** — name service / directory.
3. **Parameter Server** — global key-value store.
4. **Topics** — async pub/sub buses.
5. **Services** — sync request/reply RPCs.
6. **Messages** — typed data records.
7. **Bags** — recorded topic streams.

**Role of the Master:** The Master registers nodes, tracks publishers/subscribers of each topic, hosts the Parameter Server, and **negotiates direct peer-to-peer connections** between nodes. After handshake, data flows **bypassing the Master**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Differentiate between a **ROS Topic** and a **ROS Service**. Mention one typical use-case of each. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Topic | Service |
|---|---|---|
| Communication | Asynchronous, anonymous | Synchronous, named |
| Pattern | Many-to-many pub/sub | One-to-one RPC |
| Cardinality | N publishers, M subscribers | 1 server, many clients |
| Reply | No acknowledgement | Returns response |
| Use-case | Streaming `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` from a lidar | Calling `nav_msgs/GetPlan` to compute a path on demand |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the ROS Graph**, clearly distinguishing between the **control plane (XMLRPC)** and the **data plane (TCPROS/UDPROS)**. Identify the role of the **ROS Master** and the **Parameter Server**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **complete publisher–subscriber handshake** step by step, showing what role the Master plays in each step and at what point the data path becomes **peer-to-peer**. Explain what happens to live topic streams **if the Master crashes mid-operation**. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution (a)

The ROS Graph contains two orthogonal planes:

- **Control Plane (XMLRPC, port 11311):** Used for *registration*, *lookup*, and *parameter* operations. All nodes maintain a persistent XMLRPC connection to the Master.
- **Data Plane (TCPROS / UDPROS):** Used for *message streaming* once the connection has been negotiated.

A block diagram (see Section 4.1) shows:
- `cam_node`, `img_proc_node`, `motor_node`, `plan_server` as computational vertices.
- Topics `/camera/image_raw`, `/cmd_vel`, `/odom` as edges.
- Services `/make_plan`, `/reset_odom` as dotted edges.
- The **ROS Master** connected to every node (control plane only).
- The **Parameter Server** as a sub-component of the Master, exposing keys like `/robot/wheel_radius` and `/planner/max_vel`.

**Role of Master:** directory service — registers nodes, topics, services, and hands connection URIs to subscribers.
**Role of Parameter Server:** globally readable, sparsely writable key-value store used for static configuration.

##### Model Solution (b)

The handshake proceeds in six steps (see Section 3.1.1 derivation):

1. Publisher $P$ calls `registerPublisher(topic, P_URI)` to the Master via XMLRPC.
2. Subscriber $S$ calls `registerSubscriber(topic, S_URI)` to the Master via XMLRPC.
3. Master returns $P$'s URI to $S$.
4. $S$ opens a TCPROS connection directly to $P$ and calls `requestTopic`.
5. $P$ returns the connection header (md5sum, message type).
6. Data flows **directly $P \rightleftharpoons S$** — the Master is no longer involved.

**Master-crash behavior:** Because the Master is **not in the data path**, any **already-established** TCPROS stream continues to deliver messages at full bandwidth. However, no **new** publishers, subscribers, service calls, or parameter operations can be registered. The system is therefore **partially fault-tolerant** — graceful degradation, not hard stop.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **three communication paradigms** of ROS — **Topics, Services, and Parameter Server** — with their semantics, cardinality, and a real-world robotic example for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **ROS Bag**? Explain how `rosbag record` and `rosbag play` are used in the development workflow. Discuss **two limitations** of using bags. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution (a)

1. **Topics** — Asynchronous, anonymous, **many-to-many** publish/subscribe bus carrying strongly-typed messages. TCPROS by default. *Example:* `/scan` topic publishing `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` from a 2D lidar at 40 Hz to both the SLAM node and the obstacle-detection node simultaneously.
2. **Services** — Synchronous, **one-to-one** remote procedure call defined by a `.srv` file (request + response). *Example:* `/spawn` service on the `turtlesim` node, where a client sends a request containing the turtle's name, $(x,y,\theta)$ and receives the assigned turtle ID as a response.
3. **Parameter Server** — Globally readable, **multi-reader / multi-writer** key-value dictionary, scoped by namespace. *Example:* Setting `/camera/exposure = 100` to dynamically retune a USB camera at runtime.

##### Model Solution (b)

A **ROS Bag** is a binary file (`.bag`) that records the **serialized byte stream** of one or more topics along with their **timestamps**. It captures the *data plane* of the ROS Graph, not the *control plane*.

**`rosbag record`** — Subscribes to specified topics and writes their messages to disk in real time. Typical usage:

```text
rosbag record -O my_session.bag /scan /cmd_vel /odom
```

**`rosbag play`** — Re-publishes the recorded messages onto the same topic names, optionally with a clock multiplier, allowing algorithms to be re-run against identical inputs.

**Two limitations:**
1. Bags capture only **messages and timestamps** — the original node graph (executables, services, parameters) is *not* preserved, so a replay cannot fully restore the original runtime.
2. Bags are **append-only and format-fragile** — partial corruption of the index file can prevent playback; no native random-access or query language is provided without external tools (e.g., `rosbags` Python library).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Stating the Master is a *message router*.** It is NOT. It is a *directory / name service*. Once connections are formed, data bypasses the Master. (–2 marks in 14-mark Qs)
> 2. **Confusing Topics with Services.** Topics are *asynchronous and anonymous*; Services are *synchronous and named*. Many students write "topics are used for commands" — that is wrong; services or actions are used for commands.
> 3. **Forgetting that the Master does NOT carry data.** Any answer implying "messages flow through the Master" is factually incorrect and will lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning XMLRPC vs TCPROS separation** in 7-mark architecture questions — examiners explicitly look for the *control plane vs data plane* distinction.
> 5. **Omitting bandwidth or latency units** when discussing topics — always give concrete numbers (e.g., $40\,\text{Hz}$, $\sim 108\,\text{KiB/s}$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ The **ROS Graph** = live peer-to-peer network of ROS nodes + their communication links.
- ✅ **Node** = single-purpose executable; many small nodes is the philosophy.
- ✅ **ROS Master** = **name service + directory only**; *not* a data router.
- ✅ **Parameter Server** = global key-value store, hosted by the Master.
- ✅ **Topic** = anonymous, async, **many-to-many**, strongly-typed message bus (TCPROS by default).
- ✅ **Service** = synchronous, **one-to-one**, strongly-typed RPC with request + response.
- ✅ **Bag** = binary time-stamped log of topic messages; for off-line replay / debugging.
- ✅ **Port 11311** = default XMLRPC port of the Master; `ROS_MASTER_URI` env var selects it.
- ✅ **Control plane (XMLRPC) vs Data plane (TCPROS/UDPROS)** — strict separation.
- ✅ **Master crash behavior:** existing streams keep flowing, but no new discovery is possible.
- ✅ **Default Master URI:** `http://localhost:11311`.
- ✅ Tools: `rosnode`, `rostopic`, `rosservice`, `rosparam`, `rosbag`, `rqt_graph`.
- ✅ **TCPROS** = reliable, ordered, stream-oriented (default).
- ✅ **UDPROS** = lossy, low-latency, datagram-style (for high-rate sensors).
- ✅ **Connections are negotiated, not brokered** — Master hands URIs, nodes then talk directly.
- ✅ Nodes are **language-agnostic** (`roscpp`, `rospy`, `roslisp`, `rosjava`).
- ✅ Best practice: one node = one responsibility → fault isolation, reuse, distribution.
- ✅ KTU ESE favourite line: *"The ROS Master does not carry messages; it carries connections."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: roscore
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** roscore

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** architecture, `roscore` is a fundamental **meta-package launcher** (technically a collection of three essential core processes bundled together) that bootstraps and orchestrates the foundational infrastructure required for any ROS-based distributed robotic system. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *OERAT722 - Introduction to Robot Operating System*, `roscore` is formally defined as:

> **`roscore`** is the master initialization script that simultaneously launches the **ROS Master**, the **ROS Parameter Server**, and the **`rosout` logging node**, establishing the global name resolution namespace, the distributed parameter tree, and the centralized logging backbone that every other ROS node depends upon for communication.

The command `roscore` is the **first command** that any ROS developer executes before launching any node, topic, service, or action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The official syllabus statement specifies that students must understand *"ROS architecture, ROS Master, roscore, nodes, topics, messages, and services."* `roscore` is therefore an **exam-favorite topic** because it directly bridges the conceptual layer (architecture) with the practical layer (commands you type in the terminal).

### The Three Sub-Processes Launched by `roscore`

When the user types `roscore` in a terminal, the launch script transparently starts the following three core processes:

| Sub-Process | Executable | Primary Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| **ROS Master** | `rosmaster` (or `master`) | Provides **name registration** and **lookup services** to all nodes |
| **ROS Parameter Server** | `rosparam` / `parameter_server` | Hosts the global, distributed, key-value **parameter tree** |
| **`rosout` Node** | `rosout` | Subscribes to `/rosout` topic and logs messages to `/rosout_agg` |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are setting up a **giant international conference with thousands of participants** (the ROS nodes):

- The **ROS Master** acts as the **reception desk and telephone directory**. When a node named `camera_publisher` arrives, it registers its name and address (its **URI** like `http://192.168.1.5:45123`) at the reception. Other nodes that want to talk to `camera_publisher` call the reception desk to ask, *"Where do I find camera_publisher?"*
- The **Parameter Server** is like a **shared noticeboard** pinned to the wall of the lobby. Anyone can post a sticky note like `"robot_max_speed": 1.5` or read someone else's sticky note. It is a **shared, globally-readable dictionary**.
- The **`rosout` node** is the **CCTV and incident logbook** of the conference. Every participant is required to report their status ("I started", "I am publishing 30 Hz", "I crashed with error XYZ") to a central recorder, which keeps a permanent history.

> [!NOTE]
> **Without `roscore` running, NO ROS node can discover any other node, NO topic connection can be made, and NO parameter can be shared.** Running `roscore` is therefore the *sine qua non* (essential precondition) of every ROS session.

### Key Terminology Box

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Master URI** | The network address (default `http://localhost:11311`) where the ROS Master listens for registration requests |
| **Port 11311** | Default TCP port reserved by the ROS Master; this is a **de facto standard constant** |
| **`ROS_MASTER_URI`** | An **environment variable** that overrides the default Master location for multi-machine setups |
| **`ROS_HOSTNAME`** | An **environment variable** that tells nodes which IP address to advertise as their own |
| **`rosout`** | The standardized logging topic (level `INFO`, `WARN`, `ERROR`, `FATAL`) |
| **`roscore` exit code** | Returned when the user presses `Ctrl+C`; cleanly shuts down all three sub-processes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default port number `11311` and default URI `http://localhost:11311` are board-exam constants** — memorize them. They have appeared in previous university examinations for 3-mark sub-questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Lifecycle of `roscore`

The execution of `roscore` follows a deterministic five-stage lifecycle. Each stage has a verifiable outcome that examiners may test.

**Stage 1 — Invocation and Environment Resolution**
The user issues the command in any active shell:
```bash
$ roscore
```
The shell executes the **`roscore` bash script** located at `/opt/ros/<distro>/bin/roscore`. The script first checks for the mandatory environment variables `ROS_MASTER_URI` and `ROS_HOSTNAME`. If unset, defaults are applied: `http://localhost:11311` and the machine's IP.

**Stage 2 — Master Process Spawn**
The script launches the **`rosmaster`** Python process. This process opens a **TCP socket on port `11311`** using `xmlrpc` protocol (XML-RPC over HTTP). It maintains:
- A **registration table** mapping node names → URIs.
- A **topic/service registry** mapping topic names → publisher/subscriber URIs.

**Stage 3 — Parameter Server Spawn**
The script launches the **`parameter_server`** process, also on the same XML-RPC infrastructure. It exposes a hierarchical key-value store accessible through `rosparam` command-line tools or client library APIs (`rospy`, `roscpp`).

**Stage 4 — `rosout` Node Spawn**
A specialized C++ node named `/rosout` is launched. This node:
- Subscribes to the global `/rosout` topic.
- Aggregates messages to `/rosout_agg`.
- Writes logs to `$ROS_HOME/log/` (typically `~/.ros/log/`).

**Stage 5 — Steady-State and Cleanup**
The terminal becomes a **live log console** for the master. The session ends when the user presses `Ctrl+C`, triggering a graceful shutdown via `SIGINT` (signal 2). All spawned child processes receive the signal and terminate in reverse order.

### 2.2 Why `roscore` is Architecturally Central

ROS follows the **publish/subscribe (pubsub) pattern** combined with **Remote Procedure Calls (RPC)**. In a pure pubsub system, any node could theoretically broadcast to any topic. ROS chose a **centralized directory** model for two engineering reasons:

1. **Discovery Scalability**: A purely-broadcast discovery (every node shouts "I exist" to the whole LAN) does not scale beyond ~50 nodes on a busy network. A central registry allows **O(1) lookups**.
2. **Late Binding & Dynamic Reconfiguration**: Robots need to discover new sensors plugged in via USB *at runtime*. The Master supports **late binding** — connections are made *only when both ends are running*, not at compile time.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why Behind the Architecture:** Note that `roscore` is a **single point of failure** in classic ROS 1. If it crashes, *no new connections can be made* (existing pubsub links keep working, but discovery is lost). This limitation motivated **ROS 2** to switch to **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** with decentralized discovery. KTU examiners sometimes ask this as a 7-mark question comparing ROS 1 vs ROS 2.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since `roscore` is a systems/architecture topic rather than a numerical-engineering topic, the "formula sheet" below summarizes the **constants, environment variables, ports, and operational metrics** you must memorize.

| Symbol / Constant | Value / Definition | Unit / Format | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_{master}$ | Default Master TCP port | **`11311`** | Reserved IANA-equivalent ROS port |
| $U_{master}$ | Default Master URI | `http://localhost:11311` | Override via `ROS_MASTER_URI` |
| $T_{reg}$ | Node registration table size | **unbounded** | Cleared only at master shutdown |
| $T_{topic}$ | Topic registry size | **unbounded** | Pub/sub metadata store |
| $N_{processes}$ | Sub-processes spawned | **`3`** (master + param + rosout) | Required to be exact in answers |
| $F_{xmlrpc}$ | RPC protocol | **XML-RPC over HTTP** | Application-layer protocol |
| $V_{log}$ | Log directory | `~/.ros/log/` | Controlled by `$ROS_HOME` |
| $S_{signal}$ | Termination signal | **SIGINT (signal 2)** | From `Ctrl+C` |
| $\Delta t_{boot}$ | Typical boot time | ~1–3 seconds | Time from `roscore` to `started core service` banner |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

`roscore` (and its ROS 2 equivalent `daemon` + DDS layer) underpins:

- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs)** in warehouses (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics).
- **Autonomous Vehicles** at the prototyping stage (Autoware).
- **Drone swarms** (PX4-ROS coupling).
- **Industrial Robotic Arms** (Universal Robots, ABB via ROS drivers).
- **Research Humanoids** (PR2, Atlas, NAO).

> Every ROS-based robot you read about in industry journals has a `roscore` (or DDS equivalent) running on its onboard computer as the first user-space process after boot.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Because `roscore` is a *systems* topic rather than a *mathematical* one, the "derivations" below are **architectural and protocol-level step-by-step walkthroughs** that KTU examiners expect for full 7-mark answers. Each transition is explicitly stated.

### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: What Happens When You Type `roscore`?

We model the runtime as a sequence of **events** $E_1, E_2, \dots, E_n$ ordered by causality.

$$E_1 \rightarrow E_2 \rightarrow E_3 \rightarrow E_4 \rightarrow E_5 \rightarrow E_6 \rightarrow E_7$$

We now explicitly write out every transition.

**Step 1 — Shell parses the command and locates the script.**

The shell consults `$PATH` and resolves `roscore` to `/opt/ros/<distro>/bin/roscore`. This is a **bash script**, not a binary. The script sources `/opt/ros/<distro>/setup.bash` to ensure `PYTHONPATH` and `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` include ROS libraries.

**Step 2 — Environment variable resolution.**

The script checks for the existence of `$ROS_MASTER_URI`. We can formally write this conditional as:

$$
U_{master} = \begin{cases} \$(\text{ROS\_MASTER\_URI}) & \text{if defined} \\ \text{"http://localhost:11311"} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

This is the **defaulting rule** — worth memorising because examiners love environment-variable questions.

**Step 3 — ROS Master spawn.**

The script invokes `rosmaster --core -p 11311`. This Python process:
- Opens TCP listening socket on port $P_{master} = 11311$.
- Initializes two in-memory data structures: the **registration table** $R$ and the **topic/service registry** $T$.

$$
R = \{ (n_i, u_i) \mid n_i \text{ is a node name, } u_i \text{ is its XML-RPC URI} \}
$$

$$
T = \{ (t_j, P_j, S_j) \mid t_j \text{ topic name, } P_j \text{ publishers, } S_j \text{ subscribers} \}
$$

**Step 4 — Parameter Server spawn.**

Launches `parameter_server` which shares the same XML-RPC server socket and exposes CRUD primitives (`set`, `get`, `has`, `delete`, `search`) on a hierarchical namespace. The parameter tree is logically:

$$
\Pi = \{ (k, v) \mid k \in \text{namespace path}, v \in \{ \text{int, float, string, bool, list, dict, yaml} \} \}
$$

**Step 5 — `rosout` node spawn.**

Launches a C++ executable `rosout` which:
- Advertises itself as subscriber of `/rosout` topic (of message type `rosgraph_msgs/Log`).
- Publishes to `/rosout_agg` (aggregated stream).
- Opens a file handle to `$ROS_HOME/log/rosout.log` for persistent disk logging.

**Step 6 — Steady-state banner printed to terminal.**

A standard banner appears:

```
SUMMARY
========
PARAMETERS
 * /rosdistro: noetic
 * /rosversion: 1.16.0

NODES
auto-starting new master
process[master-1]: started with pid [12345]
ROS_MASTER_URI=http://localhost:11311/
```

The banner reveals the three live sub-processes plus the parameters read from the server.

**Step 7 — Idle await.**

The master process loops indefinitely on the XML-RPC server, awaiting registration calls. The terminal is *blocked* — this is why you must open a **second terminal** for every other node.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Sub-Processes

| Property | ROS Master | Parameter Server | rosout Node |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Language** | Python | Python | C++ |
| **Protocol** | XML-RPC | XML-RPC | Native ROS pubsub |
| **Port** | 11311 | 11311 (shared) | None (uses normal ROS port) |
| **Data Model** | Tables $R$, $T$ | Hierarchical key-value $\Pi$ | Append-only log stream |
| **Mutability** | Read/write via RPC | Read/write via RPC | Read-only consumer |
| **Failure impact** | **Catastrophic** — no new connections | Read defaults only | Logs simply stop |
| **Replacement in ROS 2** | Decentralized via DDS | Replaced by parameter services | Replaced by `ros2 topic echo /rosout` |

### 3.3 The Mathematical Model of Late Binding

A key 7-mark KTU question is: *"How does ROS achieve late binding via roscore?"* We model this rigorously.

Let a publisher node $P$ and subscriber node $S$ both register with the Master at times $t_P$ and $t_S$ respectively. The Master stores:

$$
R(t) = \{ (P, u_P, t_P),\ (S, u_S, t_S) \}
$$

When both are present, the connection time $t_{conn}$ is:

$$
t_{conn} = \max(t_P, t_S) + \Delta_{rpc}
$$

where $\Delta_{rpc}$ is the round-trip time of the XML-RPC handshake (typically < 5 ms on localhost). Before this time, no TCP connection exists; after this time, the publisher and subscriber communicate **directly**, **bypassing the Master**. The Master only **serves the URI lookup**, not the data plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master is NOT in the data path.** This is a frequent board-exam trap. Once pubsub links are established, sensor data flows **directly** between nodes via TCP. The Master is only consulted for discovery.

### 3.4 Exhaustive Content: What Happens When `roscore` Crashes?

We extend the event model. Suppose at time $t_c$, the Master process receives `SIGSEGV` (segmentation fault) due to a malformed RPC payload.

$$
\forall i : \text{node}_i \in R(t_c) \implies \text{node}_i \text{ detects failure via heartbeat timeout}
$$

The default heartbeat timeout is governed by the parameter `XMLRPC heartbeat timeout` (typically 30 seconds). When exceeded, every node marks the Master as **dead** and:
1. Existing TCP pubsub links **continue** (data flows unaffected).
2. New nodes cannot register.
3. New publishers cannot be discovered.
4. Parameter lookups return `None`.

This is why KTU Module 1 emphasizes that `roscore` is **not designed for high-availability robotic deployments** — ROS 2 was built precisely to eliminate this single point of failure.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of `roscore` Components

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the three sub-processes, their ports, and the data flow during a typical ROS session.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    User["User Terminal Ctrl+C signal"] --> RoscoreScript["roscore bash script /opt/ros/distro/bin/roscore"]
    RoscoreScript --> EnvCheck{"ROS_MASTER_URI set?"}
    EnvCheck -- Yes --> UseUri["Use existing URI"]
    EnvCheck -- No --> DefaultUri["Default http://localhost:11311"]

    UseUri --> SpawnMaster["rosmaster --core -p 11311"]
    DefaultUri --> SpawnMaster

    SpawnMaster --> MasterProcess["ROS MASTER Python XML-RPC server port 11311"]
    MasterProcess --> RegTable["Registration Table R node to URI mapping"]
    MasterProcess --> TopicTable["Topic Registry T pubsub metadata"]

    SpawnMaster --> SpawnParam["parameter_server same XML-RPC port"]
    SpawnParam --> ParamTree["Parameter Tree Pi key-value YAML"]

    SpawnMaster --> SpawnRosout["rosout C++ node subscriber of /rosout"]
    SpawnRosout --> RosoutLog["File ~/.ros/log/rosout.log"]
    SpawnRosout --> RosoutAgg["Publishes to /rosout_agg"]

    MasterProcess -.lookup.-> Pub["Publisher Node"]
    MasterProcess -.lookup.-> Sub["Subscriber Node"]
    MasterProcess -.lookup.-> Srv["Service Server"]
    Pub <==> Sub
    Sub <==> Srv
    ParamTree -.get/set.-> Pub
    ParamTree -.get/set.-> Sub
    SpawnRosout -.logs.-> Pub
    SpawnRosout -.logs.-> Sub
```

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram: Discovery Workflow Using `roscore`

The diagram below shows the temporal sequence of XML-RPC calls when a publisher and subscriber discover each other through `roscore`.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Shell
    participant RC as roscore
    participant P as Publisher Node
    participant S as Subscriber Node

    U->>RC: roscore (terminal 1)
    RC->>RC: spawn ROS Master on port 11311
    RC->>RC: spawn Parameter Server
    RC->>RC: spawn rosout node
    RC-->>U: banner "started core service"

    U->>P: rosrun pkg pub_node (terminal 2)
    P->>RC: registerPublisher(topic=chatter, uri=p_uri)
    RC-->>P: ACK with subscriber URIs

    U->>S: rosrun pkg sub_node (terminal 3)
    S->>RC: registerSubscriber(topic=chatter, uri=s_uri)
    RC-->>S: ACK with publisher URIs
    RC->>P: publisherUpdate(topic=chatter, new subscriber s_uri)
    RC->>S: subscriberUpdate(topic=chatter, new publisher p_uri)

    P->>S: TCP connection to s_uri (DIRECT)
    S->>P: ACK handshake
    Note over P,S: Data flows DIRECTLY, Master is bypassed

    P->>RC: log /rosout "Publishing at 10 Hz"
    RC->>RC: rosout writes to ~/.ros/log/rosout.log
```

### 4.3 Lifecycle State Machine of `roscore`

The diagram below captures the deterministic state transitions of `roscore` from invocation to termination.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Invoked: user types roscore
    Invoked --> EnvResolution: parse ROS_MASTER_URI
    EnvResolution --> MasterBoot: spawn rosmaster
    MasterBoot --> ParamBoot: port 11311 listening
    ParamBoot --> RosoutBoot: spawn parameter_server
    RosoutBoot --> SteadyState: spawn /rosout node
    SteadyState --> SteadyState: serve XML-RPC forever
    SteadyState --> Shutdown: SIGINT via Ctrl+C
    Shutdown --> TerminateMaster: kill rosmaster
    TerminateMaster --> TerminateParam: kill parameter_server
    TerminateParam --> TerminateRosout: kill /rosout
    TerminateRosout --> [*]: exit code 0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each transition here corresponds to one of the seven lifecycle events $E_1 \ldots E_7$ derived in Section 3.1. Examiners often ask students to draw this state machine for 7 marks.

### 4.4 Master-Data Plane Separation

A concise conceptual map highlighting that the Master handles only **discovery**, while the **data plane** is fully decentralized.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ControlPlane["CONTROL PLANE discovery via roscore"]
        Master["ROS Master port 11311"]
    end
    subgraph DataPlane["DATA PLANE direct pubsub bypass Master"]
        Cam["/camera_node"]
        ImgProc["/image_processor"]
        Ctrl["/motion_controller"]
    end
    Master -.URI lookup.-> Cam
    Master -.URI lookup.-> ImgProc
    Master -.URI lookup.-> Ctrl
    Cam -- direct TCP --> ImgProc
    ImgProc -- direct TCP --> Ctrl
    Cam -- direct TCP --> Ctrl
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**  
What is `roscore`? List the three core processes launched by it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`roscore` is the **master initialization script** in ROS that bootstraps the foundational services required for all ROS nodes to communicate. It must be run before launching any other node.

When executed, `roscore` launches three core processes:

1. **ROS Master** — Provides name registration and lookup services for nodes, topics, and services. Listens on default TCP port **`11311`**.
2. **ROS Parameter Server** — Hosts a globally shared, hierarchical key-value parameter tree accessible by all nodes via `rosparam` or client APIs.
3. **`rosout` Node** — A specialized logging node that subscribes to the `/rosout` topic and persists log messages to disk in `~/.ros/log/rosout.log`.

> **Marking tip:** Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for listing the three processes, and 1 mark for mentioning port `11311` and/or the default URI.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**  
What is the default port number and URI of the ROS Master? How can it be overridden?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The default **TCP port** of the ROS Master is **`11311`**, and the default **Master URI** is **`http://localhost:11311`**.

The default URI can be overridden by setting the environment variable `ROS_MASTER_URI` before launching `roscore`. For example, in a multi-machine setup where the Master runs on a remote computer with IP `192.168.1.10`:

```bash
export ROS_MASTER_URI=http://192.168.1.10:11311
roscore
```

On every client machine, the same `ROS_MASTER_URI` must be exported so that nodes register and discover against the remote Master.

> **Marking tip:** 1 mark for port number `11311`, 1 mark for default URI, 1 mark for `ROS_MASTER_URI` override syntax.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[Module 1 Internal Choice — students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** **[7 marks]** Explain the architecture of `roscore` with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each of its three sub-processes in detail.

**(b)** **[7 marks]** With the help of a sequence diagram, explain how two nodes (a publisher and a subscriber) discover each other through `roscore`. Discuss what happens when `roscore` is terminated mid-session.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture of `roscore` — 7 marks**

`roscore` is a launcher script (`/opt/ros/<distro>/bin/roscore`) that spawns three cooperating processes. The block diagram is as follows:

```
┌─────────────────────── roscore ─────────────────────────┐
│                                                          │
│   ┌─────────────────────┐   ┌──────────────────────┐     │
│   │   ROS Master        │   │ Parameter Server     │     │
│   │   rosmaster (Python)│   │ parameter_server     │     │
│   │   XML-RPC:11311     │   │ XML-RPC:11311        │     │
│   │   • node registry   │   │ • key-value tree     │     │
│   │   • topic registry  │   │ • set/get/delete     │     │
│   │   • service registry│   │ • namespaced YAML    │     │
│   └─────────────────────┘   └──────────────────────┘     │
│                                                          │
│   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│   │ /rosout node (C++)                             │     │
│   │ • subscribes /rosout  → message Log            │     │
│   │ • publishes /rosout_agg → aggregated stream    │     │
│   │ • writes ~/.ros/log/rosout.log                  │     │
│   └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Roles:**

- **ROS Master (2 marks):** Acts as the *directory service*. Maintains a mapping from symbolic node names to network URIs. When a node wants to publish or subscribe, it asks the Master for the URIs of counterpart nodes. After discovery, the Master is no longer involved — data flows directly between nodes.

- **Parameter Server (2 marks):** Provides a *shared global configuration dictionary*. Nodes can read parameters like `robot_max_speed`, `camera_frame_rate`, `pid_gains` at startup. Supports hierarchical namespaces (e.g., `/camera/intrinsics/fx`). Parameter values can be changed at runtime via `rosparam set`.

- **`rosout` node (2 marks):** Centralized logging aggregator. Every ROS node by default logs to `/rosout`. The `/rosout` node collects these logs, levels them (DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, FATAL), persists to `~/.ros/log/rosout.log`, and republishes aggregated logs to `/rosout_agg` for tools like `rqt_console`.

**Connectivity (1 mark):** Master and Parameter Server share the same XML-RPC socket on port 11311. The `rosout` node is a normal ROS node and communicates via standard pubsub.

---

**(b) Discovery via `roscore` — 7 marks**

The discovery sequence is as follows:

1. **Step 1 (1 mark):** User opens terminal 1 and runs `roscore`. The Master begins listening on port 11311.

2. **Step 2 (1 mark):** User opens terminal 2 and runs `rosrun pkg talker`. The publisher node starts and sends an XML-RPC `registerPublisher` call to the Master with the topic name and its URI.

3. **Step 3 (1 mark):** The Master records the publisher in its topic registry $T$ and replies with the current list of subscriber URIs (initially empty).

4. **Step 4 (1 mark):** User opens terminal 3 and runs `rosrun pkg listener`. The subscriber sends `registerSubscriber` to the Master.

5. **Step 5 (1 mark):** The Master replies to the subscriber with the publisher's URI, and simultaneously sends a `publisherUpdate` notification to the publisher with the new subscriber's URI.

6. **Step 6 (1 mark):** The publisher and subscriber now establish a **direct TCP connection** using the exchanged URIs. The Master is bypassed — all subsequent message data flows peer-to-peer.

7. **Step 7 (1 mark): What happens if `roscore` is killed?** Existing direct TCP pubsub links continue to function. However, new nodes cannot register, new topics cannot be discovered, and parameter lookups return `None`. The robot effectively loses *dynamic reconfiguration* capability until `roscore` is restarted.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** **[7 marks]** What is the role of the ROS Parameter Server? Explain the namespace hierarchy with an example. List any four `rosparam` commands.

**(b)** **[7 marks]** Compare and contrast ROS 1 (`roscore`-based) architecture with ROS 2 (DDS-based) architecture. Why did ROS 2 eliminate `roscore`?

#### Model Solution

**(a) ROS Parameter Server — 7 marks**

**Role (2 marks):** The Parameter Server is a shared, distributed key-value store maintained by the `parameter_server` process inside `roscore`. It allows nodes to read configuration at startup (or runtime) without hard-coding values. Values are stored as YAML primitives: `int`, `float`, `string`, `bool`, `list`, `dict`.

**Namespace Hierarchy (3 marks):** Parameter keys use a hierarchical namespace separated by `/`, identical to filesystem paths.

Example configuration:
```yaml
/robot/physical/max_speed: 1.5
/robot/physical/wheel_radius: 0.075
/camera/intrinsics/fx: 525.0
/camera/intrinsics/fy: 525.0
/camera/intrinsics/cx: 320.0
```

In C++ (`roscpp`):
```cpp
ros::NodeHandle nh("camera/intrinsics");
double fx;
nh.param("fx", fx, 525.0);   // default 525.0 if missing
```

In Python (`rospy`):
```python
fx = rospy.get_param('/camera/intrinsics/fx', 525.0)
```

**Four `rosparam` commands (2 marks):**
1. `rosparam list` — list all parameter keys on the server.
2. `rosparam set /robot/max_speed 2.0` — set a parameter.
3. `rosparam get /robot/max_speed` — read a parameter.
4. `rosparam load params.yaml /my_robot` — bulk load from YAML.
5. `rosparam dump params.yaml` — bulk save to YAML.
6. `rosparam delete /robot/max_speed` — remove a parameter.

---

**(b) ROS 1 vs ROS 2 — 7 marks**

| Property (1 mark each, pick 7) | ROS 1 (`roscore`) | ROS 2 (DDS-based) |
|---|---|---|
| **Discovery** | Centralized via ROS Master | Decentralized via DDS discovery (RTPS protocol) |
| **Middleware** | Custom XML-RPC + TCPROS/UDPROS | OMG DDS standard (CycloneDDS, Fast DDS, RTI Connext) |
| **Single point of failure** | **Yes** — Master crash breaks discovery | **No** — fully decentralized |
| **Real-time support** | None native | Real-time capable via DDS QoS policies |
| **Multi-robot support** | Awkward (one Master per cluster) | Native |
| **Security** | None native | SROS2 — encryption + access control |
| **Platform support** | Linux primary, macOS/Windows partial | Linux, Windows, macOS, RTOS, embedded |
| **Node lifecycle** | Ad-hoc | Managed via lifecycle nodes (configure → activate → deactivate → cleanup) |
| **Default port** | 11311 | No fixed port — DDS uses UDP multicast |

**Why ROS 2 eliminated `roscore` (3 marks):**

1. **Elimination of single point of failure** — A crashed Master breaks all dynamic discovery, which is unacceptable in safety-critical robots (autonomous cars, surgical robots).
2. **Real-time guarantees** — Centralized RPC adds latency jitter that breaks real-time schedulers. DDS uses UDP and zero-copy shared memory for deterministic latency.
3. **Standardization** — DDS is an **Object Management Group (OMG) industry standard** with mature implementations in aerospace, defense, and automotive. ROS 2 inherits this maturity rather than reinventing it.
4. **Multi-machine and embedded support** — DDS works over lossy wireless networks and constrained embedded RTOS environments that ROS 1's XML-RPC cannot.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Lossing Mistakes on `roscore` Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the Master with the data plane.** Many students write *"the Master forwards messages between nodes"*. This is **wrong** and loses 2 marks. The Master only does discovery; data flows directly. *(See Section 3.3.)*
> 2. **Forgetting the `rosout` node.** Students often list only Master + Parameter Server. The `rosout` node is the **third** sub-process and is mandatory in any 7-mark answer.
> 3. **Wrong port number.** The port is **`11311`**, not `8080` (web), not `22` (SSH). Memorise it.
> 4. **Treating `roscore` as a single process.** It is a **meta-launcher** (bash script) that spawns three child processes. Saying "roscore is a process" loses 1 mark in 14-mark answers.
> 5. **Ignoring environment variables.** Any answer that does not mention `ROS_MASTER_URI` and `ROS_HOSTNAME` for multi-machine questions will be marked down 2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing `rosrun` with `roscore`.** `roscore` starts the infrastructure; `rosrun` launches user nodes. Examiners love this as a 3-mark trap.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist for `roscore`**

- ⭐ `roscore` is the **first command** to run in any ROS session; it is the **meta-launcher**.
- ⭐ It spawns **exactly three** sub-processes: **ROS Master**, **Parameter Server**, **`rosout` node**.
- ⭐ Default **TCP port = `11311`**, default **URI = `http://localhost:11311`**.
- ⭐ Master uses **XML-RPC over HTTP** as the RPC mechanism.
- ⭐ Master is **not in the data path** — only in the discovery path. Data flows **peer-to-peer** via TCPROS.
- ⭐ Parameter Server stores **hierarchical YAML key-value pairs** under namespace prefixes like `/camera/intrinsics/fx`.
- ⭐ `rosout` logs to **`~/.ros/log/rosout.log`** and republishes aggregated logs to `/rosout_agg`.
- ⭐ Environment variables: **`ROS_MASTER_URI`** (overrides Master location), **`ROS_HOSTNAME`** (advertises node's own IP).
- ⭐ Termination signal is **SIGINT** from `Ctrl+C`. Clean shutdown is automatic.
- ⭐ `roscore` is a **single point of failure** in ROS 1 — ROS 2 replaces it with **DDS-based decentralized discovery**.
- ⭐ Without `roscore` running, **no new node can register, no new topic can be discovered, and no parameter can be looked up**.
- ⭐ Once pubsub links are made, they survive `roscore` crashes — only **new discovery** is blocked.
- ⭐ KTU weight: `roscore` typically appears as a **3-mark short question** (definition + sub-processes + port) **plus** a **7-mark sub-part** in long questions (architecture diagram or ROS 1 vs ROS 2 comparison).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: catkin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** catkin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Catkin** is the official, default **build system** of the **Robot Operating System (ROS)**, engineered as a successor to the legacy `rosbuild` system. It is fundamentally a *thin, opinionated extension layer* built on top of the cross-platform **CMake** build tool, but augmented with robotics-specific primitives such as message generation, service handling, action generation, and rospack-aware dependency resolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Board Standard):** Catkin is a *meta-build system* that uses parallelized CMake jobs, isolated *devel spaces* per package, and a unified *workspace* model to compile, link, and stage ROS packages written in **C++**, **Python**, or mixed languages into a consistent runtime layout consumable by `roscore`, `roslaunch`, and `rosrun`.

The word *catkin* itself is borrowed from botany — the dangling flower cluster of a birch or willow tree — chosen because **multiple packages can be branched together inside a single workspace** while remaining individually addressable.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy (read this first-time friendly):**
> Imagine a **large commercial kitchen** that serves thousands of dishes (your robot's behaviours).
> - The **kitchen building** = your *catkin workspace*.
> - Each **recipe card** = a *ROS package* (with its own `package.xml` describing ingredients and `CMakeLists.txt` describing cooking steps).
> - The **head chef** = *catkin*, who reads every recipe, gathers ingredients (dependencies), preheats ovens (compilers), and plates everything neatly so that **waiters** (`rosrun`, `roslaunch`) can pick up any dish instantly.
> - The **service window** = the *devel space*, where finished dishes are kept warm and ready.
> - The **walk-in freezer** = the *install space*, where fully-packaged dishes can be stored long-term.

Without a head chef (catkin), every cook would duplicate work, miss dependencies, and serve dishes in incompatible formats. **Catkin enforces order, parallelism, and reproducibility.**

### 1.3 Physical Standards & Metrics Highlighted in Bold

- **Default workspace root directory:** `~/<workspace_name>/`
- **Build concurrency model:** Parallel CMake jobs (one per CPU core by default; KTU board answer expects mention of **`make -j$(nproc)`** semantics).
- **Language support metrics:** C++ (>= C++11), Python 2/3, and any language with a CMake-compatible toolchain.
- **Single source-of-truth metadata file:** `package.xml` (REP-127 compliant).
- **Build-system minimum version:** **CMake $\geq$ 3.0.2** (KTU expects this in numerical answers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 specifically lists *catkin*, *workspaces*, *packages*, *manifest (package.xml)*, and *CMakeLists.txt* as core examinable entities.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of Catkin — Structured Logic Breakdown

Catkin's operation can be decomposed into **six sequential yet parallelisable phases**:

1. **Workspace Discovery Phase**
   - Catkin walks the `src/` directory tree recursively.
   - Every folder containing a `package.xml` is treated as a *catkin package*.
   - The topological dependency graph is computed via `catkin_pkg` (Python API) or `rospack`.

2. **Configuration Phase (`cmake`)**
   - For each package, a CMake project is spawned inside `build/<package_name>/`.
   - `find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS ...)` resolves ROS-specific libraries.
   - Message generators (`genmsg`, `gencpp`, `genpy`) are configured.

3. **Generation Phase**
   - `.msg` files → language-specific headers (`.h`, `.py`).
   - `.srv` files → service stubs.
   - `.action` files → action headers.

4. **Compilation Phase (`make`)**
   - Parallel compilation across all enabled packages.
   - Libraries placed in `devel/lib/`, headers in `devel/include/`, Python modules in `devel/lib/pythonX.Y/dist-packages/`.

5. **Linking & Staging Phase**
   - Public and private headers are *staged* so dependent packages can consume them at build time.
   - Run-time executables become resolvable via ROS environment overlays.

6. **Source / Overlay Phase**
   - User executes `source devel/setup.bash`, which appends `devel/` paths to `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`, `PYTHONPATH`, and `PATH`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Examiners award marks for the *sequence* (discovery → config → generate → compile → stage → overlay) and for naming the **isolated devel space** as a key advantage over rosbuild.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Catkin Concepts at a Glance

| # | Concept | File / Command | Purpose / Function | Exam Frequency |
|---|---------|----------------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Workspace root | `catkin_ws/` | Container for all catkin packages | Very High |
| 2 | Source space | `src/` | Stores package source code | Very High |
| 3 | Build space | `build/` | Holds intermediate CMake/Make artifacts | High |
| 4 | Devel space | `devel/` | Holds built targets + setup scripts | Very High |
| 5 | Install space | `install/` | Holds final installable artifacts | Medium |
| 6 | Init workspace | `catkin_init_workspace` | Creates top-level `CMakeLists.txt` symlink | High |
| 7 | Build all | `catkin_make` | Sequential CMake + Make for whole workspace | Very High |
| 8 | Isolated build | `catkin build` (catkin_tools) | Per-package isolated devel spaces, parallel | High |
| 9 | Package manifest | `package.xml` | Declares name, version, deps, maintainer | Very High |
| 10 | Build script | `CMakeLists.txt` | Declares targets, deps, msg/srv generation | Very High |
| 11 | Overlay env | `source devel/setup.bash` | Adds workspace to ROS_PACKAGE_PATH | High |
| 12 | Find component | `find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS roscpp std_msgs)` | Pulls ROS headers/libs | High |
| 13 | Declare run dep | `<exec_depend>roscpp</exec_depend>` | Required at *runtime* | High |
| 14 | Declare build dep | `<build_depend>roscpp</build_depend>` | Required at *compile time* | High |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Rule:** In the table above, every `|` separating columns is *markdown syntax only*. When writing absolute-value notation in exam answers (e.g., $\vert x \vert$), always use **`\vert`** or **`\mid`** in LaTeX so that the table never breaks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Catkin's Role |
|--------|--------------|
| Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs) | Builds multi-package perception + planning stacks. |
| Industrial Robotic Arms | Compiles MoveIt! configurations alongside drivers. |
| Drone Swarms | Parallel build of 50+ packages cuts CI time from minutes to seconds. |
| Research Prototyping | Clean overlay model lets students swap navigation stacks without re-installing the OS. |
| Production CI/CD Pipelines | Reproducible isolated builds for Docker images. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — The Catkin Build Lifecycle

Below is the **complete algebraic-style flow** of how catkin transforms raw source into a runnable ROS workspace. Each step states the *input*, *transformation*, and *output* explicitly.

### Step 1 — Workspace Initialisation

The user executes, from the terminal:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src
cd ~/catkin_ws/src
catkin_init_workspace
```

**Input state:**
$$
\text{Filesystem} = \{\sim/\text{catkin\_ws}/\text{src}/\}
$$

**Transformation:**
$$
\text{catkin\_init\_workspace} \rightarrow \text{creates} \;\; \sim/\text{catkin\_ws/src/CMakeLists.txt}
$$

**Output state:**
$$
\text{Filesystem} = \{\sim/\text{catkin\_ws}/\text{src}/\text{CMakeLists.txt}\}
$$

The generated `CMakeLists.txt` contains only one functional line:

```cmake
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.0.2)
project(catkin_ws)
```

### Step 2 — Adding a Package

A package may be created manually or generated via:

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws/src
catkin_create_pkg beginner_tutorials std_msgs rospy roscpp
```

**Transformation logic:**
$$
\{\text{std\_msgs},\;\text{rospy},\;\text{roscpp}\} \xrightarrow{\text{catkin\_create\_pkg}} \text{package skeleton}
$$

This generates three artefacts inside `beginner_tutorials/`:

1. `package.xml` — metadata manifest.
2. `CMakeLists.txt` — build script.
3. `include/beginner_tutorials/` — public header directory.
4. `src/beginner_tutorials.cpp` — example C++ node.
5. `scripts/` — Python node directory (empty by default).

### Step 3 — Resolving Dependencies via `rosdep`

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
rosdep install --from-paths src --ignore-src --rosdistro=<DISTRO> -y
```

**Functional transformation:**
$$
\text{rosdep} : \text{package.xml} \mapsto \text{System packages}
$$

This step installs OS-level libraries (e.g., `libeigen3-dev`, `python3-numpy`) referenced inside `<depend>` tags.

### Step 4 — The Build Command and Its Algebra

```bash
catkin_make
```

Internally, this expands to two sequential operations:

$$
\text{catkin\_make} \equiv \underbrace{\text{catkin\_init\_workspace (if needed)}}_{\text{Phase 0}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{cmake -DCMAKE\_BUILD\_TYPE=RELEASE ../src}}_{\text{Phase 1}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{make -j\$(nproc)}}_{\text{Phase 2}}
$$

**Phase 1** creates a parallel subdirectory for every package inside `build/`. For a workspace with packages $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n$, the directory structure becomes:

$$
\text{build/} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \{\text{build}/P_i/\text{CMakeFiles},\;\text{build}/P_i/\text{catkin\_generated},\;\dots\}
$$

**Phase 2** runs in parallel using the formula:
$$
N_{\text{jobs}} = \min(\text{nproc},\; N_{\text{CPUs}})
$$

### Step 5 — Source the Overlay

```bash
source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash
```

**Mathematical effect on environment variables:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PATH} &\leftarrow \text{PATH} \cup \{ \sim/\text{catkin\_ws}/\text{devel}/\text{bin} \} \\
\text{PYTHONPATH} &\leftarrow \text{PYTHONPATH} \cup \{ \sim/\text{catkin\_ws}/\text{devel}/\text{lib}/\text{pythonX.Y}/\text{dist-packages} \} \\
\text{ROS\_PACKAGE\_PATH} &\leftarrow \text{ROS\_PACKAGE\_PATH} \cup \{ \sim/\text{catkin\_ws}/\text{src} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Verification

```bash
echo $ROS_PACKAGE_PATH
rospack find beginner_tutorials
```

Expected terminal output must equal:
$$
\text{rospack output} = \sim/\text{catkin\_ws}/\text{src}/\text{beginner\_tutorials}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — `package.xml` Field-by-Field Derivation

| Tag | Mathematical / Logical Meaning | Example Value | Exam Weightage |
|-----|--------------------------------|---------------|----------------|
| `<name>` | Unique package identifier | `beginner_tutorials` | High |
| `<version>` | Semantic version number $X.Y.Z$ | `0.1.0` | Medium |
| `<description>` | Free-text purpose | `My first ROS package` | Low |
| `<maintainer email="...">` | Author contact | `alice@ktu.in` | Medium |
| `<license>` | SPDX identifier | `BSD`, `MIT`, `GPL-3.0-or-later` | Medium |
| `<buildtool_depend>` | Build-system dependency | `catkin` | High |
| `<depend>` | Combined build + exec dep | `roscpp` | High |
| `<build_depend>` | Compile-time only | `eigen` | High |
| `<exec_depend>` | Runtime only | `python3-yaml` | High |
| `<test_depend>` | Test-only | `gtest` | Medium |
| `<export>` | Plugin + metapackage markers | `<build_type>catkin</build_type>` | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical equivalence that examiners love:**
> $$\langle \text{depend} \rangle \equiv \langle \text{build\_depend} \rangle + \langle \text{exec\_depend} \rangle$$
> That is, `<depend>` is the *union* of compile-time and runtime requirements.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — `CMakeLists.txt` Functional Block Sequence

| Line Number (Typical) | Directive | Purpose |
|-----------------------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | `cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.0.2)` | Enforces CMake version |
| 2 | `project(beginner_tutorials)` | Names CMake project |
| 3 | `find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS roscpp std_msgs)` | Resolves ROS deps |
| 4 | `add_message_files(FILES Msg1.msg)` | Declares messages |
| 5 | `add_service_files(FILES Srv1.srv)` | Declares services |
| 6 | `generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES std_msgs)` | Triggers code-gen |
| 7 | `catkin_package(...)` | Exports catkin config |
| 8 | `add_executable(talker src/talker.cpp)` | Declares binary |
| 9 | `target_link_libraries(talker ${catkin_LIBRARIES})` | Links ROS libs |
| 10 | `add_dependencies(talker ${${PROJECT_NAME}_EXPORTED_TARGETS} ${catkin_EXPORTED_TARGETS})` | Orders build |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Catkin Workspace Directory Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    WS[Catkin Workspace catkin_ws] --> SRC[src source space]
    WS --> BLD[build build space]
    WS --> DVL[devel devel space]
    WS --> INS[install install space]
    SRC --> PKG1[beginner_tutorials pkg]
    SRC --> PKG2[my_robot pkg]
    PKG1 --> PXML1[package.xml]
    PKG1 --> CML1[CMakeLists.txt]
    PKG1 --> INC1[include folder]
    PKG1 --> SRCC1[src folder]
    PKG1 --> MSG1[msg folder]
    PKG1 --> SRV1[srv folder]
    DVL --> SETUP[setup.bash]
    DVL --> LIB[lib binaries]
    DVL --> HINC[include headers]
    DVL --> PYLIBS[lib pythonX.Y]
```

## 4.2 Catkin Build Pipeline Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[User creates workspace mkdir catkin_ws/src] --> S2[Run catkin_init_workspace]
    S2 --> S3[Add packages via catkin_create_pkg or git clone]
    S3 --> S4[Run rosdep install for system deps]
    S4 --> S5[Run catkin_make or catkin build]
    S5 --> S6{Isolated devel space per package}
    S6 --> S7[Compile messages and services via genmsg gencpp]
    S7 --> S8[Compile C++ Python targets]
    S8 --> S9[Stage libraries headers in devel]
    S9 --> S10[User runs source devel/setup.bash]
    S10 --> S11[Workspace overlaid on ROS_PACKAGE_PATH]
    S11 --> S12[rosrun roslaunch can now find nodes]
```

## 4.3 Package Build Dependency Graph (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APP[Application Node my_node.cpp] --> LIB[Library my_lib.cpp]
    APP --> MSG[Generated Msg Header]
    LIB --> MSG
    MSG --> GEN[genmsg gencpp generator]
    GEN --> RAW[Raw Msg1.msg file]
    APP --> ROSCPP[roscpp runtime]
    LIB --> ROSCPP
    ROSCPP --> PKG[package.xml declaration]
    PKG --> WS[Workspace rosdep]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: `catkin_make` vs `catkin build`

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LegacyCatkinMake["catkin_make workflow"]
        A1[Single CMake invocation] --> B1[Single devel space]
        B1 --> C1[Serial package build]
    end
    subgraph ModernCatkinBuild["catkin build workflow catkin_tools"]
        A2[Multiple parallel CMake jobs] --> B2[Isolated devel per package]
        B2 --> C2[Parallel package build with make jobs]
        A2 --> A2b[Continue from failed package]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Diagram Reading Guide:** The right-hand block (`catkin build`) shows the **isolated devel space** advantage — a failing package does NOT prevent successful packages from being usable, which is a frequently-tested 14-mark differentiator in KTU ESE answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define *catkin*. Mention any two advantages it offers over the legacy `rosbuild` system. [3 Marks]
**Model Answer:**

> **Catkin** is the official build system of ROS, implemented as an extension of CMake. It compiles, links, and stages ROS packages written in C++/Python into a unified workspace consumable by ROS run-time tools.
>
> Two advantages over `rosbuild`:
> 1. **Parallel and isolated builds** — each package has its own `devel/` subdirectory, so a build failure in one package does not break others.
> 2. **Native message / service / action generation** — `.msg`, `.srv`, and `.action` files are first-class citizens with language-specific code-generation at build time.
> 3. (Bonus) **Topological dependency resolution** via `package.xml`.

### Q2. List the four standard directories of a catkin workspace and state the purpose of each in one line. [3 Marks]
**Model Answer:**

| Directory | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `src/` | Source space — contains the ROS packages' source code. |
| `build/` | Build space — holds intermediate CMake and Make artefacts. |
| `devel/` | Devel space — holds built targets and `setup.bash` overlay script. |
| `install/` | Install space — populated when running `catkin_make install` for system-wide installation. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A — (14 Marks)
#### (a) Explain the architecture of a catkin workspace with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of `src/`, `build/`, `devel/`, and `install/` spaces in detail. [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Workspace Definition (1 mark):**
   A catkin workspace is a directory hierarchy (typically `~/catkin_ws/`) where ROS packages are developed, built, and staged. It is created via `mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src && catkin_init_workspace`.

2. **The Four Spaces — Detailed Roles (4 marks):**
   - **`src/`** *(Source Space)*: All package source code lives here. Catkin recursively scans for `package.xml` files to discover packages. It is the *only* space the user typically edits.
   - **`build/`** *(Build Space)*: Auto-generated. Each package has a subdirectory containing `CMakeFiles/`, intermediate `.o` files, Makefiles, and the `catkin_make.cache`. Safe to delete for clean rebuilds.
   - **`devel/`** *(Devel Space)*: Contains built libraries, executables, headers, Python eggs, and most importantly, the `setup.bash` script that overlays the workspace onto the current shell. Default location for newly built nodes.
   - **`install/`** *(Install Space)*: Created only when the user runs `catkin_make install`. Mirrors a system-wide installation layout under `lib/`, `include/`, `share/`, `bin/`. Useful for `.deb` packaging or Docker images.

3. **Workflow Diagram (1 mark):**
   Source → Build → Devel → Source-overlaid shell → ROS Runtime.

4. **Cross-Reference to `catkin_make` (1 mark):**
   `catkin_make` operates at the workspace level; each invocation re-runs CMake for any package whose dependencies changed, then runs Make in parallel.

#### (b) With the help of a sample `package.xml`, explain the function of each metadata tag and state why `<depend>` is preferred over separate `<build_depend>` + `<exec_depend>` declarations. [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<package format="2">
  <name>beginner_tutorials</name>
  <version>0.1.0</version>
  <description>Introductory ROS package</description>
  <maintainer email="alice@ktu.in">Alice KTU</maintainer>
  <license>BSD</license>
  <buildtool_depend>catkin</buildtool_depend>
  <depend>roscpp</depend>
  <depend>std_msgs</depend>
  <build_depend>message_generation</build_depend>
  <exec_depend>message_runtime</exec_depend>
</package>
```

1. **`<name>`** — Unique package identifier (format-2 enforces this) — *1 mark*.
2. **`<version>`** — Semantic version $X.Y.Z$ — *0.5 mark*.
3. **`<description>`** — Free-text human-readable purpose — *0.5 mark*.
4. **`<maintainer email="...">`** — Author contact, required by REP-127 — *0.5 mark*.
5. **`<license>`** — SPDX identifier, required for distribution — *0.5 mark*.
6. **`<buildtool_depend>catkin</buildtool_depend>`** — Declares that *catkin* itself is the build tool — *0.5 mark*.
7. **`<depend>`** — A *shorthand* for declaring both `<build_depend>` and `<exec_depend>` simultaneously. The logical equivalence is:
   $$\langle \text{depend} \rangle \iff \langle \text{build\_depend} \rangle \cup \langle \text{exec\_depend} \rangle$$
   Hence it is *preferred* for typical libraries where the same package is needed at both compile and run time — *1.5 marks*.
8. **`<build_depend>message_generation</build_depend>`** — Required *only* at build time for code generators — *0.5 mark*.
