9. **`<exec_depend>message_runtime</exec_depend>`** — Required *only* at runtime for message deserialisation — *0.5 mark*.
10. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Format-2 is the modern KTU-expectation; `<depend>` is concise, while separate tags give finer control for special cases (e.g., header-only vs runtime libs).

---

### Question B — (14 Marks)
#### (a) Compare `catkin_make` and `catkin build` (catkin_tools) in terms of build behaviour, parallelism, isolated devel spaces, and failure handling. [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

| Feature | `catkin_make` | `catkin build` |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| Invocation | Single shell command | Iterative, profile-aware |
| Build parallelism | Limited (workspace-wide `make -j`) | Per-package parallel CMake + Make |
| Devel space | Single shared `devel/` | **Isolated** `devel_isolated/<pkg>/` |
| Failure handling | Whole-workspace abort | **Continues past failures** |
| Configuration persistence | None | `.catkin_tools.yaml` profiles |
| Underlying tool | CMake + Make | CMake + GNU Make / Ninja |
| CATKIN_ENABLE_DIRS | Less granular | Fine per-package |
| Exam popularity | Very High | High (preferred in modern answers) |

1. **Behaviour (2 marks):** `catkin_make` re-runs CMake for the entire workspace on every invocation, while `catkin build` maintains incremental state and only reconfigures changed packages.
2. **Parallelism (2 marks):** `catkin build` can build independent packages truly in parallel (each as its own CMake project), whereas `catkin_make` is limited by the top-level CMake invocation.
3. **Failure handling (2 marks):** A failed package in `catkin_make` may corrupt the shared `devel/`; with `catkin build`, successfully built packages remain usable, and the user can fix and rebuild the failing package in isolation.
4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** For modern KTU 2024 answers, `catkin build` is the recommended tool for professional pipelines.

#### (b) Write a complete `CMakeLists.txt` for a ROS package named `my_publisher` that depends on `roscpp` and `std_msgs`, declares one message `Num.msg` under `msg/`, and compiles a single executable `talker` from `src/talker.cpp`. Explain each directive. [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

```cmake
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.0.2)                         # (1) 0.5 mark
project(my_publisher)                                          # (2) 0.5 mark

find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS                       # (3) 1 mark
  roscpp
  std_msgs
  message_generation
)

add_message_files(FILES Num.msg)                              # (4) 1 mark
generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES std_msgs)                       # (5) 1 mark

catkin_package(                                               # (6) 1 mark
  CATKIN_DEPENDS message_runtime std_msgs
)

include_directories(${catkin_INCLUDE_DIRS})                    # (7) 0.5 mark

add_executable(talker src/talker.cpp)                          # (8) 1 mark
add_dependencies(talker ${${PROJECT_NAME}_EXPORTED_TARGETS}    # (9) 1 mark
  ${catkin_EXPORTED_TARGETS})
target_link_libraries(talker ${catkin_LIBRARIES})              # (10) 0.5 mark
```

**Directive Explanations:**
1. **Line 1** enforces CMake 3.0.2+, the catkin minimum.
2. **Line 2** declares the package name (must match `package.xml`).
3. **Line 3** resolves ROS components. `message_generation` is needed because we declare a custom message.
4. **Line 4** registers `Num.msg` for code generation.
5. **Line 5** triggers generation of language-specific headers (e.g., `Num.h`) used at compile time.
6. **Line 6** exports `catkin` config; dependent packages can `find_package(catkin ...)` again to link this library.
7. **Line 7** includes the staged header paths.
8. **Line 8** declares the executable target.
9. **Line 9** forces `talker` to depend on the *generated* message headers (otherwise build-order errors occur).
10. **Line 10** links against the ROS libraries resolved in step 3.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Missing `add_dependencies()` with `${catkin_EXPORTED_TARGETS}`** → causes *"undefined reference to Num::serialize"* runtime-link errors. **−2 marks** in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Confusing `<depend>` with `<exec_depend>`** → remember: `<depend>` = `<build_depend>` + `<exec_depend>`; stating otherwise loses **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting `catkin_init_workspace`** before `catkin_make` → examiner deducts **−1 mark** for "incomplete workspace setup".
> 4. **Writing `catkin_make` from inside `src/`** instead of workspace root → **−1 mark** as per REP-140.
> 5. **Using `rosbuild` syntax** (`rosbuild_init`, `rosbuild_add_executable`) in catkin answer → **−3 marks**, this is the single biggest error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Catkin = CMake + ROS extensions** for parallel, isolated, reproducible builds.
- **Workspace root** contains **four spaces**: `src/`, `build/`, `devel/`, `install/`.
- **`catkin_init_workspace`** creates the top-level `CMakeLists.txt` symlink in `src/`.
- **`catkin_make`** = legacy, single-devel, sequential-friendly tool.
- **`catkin build`** = modern catkin_tools, **isolated devel spaces**, parallel.
- **`package.xml`** declares metadata; **format="2"** is the modern REP-127 standard.
- **`<depend>` ≡ `<build_depend>` ∪ `<exec_depend>`** — write this equivalence in long answers for full marks.
- **`CMakeLists.txt`** must always end executable targets with `add_dependencies()` linking `${catkin_EXPORTED_TARGETS}`.
- **Overlay** is achieved by `source devel/setup.bash`, which mutates `PATH`, `PYTHONPATH`, and `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`.
- **Build flow:** `mkdir → init → add pkg → rosdep install → catkin_make → source overlay → run`.
- **Numerical constants to memorise:** `cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.0.2)`, parallel jobs `$(nproc)`.
- **Don't confuse:** `catkin_make` (CMake/Make) vs `catkin_make_isolated` (one CMake per package) vs `catkin build` (catkin_tools, isolated devel).
- **One-line exam mantra:**
  $$\boxed{\text{Catkin} = \text{CMake} + \text{ROS message-gen} + \text{Workspace Overlay}}$$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Workspaces and ROS Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Workspaces and ROS Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a ROS Workspace?

A **ROS Workspace** is a specially structured directory hierarchy that serves as a centralized, organized container for developing, building, and managing one or more ROS (Robot Operating System) packages. In the traditional ROS 1 architecture, this is commonly referred to as a **Catkin Workspace**, named after the Catkin build system — the official, CMake-based build orchestration tool that the Open Source Robotics Foundation (OSRF) endorsed for ROS 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (ROS 1 Context):**
> A Catkin Workspace is a file-system directory in which the `catkin_make` or `catkin build` tool can compile ROS source code into executable nodes, libraries, and message-generation artifacts. It MUST contain a dedicated `src/` subdirectory where the developer places their package source trees. After compilation, Catkin automatically populates sibling directories such as `build/`, `devel/`, and optionally `install/`.

> [!NOTE]
> In the modern ROS 2 ecosystem (Foxy, Humble, Iron, Jazzy), the build system has been migrated from **Catkin** to **Colcon** (collective construction). However, since the KTU 2024 syllabus for OERAT722 begins with foundational ROS 1 concepts, this module treats the **Catkin Workspace** as the canonical reference model.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Robotics Workshop"

Imagine you walk into a **professional car garage** that customizes vehicles. The garage (your **workspace**) has a clear physical layout:

- A **front office** (`src/`) where incoming customer cars arrive for modification.
- A **service bay** (`build/` or `devel/`) where mechanics actually turn wrenches, swap engines, and install parts.
- A **showroom** (`install/`) where the finished, polished car is displayed before delivery.

Each **toolbox** that the mechanic wheels out — one for electrical diagnostics, another for bodywork, another for engine tuning — represents a single **ROS Package**. A workspace is meaningless without packages; packages are useless without the supporting infrastructure of a workspace. This inseparable bond is what makes ROS builds *reproducible* and *modular*.

## 1.2 What is a ROS Package?

A **ROS Package** is the fundamental, atomic unit of software organization in ROS. It is a self-contained directory tree that bundles together:

1. Source code (C++ in `src/`, Python scripts in `scripts/` or directly in `package_name/`)
2. A mandatory manifest file `package.xml` declaring metadata and dependencies
3. Build configuration (e.g., `CMakeLists.txt` for C++ packages)
4. Launch files (`launch/`), configuration files (`config/`), and message/service definitions (`msg/`, `srv/`)
5. Documentation (README files within the package root)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A ROS Package is the smallest buildable, versionable, and distributable unit of ROS software. It encapsulates a coherent piece of robot functionality — for example, a single sensor driver, a motion planner, or a perception node — and exposes a well-defined public interface to other packages via ROS Topics, Services, and Actions.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "App" on Your Smartphone

Think of a ROS Package like a **single application** on your Android phone:

- It has a **manifest** (`package.xml`) — equivalent to the app's listing on the Play Store (name, version, what permissions it needs).
- It has **executable entry points** (nodes) — like the home-screen icon you tap.
- It has **assets** (`launch/`, `config/`, `urdf/`, `meshes/`) — like the in-app images and sounds.
- It can call **other packages** as dependencies — like how WhatsApp uses your phone's Contacts and Camera apps.

You would never install "half" of WhatsApp. Likewise, a ROS package is an indivisible, complete unit — you clone it, build it, and run it as one logical whole.

## 1.3 Why Are These Concepts Mandatory?

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OERAT722, Module 1):**
> Mastering the **directory layout** of workspaces and the **file conventions** of packages is the prerequisite for *every* downstream ROS activity — writing publishers/subscribers, launching multi-node systems, recording bagfiles, and parameter tuning. The KTU examiner routinely allocates 6–10 marks out of the 50-mark ESE paper to questions that test pure structural knowledge of workspaces and packages.

| Term | One-line Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Workspace** | Directory tree holding packages, managed by Catkin/Colcon |
| **Package** | Smallest organized software unit in ROS |
| **Catkin** | CMake-based build system for ROS 1 |
| **Manifest** | `package.xml` — the package's identity card |
| **Source Space** | `src/` — where you place package source code |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Catkin Workspace

The Catkin Workspace enforces a **strict directory contract**. Once you run `catkin_make` for the first time inside an empty workspace, the following four directories are auto-generated:

### 2.1.1 The Four Canonical Subdirectories

| Directory | Role | Mutability |
|---|---|---|
| `src/` | **Source Space** — contains hand-written packages, cloned repositories, and the top-level `CMakeLists.txt` symlink that Catkin auto-injects. | **Read-mostly** (developer edits here) |
| `build/` | **Build Space** — intermediate CMake/Make artifacts, object files, message-generated headers, and test results. | Catkin-managed, do not edit |
| `devel/` | **Development Space** — compiled binaries, generated setup scripts (`setup.bash`, `setup.zsh`), and the `lib/python3/` site-packages overlay. | Catkin-managed |
| `install/` | **Install Space** — only populated when `catkin_make install` is invoked or when using `catkin build --install`. Used to make the workspace relocatable. | Optional, for production |

### 2.1.2 The Build Pipeline — What Happens When You Compile

1. **Discovery** — Catkin recursively walks `src/`, discovers every folder containing a valid `package.xml`, and treats each as a "catkin package."
2. **Topological Sort** — Catkin computes the dependency graph between packages (read from `<build_depend>` tags in each `package.xml`) and orders the compilation so that dependencies are built **before** dependents.
3. **Configuration** — CMake runs inside `build/`, generating Makefiles that know how to compile each package's C++ nodes and generate Python message bindings.
4. **Compilation** — `make` (or `ninja`) executes, producing shared libraries (`.so`) and executable nodes inside `devel/lib/<package_name>/`.
5. **Environment Generation** — Catkin writes a `setup.bash` into `devel/` that, when sourced, prepends all of the workspace's `bin/` and `lib/` paths to `$PATH`, `$LD_LIBRARY_PATH`, and `PYTHONPATH`. **This is why you must source it in every new terminal.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Sourcing" Rule:**
> After every successful build, you must execute `source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash` (or add it to `~/.bashrc`) so that ROS tools like `rosrun`, `roslaunch`, and `roscd` can locate your newly compiled packages.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a ROS Package

A canonical ROS package directory is laid out as follows:

```
my_robot_pkg/
├── CMakeLists.txt          ← Build recipe (C++ packages) OR setup.py (Python packages)
├── package.xml             ← Mandatory manifest
├── src/                    ← C++ source (.cpp files)
├── include/my_robot_pkg/   ← Public C++ headers
├── scripts/                ← Executable Python scripts
├── launch/                 ← .launch files (XML-based node orchestration)
├── config/                 ← YAML parameter files
├── msg/                    ← Custom .msg definitions
├── srv/                    ← Custom .srv definitions
├── action/                 ← Custom .action definitions
├── urdf/                   ← Robot description XML
├── meshes/                 ← STL/DAE 3D meshes referenced by URDF
├── README.md               ← Human-readable documentation
└── test/                   ← rostest/pytest integration tests
```

> [!NOTE]
> Only `package.xml` is **strictly required**. The presence of any of the other folders is *opportunistic* — Catkin will inspect them only if they exist. For example, a pure Python package needs only `package.xml` + `setup.py`.

### 2.2.1 The `package.xml` Manifest — Field-by-Field

The `package.xml` file uses a simple XML schema standardized by REP-127 (ROS Enhancement Proposal 127). The key tags are:

| Tag | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `<name>` | Package name (must match directory name) | `my_robot_pkg` |
| `<version>` | Semantic version | `0.1.0` |
| `<description>` | One-sentence summary | `Driver for the XYZ lidar` |
| `<maintainer>` | Email + name of the maintainer | `alice@example.com (Alice)` |
| `<license>` | SPDX license identifier | `BSD`, `MIT`, `Apache-2.0` |
| `<buildtool_depend>` | Build-system dependency | `catkin` |
| `<depend>` | Combined build + exec + runtime dep | `roscpp`, `std_msgs` |
| `<build_depend>` | Required only at compile time | `message_generation` |
| `<exec_depend>` | Required only at runtime | `message_runtime` |

### 2.2.2 The `CMakeLists.txt` File — Build Recipe

For C++ packages, `CMakeLists.txt` orchestrates the build using standard CMake directives wrapped with Catkin-specific macros:

| Directive | Function |
|---|---|
| `cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.0.2)` | Sets the minimum CMake version |
| `project(my_robot_pkg)` | Declares the CMake project name |
| `find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS roscpp std_msgs)` | Resolves package dependencies |
| `add_message_files(FILES Num.msg)` | Registers custom message types |
| `generate_messages()` | Invokes the message generator |
| `catkin_package(...)` | Declares exported libs and runtime deps |
| `add_executable(talker src/talker.cpp)` | Defines an executable target |
| `target_link_libraries(talker ${catkin_LIBRARIES})` | Links against Catkin libs |
| `catkin_install_python(PROGRAMS scripts/listener.py ...)` | Installs Python scripts |

### 2.3 High-Yield Command Reference (KTU Cheat Sheet)

| Command | Function |
|---|---|
| `mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src && cd ~/catkin_ws/src` | Initialize an empty workspace skeleton |
| `catkin_init_workspace` | Adds the top-level CMakeLists.txt symlink into `src/` (legacy step, often optional in modern ROS) |
| `cd ~/catkin_ws && catkin_make` | Builds the entire workspace using the Make-based backend |
| `catkin_make -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release` | Builds with compiler optimizations enabled |
| `catkin_make install` | Populates the `install/` directory |
| `source devel/setup.bash` | Adds workspace to current shell's environment |
| `catkin_create_pkg <pkg> [deps...]` | Generates a starter package with boilerplate |
| `rospack find <pkg>` | Prints the absolute path of an installed package |
| `rospack depends <pkg>` | Lists all transitive dependencies |
| `roscd <pkg>` | `cd`s the shell into a package directory (requires sourcing) |
| `rosls <pkg>` | Lists files inside a package directory |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In production robotics deployments (autonomous vehicles, warehouse AMRs, surgical robots), workspaces are used to **isolate development environments per robot platform**. Different robots in the same fleet can have entirely separate catkin workspaces, preventing dependency hell — a notorious failure mode in monolithic robotic stacks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Procedure A — Creating a Catkin Workspace From Scratch

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following sequence is the **exact procedural answer** KTU examiners expect when they ask, "Explain how to create a ROS workspace and build a package." Memorize each numbered step.

### Step 1 — Create the directory skeleton
```bash
mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src
cd ~/catkin_ws/src
```

### Step 2 — (Optional but recommended) Initialize the workspace
This step deposits a symbolic link `CMakeLists.txt` at the top of `src/`, allowing Catkin to recognize the directory as a valid source space.

```bash
catkin_init_workspace
```

### Step 3 — Return to the workspace root and invoke the builder
```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make
```

### Step 4 — Observe the generated output
After Step 3 completes, three new directories appear:
- `~/catkin_ws/build/`
- `~/catkin_ws/devel/`
- `~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash`

### Step 5 — Source the setup script in the current terminal
```bash
source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash
```

### Step 6 — (Recommended) Add the source line to `~/.bashrc` for persistence
```bash
echo "source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash" >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

### Step 7 — Verify the workspace is on the ROS path
```bash
echo $ROS_PACKAGE_PATH
# Expected output includes: /home/<user>/catkin_ws/src:...
```

---

## 3.2 Procedure B — Creating a New Package Inside the Workspace

Assume we want a package named `beginner_tutorials` that depends on `std_msgs`, `roscpp`, and `rospy`.

### Step 1 — Navigate into the source space
```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws/src
```

### Step 2 — Invoke the package-creation tool
```bash
catkin_create_pkg beginner_tutorials std_msgs roscpp rospy
```

### Step 3 — Inspect the generated structure
The command above creates a folder `beginner_tutorials/` containing:
- `package.xml` (with the dependencies pre-populated)
- `CMakeLists.txt` (with `find_package()` calls pre-populated)

### Step 4 — Build the new package
```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make
```

### Step 5 — Verify the package is discoverable by ROS
```bash
rospack find beginner_tutorials
# Expected output: /home/<user>/catkin_ws/src/beginner_tutorials
```

### Step 6 — Verify dependencies
```bash
rospack depends1 beginner_tutorials
# Expected output: roscpp rospy std_msgs message_runtime catkin
```

---

## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough — What `catkin_make` Actually Executes

When you run `catkin_make` from `~/catkin_ws`, the tool performs the following internal command sequence (simplified for clarity):

```
cd ~/catkin_ws/build && cmake .. -DCATKIN_DEVEL_PREFIX=../devel \
   -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=../install \
   -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
   && make -j$(nproc) -C ~/catkin_ws/build install
```

The mathematical decomposition of the build parallelism flag is:

$$
N_{\text{parallel}} = \text{nproc} = \text{Number of logical CPU cores available}
$$

Hence on a typical quad-core laptop, `make -j8` (note: `-j` usually defaults to $2 \times N_{\text{parallel}}$ in Catkin) is invoked, allowing concurrent compilation of independent package targets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why parallel builds matter:** A workspace with 20 ROS packages may take ~15 minutes on a single-core build but only ~3 minutes with `-j8`. This speed-up is **linear** in the number of independent packages (no inter-package dependencies) and **sub-linear** otherwise, governed by Amdahl's Law:
>
> $$
> S_{\text{speedup}} = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N_{\text{parallel}}}}
> $$
>
> where $p$ is the fraction of the build that can be parallelized.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Workspace vs. Package Comparison

| Aspect | Workspace (Catkin) | Package |
|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Coarse — contains many packages | Fine — atomic unit |
| **Build tool** | `catkin_make` / `catkin build` | `catkin_make` operates at workspace level; individual CMake targets are invoked via `make <target>` |
| **Mandatory artifact** | `src/` directory | `package.xml` file |
| **Generated artifact** | `build/`, `devel/`, `install/` | Executables inside `devel/lib/<pkg>/` |
| **Environment file** | `devel/setup.bash` | None — sourced through the parent workspace |
| **Version control** | Entire workspace (rare) or only `src/` (recommended) | Per-package Git repository (common) |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Sourcing Path Computation

Suppose your shell environment variables are initialized as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{\$PATH}_{\text{default}} &= /usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin \\
\text{\$ROS_PACKAGE_PATH}_{\text{base}} &= /opt/ros/noetic/share \\
\text{Workspace path} &= \texttt{/home/alice/catkin\_ws}
\end{aligned}
$$

After running `source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash`, the effective `$ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` becomes:

$$
\text{\$ROS_PACKAGE_PATH}_{\text{effective}} = \underbrace{\texttt{/home/alice/catkin\_ws/src}}_{\text{workspace}} : \underbrace{\texttt{/opt/ros/noetic/share}}_{\text{underlay}}
$$

**Explanation of the `:` separator:** The colon is the standard Unix path-list delimiter. ROS searches paths **left-to-right**, so the workspace takes precedence — packages in your workspace shadow identically named packages in the underlay. This is the mechanism by which you can *override* upstream ROS packages locally during debugging.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Catkin Workspace — Directory Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph WORKSPACE_ROOT[catkin_ws]
        A[src SOURCE SPACE]
        B[build BUILD SPACE]
        C[devel DEV SPACE]
        D[install INSTALL SPACE optional]
    end

    subgraph SRC_CONTENT[Inside src]
        A1[package_beginner_tutorials]
        A2[package_my_robot_driver]
        A3[third_party_repo cloned]
        A4[CMakeLists.txt symlink auto generated]
    end

    subgraph BUILD_CONTENT[Inside build]
        B1[CMakeCache.txt]
        B2[Object files .o]
        B3[Generated message headers]
    end

    subgraph DEVEL_CONTENT[Inside devel]
        C1[setup.bash environment script]
        C2[lib package shared libraries]
        C3[bin package executables]
        C4[include package public headers]
        C5[lib python3 site packages overlay]
    end

    A --> SRC_CONTENT
    B --> BUILD_CONTENT
    C --> DEVEL_CONTENT

    A1 --> C3
    A2 --> C3
    B3 --> C4

    style WORKSPACE_ROOT fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    style SRC_CONTENT fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style BUILD_CONTENT fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style DEVEL_CONTENT fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 4.2 ROS Package — Internal Structure (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Package Root: my_robot_pkg]

    P --> M[package.xml manifest]
    P --> C[CMakeLists.txt build recipe]
    P --> R[README.md documentation]

    P --> SRC[src folder C plus plus sources]
    P --> INC[include folder public headers]
    P --> SCR[scripts folder python executables]
    P --> LCH[launch folder launch xml files]
    P --> CFG[config folder yaml parameters]
    P --> MSG[msg folder custom message defs]
    P --> SRV[srv folder custom service defs]
    P --> ACT[action folder custom action defs]
    P --> URD[urdf folder robot description]
    P --> TST[test folder rostest files]

    M -. declares .-> C
    M -. declares .-> MSG
    M -. declares .-> SRV

    style P fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:3px
    style M fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    style MSG fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e
    style SRV fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e
    style ACT fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e
```

## 4.3 Catkin Build Pipeline — Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Developer edits source in src] --> S2[Run catkin_make in workspace root]
    S2 --> S3[CMake configures project in build]
    S3 --> S4[Message generator runs on msg and srv]
    S4 --> S5[Make compiles all targets]
    S5 --> S6{Build success?}
    S6 -- No --> S7[Print compiler errors to terminal]
    S7 --> S1
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[Populate devel with binaries]
    S8 --> S9[Generate setup.bash]
    S9 --> S10[User sources setup.bash]
    S10 --> S11[rosrun can now locate nodes]

    style S1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style S8 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    style S11 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Package Dependency Resolution Graph (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[beginner_tutorials] --> B[roscpp]
    A --> C[rospy]
    A --> D[std_msgs]
    B --> E[roscpp core catkin lib]
    C --> F[rospy core python lib]
    D --> G[std_msgs default message set]
    E --> H[catkin build tool]
    F --> H
    G --> H

    style A fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a ROS catkin workspace? List its main sub-directories.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **ROS catkin workspace** is a specially structured directory hierarchy in which ROS source code is organized, compiled, and made runnable using the Catkin build system. It is created using the `mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src` command and built using `catkin_make`.

Its **four main sub-directories** are:

| # | Directory | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `src/` | **Source space** — holds ROS package source trees and the auto-generated top-level `CMakeLists.txt` symlink |
| 2 | `build/` | **Build space** — stores intermediate CMake/Make artifacts, object files, and message-generation output |
| 3 | `devel/` | **Development space** — contains compiled binaries, libraries, and the `setup.bash` environment script |
| 4 | `install/` | **Install space** — populated only via `catkin_make install` for relocatable deployments |

The workspace must be **sourced** via `source devel/setup.bash` after each build so that ROS tools can discover newly compiled packages.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for correctly listing all four sub-directories with their roles.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the role of `package.xml` in a ROS package. Mention any four key tags.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **`package.xml`** file is the **mandatory manifest** of every ROS package. It serves as the package's **identity card and dependency declaration**, conforming to the REP-127 XML schema. It is read by the Catkin build tool, by package managers like `rosdep`, and by tools like `rospack` to perform dependency resolution.

**Four key tags:**

| Tag | Function |
|---|---|
| `<name>` | Declares the unique package name (must match directory name) |
| `<version>` | Specifies the semantic version of the package |
| `<maintainer>` | Provides the maintainer's email and name for support |
| `<license>` | Declares the open-source license (e.g., BSD, MIT, Apache-2.0) |

Two more high-yield tags are `<buildtool_depend>` (which usually declares `catkin`) and `<depend>` (a unified declaration of build + runtime dependencies such as `roscpp` or `std_msgs`).

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for explaining the role, 2 marks for correctly stating any four tags with brief descriptions.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat directory diagram, explain the structure of a **catkin workspace**. Discuss the role of the `src/`, `build/`, and `devel/` directories in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **ROS package**? Explain the procedure to create a new package named `my_first_pkg` with dependencies `std_msgs` and `roscpp`. Also list the files that get auto-generated. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
A **Catkin Workspace** is a filesystem directory in which the Catkin build system organizes, compiles, and links ROS packages. It is the unit at which `catkin_make` operates and produces the artifacts required to run ROS nodes.

**2. Directory Layout Diagram (2 marks):**

```
catkin_ws/
├── src/                ← Source space (developer-edited)
│   ├── CMakeLists.txt   ← auto-generated symlink
│   ├── my_first_pkg/
│   └── beginner_tutorials/
├── build/              ← Build space (CMake/Make artifacts)
│   ├── CMakeCache.txt
│   ├── catkin_tools/
│   └── *.o (object files)
└── devel/              ← Development space (binaries)
    ├── setup.bash
    ├── lib/<pkg>/
    ├── include/<pkg>/
    └── bin/
```

**3. Role of `src/` (1.5 marks):**
- **Source space** — the only directory the developer normally modifies.
- Catkin recursively traverses every subdirectory and identifies a folder as a valid ROS package iff it contains a `package.xml` file.
- Common operations: cloning Git repositories, creating new packages via `catkin_create_pkg`, and editing source code.

**4. Role of `build/` (1.5 marks):**
- **Build space** — completely managed by Catkin and CMake; **never edited manually**.
- Stores: object files (`.o`), CMake configuration cache, generated message headers (`.h`), and test result logs.
- Invoking `rm -rf build/` is a safe operation to force a clean rebuild.

**5. Role of `devel/` (1 mark):**
- **Development space** — contains the compiled, *runnable* output: shared libraries (`.so`), Python modules, executable node scripts, public headers, and most importantly the `setup.bash` script that overlays the workspace onto the current shell's `$PATH`, `$LD_LIBRARY_PATH`, and `$PYTHONPATH`.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks

**1. Definition of a ROS Package (1 mark):**
A **ROS package** is the smallest, atomic, self-contained software unit in ROS. It bundles together source code, build configuration, dependencies, message definitions, and documentation into a single directory tree that can be built, version-controlled, and distributed as one logical module.

**2. Procedure to Create `my_first_pkg` (4 marks):**

```bash
# Step 1 — Move into the source space of the workspace
cd ~/catkin_ws/src

# Step 2 — Invoke the package-creation utility with dependencies
catkin_create_pkg my_first_pkg std_msgs roscpp

# Step 3 — Verify the package was created
ls my_first_pkg

# Step 4 — Return to workspace root and build
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make

# Step 5 — Source the setup script
source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash

# Step 6 — Verify discoverability
rospack find my_first_pkg
```

**3. Auto-generated files (2 marks):**

After running `catkin_create_pkg my_first_pkg std_msgs roscpp`, the following files are produced:

| File | Content |
|---|---|
| `my_first_pkg/CMakeLists.txt` | Pre-populated with `find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS roscpp std_msgs)` and other boilerplate directives |
| `my_first_pkg/package.xml` | Manifest with `<name>`, `<maintainer>`, `<license>` fields filled, and the dependencies listed under `<build_depend>` and `<run_depend>` |

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the **CMakeLists.txt** file of a ROS C++ package. List and describe any five important Catkin/CMake directives used in it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between a **workspace** and a **package**. Discuss the concept of the `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` environment variable and explain the role of **sourcing** the workspace's `setup.bash`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks

**1. Purpose of `CMakeLists.txt` (1 mark):**
The `CMakeLists.txt` file is the **build recipe** for a C++ ROS package. It uses standard CMake syntax augmented with Catkin-specific macros to declare dependencies, generate messages, and define compilation targets.

**2. Five Important Directives (6 marks — 1.2 marks each):**

| # | Directive | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.0.2)` | Declares the minimum CMake version required to parse this file |
| 2 | `project(my_robot_pkg)` | Defines the CMake project name — must match the directory and `package.xml` name |
| 3 | `find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS roscpp std_msgs)` | Resolves package dependencies at configure-time; aborts if any are missing |
| 4 | `add_message_files(FILES Num.msg)` and `generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES std_msgs)` | Registers custom message types and triggers the message-generation step |
| 5 | `add_executable(talker src/talker.cpp)` followed by `target_link_libraries(talker ${catkin_LIBRARIES})` | Declares a runnable target and links it against Catkin-provided libraries |

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks

**1. Workspace vs. Package — Differences (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Workspace | Package |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A directory that hosts one or more packages and provides the build infrastructure | The atomic software unit — contains source code and metadata |
| **Scope** | Coarse-grained — system level | Fine-grained — single functionality |
| **Mandatory artifact** | The `src/` subdirectory | The `package.xml` file |
| **Build tool entry point** | `catkin_make` invoked at workspace root | Per-target builds via `make <target>` from `build/` |
| **Environment** | Provides `setup.bash` after build | None — inherits from parent workspace |

**2. The `$ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` Variable (2 marks):**
`$ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` is a colon-separated list of directory roots that ROS tools (like `rospack`, `roscd`, and `rosrun`) search when looking for packages. It is **prepended** with the workspace's `src/` directory every time you source `devel/setup.bash`. For example:

```
/home/alice/catkin_ws/src:/opt/ros/noetic/share
```

The **leftmost** entry takes precedence — this is how developers can override upstream ROS packages with local modifications without uninstalling them.

**3. Role of Sourcing (2 marks):**
The `setup.bash` script exported from `devel/` (or `install/`) modifies the current shell's environment so that:
- Compiled executables in `devel/lib/<pkg>/` become discoverable by `rosrun`.
- The compiled Python modules in `devel/lib/python3/dist-packages/` are added to `$PYTHONPATH`.
- Compiled message/service Python modules become importable.
- The workspace's `src/` is added to `$ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`.

**Without sourcing**, `rosrun my_first_pkg my_node` will fail with the error: `[rospack] Error: package 'my_first_pkg' not found`.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the trailing slash of the `src/` directory.** The workspace MUST contain `src/`. A workspace with only `catkin_ws/` and no `src/` subdirectory will NOT build.
> 2. **Sourcing `setup.bash` only once and expecting it to persist.** Each new terminal session requires a fresh `source devel/setup.bash` unless the command is appended to `~/.bashrc`.
> 3. **Renaming a package directory without renaming the `<name>` field in `package.xml`** — this triggers Catkin to silently skip the package during build, leading to "package not found" errors that cost 1–2 marks in practical exams.
> 4. **Confusing `catkin_make` (workspace-level) with `make` (single-target).** Examiners frequently award a 1-mark differentiation between these two commands.
> 5. **Omitting the `source` step in written answers.** A "create workspace" answer that ends at `catkin_make` without mentioning the `source devel/setup.bash` step loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] A **Catkin Workspace** is the directory tree `catkin_ws/` containing `src/`, `build/`, `devel/`, and optionally `install/`.
- [x] A **ROS Package** is the atomic software unit containing `package.xml` and optionally `CMakeLists.txt`, `src/`, `msg/`, `srv/`, `launch/`, etc.
- [x] The command `mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src` initializes a workspace skeleton.
- [x] The command `catkin_make` (run from the workspace **root**) compiles the entire workspace.
- [x] The command `catkin_create_pkg <name> [deps...]` generates a starter package with dependencies pre-declared.
- [x] After every build, the developer MUST execute `source devel/setup.bash` to overlay the workspace onto the shell.
- [x] `package.xml` declares metadata (`<name>`, `<version>`, `<description>`, `<license>`, `<maintainer>`) and dependencies (`<build_depend>`, `<exec_depend>`, `<depend>`).
- [x] `CMakeLists.txt` is the build recipe for C++ packages, using directives like `find_package(catkin ...)`, `add_executable()`, and `target_link_libraries()`.
- [x] `$ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` is a colon-separated list of search roots; the workspace's `src/` is prepended upon sourcing.
- [x] Workspace-level builds are parallel; build speed scales with the number of independent packages (Amdahl's Law).
- [x] The Catkin build system is for **ROS 1**; ROS 2 uses **Colcon** — but the conceptual workspace/package structure remains analogous.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: rosrun
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** rosrun

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **rosrun** is a **command-line tool** in the Robot Operating System (ROS) that allows a user to execute a single **node executable** located inside a ROS package, without needing to specify the full absolute file-system path of the binary. It is part of the standard ROS distribution and is invoked from a sourced ROS environment shell.

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, **rosrun** is classified as a **node execution utility** belonging to the `rosbash` family of command-line tools. It abstracts the underlying file-system navigation, locates the executable through the package manifest (`package.xml`) and the ROS package path (`ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`), and launches the node while inheriting the current ROS environment (master URI, namespace, parameters, etc.).

The official ROS Wiki defines **rosrun** as a tool that *"allows you to run an executable in an arbitrary package without having to know its path."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of **rosrun** like a **hotel concierge** in a large resort.

Imagine you walk into a 2000-room hotel and ask: *"Please call Room 247's occupant to the lobby."* You do **not** need to know that Room 247 is in Building C, Floor 4, Wing 2. The concierge (rosrun) consults the hotel's directory (ROS package index), finds the room (executable), and summons the person (launches the node). You only provide two pieces of information: the **building name (package)** and the **room number (node name)**.

| Hotel Analogy | rosrun Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Hotel directory | `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` + package manifest |
| Building | ROS Package |
| Room Number | Node Executable |
| Concierge | `rosrun` command |
| Lobby | Current ROS Master (`roscore`) |

## 1.3 Core Syntax

The most fundamental invocation of **rosrun** follows this canonical structure:

```
rosrun <package_name> <node_name> [arguments]
```

- `<package_name>` — The name of the ROS package containing the executable (mandatory, **must exist** in the ROS package path).
- `<node_name>` — The name of the executable file (not the runtime node name) located inside the `nodes/` directory of the package (mandatory).
- `[arguments]` — Optional command-line arguments that are **passed directly** to the executable after ROS-specific arguments are processed.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Parameters

- **Default Search Locations:** `nodes/`, `src/`, or any executable subfolder of the package.
- **Mandatory Prerequisite:** A running **ROS Master** (`roscore`) must be active on the network, or the node will fail to register.
- **Process Model:** Each `rosrun` invocation spawns **one child process** in the foreground of the calling shell.
- **Environment Dependency:** Requires `ROS_MASTER_URI`, `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`, and `ROS_VERSION` to be correctly set in the shell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - OERAT722 Module 1):** The KTU module explicitly identifies `rosrun`, `rosnode`, `roscore`, `rostopic`, and `roslaunch` as the foundational command-line tools of the ROS ecosystem. Mastery of `rosrun` is considered a **prerequisite competency** for all higher modules involving node communication and launch files.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of rosrun

When the user types `rosrun my_pkg my_node arg1 arg2`, the following sequence of operations is executed by the ROS shell wrapper:

1. **Argument Parsing Layer**
   The `rosrun` shell script first separates the ROS-specific arguments (those beginning with `__` such as `__name:=`, `__log:=`, `__ns:=`, `__params:=`) from the user-space arguments intended for the node executable.

2. **Package Resolution Layer**
   The tool scans the directories listed in the environment variable `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` (or the modern `AMENT_PREFIX_PATH` for ROS 2). It searches for a package whose name matches `<package_name>`.

3. **Executable Discovery Layer**
   Once the package root is located, `rosrun` searches the package's subdirectories (typically `nodes/`, `src/`, or `scripts/`) for an executable file whose name matches `<node_name>`.

4. **Environment Setup Layer**
   The tool then constructs the runtime environment by appending the package's `lib/`, `share/`, and `include/` directories to the appropriate environment variables (`LD_LIBRARY_PATH`, `PYTHONPATH`, `PATH`).

5. **Process Launch Layer**
   Finally, `rosrun` performs an `exec`-style invocation, replacing the shell process with the target executable while preserving the modified environment.

## 2.2 Core 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it matters | How it is implemented |
|---|---|---|
| Argument Parsing | Allows users to remap node names/namespaces at runtime | Shell regex on `__*` prefix |
| Package Resolution | ROS packages are scattered across `catkin_ws`, `/opt/ros`, etc. | Iterates over `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` |
| Executable Discovery | Executables can be in multiple subfolders | Recursive `find` with permission check |
| Environment Setup | Node must link against package's shared libraries | `export` augmented before exec |
| Process Launch | Inherits stdin/stdout so the user sees node logs | `exec` replaces shell process |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every high-yield concept about `rosrun` that a KTU examiner can test in ESE or internal assessments.**

| Concept | Symbol / Syntax | Description | Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Basic syntax | `rosrun pkg node` | Launch a node from a package | ★★★★★ |
| Name remapping | `rosrun pkg node __name:=newName` | Rename the node at runtime | ★★★★★ |
| Namespace remapping | `rosrun pkg node __ns:=/my_ns` | Place node in a namespace | ★★★★☆ |
| Logging to file | `rosrun pkg node __log:=log.txt` | Redirect node output to a log file | ★★★☆☆ |
| Parameter file | `rosrun pkg node __params:=file.yaml` | Load YAML parameters | ★★★☆☆ |
| Show all args | `rosrun pkg node --help` | Display help message | ★★☆☆☆ |
| Find package path | `rospack find pkg` | Returns absolute path of a package | ★★★★★ |
| List nodes in package | `rospack list-names-by-package pkg` | Lists executables in a package | ★★★☆☆ |
| Check active nodes | `rosnode list` | Lists all running nodes | ★★★★★ |
| Inspect a node | `rosnode info /node_name` | Shows publications/subscriptions | ★★★★☆ |
| Master requirement | `roscore` | Must be running for `rosrun` to work | ★★★★★ |
| Difference from roslaunch | `roslaunch` vs `rosrun` | One node vs many nodes from XML | ★★★★★ |
| Permission check | `chmod +x` | Executable bit must be set | ★★★☆☆ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **industrial robotics**, `rosrun` is the developer's primary tool during the **debug and unit-test** phase. Before integrating multiple nodes via `roslaunch`, an engineer will:

1. Run the **sensing node** (e.g., LiDAR driver) using `rosrun`.
2. Run the **processing node** (e.g., SLAM algorithm) using `rosrun` in a separate terminal.
3. Run the **visualization node** (e.g., RViz) to observe behavior in real time.

This three-terminal pattern (driver → algorithm → visualizer) is the **canonical ROS development workflow** and is heavily emphasized in KTU's robotics lab evaluations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use `roslaunch`?** Because `roslaunch` requires a pre-written `.launch` XML file and launches nodes in the background, which makes interactive debugging harder. `rosrun` keeps the node in the foreground so logs are streamed live to the terminal.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Common Patterns

## 3.1 Canonical Usage Pattern (Detailed Walkthrough)

The following exhaustive sequence demonstrates the full lifecycle of a `rosrun` command, including **prerequisite verification**, **execution**, and **post-execution validation**.

### Step 1 — Verify the ROS Environment

Before invoking `rosrun`, the user must confirm that the ROS environment variables are set. This is typically done by sourcing the setup script:

```
source /opt/ros/<distro>/setup.bash
```

The variable `ROS_DISTRO` is a standard environment variable (e.g., `noetic`, `humble`, `foxy`) that identifies the active ROS distribution. The setup script populates `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`, `ROS_MASTER_URI`, and `PATH`.

### Step 2 — Start the ROS Master

Since `rosrun` does **not** start a master, the user must start one explicitly:

```
roscore
```

The equation describing the master-node relationship is conceptually:

$$
N_{\text{active}} = N_{\text{launched}} \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{master\_alive}}
$$

where $N_{\text{active}}$ is the number of successfully registered nodes, $N_{\text{launched}}$ is the number of nodes started with `rosrun`, and $\mathbb{1}_{\text{master\_alive}}$ is the indicator function equal to **1** if the master is running and **0** otherwise. This is a common-sense formula, not a physical law, but it captures the dependency.

### Step 3 — Locate the Target Package

To confirm that the desired package exists in the package path, the engineer uses:

```
rospack find <package_name>
```

If the package is found, its absolute path is printed; otherwise, an error is returned.

### Step 4 — Discover Available Executables

To list all executables inside a package, the engineer uses:

```
rospack list-names-by-package <package_name>
```

Or in modern ROS 2 environments:

```
ros2 pkg executables <package_name>
```

### Step 5 — Invoke rosrun

The actual command is then issued:

```
rosrun <package_name> <executable_name> [args...]
```

The `rosrun` shell script performs the steps outlined in Section 2.1 and replaces the current shell with the node process.

### Step 6 — Verify the Node is Running

In a **new terminal**, the user can confirm registration with the master:

```
rosnode list
```

If the node appears in the list (e.g., `/my_node`), the launch was successful.

### Step 7 — Inspect Node Topology

For deeper introspection:

```
rosnode info /my_node
```

This prints all **publications**, **subscriptions**, **services**, and **parameter assignments** of the running node.

## 3.2 Worked Example with Name Remapping

Suppose a user has a package called `turtlesim` and wants to run the `turtlesim_node` under a custom name `my_turtle`:

**Original invocation:**
```
rosrun turtlesim turtlesim_node __name:=my_turtle
```

**What happens internally:**

1. `rosrun` sees `__name:=my_turtle` and strips it from the user argument list.
2. The remaining argument list is empty.
3. The node is launched with the environment variable `ROS_NAMESPACE_NAME` set, which the node's initialization code reads to override its default anonymous name.
4. The new node registers with `roscore` as `/my_turtle` instead of the default `/turtlesim`.

**Verification:**
```
rosnode list
```
Expected output includes `/my_turtle`.

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: rosrun vs roslaunch

| Feature | `rosrun` | `roslaunch` |
|---|---|---|
| Number of nodes per invocation | **Exactly 1** | **1 or more** |
| Requires configuration file | No | Yes (`.launch` XML) |
| Runs in foreground by default | Yes | No (background by default) |
| Best for | Quick testing, debugging | Full system integration |
| Supports parameter files | Limited (via `__params`) | Full support |
| Supports remapping rules | Inline only | XML attributes |
| Lifecycle management | Manual kill via Ctrl+C | Automatic via `roslaunch` |
| Output streaming | Direct to terminal | To log files |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A very common KTU question asks: *"Why use `roslaunch` if `rosrun` exists?"* The answer is precisely captured in the table above — `roslaunch` enables **orchestration of multiple nodes** with a **single declarative configuration file**, which is essential for production robotic systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: The Internal Execution Pipeline of `rosrun`

The following Mermaid block visualizes the complete **logical flow** that occurs when a user types `rosrun my_pkg my_node` in a terminal. This block-level functional architecture maps the interactions between the shell, the ROS master, and the target node.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User types rosrun my_pkg my_node in terminal] --> B{Is ROS environment sourced?}
    B -- No --> C[Error: package my_pkg not found]
    B -- Yes --> D[Parse special ROS arguments with __ prefix]
    D --> E[Search ROS_PACKAGE_PATH for my_pkg]
    E --> F{Package found?}
    F -- No --> G[Error: Package my_pkg not found]
    F -- Yes --> H[Locate executable my_node in package subdirs]
    H --> I{Executable found and has x bit?}
    I -- No --> J[Error: Executable not found or not executable]
    I -- Yes --> K[Augment LD_LIBRARY_PATH and PYTHONPATH]
    K --> L{Is roscore running?}
    L -- No --> M[Node starts but fails to register with master]
    L -- Yes --> N[Launch node as foreground process]
    N --> O[Node registers with ROS Master using __name override]
    O --> P[Node begins publishing and subscribing to topics]
    P --> Q[User sees stdout and stderr live in terminal]
    Q --> R{User presses Ctrl plus C?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Node sends shutdown signal to master]
    R -- No --> P
    S --> T[Master unregisters node and cleans up resources]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Comparison of rosrun and roslaunch Workflows

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEVELOPER_TERMINAL_A["Developer Terminal A"]
        A1[rosrun lidar_pkg lidar_driver] --> A2[Foreground Process]
        A2 --> A3[Live logs streamed]
    end
    subgraph DEVELOPER_TERMINAL_B["Developer Terminal B"]
        B1[rosrun slam_pkg mapping_node] --> B2[Foreground Process]
        B2 --> B3[Live logs streamed]
    end
    subgraph DEVELOPER_TERMINAL_C["Developer Terminal C"]
        C1[rosrun viz_pkg rviz_node] --> C2[Foreground Process]
        C2 --> C3[GUI window opens]
    end
    subgraph ROS_MASTER["ROS Master - roscore"]
        M1[Central Registry]
    end
    A2 -.registers.-> M1
    B2 -.registers.-> M1
    C2 -.registers.-> M1
    M1 -.topic discovery.-> A2
    M1 -.topic discovery.-> B2
    M1 -.topic discovery.-> C2
```

## 4.3 Architectural Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The figure above illustrates the **canonical three-terminal ROS development pattern**. Each terminal invokes `rosrun` independently to launch a single node in the foreground. The **ROS Master** acts as a central registry that allows each node to discover the others dynamically. This is in direct contrast to `roslaunch`, which would consolidate all three nodes into a single declarative XML file and launch them from a single command in the background.

The **key engineering insight** is that `rosrun` prioritizes **observability and debuggability** over **convenience and orchestration**. During the development phase, this trade-off is favorable because engineers need to see immediate feedback for each node in isolation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the `rosrun` command. What is its purpose in the ROS ecosystem?**

**Model Answer:**

`rosrun` is a command-line utility provided by the Robot Operating System that allows a user to execute a single **node executable** from a specified **ROS package** without needing to know the absolute file path of the executable. Its primary purpose is to **simplify node launching** during development and debugging. The general syntax is `rosrun <package_name> <node_name>`. Internally, `rosrun` searches the `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` for the named package, locates the executable within the package's subdirectories, and runs it as a foreground process. It is the lightweight counterpart of `roslaunch` and is used when a developer needs to start only one node at a time with immediate access to its stdout and stderr streams.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any four special arguments that can be passed to a node via `rosrun` and explain the purpose of any one.**

**Model Answer:**

The four special arguments are:

1. `__name:=<new_name>` — Remaps the node's registered name with the master.
2. `__ns:=<namespace>` — Places the node inside a custom ROS namespace.
3. `__log:=<logfile>` — Redirects the node's output to a specified log file.
4. `__params:=<yaml_file>` — Loads parameters from a YAML file at launch.

**Detailed explanation of `__name:=`:** When a node is launched, it registers with the ROS Master under a default name. By passing `__name:=newName`, the developer can override this default and register the node under a different name. This is useful in **multi-instance scenarios**, such as launching two identical camera drivers on the same machine — one can be renamed `front_camera` and the other `rear_camera` to avoid naming collisions. The syntax is: `rosrun <pkg> <node> __name:=front_camera`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the complete internal execution pipeline of the `rosrun` command, listing each stage from the moment the user presses Enter to the moment the node registers with the ROS Master. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast `rosrun` and `roslaunch` in detail. Provide a real-world scenario where each is the appropriate choice. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

The execution pipeline of `rosrun` consists of **five distinct stages**:

**Stage 1 — Argument Parsing:**
When the user types `rosrun my_pkg my_node __name:=foo arg1`, the shell script first scans the argument list for tokens that begin with the double-underscore prefix `__`. These are recognized as **ROS-specific arguments** and are separated from the user arguments. In our example, `__name:=foo` is extracted, and the remaining list `[arg1]` is preserved for the node executable.

**Stage 2 — Package Resolution:**
The `rosrun` script iterates over every directory listed in the `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` environment variable. For each directory, it checks whether a subfolder named `my_pkg` exists. Once a match is found, the absolute path of the package is recorded, and the search terminates. If no match is found in any directory, the script terminates with the error `"ResourceNotFound: my_pkg"`.

**Stage 3 — Executable Discovery:**
Within the located package directory, the script performs a recursive search for a file named `my_node` that has the executable permission bit set (`+x`). The search typically prioritizes the `nodes/`, `src/`, and `scripts/` subdirectories. If found, the absolute path of the executable is recorded.

**Stage 4 — Environment Augmentation:**
Before launching, the script modifies the current shell's environment to include the package's library and Python paths:

$$
\text{LD\_LIBRARY\_PATH} \leftarrow \text{LD\_LIBRARY\_PATH} \cup \text{Package/lib}
$$

$$
\text{PYTHONPATH} \leftarrow \text{PYTHONPATH} \cup \text{Package/lib/pythonX.Y/site-packages}
$$

This ensures that any shared libraries or Python modules defined by the package are discoverable by the executable.

**Stage 5 — Process Launch and Master Registration:**
The script finally invokes the executable using the `exec` system call, which replaces the current shell process with the node process. The node's initialization code (typically `ros::init()` in C++ or `rospy.init_node()` in Python) then contacts the ROS Master at the URI specified by `ROS_MASTER_URI` and registers itself under the name determined by the `__name` argument (or the default name if absent). Upon successful registration, the node begins its main loop and is fully operational.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

| Dimension | `rosrun` | `roslaunch` |
|---|---|---|
| Configuration | No file needed | Requires a `.launch` XML file |
| Nodes launched | Exactly one per call | One or more per file |
| Execution mode | Foreground by default | Background by default |
| Output | Streamed live to terminal | Written to log files in `~/.ros/log/` |
| Use case | Single-node debugging | Multi-node system integration |
| Parameter loading | Limited (`__params`) | Full YAML support with `<rosparam>` |
| Remapping | Inline only | XML `<remap>` tags |
| Dependency management | None | `<node>` with `if/unless` conditions |

**Real-world scenario for `rosrun`:** A robotics engineer is debugging a **faulty LiDAR driver**. She needs to see the raw log output, restart the node frequently, and tweak command-line arguments iteratively. She opens a terminal and repeatedly uses `rosrun velodyne_driver velodyne_node __name:=lidar` to launch the node in the foreground, observing the log stream in real time.

**Real-world scenario for `roslaunch`:** A mobile robot is being deployed for a **warehouse navigation task**. The complete system consists of a **LIDAR driver**, a **wheel-odometry node**, a **SLAM mapping node**, a **path planner**, and a **velocity controller**. The engineer writes a single launch file `warehouse_robot.launch` that starts all five nodes with their respective parameters and topic remappings. She then runs `roslaunch warehouse_pkg warehouse_robot.launch` to bring up the entire system in one command.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**Q: (a)** Describe the use of `__name`, `__ns`, and `__log` special arguments in `rosrun` with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of a ROS Master. Why is it necessary for `rosrun` to function correctly? What happens if the master is not running? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

The **special arguments** of `rosrun` are tokens prefixed with a double-underscore `__` that are intercepted by the `rosrun` shell script before being passed to the executable. They allow **runtime configuration** of the node without modifying its source code.

**1. `__name:=` (Name Remapping):**
This argument overrides the default registered name of the node with the master.

Example:
```
rosrun my_robot_pkg camera_node __name:=front_camera
```
In this example, the `camera_node` executable would normally register as `/camera_node`, but the `__name` argument forces it to register as `/front_camera`. This is essential when launching **multiple instances** of the same node, e.g., for a stereo camera setup.

**2. `__ns:=` (Namespace Remapping):**
This argument places the node inside a **custom ROS namespace**, effectively prefixing all its topic, service, and parameter names.

Example:
```
rosrun my_robot_pkg camera_node __ns:=/robot1
```
After launch, the node's topics become `/robot1/camera_node/image_raw` instead of `/camera_node/image_raw`. This is critical for **multi-robot systems** where name collisions between robots must be avoided.

**3. `__log:=` (Log Redirection):**
This argument redirects the node's standard output and standard error streams to a file instead of the terminal.

Example:
```
rosrun my_robot_pkg camera_node __log:=/tmp/camera.log
```
All `ROS_INFO`, `ROS_WARN`, and `ROS_ERROR` messages will be written to `/tmp/camera.log`. This is useful when the node is running in the **background** or when logs must be preserved for post-mortem debugging.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Concept of a ROS Master:**
The **ROS Master** is a central lookup service that enables **peer-to-peer discovery** between ROS nodes. It is started by the `roscore` command, which actually launches three components: the **master server**, the **parameter server**, and the **rosout logging node**. The Master maintains a registry of:
- Active **nodes** and their URIs
- **Topics** and the publisher/subscriber relationships
- **Services** and their providers
- **Parameters** and their current values

**Why the Master is necessary for `rosrun`:**
When a node is launched via `rosrun`, it does **not** directly know the network addresses of other nodes. Instead, it registers itself with the Master and queries the Master to discover other nodes that publish or subscribe to topics of interest. The Master then performs the **handshake**, exchanging connection details between the nodes. After this handshake, the nodes communicate **directly peer-to-peer** without further Master involvement. Mathematically, the discovery process can be represented as:

$$
\text{Publisher}_A \xrightarrow{\text{register}} \text{Master} \xleftarrow{\text{query}} \text{Subscriber}_B
$$

$$
\text{Master} \xrightarrow{\text{reply with URI}} \text{Subscriber}_B
$$

$$
\text{Publisher}_A \xleftrightarrow{\text{TCP}} \text{Subscriber}_B
$$

**What happens if the Master is not running:**
If `roscore` is not running, a node launched by `rosrun` will start as a process but will **fail to register** with the Master. The node will print a series of error messages to the terminal, such as:

```
[ERROR] [timestamp]: Failed to contact master at [http://localhost:11311]
```

The node will continue attempting to contact the Master in a retry loop, and any topic publications or subscriptions will be buffered indefinitely. The system will appear "alive" in the process list (`ps aux`) but will be **functionally non-operational** until the Master is started.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `rosrun` with `roslaunch`:** Many students incorrectly state that `rosrun` can launch multiple nodes. It **cannot** — it launches **exactly one** node per invocation. Using `rosrun` for multi-node orchestration is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the `__` prefix:** Special arguments like `__name:=` must use **double underscores**. Writing `_name:=` or `-name:=` will be treated as a user argument and passed to the executable, causing a runtime error. This is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Omitting `roscore` prerequisite:** Students frequently forget to mention that a running Master is mandatory. Always explicitly state the prerequisite in any "explain `rosrun`" answer.
> 4. **Mixing up C++ and Python API calls:** When discussing the internal Master registration, do not confuse `ros::init()` (C++) with `rospy.init_node()` (Python). Use both to demonstrate breadth of understanding.
> 5. **Vague "it runs the node" answers:** A 7-mark question requires a **stage-by-stage pipeline**, not a one-sentence definition. Plan your answer with at least 4-5 numbered stages.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **rosrun** is a **single-node launcher**, not a multi-node orchestrator.
- The canonical syntax is `rosrun <package_name> <node_name> [args]`.
- The **ROS Master (roscore)** must be running for any `rosrun` invocation to succeed.
- Special arguments use the **double-underscore `__` prefix** (e.g., `__name`, `__ns`, `__log`, `__params`).
- `__name:=` enables **multi-instance launches** by remapping the registered node name.
- `__ns:=` enables **multi-robot systems** by placing nodes inside custom namespaces.
- `rosrun` runs in the **foreground**, streaming logs live — ideal for debugging.
- `roslaunch` runs in the **background** using a `.launch` XML file — ideal for full-system startup.
- The package is located by searching `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`; the executable is located within the package's subdirectories.
- A **failing master** does not crash the node process; it just prevents registration, leaving the node in a zombie state.
- Common companion commands: `rosnode list`, `rosnode info`, `rospack find`, `rostopic list`.
- The **three-terminal pattern** (driver, algorithm, visualizer) is the canonical ROS development workflow.
- For **production deployment**, always prefer `roslaunch` with a properly structured `.launch` file.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Names, Namespaces, and Remapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Names, Namespaces, and Remapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)**, every computational entity (Node, Topic, Service, Parameter) is identified by a hierarchical **graph resource name**. A *Name* in ROS is a string used to identify a resource within the ROS Computation Graph. These names are structured in a *Namespace*, which is a hierarchical partitioning mechanism that groups related resources under a common prefix. *Remapping* is the runtime mechanism by which a default resource name is substituted with another name, allowing flexible reconfiguration of a system without modifying the source code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Names and Namespaces are the backbone of the ROS Computation Graph. Without proper naming conventions, nodes cannot discover each other, and the entire distributed architecture collapses.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy: The Postal & Telephone System

Imagine a large corporate office with thousands of employees.

- **Names** are like **employee IDs** — every person (node) has a unique identifier.
- **Namespaces** are like **departments** (`/engineering/`, `/hr/`, `/finance/`) — they group related people logically so you don't have a single huge list. Two employees in different departments can share the same first name (e.g., two "Rahul"s) as long as their department differs.
- **Remapping** is like a **company directory reassignment** — if "Rahul from HR" gets transferred and starts doing an Engineering job, the company directory can be updated to reflect this new role (e.g., `/hr/rahul` → `/engineering/rahul`) **without changing Rahul's actual name or the HR records**.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of ROS Identification

ROS uses names to identify **four** primary resource types:

1. **Nodes** — Processes performing computation.
2. **Topics** — Named buses over which nodes exchange messages.
3. **Services** — Synchronous request/reply endpoints.
4. **Parameters** — Configuration values stored in the Parameter Server.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** ROS names are **case-sensitive**, must consist of alphanumerics, underscores (`_`), and forward slashes (`/`), and cannot begin with a digit. A name like `/turtle1/cmd_vel` is perfectly valid; `1turtle/cmd_vel` is **not**.

## 1.4 Categories of Names (Critical for KTU)

ROS classifies names into **four** primary types based on their syntactic form:

| Prefix Syntax | Name Type | Scope |
|---------------|-----------|-------|
| `/foo/bar` | **Global** | Resolves from anywhere in the system |
| `foo/bar` | **Relative** | Resolves relative to the default namespace |
| `~foo/bar` | **Private** | Resolves to a node's own private namespace |
| `Foo` | **Base** | Resolves as a search-space relative name |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Forgetting the leading slash (`/`) turns a Global name into a Relative name, completely changing its resolution path. This is one of the most frequent causes of "topic not found" errors in ROS.

## 1.5 Why Remapping Exists

A single robot program (e.g., a camera driver) should be **reusable** on different robots without code modification. If robot A uses `/front_camera/image` and robot B uses `/rear_camera/image`, the same code should work on both. **Remapping** lets the user rewrite the names at launch time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a ROS Name

A ROS graph resource name is a **Unix-style hierarchical path** (similar to a file system path). Each segment separated by `/` represents a **namespace level**. The final segment is the resource's **base name**.

**Example:** `/teleop/turtle1/cmd_vel`
- `/` → **Root namespace**
- `teleop/` → **Sub-namespace (group)**
- `turtle1/` → **Sub-sub-namespace (instance)**
- `cmd_vel` → **Base name (the actual topic)**

## 2.2 The Four Name Types — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Global Names
- **Syntax:** Begins with a leading slash (`/`)
- **Example:** `/camera/image_raw`
- **Resolution:** The name is looked up **exactly** as written, anchored at the root namespace.
- **Use case:** When you want a resource to be unambiguously identified regardless of the default namespace.

### 2.2.2 Relative Names
- **Syntax:** No leading slash or tilde
- **Example:** `cmd_vel` (when default namespace is `/turtle1`)
- **Resolution:** Prepends the **default namespace** to the relative name. With default `/turtle1`, it resolves to `/turtle1/cmd_vel`.
- **Use case:** The most common name type used in typical ROS code.

### 2.2.3 Private Names
- **Syntax:** Begins with a tilde (`~`)
- **Example:** `~max_speed`
- **Resolution:** Resolves to the node's **own private namespace**, typically `/<nodename>/<private_name>`.
- **Use case:** Configuration parameters and internal topics that should not clash with other nodes.

### 2.2.4 Anonymous Names
- **Syntax:** Special form using `__name:=` with an anonymous suffix
- **Example:** `__name:=my_node_1682345678`
- **Use case:** When you need to run multiple instances of the same node simultaneously without name collisions.

## 2.3 Namespace Resolution Algorithm (The "Why" and "How")

When a node advertises or subscribes to a name, the ROS client library executes a **name resolution algorithm** before contacting the ROS Master:

**Step 1 — Tokenize** the name using `/` as a delimiter.

**Step 2 — Determine the type** by inspecting the first character of the first token:
- If it starts with `/` → **Global name**, no modification.
- If it starts with `~` → **Private name**, replace `~` with the node's private namespace.
- Otherwise → **Relative name**, prepend the default namespace.

**Step 3 — Map the resolved name** to the underlying connection endpoint (XML-RPC URI for topics/services, in-memory value for parameters).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The ROS Master is **not** involved in the name resolution step. Resolution happens **client-side** in each node's `roscpp`/`rospy` library. The Master is only contacted to **register** and **discover** the resolved names.

## 2.4 Remapping — The Configuration Superpower

**Remapping** is a substitution rule: *"When a node tries to use name `A`, use name `B` instead."* It is applied **after** the name resolution algorithm runs.

**Sources of Remappings (in priority order):**

1. **Hard-coded remappings in source code** (highest priority for that specific call)
2. **Programmatic remapping** via `ros::init(options, "node_name")` or `rospy.init_node()`
3. **Command-line remapping arguments** (e.g., `rosrun my_pkg my_node /input:=camera/image`)
4. **Launch file `<remap>` tags** (applies to the entire launch hierarchy)
5. **Environment variable `ROS_NAMESPACE`** (sets the default namespace for all subsequent nodes)

## 2.5 The Default Namespace

The **default namespace** is the parent namespace under which all **relative** names are resolved. It is set by:
- The `ROS_NAMESPACE` environment variable, or
- The `ns="..."` attribute of a `<node>` tag in a launch file, or
- Programmatic calls like `ros::_init_options::NoSigintHandler` with `setNamespace()`.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Conceptual Table)

| Concept | Symbolic Representation | Default Value | Scope |
|---------|------------------------|---------------|-------|
| Root Namespace | `/` | Always `/` | Entire system |
| Default Namespace | `$D$` | `ROS_NAMESPACE` env or `/` | Set per-process |
| Node Private NS | `$P = /\text{nodename}$` | Auto-generated from node name | Per-node |
| Global Name | `/a/b/c` | N/A | Resolves as written |
| Relative Name | `a/b/c` | `→ $D$/a/b/c` | Resolves under $D$ |
| Private Name | `~a/b` | `→ $P$/a/b` | Resolves under $P$ |
| Remap Rule | `old_name → new_name` | N/A | Substitutes after resolution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In industrial robotics, namespaces allow multiple robots (e.g., `/robot_arm_1/`, `/robot_arm_2/`) to coexist on the same network using identical software stacks — a concept called **multi-master** and **multi-robot isolation**.

## 2.7 Special Remapping: Anonymous Names

To run multiple instances of the same node (e.g., spawning 3 turtles in `turtlesim`), you must ensure each has a unique name. ROS provides the `__name:=` remapping with `__anon:=true` to **automatically** append a unique identifier (e.g., `/turtlesim_1682345678123456`).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Two nodes with the **same name** on the ROS Master will cause the second to **immediately shut down** with a "name in use" error. Always use anonymous names or explicit remapping when spawning multiple instances.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Name Resolution Analysis

## 3.1 The Name Resolution Algorithm — Exhaustive Walkthrough

Since this topic is conceptual rather than purely mathematical, the derivation below formalizes the **algorithm** that ROS executes when a node references a resource name. This is the most KTU-relevant analytical process.

Let:

$$
N = \text{the raw name string passed by the programmer}
$$

$$
D = \text{the current default namespace (string)}
$$

$$
P = \text{the current node's private namespace (string)}
$$

$$
R = \text{the final resolved name registered with the ROS Master}
$$

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Inspect the first character of $N$

$$
\text{type} = \begin{cases}
\text{Global} & \text{if } N[0] = \text{'/'} \\
\text{Private} & \text{if } N[0] = \text{'~'} \\
\text{Relative} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Logic:** The first character is a syntactic marker. A leading `/` indicates the name is anchored at the root. A leading `~` indicates the name is anchored at the node's private space. Anything else is a relative path.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Apply the resolution rule

**Case A (Global):** No transformation.

$$
R = N
$$

**Case B (Private):** Strip the leading `~` and prepend the private namespace.

$$
R = P \,\text{+}\, \text{stripTilde}(N)
$$

where $\text{stripTilde}(N)$ removes the first character if it is `~`.

**Case C (Relative):** Prepend the default namespace. If $D$ is empty (root), the resolution still works because concatenating with an empty string is identity.

$$
R = D \,\text{+}\, N
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Apply any active remappings

After the initial resolution, scan the **remapping stack** (a list of `old → new` substitution rules) and apply each match:

$$
R = \text{remap}(R, \text{remap\_stack})
$$

A remap matches if $R$ equals the remap's `source` exactly **or** if the remap's `source` is a prefix of $R$ followed by `/`.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Register with ROS Master

The final $R$ is sent to the ROS Master via an XML-RPC call (e.g., `registerPublisher`, `subscribe`, `lookupService`).

## 3.2 Worked Example — Comprehensive Resolution

**Setup:**
- Default namespace: `D = /robot1`
- Node name: `camera_driver`
- Private namespace: `P = /robot1/camera_driver`
- Source code line: `ros::Subscriber sub = nh.subscribe("image_raw", 1, callback);`
- Active remapping: `image_raw := /sensor/cam0/image`

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$
N = \text{"image\_raw"}
$$

**Step 1:** First character is `i` (not `/`, not `~`) → **Relative name**.

**Step 2:** Prepend the default namespace.

$$
R = \text{"/robot1"} + \text{"/"} + \text{"image\_raw"} = \text{"/robot1/image\_raw"}
$$

(Note: ROS client libraries handle the slash insertion logic to avoid `//`.)

**Step 3:** Apply the remapping `image_raw := /sensor/cam0/image`. The remap source `image_raw` matches the **base name** of $R$.

$$
R_{\text{final}} = \text{"/sensor/cam0/image"}
$$

**Step 4:** Node registers subscription to `/sensor/cam0/image` with the ROS Master.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Resolution Outcomes for All Name Types

The following matrix summarizes the resolution given the same default namespace $D = \text{"/robot1"}$ and node `camera_driver`:

| Source Code Name $N$ | Type | Resolved $R$ (no remap) | Final $R$ (with remap `image_raw := /sensor/cam0/image`) |
|----------------------|------|-------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| `/global/topic` | Global | `/global/topic` | `/global/topic` (remap doesn't match prefix) |
