**Step 4 — Convert the rotation to a quaternion.** For axis $\hat{\mathbf{k}} = (0, 1, 0)$ and angle $\theta = 30° = \pi/6$:

$$ w = \cos(15°) = \cos(\pi/12) \approx 0.9659 $$

$$ x = 0 \cdot \sin(15°) = 0 $$

$$ y = 1 \cdot \sin(15°) \approx 0.2588 $$

$$ z = 0 \cdot \sin(15°) = 0 $$

**Final answer:** $P$ in frame $\{A\}$ is $(5.232,\ 1.0,\ 7.330)$ m, and the rotation quaternion is $q = (w, x, y, z) = (0.9659, 0, 0.2588, 0)$. *(7 marks: 2 for rotation matrix, 2 for point transform, 2 for quaternion, 1 for final numerical values.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on tf questions — Examiner's Notes:**
> 1. **Quaternion field order confusion (–2 marks typical):** Students write $(w, x, y, z)$ when filling the ROS message; the correct field order is $(x, y, z, w)$. Always read the field names.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4th row/column (–1 mark):** Homogeneous transforms are $4 \times 4$. Skipping the bottom row $[0\ 0\ 0\ 1]$ loses a mark.
> 3. **Confusing static and dynamic broadcasters (–1 mark):** `StaticTransformBroadcaster` is for fixed relationships (sensor mount). It latches and is sent only once — do not loop. Use `TransformBroadcaster` in a timer for moving joints.
> 4. **Neglecting `canTransform` (–1 mark):** Always check before `lookupTransform`. Examiners explicitly look for exception-safe code.
> 5. **Wrong angle units (–1 mark):** Internal ROS storage is **radians**; converting to degrees must be done at the display step.
> 6. **Wrong transform chain order (–2 marks):** $T_{A\to C} = T_{A\to B} \cdot T_{B\to C}$, *not* the reverse. The parent goes on the left.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Position** = translation only, 3 DoF. **Orientation** = rotation only, 3 DoF. **Pose** = both, 6 DoF.
- `tf2` is the ROS 2 canonical library for transforms; `tf` (no "2") is legacy ROS 1.
- The **homogeneous transform** $4 \times 4$ matrix combines $\mathbf{R}$ (rotation, top-left $3 \times 3$) and $\mathbf{t}$ (translation, top-right $3 \times 1$), with a fixed bottom row $[0\ 0\ 0\ 1]$.
- **Four orientation representations:** rotation matrix, Euler angles, axis–angle, quaternion. They are inter-convertible; `tf2` stores **quaternions** internally.
- **ROS quaternion field order is $(x, y, z, w)$** — scalar last. Math convention is $(w, x, y, z)$ — scalar first.
- **Composition rule:** $T_{A\to C} = T_{A\to B} \cdot T_{B\to C}$. The order is **parent-then-child** when read left-to-right.
- **Inverse rule:** $T_{B\to A}$ has rotation $\mathbf{R}^T$ and translation $-\mathbf{R}^T \mathbf{t}$.
- **Gimbal lock** occurs at Euler pitch $\pm 90°$ — quaternions avoid this; this is why `tf2` uses them.
- **`canTransform` before `lookupTransform`** — best practice for exception safety and time validity checks.
- **Static vs dynamic:** use `StaticTransformBroadcaster` for fixed geometry (sensor mounts, URDF-fixed joints); use `TransformBroadcaster` in a periodic loop for moving joints.
- **Tools to remember:** `tf2_echo`, `view_frames`, `tf2_monitor`, `rviz2` TF plugin.
- **Default `lookupTransform` timeout** in `tf2` is **1 second** (a common viva question).
- **Radians everywhere internally**; display in degrees only for human readability.
- The `/tf_static` topic uses **latched** semantics (newer: transient local QoS in ROS 2) and the `/tf` topic is **continuously published** with high frequency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Publishing to a Topic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Publishing to a Topic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the Robot Operating System (ROS) architecture, **Publishing to a Topic** refers to the act of a *ROS Node* (the **Publisher**) pushing structured data packets—called **Messages**—onto a named, strongly-typed, anonymous, and many-to-many communication bus known as a **Topic**. A topic is identified by a unique string name (e.g., `/turtle1/cmd_vel`) and is associated with a specific **Message Type** (e.g., `geometry_msgs/Twist`). The publisher node does not know who (if anyone) is listening; it simply "broadcasts" messages at a defined rate, and the ROS Master (ROS 1) or DDS layer (ROS 2) handles the routing to all interested subscribers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> A *Publisher* is a node that creates a `ros::Publisher` (C++) or `rospy.Publisher` (Python) object, advertises a topic name with a fixed message type, and continuously calls a `publish()` method to inject serialized message data into the ROS communication infrastructure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "FM Radio Station" Model

Imagine ROS topics as **FM Radio Frequencies** in a city:

| Radio Concept | ROS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **FM 93.5 Red FM** | Topic name: `/robot/speed_cmd` |
| **Audio signal format (Stereo/MP3)** | Message Type: `geometry_msgs/Twist` |
| **The radio tower (broadcaster)** | The Publisher Node |
| **Listeners in cars** | Subscriber Nodes |
| **The empty airwaves** | ROS Master / DDS Discovery |
| **Volume / Data Rate** | Publish Rate (Hz) |
| **Buffer of unsent audio** | Publisher Queue Size |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Analogy Works:** A radio station (publisher) does not call each listener (subscriber) personally. It simply transmits on a fixed frequency (topic), and any car tuned in (subscribed) receives the signal. Multiple stations can broadcast simultaneously on different frequencies without interfering, just as multiple topics coexist in the ROS graph.

## 1.3 Key Terminology You Must Know for KTU Exams

- **Node:** An executable process that performs computation (written using `rospy` for Python or `roscpp` for C++).
- **Master (ROS 1) / DDS (ROS 2):** The name service that enables nodes to find each other.
- **Topic:** A named bus over which messages flow (unidirectional: pub → sub).
- **Message:** A strictly typed data structure (`.msg` file) containing fields like `int32`, `float64`, `string`, etc.
- **Publisher Object:** The local handle created in code that represents the connection to the topic.
- **Queue Size:** The internal buffer in the publisher holding outgoing messages if subscribers are slow.
- **Latch:** A boolean flag (`latched=True` or `advertiseOptions latch = true`) that causes the last message to be saved and sent to any *late-joining* subscriber.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Topic Publishing

### Pillar 1: Topic Registration (Advertising)
Before a single message can be sent, the publisher must *register* its intent with the ROS Master (ROS 1) or with the DDS discovery layer (ROS 2). This is called **advertising**.

$$\text{Publisher} = \text{NodeHandle.advertise}\langle \text{MessageType} \rangle(\text{"topic\_name"}, \text{queue\_size})$$

The Master replies with the **URI** (Uniform Resource Identifier) of any currently connected subscribers. Once a subscriber joins later, the Master performs a **callback negotiation** so the publisher and subscriber establish a direct TCP/UDP connection.

### Pillar 2: Message Serialization
ROS messages are defined in `.msg` files. When you call `publish(msg)`, the message object is **serialized** (converted into a compact byte stream using the standard MD5-summed CDR or ROS wire format) and pushed into the **send queue**.

### Pillar 3: Transport & Delivery
The serialized bytes traverse the underlying transport:
- **ROS 1:** TCPROS (reliable, default), UDPROS (best-effort, low-latency).
- **ROS 2:** DDS over UDP (RTPS protocol), with configurable Quality-of-Service (QoS) profiles.

## 2.2 Why Does Publishing Matter in Robotics?

In a real autonomous robot, dozens of sensors and actuators must communicate at high frequency. Topics provide:
1. **Decoupling:** The camera node doesn't need to know whether the SLAM node or the obstacle detector is listening.
2. **Asynchrony:** The LiDAR can publish at 40 Hz while a slow image processor runs at 5 Hz—each operates independently.
3. **Modularity:** You can swap out a Hokuyo LiDAR for a Velodyne LiDAR by simply republishing the same `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` messages on `/scan`.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Parameter Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the exact parameter table you should memorize for KTU 2024 ESE questions.

| Parameter | Python (`rospy`) | C++ (`roscpp`) | Default | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Topic Name** | 1st positional arg | 1st positional arg | None (mandatory) | Unique string identifier |
| **Message Type Class** | 2nd positional arg | Template arg `<>` | None (mandatory) | Defines message schema |
| **Queue Size** | `queue_size=N` | 2nd positional arg | `1` (C++) / `None` (Py) | Buffer depth for unsent messages |
| **Latch** | `latch=True` | `latch = true` | `False` | Cache last msg for late subscribers |
| **TCP/UDP** | N/A | `tcp_nodelay` | `False` (TCP) | Disable Nagle's algorithm |
| **Anonymous** | `anonymous=True` | N/A | `False` | Appends random suffix to node name |

## 2.4 The Publish Rate vs. Queue Size Trade-off

$$\text{Required Queue Size} \approx \text{Publish Rate (Hz)} \times \text{Maximum Subscriber Latency (s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If you publish at 100 Hz and a subscriber freezes for 0.1 s, you need a queue size of at least **10** to avoid message loss. Setting it to `1` (the C++ default) is the **#1 cause** of "publishers work, but data is missing" bugs in student code.

## 2.5 Latch — The "Welcome Mat" for Late Subscribers

A latched publisher stores the **most recent message** in memory. When a brand-new subscriber connects, the publisher immediately replays the last message to it, even if the publisher is currently silent. This is essential for:
- Publishing static **transforms** (`tf2`).
- Publishing **map data** that rarely changes.
- Publishing **robot descriptions** (URDF).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Process Flow & Code Walkthrough

## 3.1 The Complete Lifecycle of a Published Message

The following derivation-style walkthrough maps the **10 exact steps** from writing code to message delivery. This is a frequent 14-mark KTU question.

### Step 1 — Initialize the ROS Node
Every ROS process must declare its identity to the Master.

```python
rospy.init_node('velocity_publisher', anonymous=True)
```

- `anonymous=True` appends a random integer to the node name (e.g., `velocity_publisher_4782`) so multiple instances don't collide.

### Step 2 — Construct the Publisher Object

```python
pub = rospy.Publisher('/turtle1/cmd_vel', Twist, queue_size=10)
```

- The variable `pub` is now a **handle** to the topic `/turtle1/cmd_vel`.
- The Master is informed: *"This node publishes `Twist` messages on `/turtle1/cmd_vel`."*

### Step 3 — Wait for Subscriber Registration
Even though `rospy.Publisher` returns immediately, the connection to a subscriber is asynchronous. Best practice is to wait.

```python
rospy.wait_for_message('/turtle1/cmd_vel', Twist, timeout=5)
# OR simply
rate = rospy.Rate(10)  # ensures 100ms loop
```

### Step 4 — Construct the Message Object

```python
vel_msg = Twist()
vel_msg.linear.x = 0.5    # forward velocity (m/s)
vel_msg.linear.y = 0.0
vel_msg.linear.z = 0.0
vel_msg.angular.x = 0.0
vel_msg.angular.y = 0.0
vel_msg.angular.z = 0.5   # rotation (rad/s)
```

### Step 5 — Publish Inside a Loop with Rate Control

```python
while not rospy.is_shutdown():
    pub.publish(vel_msg)
    rate.sleep()
```

### Step 6 — Master Performs Handshake
When a subscriber appears, the Master sends the publisher a **XMLRPC callback** with the subscriber's URI. A direct **TCPROS** connection is established.

### Step 7 — Serialization
The `Twist` object is converted into a binary stream according to the message definition.

```
0x00 0x00 0x00 0x18   ← length prefix (24 bytes)
... linear.x (8 bytes, little-endian float64) ...
... linear.y (8 bytes) ...
... linear.z (8 bytes) ...
... angular.x, y, z (24 bytes) ...
```

### Step 8 — Push to Send Queue
If the queue has space, the bytes are enqueued. If full, the **oldest message is dropped** (FIFO eviction policy).

### Step 9 — Transport Layer Sends Bytes
The TCPROS thread (or DDS thread) writes the bytes to the socket.

### Step 10 — Subscriber Callback Invoked
The subscriber's callback function executes with the deserialized message.

## 3.2 The Same Lifecycle in C++ (roscpp)

```cpp
#include "ros/ros.h"
#include "geometry_msgs/Twist.h"

int main(int argc, char **argv) {
    ros::init(argc, argv, "velocity_publisher");
    ros::NodeHandle nh;

    // Step 2: Advertise
    ros::Publisher pub = nh.advertise<geometry_msgs::Twist>("/turtle1/cmd_vel", 10);

    ros::Rate loop_rate(10);
    while (ros::ok()) {
        geometry_msgs::Twist vel_msg;
        vel_msg.linear.x = 0.5;
        vel_msg.angular.z = 0.5;
        pub.publish(vel_msg);
        ros::spinOnce();
        loop_rate.sleep();
    }
    return 0;
}
```

## 3.3 Mathematical Model — Throughput & Bandwidth

For a 14-mark KTU question, derive the **required network bandwidth** of a publisher:

$$
B_{\text{required}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \times S_i \times N_{\text{sub},i}
$$

Where:
- $B_{\text{required}}$ = Total bandwidth in bytes/second.
- $f_i$ = Publish frequency of topic $i$ (Hz).
- $S_i$ = Size of message type $i$ (bytes).
- $N_{\text{sub},i}$ = Number of subscribers to topic $i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (frequently asked):** A robot publishes `Image` messages (250 KB) at 30 Hz to 2 subscribers, and `Imu` messages (48 B) at 100 Hz to 1 subscriber. Total bandwidth = $(30 \times 250{,}000 \times 2) + (100 \times 48 \times 1) = 15{,}004{,}800$ B/s $\approx$ **14.3 MB/s**.

## 3.4 Quality of Service (QoS) in ROS 2 — The New Frontier

ROS 2 introduces **QoS profiles** that publishers must declare:

| QoS Policy | Effect | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Reliability** `RELIABLE` | TCP-like, retransmits lost packets | Commands, state |
| **Reliability** `BEST_EFFORT` | UDP-like, drops on loss | Sensor streams |
| **Durability** `TRANSIENT_LOCAL` | Late joiners get last msg (similar to latch) | Parameters, maps |
| **History** `KEEP_LAST(n)` | Store only last `n` msgs | Default |
| **History** `KEEP_ALL` | Store all (unbounded memory risk) | Logging |

> [!WARNING]
> A **subscriber and publisher QoS mismatch** (e.g., subscriber wants `RELIABLE` but publisher offers `BEST_EFFORT`) is the **#1 reason ROS 2 topics silently fail** in KTU lab exams. Always check with `ros2 topic info <topic> --verbose`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Publisher-Subscriber Communication Graph

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the full data flow from a single publisher node to multiple subscriber nodes through the ROS Master, including the serialization, queueing, and transport stages.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PUBLISHER NODE\nrospy.Publisher / ros::Publisher"]
    B["MSG OBJECT\ngeometry_msgs Twist"]
    C["SERIALIZATION LAYER\nMD5 CDR Encoding"]
    D["SEND QUEUE\nFIFO buffer size N"]
    E["TRANSPORT LAYER\nTCPROS ROS1\nDDS RTPS ROS2"]
    F["ROS MASTER ROS1\nDDS DISCOVERY ROS2"]
    G["SUBSCRIBER NODE 1\ncmd_vel listener"]
    H["SUBSCRIBER NODE 2\nplanner node"]
    I["SUBSCRIBER NODE 3\nlogger"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    F -.registers.-> A
    F -.registers.-> G
    F -.registers.-> H
    F -.registers.-> I
    E --> G
    E --> H
    E --> I

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style I fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Publisher State Machine — A Block Functional Architecture

The publisher itself transitions through well-defined states from startup to shutdown. This is critical for KTU 14-mark questions on publisher lifecycle.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Uninitialized
    Uninitialized --> Advertising: rospy.init_node called
    Advertising --> Connecting: Master replies with subscriber URI
    Connecting --> Active: First subscriber callback registered
    Advertising --> Orphaned: No subscribers yet
    Orphaned --> Active: Subscriber appears later
    Active --> Active: publish call adds to queue
    Active --> Throttled: Queue full oldest msg dropped
    Throttled --> Active: Subscriber drains queue
    Active --> Shutdown: rospy shutdown or ros::shutdown
    Shutdown --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Code-to-Message Mapping Diagram

This diagram shows how a single line of code becomes a wire-level message on the network.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["pub = rospy.Publisher\nname /turtle1/cmd_vel\ntype Twist\nqueue 10"]
    P2["vel = Twist()\nvel.linear.x = 0.5\nvel.angular.z = 0.5"]
    P3["pub.publish vel"]
    P4["SERIALIZE\nTwist to bytes"]
    P5["PACKET ON WIRE\nlength prefix 24\nlinear x 00 00 00 00 00 00 E0 3F\nlinear y 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00\nlinear z 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00\nangular z 00 00 00 00 00 00 E0 3F"]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5

    style P1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333
    style P2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333
    style P3 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333
    style P4 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333
    style P5 fill:#F5DEB3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the role of a ROS Publisher with a suitable real-world analogy. List the parameters required to create a `rospy.Publisher` object in Python.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A ROS Publisher is a node that **broadcasts structured messages** on a named topic, decoupling it from any subscriber. Analogy: an **FM radio station** — the station (publisher) transmits on a fixed frequency (topic) without knowing who is listening; any receiver (subscriber) tuned in receives the broadcast.

Required `rospy.Publisher` parameters:
1. **Topic name** (string, e.g., `/cmd_vel`)
2. **Message type class** (e.g., `Twist`)
3. **Queue size** (integer buffer depth, e.g., `10`)

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for analogy, 1 mark for parameters.)*

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*What is the purpose of the `latch` option in a ROS publisher? When would you use `latch=True`?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `latch` option causes the publisher to **cache the most recently published message** in memory and automatically deliver it to any *new* subscriber that connects later. This is critical when a late-joining subscriber must receive the current state of the robot (e.g., robot URDF, static transforms, current map) **even if the publisher is no longer actively publishing**.

Use cases: `robot_state_publisher` (URDF), `map_server` (static map), `tf2 static_transform_publisher`. In ROS 2, the equivalent is the `Durability` QoS policy set to `TRANSIENT_LOCAL`.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for use case, 1 mark for ROS 2 equivalent.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Describe the **complete lifecycle of a published message** in ROS 1, from node initialization to subscriber callback. Include the role of the ROS Master, XMLRPC, and TCPROS in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete **Python ROS node** that publishes `geometry_msgs/Twist` messages on the topic `/turtle1/cmd_vel` to make the `turtlesim` turtle move in a **circle of radius 1 meter** at a forward speed of 2 m/s. Show all imports, the publisher creation, the rate, and the math. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (A-a) — Lifecycle Walkthrough (7 marks)

1. **(1 mark)** `rospy.init_node('node_name')` registers the node with the Master via XMLRPC. The Master assigns a unique name and stores its URI.
2. **(1 mark)** `rospy.Publisher('/topic', MsgType, queue_size=N)` calls `advertise()` on the Master. The Master stores the topic registration in its registry.
3. **(1 mark)** Master sends an XMLRPC callback to the publisher with the **URIs of all current subscribers** matching the topic+type.
4. **(1 mark)** Publisher opens a **TCPROS connection** (TCP socket) to each subscriber.
5. **(1 mark)** When `pub.publish(msg)` is called, the message is **serialized** (Twist → bytes) using the standard CDR encoding, prefixed with a 4-byte length, and **enqueued** in the send buffer.
6. **(1 mark)** The TCPROS thread drains the buffer over the socket. If the queue is full, the **oldest message is dropped** (FIFO).
7. **(1 mark)** The subscriber receives the bytes, **deserializes** them back into a `Twist` object, and invokes its **callback function** in the subscriber's thread.

#### Model Solution (A-b) — Circle Trajectory Code (7 marks)

For a circle of radius $r$ at forward speed $v$:

$$
\omega = \frac{v}{r} = \frac{2}{1} = 2 \text{ rad/s}
$$

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import rospy
from geometry_msgs.msg import Twist

# Step 1: Initialize node
rospy.init_node('circle_publisher', anonymous=True)

# Step 2: Create publisher
pub = rospy.Publisher('/turtle1/cmd_vel', Twist, queue_size=10)

# Step 3: Set rate
rate = rospy.Rate(10)  # 10 Hz

# Step 4: Construct and publish
vel = Twist()
vel.linear.x = 2.0    # forward speed 2 m/s
vel.angular.z = 2.0   # omega = v/r = 2/1 = 2 rad/s

while not rospy.is_shutdown():
    pub.publish(vel)
    rate.sleep()
```

**Mark split:** 1 mark math derivation, 2 marks imports + init, 2 marks publisher + rate, 2 marks loop logic.

---

### Question B. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Queue Size** in a ROS publisher. What happens when the queue is full? Derive the minimum queue size required for a publisher running at 50 Hz with a maximum subscriber processing latency of 0.08 seconds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **TCPROS** and **UDPROS** transport protocols. When would you choose each? Mention the corresponding ROS 2 QoS policies. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (B-a) — Queue Size (7 marks)

- **(2 marks)** **Definition:** The queue size is the integer buffer capacity in the publisher that stores outgoing messages awaiting transmission. In C++, the default is `1`; in Python, it is **mandatory** and there is no default.
- **(2 marks)** **Overflow behavior:** When the queue is full, the **oldest message is discarded** (FIFO eviction) to make room for the newest. The publisher does not block the calling thread; this is non-blocking.
- **(3 marks)** **Derivation:**

$$
Q_{\min} = f_{\text{publish}} \times t_{\text{latency}} = 50 \text{ Hz} \times 0.08 \text{ s} = 4
$$

However, a safety factor of $2\times$ to $5\times$ is recommended in production code:

$$
Q_{\text{recommended}} = 5 \times Q_{\min} = 5 \times 4 = 20
$$

So a queue size of **20** is recommended; a minimum of **4** is mathematically valid.

#### Model Solution (B-b) — TCPROS vs UDPROS (7 marks)

| Aspect | TCPROS | UDPROS |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP (stream, reliable) | UDP (datagram, best-effort) |
| **Packet Loss** | Retransmitted | Dropped silently |
| **Latency** | Higher (Nagle's algorithm) | Lower |
| **Use Case** | Commands, state, infrequent large msgs | High-frequency sensors (cameras, LiDAR) |
| **Header** | `TCPROS` magic bytes | `UDPROS` magic bytes |
| **ROS 2 QoS Equivalent** | `Reliability = RELIABLE` | `Reliability = BEST_EFFORT` |

- **(3 marks)** for the comparison table.
- **(2 marks)** for stating use-case rule: *"Commands use TCPROS / RELIABLE; sensors use UDPROS / BEST_EFFORT."*
- **(2 marks)** for the explicit ROS 2 mapping.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to call `rospy.init_node()`** → no Master connection → silent failure. *(−2 marks)*
> 2. **Confusing `Publisher()` with `publish()`** — the former *advertises*, the latter *sends*. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Not setting `queue_size`** in Python (mandatory since ROS Noetic). *(−2 marks)*
> 4. **Wrong message type** (e.g., importing `Twist` from `nav_msgs` instead of `geometry_msgs`). *(−2 marks)*
> 5. **Missing `rospy.is_shutdown()`** check in the loop → node cannot be killed cleanly with Ctrl-C. *(−1 mark)*
> 6. **Calling `rate.sleep()` BEFORE `publish()`** in some incorrect order — it still works, but conventionally `publish` first. *(Not penalized, but flag.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Publisher** broadcasts messages on a topic; it does **not** know or care who subscribes.
- ✅ A topic is defined by a **(name, message_type)** pair — both must match between pub and sub.
- ✅ `rospy.Publisher(name, type, queue_size)` — Python **requires** `queue_size`.
- ✅ `nh.advertise<T>(name, queue_size)` — C++ defaults to queue_size = `1` (often too small!).
- ✅ **Latch** (ROS 1) / **Durability = TRANSIENT_LOCAL** (ROS 2) caches the last message for late subscribers.
- ✅ Publish rate $\times$ latency = minimum queue size (add 2–5× safety factor).
- ✅ Use `rospy.Rate(hz)` or `ros::Rate` to control loop frequency; do **not** use `time.sleep()`.
- ✅ A running `roscore` is **mandatory** for ROS 1 publishers; ROS 2 does not need a master.
- ✅ Verify with `rostopic list`, `rostopic echo /topic`, `rostopic hz /topic`, and `rqt_graph`.
- ✅ In ROS 2, **QoS mismatch** is the #1 cause of silent topic failure.
- ✅ The Master-to-publisher callback uses **XMLRPC**; the data path uses **TCPROS** (a separate socket).
- ✅ Bandwidth equation: $B = \sum f_i \cdot S_i \cdot N_{\text{sub},i}$ — useful for KTU numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subscribing to a Topic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Subscribing to a Topic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2)**, *subscribing to a topic* is the act of a **ROS node registering its interest in receiving messages of a particular type that are published on a named communication channel (topic)*. Subscription is implemented through the client libraries `rclpy` (Python) and `rclcpp` (C++), which interface with the underlying **Data Distribution Service (DDS)** middleware to perform automatic peer-to-peer discovery, matching, and reliable message delivery.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> A *Subscriber* is the receiving end of the ROS 2 publish/subscribe paradigm. A node that creates a subscription declares: "I want all messages published on topic `<topic_name>` of type `<MessageType>` to be delivered to my callback function."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Functional Triad of a Subscriber**
> 1. **Topic Name** — the string identifier (e.g., `/robot/cmd_vel`).
> 2. **Message Type** — strictly typed schema (e.g., `geometry_msgs/msg/Twist`).
> 3. **Callback Function** — user-defined handler invoked on every incoming message.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **newspaper subscription system**:

- The **newspaper name** (e.g., "Daily Robotics Times") is the **Topic**.
- The **publisher's press** is the **Publisher Node** that prints the paper.
- **You**, who fill out a subscription form to receive the paper at your doorstep, are the **Subscriber Node**.
- The **postman** delivering the paper to your door is the **DDS middleware**.
- The act of **reading the paper** once it arrives is invoking the **callback function**.

> You don't go fetch the paper from the press yourself — you simply declare your interest, and the delivery system (DDS) ensures the right data reaches you *automatically and asynchronously*.

## 1.3 Why Subscribers Matter in Robotics

A robot's perception, decision, and actuation pipelines are almost entirely **event-driven**. A subscriber:

- Receives **sensor data** (LiDAR, camera, IMU) without polling.
- Decouples the **producer** of data from its **consumers**.
- Allows **many-to-many** and **many-to-one** topologies (e.g., 1 camera publisher → 5 processing subscribers).
- Enables **modularity**: a navigation node and a visualization node can independently subscribe to the same `/scan` topic.

> [!TIP]
> A single node can be **both a publisher and a subscriber** simultaneously. For example, a `teleop_twist_node` subscribes to joystick input and publishes velocity commands.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Publish–Subscribe (Pub–Sub) Architecture in ROS 2

ROS 2 abandons the centralized ROS 1 master in favor of **DDS-based decentralized discovery**. The lifecycle of a subscription is governed by the following structured logic:

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Node Initialization**
   - The `rclpy.init()` call boots the ROS 2 client library, creating a context for the node.
   - A `Node` object is instantiated (e.g., `node = rclpy.node.Node('listener')`).

2. **Subscription Declaration**
   - The node calls `create_subscription(MessageType, 'topic_name', callback, qos_profile)`.
   - This registers the subscription with the underlying DDS participant.

3. **Discovery Phase (DDS Layer)**
   - DDS uses **RTPS (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe)** protocol for automatic peer discovery over **UDP multicast** (or shared memory in intra-process scenarios).
   - The subscriber announces its interest via **SEDP (Simple Endpoint Discovery Protocol)** announcements.
   - Discovery typically completes in **< 1 second** on a local network.

4. **Matching & Handshake**
   - When a matching publisher is discovered (same topic name + compatible message type + compatible QoS), DDS establishes a logical data path.

5. **Message Reception**
   - Published messages traverse the DDS stack and are deserialized into Python/C++ objects.
   - They are pushed onto the node's **executor queue**.

6. **Callback Invocation**
   - The **executor** (`rclpy.spin(node)`) dequeues messages and invokes the registered callback function with the message as its argument.
   - The callback executes **synchronously in the executor thread**.

7. **Graceful Shutdown**
   - On `rclpy.shutdown()` or `destroy_node()`, the subscription is unregistered and DDS resources are released.

## 2.2 Quality of Service (QoS) — The Hidden Contract

QoS policies define the *behavior contract* between publisher and subscriber. If QoS is **incompatible**, no messages are delivered — a common KTU exam pitfall.

| QoS Policy | Purpose | Common Values |
|---|---|---|
| `reliability` | Guaranteed vs. best-effort delivery | `RELIABLE`, `BEST_EFFORT` |
| `durability` | Late-joiner message retention | `VOLATILE`, `TRANSIENT_LOCAL` |
| `history` | Sample storage strategy | `KEEP_LAST(n)`, `KEEP_ALL` |
| `depth` | Queue size for `KEEP_LAST` | Integer $\geq 1$ (e.g., **10**) |
| `deadline` | Max period between messages | `Duration` (e.g., **20 ms**) |
| `lifespan` | Max age of a sample | `Duration` (e.g., **1 s**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compatibility Rule (Exam Favorite):**
> `RELIABLE` publisher → `RELIABLE` subscriber: ✅ Compatible
> `BEST_EFFORT` publisher → `BEST_EFFORT` subscriber: ✅ Compatible
> `RELIABLE` publisher → `BEST_EFFORT` subscriber: ❌ Incompatible (no delivery)
> `BEST_EFFORT` publisher → `RELIABLE` subscriber: ❌ Incompatible

## 2.3 Reference Code Blueprint (Python / `rclpy`)

> [!NOTE]
> ROS programming in `rclpy` is the standard **CS-aligned implementation** expected in KTU OE practicals. Code is permitted for this CS/Robotics course.

```python
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from std_msgs.msg import String  # Standard message type

class MinimalSubscriber(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('minimal_subscriber')
        # create_subscription(MessageType, topic_name, callback, qos_depth)
        self.subscription = self.create_subscription(
            String,
            'chatter',
            self.listener_callback,
            10)   # QoS depth = 10 (KEEP_LAST)
        self.subscription  # prevent unused variable warning

    def listener_callback(self, msg):
        self.get_logger().info(f'I heard: "{msg.data}"')

def main(args=None):
    rclpy.init(args=args)
    minimal_subscriber = MinimalSubscriber()
    rclpy.spin(minimal_subscriber)        # blocks; processes callbacks
    minimal_subscriber.destroy_node()
    rclpy.shutdown()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Term | Definition / Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Topic** | Named bus for message transport (e.g., `/cmd_vel`) |
| **Subscriber** | A registered receiver for a topic's messages |
| **Callback** | Function executed on each received message |
| **QoS Profile** | Set of policies governing delivery semantics |
| **DDS** | Industrial middleware standard beneath ROS 2 |
| **Executor** | Threaded scheduler invoking callbacks (`spin`) |
| **RMW** | ROS Middleware interface (e.g., `rmw_fastrtps`) |
| **Discovery** | Automatic peer detection via RTPS multicast |
| **Spin** | Blocking call that pumps the executor loop |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Multiple perception nodes (camera, radar, LiDAR) publish raw data; a fusion node subscribes to all.
- **Industrial Manipulators**: Joint state subscribers receive proprioceptive feedback at **1 kHz**.
- **Drone Swarms**: Ground control subscribers receive telemetry from $\geq 10$ airborne publishers simultaneously.
- **Humanoid Robotics**: A `teleop` subscriber receives joystick input and forwards it to a locomotion controller.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Logical Derivation & Analytical Matrix

This section decomposes the **subscription handshake** from first principles, deriving the message-delivery pipeline and the QoS compatibility matrix used by KTU examiners.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Subscription Matching Condition

Let a publisher $P$ advertise topic $T$ with QoS vector $\vec{Q_P}$ and message type $M_P$. Let a subscriber $S$ declare subscription on topic $T$ with QoS vector $\vec{Q_S}$ and message type $M_S$.

A subscription becomes **active** (i.e., messages flow) if and only if all of the following hold simultaneously:

$$
\text{TopicMatch} \equiv (T_P \equiv T_S)
$$

$$
\text{TypeMatch} \equiv (M_P \equiv M_S)
$$

$$
\text{QoSMatch} \equiv \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \text{compatible}(\vec{Q_P}[i], \vec{Q_S}[i])
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Active}(P, S) = \text{TopicMatch} \land \text{TypeMatch} \land \text{QoSMatch}\;}
$$

Where $\land$ denotes logical AND across all three conditions, and $n$ is the number of QoS policies defined.

### Step-by-Step Logical Deduction

**Step 1 — Topic Name Equality Check**
DDS compares the topic string identifiers byte-for-byte. If `T_P = "/scan"` and `T_S = "/scan"`, this condition is satisfied. A mismatch in case or leading slash causes failure.

**Step 2 — Type Schema Verification**
DDS verifies that the **TypeObject hash** (computed from the IDL `.msg` definition) matches. If a publisher sends `sensor_msgs/msg/LaserScan` but the subscriber expects `sensor_msgs/msg/PointCloud2`, the subscription is **silently rejected** — a notorious debugging pitfall.

**Step 3 — QoS Policy Pairwise Compatibility**
Each QoS policy is evaluated independently. For `reliability` and `durability`, the compatibility logic is:

$$
\text{compatible}(Q_P^{\text{reliability}}, Q_S^{\text{reliability}}) = \begin{cases} \text{true} & \text{if } Q_P^{\text{reliability}} = Q_S^{\text{reliability}} \\ \text{true} & \text{if } Q_P^{\text{reliability}} = \text{BEST\_EFFORT} \;\text{and}\; Q_S^{\text{reliability}} = \text{BEST\_EFFORT} \\ \text{false} & \text{otherwise (when one side is RELIABLE)} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Endpoint Activation**
Upon passing all three gates, DDS creates a logical data writer → data reader association. The reader's history cache (depth $d$) is allocated.

**Step 5 — Steady-State Delivery**
For every message published at time $t_k$, the delivery latency is:

$$
\Delta t_{\text{delivery}} = t_{\text{callback}} - t_{\text{publish}}
$$

The expected latency on a local network is typically:

$$
\Delta t_{\text{delivery}} \approx \mathbf{1 \text{ ms to 5 ms}}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Subscription Scenarios

| Scenario | Topic Match | Type Match | QoS Match | Result | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Camera pub → Image proc sub | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Messages flow | Computer vision pipelines |
| LiDAR pub (`BEST_EFFORT`) → Safety sub (`RELIABLE`) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | **No delivery** | Misconfigured system |
| `/cmd_vel` pub → `/cmd_vel` sub | ✅ | ❌ (`Twist` vs `String`) | ✅ | **No delivery** | Type mismatch |
| `/odom` pub → 2× subscribers | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Both receive all msgs | Multi-consumer fan-out |
| Subscribed topic never published | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Callback **never fires** | Idle subscriber |

## 3.3 Mathematical Model of Callback Throughput

Let $\lambda$ be the message arrival rate (messages/sec) and $\mu$ be the callback processing rate. The system is stable when:

$$
\lambda < \mu
$$

If the executor's callback queue depth $d$ is exceeded (i.e., $\lambda > \mu$ for a sustained period), `KEEP_LAST(d)` discards older samples. For `KEEP_ALL`, the queue grows unboundedly and may cause memory exhaustion.

$$
\text{Dropped samples per second} = \max(0, \lambda - \mu) \quad \text{(for KEEP\_LAST)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2-Mark Quick Recall:** The QoS policy that controls queue length for late callbacks is `history` with `depth` parameter; default depth in `rclpy` tutorials is **10**.

## 3.4 Lifecycle State Transitions of a Subscriber

$$
\text{Unconfigured} \xrightarrow{\text{create\_subscription()}} \text{Inactive}
$$

$$
\text{Inactive} \xrightarrow{\text{discovery match found}} \text{Active}
$$

$$
\text{Active} \xrightarrow{\text{destroy\_node() / context shutdown}} \text{Finalized}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Subscriber Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Application User Code"] --> B["rclpy Node Object"]
    B --> C["create_subscription API Call"]
    C --> D["DDS Participant Layer"]
    D --> E["RTPS Discovery Multicast"]
    E --> F{"Publisher Discovered?"}
    F -- "Yes, QoS Match" --> G["Data Reader Active"]
    F -- "No" --> H["Wait for Peer"]
    G --> I["Message Arrives via UDP"]
    I --> J["Deserialization to Python Object"]
    J --> K["Push to Executor Queue"]
    K --> L["Callback Function Invocation"]
    L --> M["User Logic Executes"]
    M --> N["Return to spin loop"]
    N --> G
```

## 4.2 End-to-End Pub–Sub Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PubNode as Publisher Node
    participant DDSLayer as DDS Middleware
    participant SubNode as Subscriber Node
    participant Exec as rclpy Executor
    participant CB as Callback Function
    PubNode->>DDSLayer: Advertise topic '/chatter'
    SubNode->>DDSLayer: Subscribe topic '/chatter'
    DDSLayer-->>PubNode: SEDP Endpoint Announcement
    DDSLayer-->>SubNode: SEDP Endpoint Announcement
    DDSLayer->>SubNode: Match Confirmed (QoS Compatible)
    PubNode->>DDSLayer: Publish msg1
    DDSLayer->>SubNode: Deliver msg1
    SubNode->>Exec: Enqueue msg1
    Exec->>CB: Invoke listener_callback(msg1)
    CB-->>Exec: Log output
    PubNode->>DDSLayer: Publish msg2
    DDSLayer->>SubNode: Deliver msg2
    SubNode->>Exec: Enqueue msg2
    Exec->>CB: Invoke listener_callback(msg2)
    CB-->>Exec: Log output
```

## 4.3 Many-to-Many Fan-Out Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Camera Publisher Node"] --> T1["/image_raw Topic"]
    P2["LiDAR Publisher Node"] --> T2["/scan Topic"]
    T1 --> S1["Image Processing Subscriber"]
    T1 --> S2["Visualization Subscriber"]
    T1 --> S3["ML Inference Subscriber"]
    T2 --> S4["Obstacle Detection Subscriber"]
    T2 --> S5["SLAM Subscriber"]
    T2 --> S6["Safety Monitor Subscriber"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for KTU:**
> - A single topic can have **multiple subscribers** (fan-out).
> - A single subscriber can listen to **multiple topics** (e.g., a sensor fusion node).
> - The network is **loosely coupled**: publishers and subscribers need not know each other's existence.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a ROS 2 Subscriber. What are the three mandatory arguments passed to `create_subscription()` in `rclpy`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Subscriber** is a communication endpoint in a ROS 2 node that registers interest in receiving messages published on a specific topic. (1 mark)

The three mandatory arguments to `create_subscription()` in `rclpy` are:

1. **Message Type** — the `.msg` class (e.g., `String`, `Twist`, `LaserScan`). (1 mark)
2. **Topic Name** — the string identifier of the topic (e.g., `'chatter'`). (0.5 marks)
3. **Callback Function** — the handler invoked upon message arrival. (0.5 marks)

*(A fourth optional argument is the QoS profile / queue depth.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a Publisher and a Subscriber in ROS 2.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Publisher | Subscriber |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Sends data out to a topic | Receives data from a topic |
| **Method** | `create_publisher()` | `create_subscription()` |
| **Trigger** | Actively pushes via user code | Passively receives via callback |
| **Returns** | A `Publisher` object | A `Subscription` object |
| **DDS Role** | Data Writer | Data Reader |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **ROS 2 Publisher–Subscriber communication model**. Describe the role of **DDS middleware** and **RTPS discovery** in enabling peer-to-peer communication. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete **Python subscriber node** in `rclpy` that subscribes to a topic named `/robot/speed` of message type `std_msgs/msg/Float32`. The callback should log the received value and compute a running average of the last 5 samples. Show the QoS configuration with `depth = 10`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Publisher Node"] -->|publish| B["DDS Layer RTPS"]
    B -->|discover via SEDP| C["Subscriber Node"]
    B -->|deserialize| D["Executor Queue"]
    D -->|invoke| E["Callback Function"]
```

**Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Publish–Subscribe Pattern** (2 marks): ROS 2 decouples data producers (publishers) from consumers (subscribers) using named topics. Nodes communicate anonymously — they do not need to know each other's address or existence.

2. **DDS Middleware** (2 marks): ROS 2 builds on the **Data Distribution Service (DDS)** standard, an OMG-spec industrial middleware providing discovery, serialization, and delivery semantics. The RMW (ROS Middleware) layer abstracts the specific DDS vendor (Fast DDS, Cyclone DDS, RTI Connext).

3. **RTPS Discovery** (2 marks): Real-Time Publish-Subscribe (RTPS) protocol uses **UDP multicast** for automatic peer discovery. Each participant announces endpoints via the **Simple Endpoint Discovery Protocol (SEDP)**, allowing subscribers to find matching publishers without centralized brokers.

4. **Message Flow** (1 mark): Publisher → DDS DataWriter → RTPS → DDS DataReader → Executor Queue → Callback.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Code (7 marks):**

```python
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from std_msgs.msg import Float32

class SpeedSubscriber(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('speed_subscriber')
        # QoS: KEEP_LAST with depth=10
        self.subscription = self.create_subscription(
            Float32,
            '/robot/speed',
            self.listener_callback,
            10)
        self.subscription  # prevent unused warning
        self.samples = []   # store last 5 samples

    def listener_callback(self, msg):
        self.samples.append(msg.data)
        # Keep only the last 5 samples
        if len(self.samples) > 5:
            self.samples.pop(0)
        # Compute running average
        avg = sum(self.samples) / len(self.samples)
        self.get_logger().info(
            f'Received: {msg.data:.3f} | Running Avg: {avg:.3f}'
        )

def main(args=None):
    rclpy.init(args=args)
    node = SpeedSubscriber()
    try:
        rclpy.spin(node)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        pass
    node.destroy_node()
    rclpy.shutdown()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

**Code Walkthrough (tick-marks breakdown):**

- Import statements and node class: (1.5 marks)
- `create_subscription()` with correct args: (2 marks)
- Callback logic + 5-sample buffer: (2 marks)
- `main()` with `spin()` and graceful shutdown: (1.5 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Quality of Service (QoS)** in ROS 2. Construct a truth table showing the compatibility of the `reliability` QoS policy between publishers and subscribers. What happens when QoS profiles are **incompatible**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a sequence diagram, illustrate the **discovery and message delivery process** between a publisher and a subscriber over DDS. Identify the protocol used and describe the role of the **Executor** in callback dispatch. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**QoS Definition (2 marks):**
Quality of Service (QoS) policies define the *behavioral contract* between publisher and subscriber — reliability, durability, deadline, history, and lifespan of messages. QoS decouples application logic from network behavior.

**Key Policies (2 marks):**

- `reliability`: RELIABLE (TCP-like, retransmits) vs BEST_EFFORT (UDP-like, no retries).
- `durability`: VOLATILE (no late-joiner data) vs TRANSIENT_LOCAL (cache for newcomers).
- `history`: KEEP_LAST(n) vs KEEP_ALL.

**Reliability Compatibility Truth Table (2 marks):**

| Publisher Reliability | Subscriber Reliability | Compatible? | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| RELIABLE | RELIABLE | ✅ Yes | Both guarantee delivery |
| RELIABLE | BEST_EFFORT | ❌ No | Subscriber cannot handle retransmits |
| BEST_EFFORT | RELIABLE | ❌ No | Subscriber demands guarantees |
| BEST_EFFORT | BEST_EFFORT | ✅ Yes | Both accept loss |

**Incompatibility Consequence (1 mark):**
When QoS profiles are incompatible, DDS **silently refuses to establish the data path**. No messages flow, no error is raised at runtime — only a warning log appears. This is one of the most common debugging pitfalls in ROS 2.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Sequence Diagram (4 marks):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Publisher Node
    participant D as DDS / RTPS
    participant S as Subscriber Node
    participant E as Executor
    participant C as Callback
    P->>D: Create DataWriter (advertise endpoint)
    S->>D: Create DataReader (subscribe endpoint)
    D-->>P: SEDP multicast announcement
    D-->>S: SEDP multicast announcement
    D->>S: Match found, allocate queue
    P->>D: Write sample
    D->>S: Deliver sample
    S->>E: Enqueue message
    E->>C: Dispatch to callback
    C-->>E: Return
    E->>S: Await next sample
```

**Protocol Used (1.5 marks):**
**RTPS (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe)** over UDP multicast is the discovery and transport protocol. For intra-process communication, ROS 2 uses **shared memory** for zero-copy performance.

**Role of the Executor (1.5 marks):**
The `rclpy` Executor is a **threaded event loop** that:
- Watches all subscriptions, timers, and services in a node.
- Dequeues incoming messages from the DDS reader cache.
- **Synchronously invokes the registered callback** in the executor's thread context.
- The `spin()` function is a blocking call that keeps the executor running. Without `spin()`, callbacks are **never invoked**, even if messages arrive.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `rclpy.spin(node)`** (–2 marks): The node initializes but callbacks never fire. A common bug.
> 2. **Using `BEST_EFFORT` subscriber for `RELIABLE` publisher** (–1.5 marks): Mismatch silently kills delivery; students often blame "the code" when it's QoS.
> 3. **Wrong message type import** (–1.5 marks): e.g., importing `String` from `std_srvs` instead of `std_msgs`. Always verify the package.
> 4. **Topic name typo / missing leading slash** (–1 mark): `/cmd_vel` vs `cmd_vel` are **different topics** in ROS 2.
> 5. **Not calling `destroy_node()` and `rclpy.shutdown()`** (–0.5 marks): Causes zombie processes and resource leaks; the examiner checks for clean termination.
> 6. **Confusing QoS `depth` with callback latency** (–1 mark): `depth` controls the **queue size**, not the **callback processing rate**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **subscriber** is a node-level object that registers interest in a topic via `create_subscription()`.
- ✅ The three mandatory arguments are: **MessageType**, **topic_name**, and **callback**.
- ✅ The fourth optional argument is the **QoS profile / queue depth** (default = **10** in tutorials).
- ✅ `rclpy.spin(node)` is **mandatory** — without it, no callback ever fires.
- ✅ **DDS** (with the **RTPS** protocol) is the underlying middleware for discovery and delivery.
- ✅ **QoS compatibility is a HARD requirement**: incompatible QoS = silent delivery failure.
- ✅ `RELIABLE` ↔ `RELIABLE` ✅ ; `BEST_EFFORT` ↔ `BEST_EFFORT` ✅ ; all other `reliability` combinations ❌.
- ✅ Default QoS in `rclpy.create_publisher/subscription` is **`depth=10, KEEP_LAST`**.
- ✅ A single topic can support **many subscribers** (fan-out); a node can subscribe to **many topics**.
- ✅ The **callback is executed synchronously** in the executor's thread — long callbacks block other callbacks.
- ✅ Use `Float32`, `String`, `Twist`, `LaserScan` etc. from the **standard ROS message packages**.
- ✅ On shutdown, always call `destroy_node()` then `rclpy.shutdown()` for clean resource release.
- ✅ Discovery typically completes in **< 1 second** on a local network via UDP multicast (port range configurable).
- ✅ ROS 2 is **decentralized** — no `roscore` master; nodes find each other automatically.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Latched Topics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Latched Topics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Latched Topic** (in ROS 1) is a special topic publication mechanism in which the publisher retains the **last published message** in a small internal buffer. When a new subscriber connects to this topic, the ROS Master (or the publisher node itself in later versions) **automatically replays** that last message to the new subscriber without requiring the publisher to re-publish it. The mechanism is conceptually known as `latch=True` on the publisher side.

In **ROS 2**, the equivalent concept is implemented through the **QoS (Quality of Service) policy** called `transient_local` durability, which serves the exact same purpose: ensuring late-joining subscribers still receive the most recent message.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):**
> Latched Topics are classified under the **"ROS Architecture – Publisher/Subscriber Model – Advanced Features"** section. They are a **frequently asked 3-mark question** in KTU ESE because they test the student's understanding of *stateful* vs *stateless* communication in ROS.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **notice board in a college corridor**:
- A **regular topic** is like a teacher giving a verbal announcement in class — if you walk in 5 minutes late, you **missed it**. Nobody replays the announcement for you.
- A **latched topic** is like a **pinned notice on the board**. Even if you arrive late, you can still read the most recently pinned notice. The "latched" message is *stuck* there for any newcomer to read.

The "latch" simply means: *"Remember the last message. Anyone who subscribes later should automatically get a copy of it."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box**
> **Latched Topic:** A topic where the publisher stores the last message and automatically delivers it to any newly connected subscriber. In ROS 1, set via `pub = nh.advertise('topic', queue_size, latch=True)`. In ROS 2, set via `QoSProfile(durability=DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL)`.

## 1.3 Why Latched Topics Exist – The Core Problem

In robotics, certain data is **static or slow-changing** but **absolutely critical at startup**:
- The robot's URDF description (`/robot_description`)
- The pre-built map (`/map`)
- Static transforms (`/tf_static`)
- Configuration parameters

If a navigation node subscribes to `/map` 2 seconds *after* the mapping node published it, the navigation node will simply **wait forever** — there is no new message coming! The solution is latching: store the map, replay it once to any new subscriber.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Latch** | The act of "pinning" the last message to a topic. |
| **`latch=True`** | The Python/C++ flag in ROS 1 to enable latching. |
| **`TRANSIENT_LOCAL`** | The ROS 2 QoS durability policy equivalent to latching. |
| **Late-joining Subscriber** | Any subscriber that connects *after* the message was published. |
| **Stateless vs Stateful Pub/Sub** | Regular topics are *stateless*; latched topics are *stateful* (they remember). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How Latching Works Internally

The lifecycle of a latched topic publication follows a strict, deterministic sequence:

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **Publisher Initialization**
   - Node `N_p` calls `advertise(topic_name, queue_size, latch=True)`.
   - ROS Master registers the publisher and notes the `latch` flag.

2. **First Publication**
   - Node `N_p` calls `publish(msg)` for the first time.
   - The message is sent to all *currently connected* subscribers.
   - **Additionally**, the message is stored in the publisher's internal latched-message buffer (typically holding the **single most recent message**).

3. **Ongoing Publications**
   - Each new `publish(msg)` call:
     - (a) Sends to all live subscribers.
     - (b) **Overwrites** the previous latched message with the new one.

4. **New Subscriber Joins**
   - Node `N_s` calls `subscribe(topic_name, ...)`.
   - The ROS Master (ROS 1) or DDS layer (ROS 2) informs the publisher of the new connection.
   - The publisher **immediately replays** the latched message to `N_s` *once*.
   - After this replay, `N_s` behaves exactly like a normal subscriber (receives future messages only).

5. **Publisher Dies**
   - The latched message is **lost**. There is no persistence to disk.
   - If a new subscriber joins after publisher death, it will receive **nothing** (or the new publisher's own latched message if one exists).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for Exams:**
> Latching is **NOT** persistence. It is in-memory caching of the *last* message. It is **NOT** a recording/logger (that is `rosbag`).

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

ROS does not use numerical formulas, but it has **semantic rules** that students must memorize for marks. The following table consolidates the complete decision matrix.

| Scenario | Regular Topic | Latched Topic (ROS 1) / Transient Local (ROS 2) |
|---|---|---|
| Subscriber joins **before** publisher publishes | Receives the message normally | Receives the message normally |
| Subscriber joins **after** publisher publishes | **Waits forever** (no new message) | **Receives the last message immediately** |
| Multiple subscribers join at different times | Each must wait for the next publish | Each gets the stored message on join |
| Publisher dies | Subscribers wait for next publisher | Latched message lost (no recovery) |
| Message storage location | Not stored | Publisher's RAM buffer (1 message) |
| `queue_size` interaction | Buffers up to N messages for slow subscribers | Buffer is **irrelevant** for the latch (latch is separate) |
| ROS 1 syntax | `pub = nh.advertise('t', 10)` | `pub = nh.advertise('t', 10, latch=True)` |
| ROS 2 syntax | `QoSProfile(depth=10)` | `QoSProfile(depth=10, durability=DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL)` |

## 2.3 When to Use Latched Topics (Engineering Utility)

Latched topics are the **correct engineering choice** under all of the following conditions:

- The data is **static or slowly changing** (rate ≪ 1 Hz, often once at startup).
- The data is **critical at initialization** of other nodes.
- Late subscribers **must not have to wait** for a re-publication.

### Canonical Real-World Use Cases

| ROS Topic | Publisher | Why Latched? |
|---|---|---|
| `/map` | `map_server` | Built once from a `.pgm`/`.yaml` file; never changes during navigation. |
| `/robot_description` | `robot_state_publisher` | URDF is loaded once at launch and rarely changes. |
| `/tf_static` | `tf2_static_transform_publisher` | Static transforms (e.g., sensor mount offsets) are constant. |
| `/joint_states` (in some stacks) | `joint_state_publisher_gui` | Often published once with default values. |
| `/diagnostics` (aggregated) | `diagnostic_aggregator` | Summary status is useful to late subscribers. |

## 2.4 Common Misconceptions (Examiner Traps)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Traps — Memorize These:**
> 1. Latching is **not** a substitute for `rosbag` recording.
> 2. Latching is **not** automatic — you must explicitly opt-in (`latch=True`).
> 3. Latching the subscriber side is **not possible** — it is a **publisher-side** property.
> 4. In ROS 2, you cannot mix `volatile` and `transient_local` on the same topic — both ends must agree.
> 5. `queue_size` does **not** control the latch buffer (latch buffer always holds exactly **1** message).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walk-Through: Subscriber Join Sequence

Let us derive the exact message-delivery behavior using a temporal analysis. Consider:

- Publisher node `P` advertises topic `/static_config` as **latched** at time $t = 0$.
- Publisher `P` publishes message $M_1$ at $t = 1$ s.
- Publisher `P` publishes message $M_2$ at $t = 3$ s.
- Subscriber `S_1` connects at $t = 2$ s.
- Subscriber `S_2` connects at $t = 4$ s.

### Derivation Table

| Subscriber | Connection Time $t_s$ | Last Published Message at $t_s$ | Latched Buffer Content Delivered | Future Messages Received? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ | $2$ s | $M_1$ (since $M_2$ not yet published) | $M_1$ (immediately on join) | Yes (will get $M_2$ when published at $t=3$) |
| $S_2$ | $4$ s | $M_2$ | $M_2$ (immediately on join) | Yes (any new $M_3, M_4, \dots$) |

### General Rule (Algorithm)

For any subscriber $S$ that joins at time $t_s$:

$$
M_{\text{delivered}} = \arg\max_{M \in \mathcal{P}} \; t_{\text{published}}(M) \;\big|\; t_{\text{published}}(M) \leq t_s
$$

where $\mathcal{P}$ is the set of all messages published before $t_s$. The subscriber receives $M_{\text{delivered}}$ **once** on join, and then continues to receive every subsequent message as in a normal topic.

## 3.2 Worked Example — ROS 1 Python Publisher

Below is a minimal, exam-ready code snippet a student could write on paper. *(Note: This is the ONLY code block allowed because ROS is a programming-centric course. Non-CS subjects in the KTU scheme are excluded from code per the protocol.)*

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import rospy
from std_msgs.msg import String

rospy.init_node('latched_publisher_node')
# latch=True is the KEY difference from a regular publisher
pub = rospy.Publisher('/robot_status', String, queue_size=10, latch=True)

rate = rospy.Rate(1)  # 1 Hz
status_msg = String()
status_msg.data = "Robot is OPERATIONAL"

while not rospy.is_shutdown():
    pub.publish(status_msg)
    rate.sleep()
```

**Explanation of the critical line:**

- `latch=True` → the publisher caches the last `status_msg` in its internal buffer.
- Any node that subscribes to `/robot_status` *after* this line begins executing will receive `"Robot is OPERATIONAL"` exactly once, immediately on subscription.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Subscriber on a Latched Topic

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import rospy
from std_msgs.msg import String

def callback(msg):
    rospy.loginfo("Received: %s", msg.data)

rospy.init_node('subscriber_node')
# No special flag is needed on the SUBSCRIBER side
sub = rospy.Subscriber('/robot_status', String, callback)
rospy.spin()
```

**Examiner Note:** Students often incorrectly write `latch=True` on the subscriber. This is a guaranteed **mark deduction** in KTU valuation.

## 3.4 ROS 2 Equivalent

```python
from rclpy.node import Node
from std_msgs.msg import String
from rclpy.qos import QoSProfile, DurabilityPolicy

qos_profile = QoSProfile(depth=10)
qos_profile.durability = DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL

self.publisher_ = self.create_publisher(String, '/robot_status', qos_profile)
```

The `TRANSIENT_LOCAL` durability policy is the **conceptual and functional successor** of ROS 1's `latch=True`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Regular vs Latched Topic Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Publisher Node P starts at t=0] --> B{Publishes M1 at t=1}
    B --> C[Latched Buffer stores M1]
    B --> D[Subscriber S1 subscribes at t=1.5]
    D --> E[S1 receives M1 normally]
    B --> F[Publisher publishes M2 at t=3]
    F --> G[Latched Buffer overwrites with M2]
    F --> H[Subscriber S2 subscribes at t=4]
    H --> I[S2 receives M2 automatically from latched buffer]
    I --> J[S2 continues to receive future M3, M4 normally]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style C fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff9900,color:#000
    style G fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff9900,color:#000
    style I fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009933,color:#000
    style J fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009933,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Late Subscriber Replay

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Publisher Node
    participant L as Latched Buffer
    participant R as ROS Master
    participant S as Late Subscriber

    Note over P: advertise('/map', latch=True)
    P->>L: publish(map_msg)
    L-->>L: Store map_msg
    Note over S: S joins at t=4s
    S->>R: subscribe('/map')
    R->>P: Notify new subscriber
    P->>S: Replay stored map_msg automatically
    Note over S: S now has map and processes it
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Should I Use a Latched Topic?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the data static or rarely changing?}
    Q1 -- No --> R1[Use REGULAR topic]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Do late subscribers need immediate access?}
    Q2 -- No --> R1
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is the data critical at node startup?}
    Q3 -- No --> R1
    Q3 -- Yes --> OUT1[Use LATCHED topic in ROS 1]
    Q3 -- Yes --> OUT2[Use TRANSIENT_LOCAL QoS in ROS 2]

    style OUT1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style OUT2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — ROS 1 vs ROS 2 Latching Equivalence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ROS1[ROS 1 Architecture]
        R1A[Publisher Node] -->|latch=True flag| R1B[XMLRPC Connection to Master]
        R1B --> R1C[Last Message Cached in Publisher RAM]
    end

    subgraph ROS2[ROS 2 Architecture]
        R2A[Publisher Node] -->|DurabilityPolicy TRANSIENT LOCAL| R2B[DDS Middleware Layer]
        R2B --> R2C[Last Message Cached in DDS Participant]
    end

    ROS1 -.Same Outcome.-> OUT[Late subscriber receives last message on join]
    ROS2 -.Same Outcome.-> OUT

    style OUT fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    style R1C fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style R2C fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a latched topic in ROS? Why is it used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **latched topic** is a topic on which the publisher retains the **last published message** in its buffer. When a new subscriber connects, the publisher automatically sends this stored message to the subscriber without waiting for a new publication. It is enabled by setting `latch=True` in ROS 1 (or `DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL` in ROS 2).

**Use:** It is used for **static or rarely changing data** such as `/map`, `/robot_description`, and `/tf_static`, so that nodes subscribing late do not have to wait for the next publish cycle and can begin processing immediately. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mechanism, 1 mark for use case.)*

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Can a subscriber enable latching? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**No.** Latching is a **publisher-side property**, not a subscriber-side property. The flag `latch=True` must be set when the publisher calls `advertise()`. A subscriber simply calls `subscribe()` with no special flag and will automatically benefit from the latched message if the publisher has enabled it. Attempting to set `latch=True` on a subscriber would either be ignored or raise an error depending on the ROS client library. *(1 mark for the No, 1 mark for publisher-side justification, 1 mark for the consequence.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of latched topics in ROS with a suitable example. Compare it with a regular (non-latched) topic in terms of subscriber join-time behavior. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a publisher code snippet, show how latched topics are implemented in ROS 1 and ROS 2. Discuss any **three** real-world robotics scenarios where latched topics are the correct design choice. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Concept and Comparison (7 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** A latched topic is one where the publisher caches the last message in memory and replays it automatically to any subscriber that connects after the message was originally published. The subscriber does not need to wait for a new publication.

- **Subscriber Join-Time Behavior Comparison (3 marks):**
  - *Regular topic:* A subscriber joining at $t=5$ s after a message was published at $t=2$ s will **wait indefinitely** until the publisher publishes a *new* message. There is no replay mechanism.
  - *Latched topic:* The same late-joining subscriber at $t=5$ s will **immediately** receive the cached message from $t=2$ s (assuming no newer message overwrote it). This is the critical difference.

- **Internal Mechanism (2 marks):** The cache is held in the publisher's RAM. Only the **most recent** message is retained. Latching does not persist across publisher restarts.

**(b) Implementation and Use Cases (7 marks):**

- **ROS 1 Implementation (2 marks):**
  ```python
  pub = rospy.Publisher('/map', OccupancyGrid, queue_size=1, latch=True)
  ```
  The keyword argument `latch=True` is the only difference from a regular publisher.

- **ROS 2 Implementation (2 marks):**
  ```python
  qos = QoSProfile(depth=1, durability=DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL)
  pub = self.create_publisher(OccupancyGrid, '/map', qos)
  ```
  Here, the QoS profile's `durability` field is set to `TRANSIENT_LOCAL` to achieve the same effect.

- **Three Real-World Scenarios (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
  1. **`/map` topic:** Published once by `map_server` from a saved file. Navigation nodes (AMCL, move_base) must receive it immediately regardless of when they start.
  2. **`/robot_description` topic:** Contains the URDF. RViz and `robot_state_publisher` need it at startup to render the robot.
  3. **`/tf_static` topic:** Static sensor-to-base transforms are constant; latching ensures all subscribers see them without re-publishing.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*: `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Differentiate between a regular topic and a latched topic in ROS. State the **four** conditions under which a latched topic should be used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot uses a `map_server` node to publish the occupancy grid map on the topic `/map`. Explain step-by-step what happens when (i) a `move_base` node starts **before** `map_server`, and (ii) a `move_base` node starts **5 seconds after** `map_server`. Use a latched topic in your explanation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Differences and Conditions for Use (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Regular Topic | Latched Topic |
|---|---|---|
| Cache of last message | No | Yes (1 message in RAM) |
| Behavior of late subscriber | Waits for new publish | Receives cached message |
| `latch` flag | Absent (default) | Set to `True` |
| Use case | Streaming sensor data | Static configuration data |
| Memory cost | None (beyond queue) | Negligible (1 message) |

**Four Conditions for Use (any 4 × 1 mark = 4 marks):**
1. Data is **static or rarely changing** (publish rate ≪ 1 Hz).
2. Data is **critical at startup** of dependent nodes.
3. **Late-joining subscribers** must be functional immediately.
4. The cost of re-publishing the message is **high or impossible** (e.g., it requires loading a large file).

**(b) Step-by-Step Scenario (7 marks):**

- **Setup (1 mark):** `map_server` advertises `/map` as a latched topic: `pub = nh.advertise('/map', OccupancyGrid, 1, latch=True)`.

- **Case (i): `move_base` starts BEFORE `map_server` (3 marks):**
  1. `move_base` subscribes to `/map` at $t=0$.
  2. At $t=0$, there is no latched message in the buffer.
  3. `map_server` starts at $t=2$ s and publishes the map.
  4. `move_base` receives the map **normally** as a live subscriber.
  5. The map is also stored in the latched buffer for any future late subscribers.

- **Case (ii): `move_base` starts 5 s AFTER `map_server` (3 marks):**
  1. `map_server` publishes the map at $t=2$ s; it is stored in the latched buffer.
  2. `move_base` subscribes at $t=7$ s.
  3. ROS Master notifies `map_server` of the new subscriber.
  4. `map_server` **automatically replays** the cached map message to `move_base`.
  5. `move_base` can immediately begin localization and path planning without waiting for a re-publication.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing latching with `rosbag`:** Latching is in-memory, one message, not a recording. *(-2 marks if confused.)*
> 2. **Writing `latch=True` on the subscriber:** This is syntactically wrong in ROS 1. *(-1 mark per occurrence.)*
> 3. **Forgetting the ROS 2 QoS equivalent:** Examiners often award 1–2 marks for mentioning `DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL`. Missing it costs easy marks.
> 4. **Not stating the limitation:** Latched messages are **lost on publisher death**. Students who claim latching provides "reliable delivery" or "persistence" will be penalized.
> 5. **Vague use cases:** Always cite **specific topic names** (`/map`, `/tf_static`, `/robot_description`) rather than generic "configuration data".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Latched Topic = Publisher Caches Last Message + Replays to New Subscribers.** *(One-line definition — write this first in any answer.)*
- **ROS 1 syntax:** `pub = nh.advertise(topic, queue_size, latch=True)`.
- **ROS 2 syntax:** `DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL` inside a `QoSProfile`.
- **It is a publisher-side property only** — never on the subscriber.
- **Buffer size = 1 message**, held in publisher RAM, **not persistent**.
- **Latched message is lost** if the publisher node dies or restarts.
- **Use it for:** `/map`, `/robot_description`, `/tf_static`, and any other rarely-changing startup data.
- **Do NOT use it for:** High-frequency sensor streams (`/scan`, `/camera/image_raw`) — wastes CPU and offers no benefit.
- **Common examiner traps:** (i) confusing with `rosbag`, (ii) writing `latch=True` on subscriber, (iii) forgetting ROS 2 QoS counterpart, (iv) claiming persistence, (v) vague use cases.
- **Queue size vs latch:** They are **independent**. The queue buffers messages for *slow* live subscribers; the latch caches the *last* message for *future* subscribers.
- **One-word mnemonic for the exam:** **"PIN-ONCE-AUTO"** — *P*ublisher *I*n-memory *N*otification, *O*nce per subscriber, *A*utomatic *U*ntil publisher dies, *O*nly for static data, *C*ached *E*xactly once.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defining Message Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Defining Message Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of ROS Message Types

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** architecture, a **Message Type** is a strongly-typed, language-independent data structure definition that strictly specifies the format, sequence, and semantic meaning of data exchanged between ROS nodes over topics, services, or actions. Message types are declared in plain-text files with the extension **`.msg`** (for topics), **`.srv`** (for services), and **`.msg`** (for actions, though technically stored as **`.action`** files in practice).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every communication primitive in ROS — whether a *Topic*, *Service*, or *Action* — must reference a defined message type. The message type acts as a **contract** between publisher and subscriber, requester and responder, or client and action server.

A message is composed of two fundamental field categories:

1. **Built-in (Primitive) Field Types** — `int8`, `int16`, `int32`, `int64`, `uint8`, `uint16`, `uint32`, `uint64`, `float32`, `float64`, `string`, `bool`, `time`, `duration`, `char`, `byte`.
2. **Complex Field Types** — fixed or variable-length arrays of primitives, and **nested messages** of other custom message types.

Each message instance is serialized over the wire using **CDR (Common Data Representation)** or **ROS1's native binary serialization**, with ROS2 defaulting to **CDR (DDS standard)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Letter" Model

Imagine the Indian Postal System:

| ROS Concept | Postal Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Message Type** (`.msg` file) | The **pre-printed government form template** that defines what fields a letter must have (To, From, Subject, Body, Signature). |
| **Message Instance** | An **actual filled-out letter** with real values written into each field. |
| **Topic** | The **post-office box number** where letters are dropped off. |
| **Publisher Node** | The **sender** writing letters and dropping them in the box. |
| **Subscriber Node** | The **receiver** who checks the box and reads only letters of the form template they understand. |

If the receiver's expected form template does not match the sender's form template (e.g., the sender uses the *Income-Tax Form* but the receiver only understands the *Passport Application Form*), the receiver **silently discards the message** — there is no compile-time error between mismatched nodes in ROS; the communication simply fails at runtime.

> [!NOTE]
> This loose coupling is called **"Type Compatibility by Convention"** — both nodes must use the *same* message type or a *type-compatible* one for communication to succeed. The ROS Master (in ROS1) or DDS Discovery (in ROS2) handles this negotiation.

## 1.3 The Three Communication Message Categories in ROS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Favored Triad — Memorize This:**

| Category | File Extension | Structure | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Topic Message** | `.msg` | Single data structure (one section) | Continuous, asynchronous streaming (sensor data, odometry) |
| **Service Message** | `.srv` | **Request** `---` **Response** (two sections) | Synchronous, one-shot request/reply (trigger a camera, query a database) |
| **Action Message** | `.action` | **Goal** `---` **Result** `---` **Feedback** (three sections) | Long-running, preemptible tasks with periodic feedback (navigate to goal, grasp object) |

The literal string **`---`** is a **delimiter line** separating the message sections and is mandatory in `.srv` and `.action` files.

---

## 1.4 Canonical Example — The `geometry_msgs/Twist` Message

The most commonly used message in mobile robotics for commanding velocity is:

```
# geometry_msgs/msg/Twist.msg
Vector3  linear
Vector3  angular
```

Where `Vector3` is itself a nested message containing `x`, `y`, `z` floating-point fields. A mobile robot base controller subscribes to a topic carrying this message and applies the linear and angular velocities to the wheels.

**Physical interpretation:**

$$\vec{v}_{\text{robot}} = (v_x, v_y, v_z) \quad \text{(m/s, translational)}$$

$$\vec{\omega}_{\text{robot}} = (\omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z) \quad \text{(rad/s, rotational)}$$

A typical "move forward at 0.5 m/s" command would set $v_x = 0.5$, all other components = 0.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a `.msg` File — Field-Level Decomposition

A ROS message file is a **declarative DSL (Domain-Specific Language)** — it contains no executable logic, only field declarations. Each line follows the rule:

```
field_type1 field_name1
field_type2 field_name2
```

**Field modifiers** alter the semantics of a field:

| Modifier | Syntax | Meaning | Memory Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant** | `string FRAME_ID="world"` | Compile-time immutable; usable in `if` checks (rare in topic msgs) | Zero runtime cost |
| **Variable-length array** | `float32[] samples` | Dynamic `std::vector`-style list; only one allowed per message (ROS1) | Dynamic allocation |
| **Fixed-length array** | `float32[12] coeffs` | Static C-array of length 12 | Fixed allocation |
| **Header** | `Header header` | Standardized metadata: `seq`, `stamp`, `frame_id` | Always at top |

> [!NOTE]
> **ROS1 Constraint:** A `.msg` file may contain **at most one variable-length array**, and if present, it **must be the last field** in the message. This restriction was lifted in ROS2 with full CDR support.

## 2.2 The Mandatory `Header` Stamp

For sensor and kinematic messages, ROS mandates inclusion of the `std_msgs/Header` as the first field:
