```
std_msgs/Header header
```

The `Header` provides:

$$H = (s, t_{sec}, t_{nsec}, f)$$

| Symbol | Field | Type | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s$ | `seq` | `uint32` | Auto-incrementing sequence number for ordering detection |
| $t_{sec}$ | `stamp.secs` | `int32` | UNIX epoch seconds at message creation |
| $t_{nsec}$ | `stamp.nsecs` | `int32` | Sub-second nanosecond resolution |
| $f$ | `frame_id` | `string` | TF reference frame (e.g., `"base_link"`, `"odom"`) |

The combined timestamp forms:

$$t_{\text{stamp}} = t_{sec} + t_{nsec} \times 10^{-9} \quad \text{(seconds, SI)}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Message Type Composition

| Concept | Symbolic Form / Syntax | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Message composition | $M = \{ (t_i, n_i) \mid i = 1 \ldots N \}$ | A message is a tuple of `(type, name)` pairs |
| Total wire-size (fixed) | $\displaystyle S_{wire} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{sizeof}(t_i)$ | Pre-allocatable; used for buffer sizing |
| Nested message offset | $o_{nested} = \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \text{sizeof}(t_j) + \text{alignment\_pad}$ | CDR uses natural alignment |
| Service split | $S = (M_{req}, M_{res})$ | Two messages, one delimiter `---` |
| Action split | $A = (M_{goal}, M_{result}, M_{feedback})$ | Three messages, two delimiters `---` |
| Timestamp resolution | $\Delta t_{\min} = 1\,\text{ns}$ | Guaranteed by `nsecs` field |
| Header presence rule | First field $\Rightarrow$ standardized | **Mandatory** for `sensor_msgs`, `nav_msgs`, `geometry_msgs` |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Real Robotics

1. **Interoperability:** A LiDAR manufacturer in Japan and a SLAM algorithm in India can exchange point clouds because both adhere to `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2`.
2. **Language-agnostic introspection:** The same `.msg` compiles to C++, Python, and Java classes — eliminating manual serialization code.
3. **Schema versioning:** When a message evolves (a new field is added), the major version is bumped (`geometry_msgs/Twist` → `geometry_msgs/Twist2`) so older nodes still work.
4. **Safety in safety-critical systems:** A 14-marks KTU question on "defining a message for a self-driving car's lane-detection output" would demand the `Header`, an `int32` lane-count, and `float64` confidence values.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derived From First Principles — Building a Custom Message End-to-End

**Problem Scenario (typical 14-mark KTU question):**
> *"Define a custom ROS message named `RobotStatus.msg` to publish the battery percentage, temperature in Celsius, a human-readable status string, and a 3-axis accelerometer reading. Show every step from file creation to verification."*

### Step 1 — Create the Package (if not already done)

A message file must live inside a **catkin package that depends on `message_generation`** (ROS1) or `rosidl_default_generators` (ROS2). The file physically resides at:

```
<pkg_name>/msg/RobotStatus.msg
```

### Step 2 — Write the `.msg` File

The required composition is $M = \{(t_i, n_i)\}$ with the Header convention applied.

```
# RobotStatus.msg
# Custom message for robot health monitoring
# Author: <student_name>
# Course: OERAT722 - KTU 2024

std_msgs/Header header          # Standard metadata - always first

float32 battery_percentage      # 0.0 to 100.0
float32 temperature_celsius     # Operating temperature
string  status_message          # Human-readable state
float32[3] accelerometer        # [ax, ay, az] in m/s^2
```

**Step-by-step justification of each field:**

| # | Field | Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `header` | `std_msgs/Header` | Provides timestamp for synchronizing with TF tree |
| 2 | `battery_percentage` | `float32` | Range 0–100, single-precision adequate, **4 bytes** |
| 3 | `temperature_celsius` | `float32` | Range roughly −40 to +150°C, **4 bytes** |
| 4 | `status_message` | `string` | Diagnostic text for logging/UI |
| 5 | `accelerometer` | `float32[3]` | Fixed-length, since exactly 3 axes are required |

### Step 3 — Edit `package.xml` to Declare Build and Runtime Dependencies

In ROS1, add the lines:

```xml
<build_depend>message_generation</build_depend>
<exec_depend>message_runtime</exec_depend>
<depend>std_msgs</depend>
```

> **Logic:** `message_generation` is needed at compile-time to generate the language bindings; `message_runtime` is needed at run-time to deserialize.

### Step 4 — Edit `CMakeLists.txt` to Register the Message

```cmake
find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS
  std_msgs
  message_generation
)

add_message_files(FILES
  RobotStatus.msg
)

generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES
  std_msgs
)
```

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. `find_package` — locates ROS dependencies on the system.
2. `add_message_files` — tells the build system **which `.msg` files to compile**.
3. `generate_messages` — invokes the message generator for each listed dependency.

### Step 5 — Compile and Verify

```bash
$ cd ~/catkin_ws
$ catkin_make
$ rosmsg show RobotStatus
```

The `rosmsg show` command should print:

```
std_msgs/Header header
float32 battery_percentage
float32 temperature_celsius
string status_message
float32[3] accelerometer
```

### Step 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Total Memory Footprint Calculation

The wire-size of `RobotStatus` is the sum of the byte-widths of all constituent fields, plus alignment padding:

$$S_{wire} = \underbrace{12}_{\text{Header}} + \underbrace{4}_{\text{battery}} + \underbrace{4}_{\text{temp}} + \underbrace{4}_{\text{string-len}} + \underbrace{12}_{\text{3 floats}} + \underbrace{\text{alignment}}_{0\text{–}3} \quad \text{bytes}$$

$$\boxed{S_{wire} \approx 36 \text{ bytes (minimum, excluding variable-length string payload)}}$$

This small footprint matters in high-frequency publishing (e.g., publishing at **100 Hz**, the bandwidth consumed is $36 \times 100 = 3600$ bytes/s ≈ **3.5 KB/s** — trivially low, demonstrating ROS efficiency).

### Step 3.3 Service Message Construction (Analytical Extension)

To define a service that **requests** a robot ID and **responds** with its status:

```
# GetRobotStatus.srv

string robot_id
---
RobotStatus status
bool found
```

The `---` delimiter divides $M_{req}$ (top) from $M_{res}$ (bottom). The response reuses the previously defined `RobotStatus` message — this is **message composition through nesting**, a core KTU concept.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Message-Definition-to-Runtime-Communication Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart traces the **complete lifecycle** of a ROS message type, from its declarative definition to its runtime instantiation on a publish/subscribe channel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer Writes .msg File in pkg/msg/] --> B[Edit package.xml - Add message_generation dep]
    B --> C[Edit CMakeLists.txt - add_message_files and generate_messages]
    C --> D[Run catkin_make or colcon build]
    D --> E[Build System Invokes genmsg / rosidl]
    E --> F[Language Bindings Generated: Cpp Python Java]
    F --> G[rosmsg show Verifies Type Structure]
    G --> H{Communication Pattern?}

    H -->|Async Stream| I[Topic: Publisher Node Sends Instances]
    H -->|Sync Request| J[Service: Client Sends Request Awaiting Response]
    H -->|Long Task| K[Action: Client Sends Goal Receives Feedback and Result]

    I --> L[Wire Serialization via CDR or ROS1 Binary]
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M[Receiver Node Deserializes and Uses Typed Fields]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0277bd
    style D fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style F fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style L fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b
    style M fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Anatomy of a `.msg` File

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MSG[RobotStatus.msg File Structure]
        direction TB
        H[Header Field - std_msgs/Header - 12 bytes fixed]
        B[Battery Field - float32 - 4 bytes]
        T[Temperature Field - float32 - 4 bytes]
        S[Status String Field - variable length]
        A[Accelerometer Array - float32 fixed-length 3 - 12 bytes]

        H --> B
        B --> T
        T --> S
        S --> A
    end

    subgraph COMP[Compile-time Outputs]
        direction TB
        CPP[Cpp Header: RobotStatus.h]
        PY[Python Module: RobotStatus.py]
    end

    MSG -->|genmsg| COMP

    style H fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style T fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style S fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
    style A fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style COMP fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#424242
```

## 4.3 Three-Way Communication Message Structure Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOPIC[Topic Message - Single Section]
        T1[Field A]
        T2[Field B]
        T3[Field C]
    end

    subgraph SERVICE[Service Message - Two Sections]
        S1[Request Field 1]
        S2[Request Field 2]
        S3[DELIMITER --- ]
        S4[Response Field 1]
        S5[Response Field 2]
    end

    subgraph ACTION[Action Message - Three Sections]
        A1[Goal Field]
        A2[DELIMITER --- ]
        A3[Result Field]
        A4[DELIMITER ---]
        A5[Feedback Field]
    end

    style S3 fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17
    style A2 fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17
    style A4 fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17
    style TOPIC fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style SERVICE fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style ACTION fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** What is a ROS message type? Name any **four** primitive field types supported in a `.msg` file.

**Model Answer:**
A ROS message type is a declarative, language-independent data structure specification (stored in a `.msg` file) that defines the fields, their types, and the order in which they appear when two nodes communicate over a topic, service, or action. It acts as a strict contract between publisher and subscriber.

The four primitive field types are:

1. `int32` — 32-bit signed integer.
2. `float64` — 64-bit IEEE-754 floating-point number.
3. `string` — UTF-8 encoded text.
4. `bool` — Boolean true/false.

> [!NOTE]
> Award full 3 marks only if all four field types are *correctly named with their semantic role*. Naming `int` or `string[]` alone loses one mark.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between a **topic message (`.msg`)** and a **service message (`.srv`)**. State one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `.msg` (Topic) | `.srv` (Service) |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | **Single section** — only fields, no delimiter | **Two sections** separated by `---` delimiter |
| Communication Style | Asynchronous, one-way, many-to-many | Synchronous, two-way, one-to-one |
| Response | **No reply** — fire-and-forget | **Mandatory response** to every request |
| Example | `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` (continuous LiDAR streams) | `std_srvs/Trigger` (one-shot trigger with success/failure reply) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Define a ROS custom message file named `EmergencyStop.msg` for a four-wheeled differential-drive mobile robot. The message must include: a standard ROS header, a `bool` emergency flag, a `string` reason field, and the current linear and angular velocities of the robot. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List the exact modifications required in `package.xml` and `CMakeLists.txt` so that this message is built correctly, and explain the role of the `---` delimiter in a `.srv` file with an example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The required message file content is:

```
# EmergencyStop.msg
# Defines an emergency stop telemetry for a 4WD differential robot

std_msgs/Header header    # Mandatory metadata: stamp and frame_id

bool   emergency          # True = E-stop engaged
string reason             # Human-readable cause: "Obstacle", "Battery Low", etc.

float32 linear_velocity   # Current forward speed in m/s
float32 angular_velocity  # Current yaw rate in rad/s
```

**Field-level justification (to score full 7 marks):**

1. **Header (1 mark)** — Included as the first field; provides timestamp for log synchronization and `frame_id` for TF integration.
2. **`bool emergency` (1 mark)** — Two-state flag, sufficient for binary engagement.
3. **`string reason` (1 mark)** — Diagnostic text; helps post-incident analysis.
4. **`float32 linear_velocity` (2 marks)** — Range −2.0 to +2.0 m/s; `float32` chosen because velocity precision beyond 7 decimal digits is irrelevant for navigation.
5. **`float32 angular_velocity` (2 marks)** — Range −π to +π rad/s; mirrors the linear reasoning.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Required `package.xml` additions (3 marks):**

```xml
<build_depend>message_generation</build_depend>
<exec_depend>message_runtime</exec_depend>
<depend>std_msgs</depend>
```

The `<build_depend>` declares the compile-time requirement; `<exec_depend>` declares the runtime requirement; `<depend>` declares the dependency on the `std_msgs` package (since the `Header` is used).

**Required `CMakeLists.txt` additions (2 marks):**

```cmake
find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS
  message_generation
  std_msgs
)

add_message_files(FILES
  EmergencyStop.msg
)

generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES
  std_msgs
)
```

**Role of `---` delimiter in `.srv` (2 marks):**

The `---` is a **mandatory separator line** that divides the service definition into two regions: the **Request message** (above the `---`) and the **Response message** (below the `---`). Both nodes (server and client) automatically generate two language classes from one `.srv` file. Example:

```
# ResetOdometry.srv
string robot_id
---
bool success
string message
```

A client sends the `robot_id` request; the server replies with the `success` boolean and a diagnostic `message` string.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete lifecycle** of defining and using a ROS message type, from `.msg` file creation to wire serialization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the structures of a topic message, a service message, and an action message using a unified diagram. State one real-world robotics scenario where each is the appropriate choice. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The lifecycle consists of five sequential phases:

1. **Authoring** (1 mark) — Developer writes `<pkg>/msg/MyMsg.msg` declaring fields.
2. **Manifest Update** (1 mark) — `package.xml` is updated with `message_generation` build dependency.
3. **Build Registration** (2 marks) — `CMakeLists.txt` invokes `add_message_files` and `generate_messages` so the build system knows which files to compile.
4. **Code Generation** (2 marks) — At `catkin_make`/`colcon build` time, `genmsg` or `rosidl` generates language-specific bindings (`.h`, `.py`).
5. **Wire Serialization** (1 mark) — When a node publishes, the message instance is serialized to CDR/binary form and transmitted over the ROS middleware; subscribers deserialize and access typed fields.

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Type | Sections | Real-World Scenario |
|---|---|---|
| **Topic `.msg`** | One | Streaming camera frames at 30 Hz for object detection |
| **Service `.srv`** | Two (request + response) | Sending a one-shot command to take a single photo |
| **Action `.action`** | Three (goal + result + feedback) | Sending a navigation goal to a robot, receiving periodic progress updates |

The unified block diagram (rendered via Mermaid in Section 4.3 of these notes) clearly distinguishes the single section of `.msg` from the two-section `.srv` and three-section `.action` formats.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Header:** Students often omit `std_msgs/Header header` as the first field of a custom message. This loses **1 to 2 marks** depending on the question. The Header is *expected* unless the question explicitly forbids it.
> 2. **Wrong delimiter count:** In `.srv` files, students sometimes write only one `---` when defining an `.action`, or vice versa. *Always* count sections: 1 section → `.msg`, 2 sections → `.srv`, 3 sections → `.action`.
> 3. **Modifying `package.xml` but not `CMakeLists.txt`:** The build system will silently skip message generation. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for an incomplete build configuration.
> 4. **Confusing `int8` and `uint8`:** In a LiDAR intensity field, using `int8` instead of `uint8` loses the 0–255 range and is penalized.
> 5. **Using `float64` when `float32` suffices:** Not a hard error, but KTU examiners note "unjustified precision" — prefer the smaller type unless accuracy demands it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **ROS message type** is a declarative data structure (`.msg` / `.srv` / `.action`) defining field types and names for inter-node communication.
- [x] **Primitive types**: `bool`, `int8/16/32/64`, `uint8/16/32/64`, `float32/64`, `string`, `time`, `duration`, `char`, `byte`.
- [x] **Complex types**: fixed-length arrays `float32[3]`, variable-length arrays `float32[]`, nested custom messages, and the mandatory `std_msgs/Header`.
- [x] **Topic `.msg`** → 1 section, asynchronous, streaming.
- [x] **Service `.srv`** → 2 sections separated by `---`, synchronous request/response.
- [x] **Action `.action`** → 3 sections separated by two `---` lines, supports preemptible long-running tasks with feedback.
- [x] Build requirements: `<build_depend>message_generation</build_depend>`, `<exec_depend>message_runtime</exec_depend>`, plus `find_package`, `add_message_files`, `generate_messages` in `CMakeLists.txt`.
- [x] Verification command: `rosmsg show <pkg>/<MessageName>` (ROS1) or `ros2 interface show <pkg>/msg/<MessageName>` (ROS2).
- [x] Wire-size of a message $M$ is $S_{wire} = \sum_i \text{sizeof}(t_i)$ — important for bandwidth budgeting on embedded robot platforms.
- [x] **Header convention**: timestamp $t_{stamp} = t_{sec} + t_{nsec} \times 10^{-9}$ enables multi-sensor fusion.
- [x] ROS1 restricts messages to **at most one variable-length array**, and it must be the *last* field; ROS2 removes this restriction via CDR.
- [x] **Always** put `Header` first in `sensor_msgs`, `nav_msgs`, and `geometry_msgs` for KTU-compliant answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixing Publishers and Subscribers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mixing Publishers and Subscribers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Mixing Publishers and Subscribers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In **ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2)**, a *mixed publisher–subscriber node* is a single computational entity (a `rclpy` or `rclcpp` node) that simultaneously **publishes messages to one or more topics** and **subscribes to one or more different (or identical) topics** within the same process. This architectural pattern, formally referred to as a *composite node* in the ROS 2 design documentation, allows a single robot module to act as both a **data producer** and a **data consumer**, enabling closed-loop behaviors such as sensor fusion, control feedback, and state estimation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> A mixed pub/sub node is the foundational building block of any autonomous robot. It transforms a passive listener (subscriber) into an *active decision-making agent* by allowing the receipt of one message stream to trigger the publication of another — forming the **causal backbone** of robotic control architectures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Restaurant Manager)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Floor Manager"**
> Imagine a restaurant floor manager who performs **two intertwined roles**:
> - **Subscriber Role (Listening):** She continuously *listens* to customer complaints arriving on a walkie-talkie (subscriber to `/customer_complaints`).
> - **Publisher Role (Announcing):** The moment she hears a complaint, she *announces* a resolution on the staff radio channel (publisher to `/staff_actions`).
>
> She is **one person** doing **two jobs** — reacting to inputs and producing outputs. That is exactly what a mixed pub/sub node does: a single ROS 2 process reacts to incoming messages and publishes its own responses.

### 1.3 Why Mixing Matters in Robotics

| Pure Subscriber | Pure Publisher | **Mixed Node (Both)** |
|---|---|---|
| Reads sensor data | Sends commands only | **Reads sensors AND sends commands** |
| Stateless listener | Blind broadcaster | **Closed-loop controller** |
| Example: Lidar viewer | Example: Joystick driver | **Example: Robot's main control brain** |

The **canonical example** in any mobile robot is a *differential drive controller node*:
- **Subscribes** to `/cmd_vel` (desired velocity from higher-level planner)
- **Subscribes** to `/wheel_encoders` (actual feedback)
- **Publishes** to `/motor_commands` (corrected PWM signals to wheels)
- **Publishes** to `/odom` (computed odometry)

All four operations occur in **one node**, which is precisely the *mixing* concept.

### 1.4 Core Vocabulary (Must Memorize for KTU Exam)

- **Node** — A participant in the ROS 2 graph that uses the ROS client library.
- **Topic** — A named bus over which nodes exchange messages.
- **Publisher** — An object bound to a node that sends messages to a topic.
- **Subscriber** — An object bound to a node that receives messages from a topic.
- **Callback** — A function executed upon message arrival (subscriber) or timer tick (publisher).
- **Executor** — The component that orchestrates callback dispatching (single-threaded, multi-threaded).
- **QoS (Quality of Service)** — Policy set governing reliability, durability, deadline, and history.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Architectural Layers of a Mixed Node

A mixed pub/sub node in ROS 2 is organized into **three logical layers**, and examiners frequently test whether students can identify which callback belongs to which layer.

**Layer 1 — Initialization Layer (`__init__` / constructor)**
- Creates the node handle
- Declares parameters
- Instantiates publishers using `create_publisher()`
- Instantiates subscribers using `create_subscription()`
- Optionally creates timers via `create_timer()`

**Layer 2 — Reception Layer (Subscriber Callbacks)**
- Triggered asynchronously when a message arrives
- Read incoming data, perform computation, and **may call a publisher** to emit results
- Should be **non-blocking** to avoid stalling the executor

**Layer 3 — Emission Layer (Timer + Publisher Callbacks)**
- Triggered periodically by the executor's internal clock
- Computes new state and pushes messages into the publisher queue
- The DDS (Data Distribution Service) middleware handles actual network delivery

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Timer + Publisher?**
> You cannot use a `while True` loop inside a ROS callback — it would block the executor. Instead, ROS 2 provides `create_timer(period, callback)` which fires the callback at a controlled rate, making the node **deterministic and interruptible**.

### 2.2 The Callback Chain: Cause → Effect

The mixing of publishers and subscribers creates a **callback dependency chain**. Below is the canonical ordering logic:

1. **Incoming message** arrives at a subscriber on topic `T_in`.
2. ROS 2 executor **dispatches** the user-defined callback `f_in(msg)`.
3. Inside `f_in`, a **computation** transforms the input (e.g., applies a PID law).
4. The callback **invokes** `publisher.publish(output_msg)` on topic `T_out`.
5. The message is enqueued in the publisher's internal buffer.
6. The DDS layer **serializes and transmits** the message over the network (or shared memory intra-process).
7. Downstream nodes receive it on `T_out` and the cycle may repeat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** If asked "What triggers a publisher in a mixed node?", the correct answer is **either a timer tick OR a subscriber callback** — not a `while` loop.

### 2.3 Concurrency: The Executor Model

ROS 2 provides **two main executor types** that govern how mixed nodes handle concurrency:

| Executor Type | Behavior | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| `SingleThreadedExecutor` | All callbacks run sequentially in one thread | Simple nodes, deterministic order required |
| `MultiThreadedExecutor` | Callbacks can run in parallel across a thread pool | Heavy computation, parallel I/O |

Within these executors, **Callback Groups** further partition callbacks:

- **`MutuallyExclusiveCallbackGroup`** — Only one callback in the group runs at a time (default).
- **`ReentrantCallbackGroup`** — Multiple callbacks can run concurrently.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often think a subscriber callback and a timer callback can run *simultaneously by default*. They cannot — they are both `MutuallyExclusive` by default, meaning the executor serializes them. To run them in parallel, you must assign them to a `ReentrantCallbackGroup`.

### 2.4 Quality of Service (QoS) — The Hidden Contract

When a mixed node publishes and subscribes, the **publisher's QoS profile must be *compatible* with the subscriber's QoS profile**, or no message will ever be delivered. The two critical compatibility rules are:

**Rule 1 — Reliability Matching:**
- A `RELIABLE` publisher can talk to a `RELIABLE` subscriber ✓
- A `BEST_EFFORT` publisher can talk to a `BEST_EFFORT` subscriber ✓
- A `RELIABLE` publisher can talk to a `BEST_EFFORT` subscriber ✓
- A `BEST_EFFORT` publisher to a `RELIABLE` subscriber ✗ (incompatible)

**Rule 2 — Durability Matching:**
- A `TRANSIENT_LOCAL` publisher retains messages for late-joiners
- A `VOLATILE` publisher does not retain past messages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pearl:** Always specify the **same QoS depth** (e.g., `history=KEEP_LAST, depth=10`) on both ends, or at least ensure the publisher depth is $\geq$ the subscriber depth.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Timing, Throughput, and Latency)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all the **mathematically verifiable formulas** relevant to mixed pub/sub timing analysis. These are the only numerical relationships examiners can ask about in this topic.

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Publisher Frequency | $f_{pub} = \dfrac{1}{T_{period}}$ | Hz (messages/sec) | How often the timer fires per second |
| Timer Period | $T_{period} = \dfrac{1}{f_{pub}}$ | seconds | Time gap between two consecutive `publish()` calls |
| Aggregate Throughput | $\lambda = N \cdot f_{pub}$ | msg/s | Total messages per second across $N$ publishers in the node |
| Callback Latency (approx.) | $L \approx \dfrac{Q_{size}}{r_{consumer}}$ | seconds | Time to drain a full queue at consumer rate $r$ |
| Steady-State Queue Occupancy | $B = T_{window} \cdot f_{pub}$ | messages | Buffer fill if consumer stalls for $T_{window}$ seconds |
| Effective Bandwidth | $B_{w} = \lambda \cdot S_{msg}$ | bytes/s | Network bandwidth consumed ( $S_{msg}$ = message size in bytes) |
| Deadline (QoS Policy) | $D \geq T_{period}$ | seconds | Maximum age of a sample before it is considered "missed" |
| Wavelength (period × speed) | $\lambda_{spatial} = v \cdot T_{period}$ | meters | Distance robot moves between two publications ( $v$ = linear velocity) |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value bars (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are written using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. The KTU valuation script will not parse raw `\|` symbols inside table cells.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** The perception node subscribes to camera + lidar and publishes fused obstacle lists.
- **Industrial Manipulators:** The trajectory planner subscribes to joint states and publishes corrected setpoints.
- **Drones:** The flight controller mixes IMU subscription with motor command publication at **$\mathbf{1 \text{ kHz}}$** (i.e., $f_{pub} = 1000$ Hz, $T_{period} = 1$ ms).
- **Humanoid Robots:** The behavior tree node subscribes to sensor fusion output and publishes high-level action selections.

## 3. Step-by-Step Operational Analysis & Mathematical Walkthrough

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme Part-B (14-mark) questions often ask students to "design and analyze" a mixed node. The step-by-step lifecycle below is the *exact* structure expected in a model answer. Memorize this 7-step flow.

### 3.1 The 7-Step Node Lifecycle (Exhaustive Walkthrough)

Below is the exhaustive, step-by-step operational flow of a mixed publisher-subscriber node. **Every transition is written out explicitly** — no logical gaps, no implicit jumps.

---

**Step 1 — Process Spawn and ROS 2 Initialization**

The user executes `ros2 run <package> <node_executable>`. The operating system spawns a new process. The `rclpy.init()` function (in Python) or `rclcpp::init()` (in C++) is called, which:
- Parses command-line arguments (remapping, parameters).
- Establishes a unique participant ID in the DDS global data space.
- Loads the default middleware implementation (Cyclone DDS, Fast DDS, or RTI Connext).

---

**Step 2 — Node Object Construction**

A custom class (e.g., `class MixedController(Node):`) is instantiated. The constructor calls `super().__init__('node_name')`, which:
- Registers the node with the ROS 2 graph.
- Allocates internal memory for parameter storage, publisher handles, and subscription handles.
- Acquires a `rclpy.node.Node` reference.

---

**Step 3 — Publisher Instantiation**

For each output topic, the constructor calls:

```python
self.publisher_ = self.create_publisher(MessageType, 'topic_name', qos_profile)
```

Internally, this:
- Allocates a publisher handle in the DDS DataWriter.
- Registers the topic name in the ROS graph (discoverable by other nodes).
- Initializes a message queue of `depth` size (from QoS history policy).

---

**Step 4 — Subscriber Instantiation**

For each input topic, the constructor calls:

```python
self.subscription = self.create_subscription(MessageType, 'topic_in', self.callback, qos)
```

This:
- Allocates a DataReader in DDS.
- Registers a callback binding in the executor's dispatch table.
- Establishes a `MutuallyExclusiveCallbackGroup` association by default.

---

**Step 5 — Timer Instantiation (Periodic Publisher)**

If the node needs to publish at a fixed rate:

```python
self.timer = self.create_timer(0.1, self.timer_callback)  # 10 Hz
```

The timer:
- Registers a periodic alarm with the executor.
- Each tick invokes `timer_callback` which computes new data and calls `publisher_.publish(msg)`.

---

**Step 6 — Executor Spin (The Event Loop)**

The `main` function calls `rclpy.spin(node)`, which enters an **infinite event loop** in the chosen executor:

- The executor **blocks** on a condition variable (waits for events).
- When a message arrives → the subscriber callback is **unblocked** and dispatched.
- When a timer fires → the timer callback is dispatched.
- The loop only exits when `rclpy.shutdown()` is called or `Ctrl+C` is pressed.

---

**Step 7 — Graceful Shutdown**

On SIGINT or shutdown signal:
- All timers are cancelled.
- All publishers flush their queues.
- All subscriptions are released.
- The DDS participant is finalized.
- The process exits with code 0.

---

### 3.2 Mathematical Analysis: Timing Budget for a Mixed Controller

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A mobile robot's control node subscribes to `/cmd_vel` and publishes to `/motor_cmd` at a fixed rate. The timer period is $T_{period}$. The wheel controller callback takes $t_{proc}$ seconds. The DDS round-trip latency is $t_{DDS}$. Find the **maximum sustainable control rate** and the **queue occupancy** under burst load.

**Given (example values used in derivation):**
- Timer period: $T_{period} = 0.01$ s (i.e., $100$ Hz control loop)
- Callback processing time: $t_{proc} = 0.002$ s
- DDS latency (one-way): $t_{DDS} = 0.0005$ s

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1: Compute the nominal publishing frequency**

$$f_{pub} = \frac{1}{T_{period}} = \frac{1}{0.01 \text{ s}} = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2: Compute the total time consumed per cycle**

Each control cycle consumes:

$$T_{cycle} = t_{proc} + 2 \cdot t_{DDS}$$

The factor of 2 accounts for round-trip (subscriber receive + publisher send). Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= 0.002 \text{ s} + 2 \cdot (0.0005 \text{ s}) \\
&= 0.002 \text{ s} + 0.001 \text{ s} \\
&= 0.003 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute the maximum sustainable rate**

For the node to keep up with the timer, we require $T_{cycle} \leq T_{period}$. The maximum sustainable rate is:

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{1}{0.003 \text{ s}} \approx 333.33 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4: Compute the safety margin (slack time)**

The slack time available in each period is:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{slack} &= T_{period} - T_{cycle} \\
&= 0.010 \text{ s} - 0.003 \text{ s} \\
&= 0.007 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $t_{slack} > 0$, the node is **not overloaded**.

**Step 5: Compute queue occupancy under burst load**

Suppose a burst causes the consumer to stall for $T_{window} = 0.05$ s. The number of messages buffered in the publisher queue is:

$$B = T_{window} \cdot f_{pub} = 0.05 \cdot 100 = 5 \text{ messages}$$

**Step 6: Verify QoS history depth is sufficient**

The required QoS depth is $Q \geq B = 5$. We must configure `history=KEEP_LAST, depth=5` (or greater) to avoid message loss.

**Final Conclusion:**
At $100$ Hz with a callback cost of $2$ ms, the node has a healthy $7$ ms slack and requires a publisher queue depth of at least $5$ to survive a $50$ ms consumer stall.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Subsystem Responsibilities

| Subsystem | Responsibility | Failure Mode if Misconfigured |
|---|---|---|
| Publisher | Push messages to DDS layer | Messages dropped if queue overflows |
| Subscriber | Receive messages and dispatch callbacks | Callbacks queued, latency spikes |
| Timer | Provide deterministic periodic firing | Jitter if executor is overloaded |
| Executor | Schedule callbacks on threads | Deadlock if reentrant group mis-used |
| QoS Policy | Filter incompatible peers | Silent communication failure |

### 4.1 High-Level Node Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **internal structure of a mixed publisher-subscriber node** for a differential drive robot. Note that **all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters** to comply with Mermaid parser rules, and **no markdown formatting (bold, italics, tables) appears inside any quoted label**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[User Process Main] --> B1[Node Constructor]
    B1 --> C1[Create Publisher Motor Cmd]
    B1 --> C2[Create Publisher Odom]
    B1 --> C3[Create Subscriber Cmd Vel]
    B1 --> C4[Create Subscriber Wheel Encoders]
    B1 --> C5[Create Timer 50 Hz]
    C5 --> D1[Timer Callback]
    C3 --> E1[Velocity Callback]
    C4 --> E2[Encoder Callback]
    E1 --> F1[Compute Motor Command]
    E2 --> F2[Update Odometry Estimate]
    F1 --> C1
    F2 --> C2
    D1 --> G1[Publish Diagnostic Heartbeat]
    C1 --> H1[DDS Data Writer Motor]
    C2 --> H2[DDS Data Writer Odom]
    H1 --> I1[Network or Shared Memory]
    H2 --> I1
    I1 --> J1[Downstream Consumers]
    I1 --> J2[Robot Hardware Driver]
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide:** `A1` is the program entry. The two `Subscribe` boxes (C3, C4) feed data into computation blocks (F1, F2), which in turn invoke the publishers (C1, C2). The timer (C5) independently triggers diagnostic publications. All final outputs flow through DDS (H1, H2) to physical hardware or other nodes.

### 4.2 Callback Execution Model (Single-Threaded Executor)

The diagram below shows the **temporal interleaving** of callbacks when a `SingleThreadedExecutor` is used. Notice that subscriber and timer callbacks are **serialized** — they never overlap.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Exec as Single Threaded Executor
    participant Sub as Subscriber Callback
    participant Timer as Timer Callback
    participant DDS as DDS Middleware
    Exec->>Sub: Dispatch incoming /cmd_vel message
    Sub->>Sub: Compute new motor command
    Sub->>DDS: publish(motor_cmd)
    DDS-->>Exec: Acknowledgement
    Exec->>Timer: Dispatch 20 ms tick
    Timer->>DDS: publish(heartbeat)
    DDS-->>Exec: Acknowledgement
    Exec->>Sub: Dispatch incoming /encoders message
    Sub->>Sub: Update odometry
    Sub->>DDS: publish(odom)
```

### 4.3 Publisher–Subscriber QoS Compatibility Matrix

This is a **block-level functional compatibility check** that students must memorize for KTU exams. It maps every possible publisher–subscriber QoS pairing to a binary outcome (delivered or rejected).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pub Reliable Volatile] --> Q1{Sub Reliability}
    P2[Pub Reliable Transient] --> Q1
    P3[Pub Best Effort Volatile] --> Q1
    P4[Pub Best Effort Transient] --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1[Sub Reliable: Match if Pub Reliable else Reject]
    Q1 --> R2[Sub Best Effort: Always Accept]
    R1 --> O1[Message Delivered]
    R2 --> O1
    R1 --> O2[Connection Refused]
    O2 --> O3[Node Logs Warning at Startup]
```

> **Reading the Block Diagram:** Start at any of the four publisher profiles on the left (P1–P4). The decision block (Q1) routes to either delivery (O1) or refusal (O2→O3). The four profiles encode all combinations of `RELIABLE`/`BEST_EFFORT` × `TRANSIENT_LOCAL`/`VOLATILE`.

### 4.4 Data Flow Topology (Network-Wide View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Joystick Node Publisher] -->|/cmd_vel| N2[Mixed Controller Node]
    N3[Lidar Node Publisher] -->|/scan| N2
    N4[Camera Node Publisher] -->|/image_raw| N2
    N2 -->|/motor_cmd| N5[Motor Driver Node]
    N2 -->|/odom| N6[Localization Node Subscriber]
    N2 -->|/safety_alert| N7[Safety Monitor Node Subscriber]
    N5 --> HW[Robot Hardware]
    N2 --> DBG[Debug Visualizer Subscriber]
```

> **Key Insight:** The **Mixed Controller Node (N2)** is the central hub — it has **three input topics** (subscribers) and **three output topics** (publishers). This is the canonical "mixing" topology in mobile robotics.
