## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> *Define a mixed publisher-subscriber node in ROS 2. Give one real-world robotics example where this pattern is essential.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *mixed publisher-subscriber node* is a single ROS 2 node that contains **at least one publisher object and at least one subscriber object** in the same process, allowing it to simultaneously consume data from certain topics and produce data for other topics. (1.5 marks)

**Real-world example:** The motion controller of a differential-drive mobile robot. It **subscribes** to `/cmd_vel` (desired velocity from the planner) and `/wheel_encoders` (feedback), processes the error, and **publishes** corrected PWM commands to `/motor_cmd`. It also publishes the computed position to `/odom`. (1.5 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
> *Why can't we use a `while True:` loop inside a ROS 2 callback to publish messages continuously? What is the correct alternative?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A `while True:` loop inside a callback would **block the executor's event loop**, preventing the node from receiving any subscriber messages or processing other timers. The executor would be permanently stuck. (1.5 marks)

The correct alternative is to use `create_timer(period, callback)`. The timer registers a periodic alarm with the executor, and on each tick the executor dispatches the callback. This allows the node to interleave publishing with subscriber reception in a **deterministic, non-blocking** manner. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 1 Model]**
> *You are tasked with designing a ROS 2 mixed node for a warehouse robot that must (i) subscribe to a temperature sensor stream published at 5 Hz, (ii) maintain an exponentially weighted moving average (EWMA) of the readings, and (iii) publish an alarm message if the smoothed temperature exceeds 60°C.*
>
> **(a)** Draw the complete block-level architecture of this node, identifying all publishers, subscribers, timers, and the data flow between them. State the QoS policies you would use. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** If the timer runs at 10 Hz and each callback consumes 1.5 ms, compute (i) the slack time per cycle, (ii) the maximum sustainable publishing rate, and (iii) the required queue depth if a downstream consumer stalls for 200 ms. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Architecture and QoS (7 marks):**

The mixed node will contain the following components:

**Subscribers (2 marks):**
- One subscriber to `/temperature_raw` with message type `Float32`.
- QoS: `Reliability=BEST_EFFORT, History=KEEP_LAST, Depth=5` (sensor data is periodic; missing one sample is acceptable).

**Timers (1 mark):**
- One timer at 10 Hz (period = 0.1 s) driving the EWMA computation and alarm check.

**Publishers (1 mark):**
- Publisher to `/temperature_smoothed` (always publishes EWMA value) — `Float32` type.
- Publisher to `/temperature_alarm` (publishes only when threshold breached) — `Bool` type.
- QoS for both: `Reliability=RELIABLE, History=KEEP_LAST, Depth=10` (control signals must not be lost).

**Data Flow Description (2 marks):**
The `/temperature_raw` subscriber callback appends each reading to an internal buffer. On every 100 ms timer tick, the EWMA is updated using the formula:
$$\text{EWMA}_{t} = \alpha \cdot x_{t} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \text{EWMA}_{t-1}$$
where $\alpha = 0.3$ is the smoothing constant and $x_{t}$ is the latest raw reading. If $\text{EWMA}_{t} > 60°C$, the node publishes `True` to `/temperature_alarm`; otherwise it publishes `False`. The smoothed value is always published to `/temperature_smoothed`.

> **QoS Justification (1 mark):** The raw sensor uses `BEST_EFFORT` to reduce latency, while the alarm uses `RELIABLE` to ensure the safety system never misses a critical event.

---

**Part (b) — Mathematical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T_{period} = 0.1$ s, $t_{proc} = 0.0015$ s.

**(i) Slack time per cycle (2 marks):**

The total time consumed per timer tick is the processing time itself (we assume DDS latency is negligible for intra-process):
$$T_{cycle} = t_{proc} = 0.0015 \text{ s}$$

The slack time is:
$$\begin{aligned}
t_{slack} &= T_{period} - T_{cycle} \\
&= 0.1 \text{ s} - 0.0015 \text{ s} \\
&= 0.0985 \text{ s} \approx 98.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Maximum sustainable publishing rate (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{max} &= \frac{1}{t_{proc}} = \frac{1}{0.0015 \text{ s}} \\
&\approx 666.67 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

Since the actual rate is 10 Hz, which is far below $f_{max}$, the node is operating safely with a large margin.

**(iii) Required queue depth for 200 ms consumer stall (3 marks):**

The number of messages accumulated during the stall window is:
$$\begin{aligned}
B &= T_{window} \cdot f_{pub} \\
&= 0.200 \text{ s} \cdot 10 \text{ Hz} \\
&= 2 \text{ messages}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the publisher's QoS `depth` must satisfy:
$$Q \geq 2$$

We would configure `history=KEEP_LAST, depth=10` to provide a safety margin against longer stalls.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Module 1 Model]**
> *ROS 2 supports two executor types and two callback group types. Explain how these interact when a mixed publisher-subscriber node has both a 100 Hz timer and a high-frequency lidar subscriber.*
>
> **(a)** Compare the `SingleThreadedExecutor` and `MultiThreadedExecutor` for this scenario, with a clear block diagram showing the threading. Justify your choice with a timing calculation assuming timer callback takes 0.5 ms and lidar callback takes 8 ms. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the difference between `MutuallyExclusiveCallbackGroup` and `ReentrantCallbackGroup`. Show with a timeline diagram what happens to message latency in each case if two lidar messages arrive within the same timer tick. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Executor Comparison (7 marks):**

**`SingleThreadedExecutor` (3 marks):**
- All callbacks (both timer and subscriber) run sequentially in a single OS thread.
- Timer period: $T_{timer} = 1/100 = 10$ ms.
- Lidar callback: $t_{lidar} = 8$ ms.
- Worst-case scenario: if a lidar callback fires just before a timer tick, the timer may be delayed by 8 ms, causing **jitter** of up to 8 ms.
- Total cycle time: $T_{cycle} = 8 + 0.5 = 8.5$ ms per lidar message.
- Limitation: Cannot meet a 10 ms deadline if a second lidar message arrives before the first is processed (back-to-back messages cause **queueing delay**).

**`MultiThreadedExecutor` (4 marks):**
- Callbacks are dispatched across a pool of worker threads.
- The timer (0.5 ms) and lidar (8 ms) can run *in parallel* if placed in a `ReentrantCallbackGroup`.
- Effective throughput: ~125 Hz for lidar (1/8 ms) and 100 Hz for timer (1/10 ms) — both deadlines met.
- Trade-off: Requires careful synchronization (mutexes) if both callbacks access shared state (e.g., a shared odometry variable).
- Justification: Since the lidar callback is 16× longer than the timer, multi-threading is essential to prevent the timer from being starved.

> **Decision:** Use `MultiThreadedExecutor` with `thread_count=2` and assign both callbacks to a `ReentrantCallbackGroup`, with a mutex protecting any shared variables.

---

**Part (b) — Callback Groups (7 marks):**

**`MutuallyExclusiveCallbackGroup` (3.5 marks):**
- Default group type in ROS 2.
- Only one callback in the group executes at a time.
- If two lidar messages arrive at $t=0$ ms and $t=2$ ms while the timer is running, the second lidar message must **wait** in the subscription queue until the first finishes.
- Latency for the second message: 8 ms (wait) + 8 ms (process) = **16 ms total**.
- This breaks real-time guarantees for the timer.

**`ReentrantCallbackGroup` (3.5 marks):**
- Multiple callbacks in the group can run concurrently on different threads.
- The second lidar message can be processed on a second worker thread immediately.
- Latency: ~8 ms (only the processing time, no queueing).
- However, if both callbacks **write to the same shared variable**, a data race occurs — must use a `threading.Lock()` or atomic operations.

**Timeline Comparison:**

| Time (ms) | Mutually Exclusive | Reentrant |
|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | Lidar #1 starts | Lidar #1 starts (Thread A) |
| 2.0 | Lidar #2 queued | Lidar #2 starts (Thread B) |
| 8.0 | Lidar #1 ends | Lidar #1 ends (A) |
| 8.0 | Lidar #2 starts | Lidar #2 ends (B) |
| 10.0 | Timer tick **delayed** until lidar #2 finishes | Timer tick fires on time |
| 16.0 | Lidar #2 ends | — |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting QoS Compatibility (–2 marks typical):** Many students write code/configurations with mismatched Reliability policies (e.g., `BEST_EFFORT` publisher with `RELIABLE` subscriber). Examiners will deduct marks if you do not justify QoS choices.
>
> 2. **Confusing "Publisher Rate" with "Network Throughput" (–1 mark):** $f_{pub} = 1/T_{period}$ is the *per-publisher* rate. Total node throughput requires multiplying by $N$ publishers.
>
> 3. **Ignoring Queue Depth (–1 mark):** If asked about burst handling, you must compute $B = T_{window} \cdot f_{pub}$ and verify the QoS `depth` accommodates it. Stating "use a large depth" without calculation is incomplete.
>
> 4. **Mixing Up Executor and Callback Group Concepts (–2 marks):** Executor = threading pool. Callback Group = policy on what can run together. They are *orthogonal*; you can have a single-threaded executor with a reentrant group (it will still serialize, but the *intent* is declared).
>
> 5. **No Block Diagram in Part B (–2 marks):** KTU 2024 scheme mandates a diagram for 14-mark questions. Always include a block diagram of the node architecture.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **mixed pub/sub node** in ROS 2 contains at least one publisher and one subscriber in the same process.
- The **executor** (`rclpy.spin`) is the event loop that dispatches all callbacks.
- Use **`create_timer()`** for periodic publishing; never use a blocking loop.
- **QoS compatibility** is mandatory: `BEST_EFFORT` publisher → `RELIABLE` subscriber is **rejected**.
- **Callback groups** are `MutuallyExclusive` (default) or `Reentrant` (parallel).
- **Executor types** are `SingleThreaded` (serial) or `MultiThreaded` (parallel pool).
- **Publishing frequency** = $f_{pub} = 1/T_{period}$.
- **Maximum sustainable rate** = $f_{max} = 1/t_{proc}$.
- **Queue depth required** for stall protection = $B = T_{window} \cdot f_{pub}$.
- **Slack time** = $t_{slack} = T_{period} - t_{proc}$; must be positive for stable operation.
- **Canonical robotics examples**: differential drive controllers, sensor fusion nodes, flight controllers, manipulators with feedback.
- The **DDS layer** handles all network transport — your node code never touches sockets directly.
- **Mixing enables causality**: a subscriber callback can call a publisher, creating a *reactive* node.
- Always justify **QoS choices** based on whether the data is *critical* (RELIABLE) or *lossy-but-fast* (BEST_EFFORT).
- For KTU exams, structure your Part-B answer as: **Definition → Architecture Diagram → Component List → Data Flow → Mathematical Justification → Conclusion**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defining a Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Defining a Service

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In **Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2)**, a **Service** is a synchronous, one-to-one, request/reply communication paradigm implemented on top of the Data Distribution Service (DDS) middleware. It is formally defined by a strongly-typed interface specification written in a `.srv` file, which strictly partitions the data structure into a **Request** message and a **Response** message separated by a `---` delimiter. A **Service Client** node invokes a remote procedure call, and a **Service Server** node processes the request and returns a single reply, after which the communication channel terminates for that transaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):**
> A service in ROS 2 is defined by a structured interface (`.srv` file) comprising a request field and a response field. Unlike topics, services are **synchronous**, **bidirectional**, and **transactional** — meaning each call is paired with exactly one reply.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **library** (the ROS graph) and ask the librarian (**Service Server**) a specific question: *"What is the ISBN of the book 'Introduction to Robotics'?"* You (the **Service Client**) make a *single, well-formed request*. The librarian looks it up, hands you a slip of paper with the answer, and the interaction ends. There is **no continuous stream** of data (that would be a topic). You do not send a long-running goal (that would be an action). You simply ask once and receive one reply. That transactional exchange is exactly how a ROS 2 service works.

### 1.3 Key Vocabulary (Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Service Server** | The node that advertises the service and listens for incoming requests. |
| **Service Client** | The node that sends a request and blocks (waits) until a response arrives. |
| **Service Name** | The string identifier used to look up the service (e.g., `/add_two_ints`). |
| **Service Type** | The compiled interface (Request + Response) generated from the `.srv` file. |
| **Service Definition** | The plain-text `.srv` file authored by the developer using interface definition language. |

> [!NOTE]
> Services in ROS 2 follow a **strict request/reply contract**. The compiled message classes are generated automatically by the build system (`colcon` + `rosidl`), so the developer never writes serialization code by hand.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Service Definition (`.srv` File)

A service definition is a plain-text file with the `.srv` extension that lives inside the `srv/` directory of a ROS 2 package. It uses **interface definition language (IDL)** that is identical in syntax to the message definition language used for topics. The structure has three mandatory conceptual zones:

1. **Request zone (top)** — fields the client must populate.
2. **The `---` delimiter** — strict separator (three hyphens), non-negotiable.
3. **Response zone (bottom)** — fields the server will populate before returning.

### Canonical Example: `AddTwoInts.srv`

```
int64 a
int64 b
---
int64 sum
```

When the package is compiled, this single file produces **two message classes**:
- `example_interfaces.srv.AddTwoInts_Request`
- `example_interfaces.srv.AddTwoInts_Response`

Plus a bundled convenience type `example_interfaces.srv.AddTwoInts` that contains both.

## 2.2 Operational Logic Steps

- **Step 1 — Interface Authoring:** Developer writes `my_service.srv` in `my_package/srv/`.
- **Step 2 — CMakeLists.txt Registration:** The build script invokes `rosidl_generate_interfaces()` so the IDL compiler generates Python/C++ source.
- **Step 3 — Server Node Creation:** `node.create_service(SrvType, 'service_name', callback)` registers the server on the DDS domain.
- **Step 4 — Client Node Invocation:** `client.call_async(request)` dispatches the request through DDS; the future resolves when the response arrives.
- **Step 5 — Callback Execution:** The server's callback mutates the `response` object and returns it. ROS 2 wraps and dispatches it back to the waiting client.
- **Step 6 — Resource Release:** Once the reply is delivered, the request-reply pair is closed. No persistent state is kept for that call.

## 2.3 Why and How — The Core Design Rationale

- **Why synchronous?** Services are designed for *deterministic, transactional* operations (e.g., triggering a calibration routine, querying a robot's battery percentage, requesting an inverse-kinematics solve). The client *must know* the operation has finished before proceeding, making async polling inappropriate.
- **How is it reliable under the hood?** DDS provides **Quality of Service (QoS)** policies with reliable delivery by default for services, so no request is silently dropped.
- **How does it differ from a topic?** Topics are **anonymous, many-to-many, fire-and-forget streams**. Services are **named, one-to-one, request/reply RPCs**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Value / Rule |
|---|---|
| File extension | `.srv` |
| Required directory | `<package_name>/srv/` |
| Delimiter | `---` (exactly three hyphens on their own line) |
| Communication pattern | Request / Reply (RPC) |
| Cardinality | One client $\leftrightarrow$ One server per call |
| Synchrony | **Synchronous** (client awaits response) |
| Transport | DDS (default), with configurable QoS |
| Persistent connection | **No** — created on demand, terminated on reply |
| Generated artifacts | Request class + Response class + bundled service class |
| Default QoS reliability | **Reliable** |
| Default QoS durability | Volatile |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** A service **must** have at least one field in the request or response. A `.srv` file containing only `---` is invalid and will fail at compile time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Designing a Service from Scratch

Suppose a KTU lab requires a node to compute the **hypotenuse** of a right triangle given two perpendicular sides. This is a perfect use-case for a service: the inputs are discrete, the computation is bounded, and the caller needs the answer before continuing.

### Step 3.1.1 — Author the `.srv` File

Create `ros2_ws/src/triangle_pkg/srv/Hypotenuse.srv`:

```
float64 leg_a
float64 leg_b
---
float64 hypotenuse
```

**Line-by-line explanation:**
- `float64 leg_a` — first input leg (Request zone).
- `float64 leg_b` — second input leg (Request zone).
- `---` — strict delimiter separating request from response.
- `float64 hypotenuse` — output computed by the server (Response zone).

### Step 3.1.2 — Register the Interface in `package.xml`

```xml
<build_depend>rosidl_default_generators</build_depend>
<exec_depend>rosidl_default_runtime</exec_depend>
<member_of_group>rosidl_interface_packages</member_of_group>
```

This declares the build-time code generator and the runtime dependency that downstream packages will need.

### Step 3.1.3 — Register the Interface in `CMakeLists.txt`

```cmake
find_package(rosidl_default_generators REQUIRED)

rosidl_generate_interfaces(${PROJECT_NAME}
  "srv/Hypotenuse.srv"
)
```

This invokes `rosidl` to compile the IDL into Python and C++ classes in the `triangle_pkg` namespace.

### 3.1.4 — Implementing the Server (Python)

```python
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from triangle_pkg.srv import Hypotenuse

class HypotenuseServer(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('hypotenuse_server')
        self.srv = self.create_service(
            Hypotenuse,
            'compute_hypotenuse',
            self.compute_callback
        )

    def compute_callback(self, request, response):
        # Euclidean norm: sqrt(a^2 + b^2)
        response.hypotenuse = (request.leg_a ** 2 + request.leg_b ** 2) ** 0.5
        self.get_logger().info(
            f'Received a={request.leg_a}, b={request.leg_b} -> c={response.hypotenuse}'
        )
        return response

def main():
    rclpy.init()
    rclpy.spin(HypotenuseServer())
    rclpy.shutdown()
```

### 3.1.5 — Implementing the Client (Python)

```python
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from triangle_pkg.srv import Hypotenuse

class HypotenuseClient(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('hypotenuse_client')
        self.client = self.create_client(Hypotenuse, 'compute_hypotenuse')
        while not self.client.wait_for_service(timeout_sec=1.0):
            self.get_logger().info('Waiting for service...')
        self.request = Hypotenuse.Request()

    def send_request(self, a, b):
        self.request.leg_a = a
        self.request.leg_b = b
        self.future = self.client.call_async(self.request)
        rclpy.spin_until_future_complete(self, self.future)
        return self.future.result()
```

## 3.2 Closed-Form Derivation of the Response Field

The server's callback executes the Pythagorean theorem. Let the two perpendicular legs be $a$ and $b$, and the hypotenuse be $c$. Then:

$$
c = \sqrt{a^{2} + b^{2}}
$$

In code, this becomes:

$$
\text{response.hypotenuse} = \left( \text{request.leg\_a}^{\,2} + \text{request.leg\_b}^{\,2} \right)^{0.5}
$$

Numerical test with $a = 3$, $b = 4$:

$$
c = \sqrt{3^{2} + 4^{2}} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5.0
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Service | Topic | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Communication | Request / Reply | Publish / Subscribe | Goal / Feedback / Result |
| Pattern | Synchronous RPC | Asynchronous stream | Asynchronous, long-running |
| Cardinality | 1 client $\to$ 1 server | N publishers $\to$ M subscribers | 1 client $\to$ 1 server |
| Preemption | **No** | N/A | **Yes** |
| Use-case | Discrete queries | Continuous sensor data | Long navigation tasks |
| Typical example | `add_two_ints` | `/scan` (LiDAR) | `navigate_to_pose` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Request/Reply Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant clientNode as Service Client
    participant ddsFabric as DDS Fabric
    participant serverNode as Service Server
    participant callback as Callback Function

    clientNode->>clientNode: Create request object
    clientNode->>ddsFabric: call_async(request)
    activate ddsFabric
    ddsFabric->>serverNode: Deliver request (QoS: RELIABLE)
    deactivate ddsFabric
    activate serverNode
    serverNode->>callback: Invoke handler(req, res)
    activate callback
    callback->>callback: Compute response
    callback-->>serverNode: Return populated response
    deactivate callback
    serverNode->>ddsFabric: Dispatch reply
    deactivate serverNode
    activate ddsFabric
    ddsFabric->>clientNode: Future resolves with result
    deactivate ddsFabric
    clientNode->>clientNode: Process response
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pkgA[Package A: srv folder] -->|contains| srvFile[.srv file]
    srvFile -->|rosidl compile| codeGen[Code Generator]
    codeGen --> pyMod[Python Module]
    codeGen --> cppHdr[C++ Headers]
    pyMod --> srvClass[Service Class: Request + Response]
    cppHdr --> srvClass
    srvClass --> clientApi[Client API: call_async]
    srvClass --> serverApi[Server API: create_service]
    clientApi --> runtime[ROS 2 Runtime + DDS]
    serverApi --> runtime
    runtime --> wire[Wire-level RPC over UDP/TCP]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Flow — When to Use a Service

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Need inter-node communication?]) --> q1{Is the data continuous?}
    q1 -- Yes --> topicPath[Use a TOPIC]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Need a discrete reply?}
    q2 -- No --> topicPath
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Long-running task?}
    q3 -- Yes --> actionPath[Use an ACTION]
    q3 -- No --> servicePath[Use a SERVICE]
    servicePath --> design[Write .srv file with --- delimiter]
    design --> build[Register in CMakeLists.txt]
    build --> done([Service ready])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Q:** Define a ROS 2 service. Mention two characteristics that distinguish it from a topic.

**Model Answer:**
A ROS 2 service is a **synchronous request/reply** communication mechanism defined by a `.srv` file that partitions data into a **request** and a **response** separated by `---`. Two distinguishing characteristics:
1. It is **one-to-one and transactional** — each call produces exactly one reply.
2. The client **blocks** until the response arrives, unlike topics which are fire-and-forget.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q:** What is the purpose of the `---` line in a `.srv` file?

**Model Answer:**
The `---` line is the **strict delimiter** that separates the **Request** message fields (above) from the **Response** message fields (below). It tells the IDL compiler where the client's input ends and the server's output begins. Its presence is mandatory, and an absent or misplaced delimiter causes the build to fail.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the structure of a ROS 2 service definition with a suitable example. List the steps to register a new `.srv` interface inside a ROS 2 package. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the request/reply lifecycle of a ROS 2 service. Compare services with topics in terms of cardinality, synchrony, and use-case suitability. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Service Definition Structure (7 Marks)**
1. A `.srv` file resides in `<package_name>/srv/`.
2. The **Request** section (above `---`) contains fields the client must populate.
3. The `---` delimiter partitions the request and response.
4. The **Response** section (below `---`) contains fields the server fills in.
5. Example — `AddTwoInts.srv`:
```
int64 a
int64 b
---
int64 sum
```
6. **Registration steps:**
   - Add `<member_of_group>rosidl_interface_packages</member_of_group>` to `package.xml`.
   - In `CMakeLists.txt`, call `find_package(rosidl_default_generators REQUIRED)`.
   - Invoke `rosidl_generate_interfaces(${PROJECT_NAME} "srv/AddTwoInts.srv")`.
   - Build with `colcon build`.

**(b) Lifecycle and Comparison (7 Marks)**
- **Lifecycle:** Client creates request $\to$ calls `call_async` $\to$ DDS routes to server $\to$ server callback executes $\to$ response returned $\to$ future resolves on client.
- **Cardinality:** Services are 1-to-1; topics are N-to-M.
- **Synchrony:** Services are synchronous (blocking); topics are asynchronous.
- **Use-case:** Services fit discrete queries (e.g., reset robot); topics fit continuous streams (e.g., LiDAR scans).

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Design a `.srv` file named `RectangleArea.srv` that takes `length` and `width` (both `float64`) and returns `area`. Write the Python server callback that computes and returns the area. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of DDS in ROS 2 service communication. Explain the default QoS policies applied to services and why they are appropriate. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Designing `RectangleArea.srv` (7 Marks)**

The `.srv` file:
```
float64 length
float64 width
---
float64 area
```

The Python server callback:
```python
def compute_area_callback(self, request, response):
    response.area = request.length * request.width
    self.get_logger().info(f'Computed area = {response.area}')
    return response
```

Mathematical derivation:

$$
A = \ell \times w
$$

For $\ell = 5.0$, $w = 3.0$:

$$
A = 5.0 \times 3.0 = 15.0
$$

**(b) Role of DDS in Services (7 Marks)**
- DDS (Data Distribution Service) is the **underlying industrial-grade middleware** that handles discovery, serialization, and transport for ROS 2 services.
- It provides **discovery** so client and server find each other automatically by service name.
- Default QoS for services: **Reliability = RELIABLE** (no dropped requests) and **Durability = VOLATILE** (no replay needed because each call is one-shot).
- These defaults are appropriate because services are transactional — losing a request would break the application, but historical replays are unnecessary.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently forget the `---` delimiter or place extra fields below it — this invalidates the entire `.srv` file. Always place the delimiter on its **own line**.
> 2. Many confuse **services with topics**. If your answer mentions *publishers*, *subscribers*, or *continuous streams*, you are describing a topic, not a service. The examiner will deduct marks.
> 3. Forgetting to declare the interface in **both** `package.xml` and `CMakeLists.txt` is the #1 reason `.srv` files fail to compile in lab exams.
> 4. Do **not** write `node.srv` instead of `node.create_service(...)`. `srv` is the file extension, not the API method.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **service** is a **synchronous**, **one-to-one**, **request/reply** RPC in ROS 2.
- The interface is defined in a `.srv` file with strict `---` delimiter syntax.
- Two roles exist: **Service Client** (caller) and **Service Server** (handler).
- Communication is **transactional** — one request, one reply, then closed.
- Built atop **DDS middleware**; default QoS is **RELIABLE + VOLATILE**.
- Registration requires edits in **both `package.xml` and `CMakeLists.txt`**.
- Generated artifacts include separate Request and Response classes plus a bundled service class.
- Use services for **discrete, deterministic queries** (calibration, math, configuration).
- **Avoid services for continuous sensor data or long-running tasks** — use topics and actions instead.
- The `---` delimiter is **non-negotiable** and must appear exactly on its own line.
- Field types in `.srv` mirror message field types (`int32`, `float64`, `string`, `bool`, custom messages, arrays).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing a Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Implementing a Service

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2)** architecture, a **Service** is a synchronous, bidirectional communication primitive built on top of the underlying **Data Distribution Service (DDS)** middleware. A service is defined by a pair of message structures: a **Request** message and a **Response** message, bundled together in a `.srv` file. Communication follows a strict **Client–Server (Request/Response)** model where the *service client* sends a request and **blocks (waits)** until the *service server* computes and returns a response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> A ROS 2 Service implements *synchronous, one-to-one, request/reply* communication. The requesting node (client) sends a structured request message and suspends execution until a corresponding response is received from the providing node (server). Services are best suited for **discrete, transactional** robotic operations such as triggering a specific action, querying a state, or performing configuration changes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a hotel reception (the **Service Server**) and ask the receptionist, *"What is the Wi-Fi password?"* (the **Request**). You do not leave the counter — you stand there and **wait** until the receptionist looks it up and hands you a slip of paper with the answer (the **Response**). 

- You are the **Service Client**.
- The receptionist is the **Service Server**.
- The question format (name, room number, etc.) is the **Service Type / Interface** (the `.srv` file).
- Your waiting at the counter is the **synchronous blocking call**.

In contrast, a **Topic** in ROS 2 is more like a **radio broadcast** — you speak into a microphone and anyone listening hears it, but you never get a confirmation back. Topics are *asynchronous*; Services are *synchronous*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (High-Frequency KTU Question):**
> - **Topic** = Asynchronous, many-to-many, continuous stream (publisher/subscriber).
> - **Service** = Synchronous, one-to-one, one-shot transaction (client/server).
> - **Action** = Asynchronous, one-to-one, with continuous feedback (used for long-running tasks).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Convention |
|---|---|
| Service file extension | **`.srv`** |
| Request/Response separator | **`---`** (three hyphens, mandatory line) |
| Default service name prefix in CLI | **`/service_name`** |
| Underlying middleware | **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** |
| Service discovery protocol | **DDS-RTPS (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe)** |
| Default QoS reliability for services | **RELIABLE** (guaranteed delivery) |
| Default transport | **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** for DDS |

> [!TIP]
> The `---` separator line inside a `.srv` file is **not optional**. Anything *above* the line belongs to the **Request**, and anything *below* belongs to the **Response**. KTU examiners frequently test this.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a ROS 2 Service Interface (`.srv` file)

A service interface file is a plain-text IDL (Interface Description Language) file with three logical zones:

```
# Zone 1: Request fields (caller provides these)
<type> <field_name>   # e.g., int64 a
int64 b

# Zone 2: Mandatory separator (exactly three hyphens)
---

# Zone 3: Response fields (server returns these)
<type> <field_name>   # e.g., int64 sum
```

The compiler (`rosidl`) generates language-specific bindings: in **C++**, it produces a class with two nested structs `Request` and `Response`; in **Python**, it produces a module with a `Request` and `Response` class.

## 2.2 The Request/Response Lifecycle — Structured Logic Steps

The implementation of a ROS 2 service follows a **strict six-stage lifecycle**:

1. **Definition Stage** — The developer authors a `.srv` file inside the package's `srv/` directory, declaring the request and response field types.
2. **Build Stage** — The `rosidl_default_generators` CMake toolchain (for C++) or `rosidl_default_builders` (for Python) parses the `.srv` and generates typed source code.
3. **Manifest Registration Stage** — In `CMakeLists.txt`, the macro `rosidl_generate_interfaces(${PROJECT_NAME} "srv/AddTwoInts.srv")` registers the service. In `package.xml`, the dependency `rosidl_default_runtime` and `rosidl_default_generators` must be declared.
4. **Server Instantiation Stage** — A node instantiates a service server by calling `create_service<ServiceType>("service_name", callback)`, binding a user-defined callback function that will be invoked upon every incoming request.
5. **Client Instantiation Stage** — Another node creates a client using `create_client<ServiceType>("service_name")` and may optionally `wait_for_service()` to ensure the server is alive.
6. **Invocation Stage** — The client constructs a `Request` object, populates its fields, and calls `async_send_request(request)` (non-blocking) or `call(request)` (blocking). The server's callback runs, computes a `Response`, and returns it. The client's future is fulfilled, and the response is retrieved.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' behind each step:** Service definition (Step 1) ensures *type safety* across distributed nodes written in different languages. The mandatory build registration (Steps 2–3) is what allows ROS 2 to perform **compile-time schema validation** — a request with a wrong field type will be rejected before it ever hits the network.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Element | Mathematical / Structural Representation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Service Type Symbol | $S = \langle R_{req}, R_{res} \rangle$ | A service $S$ is an ordered pair of request schema and response schema. |
| Synchronous Blocking Time | $T_{total} = T_{network} + T_{compute} + T_{serialization}$ | Client blocks for the entire duration. |
| Asynchronous Overhead | $T_{async} = T_{submit} + T_{poll\ interval}$ | Client regains control immediately after $T_{submit}$. |
| Callback Signature (C++) | `void cb(const Request::SharedPtr req, Response::SharedPtr res)` | Must return `void`; the response is mutated in-place. |
| Concurrency Model | **Multi-threaded executor** (recommended for services) | Single-threaded executor can deadlock if a service calls another service. |
| Discovery Latency | $L_d \approx 0.1$ s to $2.0$ s on local DDS | Use `wait_for_service()` to mask this. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

ROS 2 services are deployed in production robotics for:
- **Map management** in autonomous vehicles (load/save a static map).
- **Calibration routines** in robotic arms (compute kinematic offsets).
- **Lifecycle transitions** in managed nodes (configure, activate, deactivate, cleanup, shutdown).
- **State queries** in fleet management (battery level, firmware version of a specific robot).
- **Mission abort** commands in UAV swarms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Services are **NOT** suitable for high-frequency sensor streaming (use Topics) or long-running computations exceeding a few seconds (use Actions). KTU examiners often test this design judgment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Implementing `AddTwoInts` Service in C++

We will now build a complete, compilable ROS 2 service. The pedagogical goal is to show *every* configuration file, *every* command, and *every* conceptual decision.

### Step 1 — Create the Package Skeleton

A ROS 2 package is the atomic unit of code distribution. We create a C++ package named `cpp_srvcli` with the necessary dependencies:

- `rclcpp` — the ROS 2 C++ client library.
- `example_interfaces` — pre-built service definitions used for pedagogy.

```
ros2 pkg create --build-type ament_cmake cpp_srvcli \
  --dependencies rclcpp example_interfaces
```

**Conversion logic:** The `--build-type ament_cmake` flag tells the toolchain to use CMake (as opposed to `ament_python`). The `--dependencies` flag auto-injects the required entries into `package.xml`, which prevents a very common KTU-pitfall of missing dependencies.

### Step 2 — Author the Service Definition

Since we are *reusing* the pre-built `example_interfaces/srv/AddTwoInts` service, the `.srv` file is already installed by the dependency. For reference, its content is:

```
int64 a
int64 b
---
int64 sum
```

**Line-by-line meaning:**
- Line 1 & 2 — Two `int64` fields named `a` and `b` form the **Request**.
- Line 3 — The mandatory **`---`** separator.
- Line 4 — One `int64` field named `sum` forms the **Response**.

> [!NOTE]
> Had we authored a custom service, we would have placed this file in `cpp_srvcli/srv/AddTwoInts.srv` and added `rosidl_generate_interfaces(${PROJECT_NAME} "srv/AddTwoInts.srv")` to `CMakeLists.txt`, plus the `rosidl_default_runtime` and `rosidl_default_generators` dependencies in `package.xml`.

### Step 3 — Write the Service Server Node

The server source code is placed in `src/add_two_ints_server.cpp`. We will analyze it block-by-block.

**Header and class declaration:**

```cpp
#include "rclcpp/rclcpp.hpp"
#include "example_interfaces/srv/add_two_ints.hpp"

class AddTwoIntsServer : public rclcpp::Node
{
public:
  AddTwoIntsServer() : Node("add_two_ints_server")
  {
    service_ = this->create_service<example_interfaces::srv::AddTwoInts>(
      "add_two_ints",
      std::bind(&AddTwoIntsServer::add, this,
                std::placeholders::_1, std::placeholders::_2));
  }

private:
  void add(
    const example_interfaces::srv::AddTwoInts::Request::SharedPtr request,
    example_interfaces::srv::AddTwoInts::Response::SharedPtr response)
  {
    response->sum = request->a + request->b;
    RCLCPP_INFO(this->get_logger(), "Incoming: a=%ld, b=%ld",
                request->a, request->b);
    RCLCPP_INFO(this->get_logger(), "Sending back: [%ld]",
                response->sum);
  }

  rclcpp::Service<example_interfaces::srv::AddTwoInts>::SharedPtr service_;
};
```

**Conversion logic (line-by-line):**

- The constructor passes the node name `"add_two_ints_server"` to the base `Node` class.
- `create_service<ServiceType>` is a templated function. The first argument is the **service name** (a string used for DDS discovery). The second argument is a `std::function` bound to the member function `add`.
- `std::placeholders::_1` and `_2` reserve the slots for the request and response arguments that the executor will pass.
- The callback `add` **must** mutate the `response` shared-pointer in-place. Returning a value is **not** the convention in ROS 2 C++.
- The `RCLCPP_INFO` macro is the C++ equivalent of `print()` for ROS logging; it adds timestamp, severity, and node name automatically.

**The `main()` function:**

```cpp
int main(int argc, char * argv[])
{
  rclcpp::init(argc, argv);
  rclcpp::spin(std::make_shared<AddTwoIntsServer>());
  rclcpp::shutdown();
  return 0;
}
```

**Conversion logic:**
- `rclcpp::init` parses command-line arguments and initializes the DDS layer.
- `rclcpp::spin` enters the executor loop, processing all incoming service requests, topic messages, and timer events for this node.
- `rclcpp::shutdown` gracefully tears down the DDS participants, ensuring no leaked sockets.

### Step 4 — Write the Service Client Node

The client source code is placed in `src/add_two_ints_client.cpp`. The critical difference is the use of an **asynchronous future** so the client does not freeze while waiting.

```cpp
#include "rclcpp/rclcpp.hpp"
#include "example_interfaces/srv/add_two_ints.hpp"

class AddTwoIntsClient : public rclcpp::Node
{
public:
  AddTwoIntsClient() : Node("add_two_ints_client")
  {
    client_ = this->create_client<example_interfaces::srv::AddTwoInts>(
      "add_two_ints");
    while (!client_->wait_for_service(std::chrono::seconds(1))) {
      RCLCPP_WARN(this->get_logger(),
        "Service not available, waiting again...");
    }
  }

  void send_request(int64_t a, int64_t b)
  {
    auto request = std::make_shared<
      example_interfaces::srv::AddTwoInts::Request>();
    request->a = a;
    request->b = b;

    auto result_future = client_->async_send_request(request);
    if (rclcpp::spin_until_future_complete(
        this->shared_from_this(), result_future) ==
        rclcpp::FutureReturnCode::SUCCESS)
    {
      auto response = result_future.get();
      RCLCPP_INFO(this->get_logger(), "Result of %ld + %ld is %ld",
                  a, b, response->sum);
    } else {
      RCLCPP_ERROR(this->get_logger(),
        "Failed to call service add_two_ints");
    }
  }

private:
  rclcpp::Client<example_interfaces::srv::AddTwoInts>::SharedPtr client_;
};
```

**Conversion logic:**
- `wait_for_service(1s)` is a **busy-wait poll** that periodically queries DDS discovery. It returns `true` as soon as the server is visible. The `while` loop converts a one-shot check into a blocking wait.
- `async_send_request` returns a `std::shared_future`. The client can continue doing other work until the future is ready.
- `spin_until_future_complete` blocks the current thread *only* until the response arrives, without processing other callbacks (unlike `spin`).
- `result_future.get()` extracts the response; it is a blocking call if the future is not yet ready (defensive safety net).

### Step 5 — Configure the Build System

The `CMakeLists.txt` is updated with two new executables and their dependencies. The key block is:

```cmake
add_executable(server src/add_two_ints_server.cpp)
ament_target_dependencies(server rclcpp example_interfaces)

add_executable(client src/add_two_ints_client.cpp)
ament_target_dependencies(client rclcpp example_interfaces)
```

**Conversion logic:** `ament_target_dependencies` is a wrapper that translates ROS package names into CMake `find_package` calls and propagates include paths, so you do not have to write raw `target_link_libraries` boilerplate.

### Step 6 — Build, Source, and Run

```
colcon build --packages-select cpp_srvcli
source install/setup.bash
ros2 run cpp_srvcli server   # Terminal 1
ros2 run cpp_srvcli client   # Terminal 2
```

The client prints `Result of 2 + 3 is 5` and the server logs the incoming request.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Client Calls

| Property | `client_->call(req)` (Deprecated) | `async_send_request` + `spin_until_future_complete` |
|---|---|---|
| Blocking | Yes (entire app) | Partially (one thread) |
| Executor type needed | None | Multi-threaded executor recommended |
| Timeout control | None | Yes, via `timeout` argument |
| Cancellation support | No | Yes, via the returned future handle |
| Thread safety | Inherited from caller | Requires care with shared state |
| Recommended for new code | **No** | **Yes** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The legacy `call()` method has been **deprecated** in modern ROS 2 distributions. KTU 2024 answers should always demonstrate `async_send_request`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Service Communication Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

The following diagram captures the **chronological message flow** between a service client, the DDS middleware, and a service server. Notice the *blocking* arrow on the client side.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ClientNode as Client Node
    participant DDS as DDS Middleware
    participant ServerNode as Server Node

    Note over ClientNode: create_client<ServiceType>("add_two_ints")
    ClientNode->>DDS: PUBLISH Service Discovery (Participant announcement)
    DDS->>ServerNode: Discovery notification
    ServerNode-->>DDS: ACK + Service endpoint announcement
    DDS-->>ClientNode: Service "add_two_ints" is now visible

    Note over ClientNode: while (!wait_for_service(1s)) {}
    ClientNode->>ClientNode: build Request object {a=2, b=3}

    ClientNode->>DDS: SEND Request (synchronous call)
    DDS->>ServerNode: Forward Request
    activate ServerNode
    ServerNode->>ServerNode: callback(Request) -> {sum = 5}
    ServerNode->>DDS: SEND Response
    deactivate ServerNode
    DDS->>ClientNode: Forward Response
    Note over ClientNode: future.get() returns Response{sum=5}
    ClientNode->>ClientNode: RCLCPP_INFO "Result: 5"
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a ROS 2 Service Node

This diagram shows the **internal software architecture** of a single service node, illustrating how the executor, callback group, and service handle interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application main thread] --> B[rclcpp init]
    B --> C[Node Construction]
    C --> D[create_service SrvType]
    D --> E[Service Handle stored as SharedPtr]
    E --> F[Executor Spinner Loop]
    F --> G{Incoming Request?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Allocate Request SharedPtr]
    H --> I[Allocate Response SharedPtr]
    I --> J[Invoke user callback]
    J --> K[Callback mutates Response]
    K --> L[Return Response via DDS]
    L --> F
    G -- No --> M{Timer or Topic ready?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Process Timer or Subscription]
    N --> F
    M -- No --> O[Yield CPU]
    O --> F
```

## 4.3 File-Layout Diagram of a C++ Service Package

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[cpp_srvcli package] --> P1[src /]
    P1 --> P1a[add_two_ints_server.cpp]
    P1 --> P1b[add_two_ints_client.cpp]
    P --> P2[include / cpp_srvcli /]
    P2 --> P2a[visibility control hpp]
    P --> P3[CMakeLists txt]
    P --> P4[package xml]
    P --> P5[srv /]
    P5 --> P5a[Custom Srv File if any]
    P --> P6[launch /]
    P6 --> P6a[server client launch py]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `srv/` directory is **optional** when reusing pre-built service definitions from `example_interfaces` or `std_srvs`. It becomes **mandatory** when defining a custom service interface.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
*Differentiate between a ROS 2 Topic and a ROS 2 Service. When would you choose a Service over a Topic in a robotic application?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Topic** in ROS 2 implements *asynchronous, many-to-many* publish/subscribe communication, best suited for continuous data streams such as sensor readings or velocity commands. A **Service** implements *synchronous, one-to-one* request/reply communication, best suited for discrete, transactional operations such as triggering a calibration routine, querying a robot's firmware version, or loading a saved map. A Service should be chosen over a Topic when the operation has a clear start and end, requires confirmation of completion, and involves a single client needing a single response from a single server.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark allocation pattern (KTU):** 1 mark for Topic definition, 1 mark for Service definition, 1 mark for the design-selection justification.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*Explain the structure of a `.srv` file in ROS 2. What is the significance of the `---` separator line?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A `.srv` file in ROS 2 is an Interface Description Language (IDL) file that defines the schema for a service. It is divided into two sections by a mandatory `---` separator line. The lines **above** the `---` declare the **Request** message fields (data sent by the client), and the lines **below** declare the **Response** message fields (data returned by the server). The `---` separator is a syntactic boundary that the `rosidl` code generator uses to split the file into two distinct message structures, ensuring type-safe request and response objects. For example, the `AddTwoInts` service declares `int64 a` and `int64 b` above the line, and `int64 sum` below it.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that the `---` line must contain **exactly three hyphens** with **no trailing spaces or characters**. Some students place comments *on* the separator line — this is allowed but discouraged as it can confuse strict parsers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Convention:** Within a module, students are given a choice between **Question A** and **Question B**, both worth 14 marks. Each question typically has two sub-parts: (a) worth 7 marks and (b) worth 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a ROS 2 service. Discuss the roles of the **service client**, **service server**, **DDS middleware**, and the **executor** in this architecture. (7 marks)

**(b)** Write a complete C++ program to implement a ROS 2 service server that accepts a request containing two `int64` integers `a` and `b`, and returns their **sum, difference, and product** as the response. Define a custom service interface for this. Show all configuration files. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**

The ROS 2 service architecture consists of four principal components:

1. **Service Client** — A node that wants to invoke a specific operation. It uses `create_client<ServiceType>("name")` to obtain a client handle, optionally calls `wait_for_service()` to ensure discovery, builds a `Request` object, and invokes `async_send_request(request)` to dispatch the call.

2. **Service Server** — A node that registers itself as the provider of a service. It uses `create_service<ServiceType>("name", callback)` to bind a callback. When a request arrives, the executor invokes this callback, which mutates a `Response` object that is sent back automatically.

3. **DDS Middleware** — The communication substrate (e.g., Cyclone DDS, Fast DDS, RTI Connext). It handles **discovery** (matching service names across machines), **serialization** (converting C++ structs to CDR byte streams), **transport** (UDP/TCP), and **Quality of Service** enforcement (reliability, durability, deadline).

4. **Executor** — A thread-loop that polls the DDS layer for incoming service requests and dispatches them to the registered callback. The default `SingleThreadedExecutor` is simple but can deadlock if a service callback calls another service. The `MultiThreadedExecutor` is recommended for production.

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Service Client Node] -- Request --> B[DDS Layer]
    B -- Request --> C[Service Server Node]
    C -- Response --> B
    B -- Response --> A
    C --> D[Executor Spinner]
    A --> E[Executor Spinner]
    D --> F[User Callback]
    F --> D
```

**(b) Custom Service Implementation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define the service (`srv/Arithmetic.srv`):**

```
int64 a
int64 b
---
int64 sum
int64 difference
int64 product
```

**Step 2 — Update `package.xml`** to include:

```xml
<depend>rclcpp</depend>
<depend>example_interfaces</depend>
<depend>rosidl_default_runtime</depend>
<member_of_group>rosidl_interface_packages</member_of_group>
```

**Step 3 — Update `CMakeLists.txt`:**

```cmake
find_package(rosidl_default_generators REQUIRED)

rosidl_generate_interfaces(${PROJECT_NAME}
  "srv/Arithmetic.srv"
)

add_executable(arithmetic_server src/arithmetic_server.cpp)
ament_target_dependencies(arithmetic_server rclcpp)
```

**Step 4 — Server source (`src/arithmetic_server.cpp`):**

```cpp
#include "rclcpp/rclcpp.hpp"
#include "cpp_pkg/srv/arithmetic.hpp"

class ArithmeticServer : public rclcpp::Node
{
public:
  ArithmeticServer() : Node("arithmetic_server")
  {
    service_ = this->create_service<cpp_pkg::srv::Arithmetic>(
      "arithmetic",
      std::bind(&ArithmeticServer::compute, this,
                std::placeholders::_1, std::placeholders::_2));
  }

private:
  void compute(
    const cpp_pkg::srv::Arithmetic::Request::SharedPtr req,
    cpp_pkg::srv::Arithmetic::Response::SharedPtr res)
  {
    res->sum = req->a + req->b;
    res->difference = req->a - req->b;
    res->product = req->a * req->b;
    RCLCPP_INFO(this->get_logger(),
      "a=%ld b=%ld => sum=%ld diff=%ld prod=%ld",
      req->a, req->b, res->sum, res->difference, res->product);
  }

  rclcpp::Service<cpp_pkg::srv::Arithmetic>::SharedPtr service_;
};

int main(int argc, char * argv[])
{
  rclcpp::init(argc, argv);
  rclcpp::spin(std::make_shared<ArithmeticServer>());
  rclcpp::shutdown();
  return 0;
}
```

**Step 5 — Build and run:**

```
colcon build --packages-select cpp_pkg
source install/setup.bash
ros2 run cpp_pkg arithmetic_server
ros2 service call /arithmetic cpp_pkg/srv/Arithmetic "{a: 10, b: 4}"
```

**Expected output:**

```
sum: 14
difference: 6
product: 40
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** Explain the concept of a **service client** in ROS 2. How does a client perform **service discovery**, and what is the role of `wait_for_service()`? (7 marks)

**(b)** Write a complete C++ program to implement a ROS 2 service client that calls an existing service named `add_two_ints` (from `example_interfaces`). The client should take two integers as **command-line arguments**, send them to the server, and print the resulting sum. Show how to handle a scenario where the server is not yet up. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Service Client & Discovery (7 marks):**

A **service client** in ROS 2 is a handle within a node that allows the node to invoke a remote operation. The client is created via `create_client<ServiceType>("service_name")`. Internally, the client uses **DDS-RTPS discovery** to find a matching service server on the network. This discovery is **asynchronous and distributed** — there is no central broker. Each DDS participant announces its presence via multicast or peer-list messages, and once a match is found (same service name, same service type), the endpoint is considered "discovered."

`wait_for_service(timeout)` is a **convenience blocking call** that polls the discovery state at 1-second intervals until the service becomes available or the timeout expires. It is essential because, in a real robotic system, the server and client may start in **arbitrary order**; without `wait_for_service`, an `async_send_request` issued before discovery would simply time out at the DDS layer with no clear error.

**Workflow:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Node starts] --> B[create_client]
    B --> C[wait_for_service 1s]
    C --> D{Server discovered?}
    D -- No --> E[Log WARN, loop back to C]
    D -- Yes --> F[Build Request object]
    F --> G[async_send_request]
    G --> H[spin_until_future_complete]
    H --> I[future get returns Response]
    I --> J[Use response data]
```

**(b) Complete Client Code (7 marks):**

```cpp
#include "rclcpp/rclcpp.hpp"
#include "example_interfaces/srv/add_two_ints.hpp"

class AddTwoIntsClient : public rclcpp::Node
{
public:
  AddTwoIntsClient() : Node("add_two_ints_client")
  {
    client_ = this->create_client<example_interfaces::srv::AddTwoInts>(
      "add_two_ints");

    while (!client_->wait_for_service(std::chrono::seconds(1))) {
      if (!rclcpp::ok()) {
        RCLCPP_ERROR(this->get_logger(),
          "Interrupted while waiting for service. Exiting.");
        return;
      }
      RCLCPP_WARN(this->get_logger(),
        "Service 'add_two_ints' not available. Retrying...");
    }
  }

  void send_request(int64_t a, int64_t b)
  {
    auto request = std::make_shared<
      example_interfaces::srv::AddTwoInts::Request>();
    request->a = a;
    request->b = b;

    auto future = client_->async_send_request(request);
    auto status = rclcpp::spin_until_future_complete(
      this->shared_from_this(), future,
      std::chrono::seconds(5));

    if (status == rclcpp::FutureReturnCode::SUCCESS) {
      RCLCPP_INFO(this->get_logger(),
        "Result: %ld + %ld = %ld", a, b, future.get()->sum);
    } else if (status == rclcpp::FutureReturnCode::TIMEOUT) {
      RCLCPP_ERROR(this->get_logger(), "Service call timed out.");
    } else {
      RCLCPP_ERROR(this->get_logger(), "Service call failed.");
    }
  }

private:
  rclcpp::Client<example_interfaces::srv::AddTwoInts>::SharedPtr client_;
};

int main(int argc, char * argv[])
{
  rclcpp::init(argc, argv);

  if (argc != 3) {
    RCLCPP_ERROR(rclcpp::get_logger("rclcpp"),
      "Usage: ros2 run cpp_srvcli client <int1> <int2>");
    return 1;
  }

  auto node = std::make_shared<AddTwoIntsClient>();
  node->send_request(std::atoll(argv[1]), std::atoll(argv[2]));
  rclcpp::shutdown();
  return 0;
}
```

**Conversion logic highlights:**

- `std::atoll` safely converts command-line `char*` arguments to `int64_t`.
- The 5-second timeout on `spin_until_future_complete` ensures that a misbehaving server cannot hang the client forever.
- The `rclcpp::ok()` check inside the wait loop is a graceful-shutdown guard: if the user presses `Ctrl+C` while the client is waiting, the loop exits cleanly.
- The `FutureReturnCode` enum distinguishes between success, timeout, and failure, allowing precise error reporting.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using the deprecated `call()` method** — deduct 2 marks. Always use `async_send_request` + `spin_until_future_complete`.
> 2. **Forgetting to register the service in `CMakeLists.txt`** — if you author a custom `.srv` file but skip `rosidl_generate_interfaces`, the build will fail and you will receive 0 marks for the implementation part.
> 3. **Returning a value from the callback** — the C++ service callback **must** return `void` and mutate the response in-place. Returning a `Response` object is a Pythonic habit that fails silently in C++ ROS 2.
> 4. **Blocking inside a service callback with a single-threaded executor** — if your service calls another service, use `MultiThreadedExecutor` or you will deadlock.
> 5. **Missing `<member_of_group>rosidl_interface_packages</member_of_group>`** in `package.xml` — without this line, `colcon build` cannot find your custom service.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Implementing a Service"**

- A **Service** is a **synchronous, one-to-one, request/response** communication primitive in ROS 2.
- The service definition lives in a `.srv` file split by a mandatory **`---`** line (Request above, Response below).
- A **Service Server** uses `create_service<ServiceType>(name, callback)`; the callback signature is `void cb(Request::SharedPtr, Response::SharedPtr)`.
- A **Service Client** uses `create_client<ServiceType>(name)`, optionally polls with `wait_for_service(1s)`, and calls `async_send_request(request)`.
- Always use `rclcpp::spin_until_future_complete(node, future)` to retrieve the response, **never** the deprecated `call()` method.
- Discovery is **decentralized via DDS-RTPS**; there is no central ROS Master in ROS 2 (unlike ROS 1).
- Custom `.srv` files require **three** entries in `package.xml`: `rosidl_default_generators` (build), `rosidl_default_runtime` (exec), and `<member_of_group>rosidl_interface_packages</member_of_group>`.
- Custom `.srv` files require the `rosidl_generate_interfaces(${PROJECT_NAME} "srv/Foo.srv")` macro in `CMakeLists.txt`.
- Use `MultiThreadedExecutor` whenever a service callback may invoke another service or call back into the executor.
- The QoS for services is **RELIABLE by default** — you cannot use a `BEST_EFFORT` service client with a `RELIABLE` server (or vice versa) without explicit QoS overrides.
- Services are **inappropriate** for: continuous sensor data (use Topics), long-running tasks over a few seconds (use Actions), or one-to-many broadcasts (use Topics).
- The introspection command `ros2 service list -t` displays all active services and their types — use it during debugging.
- The `ros2 service call /name type data` CLI command is the **fastest way to manually test** a service server from the terminal.
- **Compile-time schema validation** means a wrong-field-type request is rejected at the IDL layer before serialization — this is a major architectural advantage over ROS 1's loose XML-RPC service model.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the Robot Operating System (ROS), a **Service** is a **synchronous, request/response based communication mechanism** used for direct, one-to-one data exchange between ROS nodes. It is formally defined as a strictly typed, blocking transaction in which a *Service Client* node sends a structured *Request* message to a *Service Server* node, and the server, upon completion of its computation, returns a single structured *Response* message back to the client.

Mathematically, a ROS Service is defined by a quintuple:

$$S = \langle N_s, N_c, M_{req}, M_{res}, C_b \rangle$$

Where:
- $N_s$ is the **Service Server Node** (the service provider)
- $N_c$ is the **Service Client Node** (the service consumer)
- $M_{req}$ is the **Request Message Structure** (input data)
- $M_{res}$ is the **Response Message Structure** (output data)
- $C_b \in \{0, 1\}$ denotes the **Blocking Call Flag** ($C_b = 1$ for synchronous blocking calls)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Services in ROS are one of the **three primary communication paradigms** alongside **Topics** (asynchronous publish/subscribe) and **Actions** (asynchronous goal/feedback/result). The official ROS documentation classifies services as the *preferred mechanism for short, discrete, transaction-like operations* (e.g., toggling a gripper, querying a sensor, switching modes).

## 1.2 The Service Description Language (`.srv`)

Every ROS service is defined by a **Service Description File** (`.srv`) which is a text file using a simplified *Interface Definition Language* (IDL). It contains exactly two fields separated by a literal `---` line:

```
# Request fields (sent from client to server)
<type> <field_name>   <-- INPUT
---
# Response fields (sent from server to client)
<type> <field_name>   <-- OUTPUT
```

The `.srv` file is compiled at build-time by the **ROS build system (`catkin` or `colcon`)** into native language headers (C++/Python), enforcing strict type-checking and API consistency.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Vending Machine 🍫**
>
> Imagine a vending machine in a cafeteria. You (the **Client**) walk up to the machine (the **Server**), insert a coin, and press the button for a specific chocolate (the **Request**). The machine then mechanically processes your request and *immediately* dispenses the chocolate (the **Response**). Notice the key characteristics:
>
> 1. **Synchronous / Blocking** — You stand there and *wait* until the chocolate is delivered. You do not walk away and do other things while the machine processes.
> 2. **One-to-One** — Only one customer (client) gets a specific item at a time per slot.
> 3. **Strictly Typed Request** — The machine accepts only specific coin denominations and button presses (typed inputs).
> 4. **Single Response** — The machine gives exactly one response per request (a chocolate or an error).
> 5. **Pull-based** — The server is *pulled* into action only when a client calls; it does not continuously broadcast.
>
> Contrast this with **Topics** (the analogy: a public radio station 📻), which continuously broadcast whether anyone is listening or not.

## 1.4 Core Distinctions: Service vs. Topic vs. Action

| Parameter | Service | Topic | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Communication Pattern | Request/Response | Publish/Subscribe | Goal/Feedback/Result |
| Synchrony | **Synchronous (Blocking)** | Asynchronous (Non-blocking) | Asynchronous (Non-blocking) |
| Directionality | Bidirectional (1 call $\rightarrow$ 1 reply) | Unidirectional (many pubs $\rightarrow$ many subs) | Bidirectional with periodic feedback |
| Use Case | Discrete, quick tasks | Continuous data streams | Long-running, preemptible tasks |
| Preemption | Not supported | Not applicable | Supported (cancel goals) |
| Examples | `/spawn_turtle`, `/reset` | `/camera/image_raw`, `/cmd_vel` | `/move_base`, `/follow_joint_trajectory` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A practical rule of thumb taught in KTU 2024 robotics labs: **"If the operation finishes in milliseconds and produces a single result, use a Service. If it produces a continuous stream, use a Topic. If it takes seconds-to-minutes and may need cancellation, use an Action."**

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Service Communication Lifecycle

The complete lifecycle of a ROS Service transaction involves **six discrete phases**, each governed by the **ROS Master** (`roscore`):

### Phase 1 — Master Registration
The **Service Server** node, upon startup, registers itself with the ROS Master using the **XML-RPC protocol** over TCP port `11311`. It publishes the tuple $\langle \text{service\_name}, \text{service\_type}, \text{URI} \rangle$ into the Master's parameter/service registry.

### Phase 2 — Client Discovery
When a **Service Client** node starts, it queries the ROS Master: *"Who provides service `/add_two_ints`?"*. The Master returns the **URI** (Uniform Resource Identifier) of the registered server.

### Phase 3 — Connection Establishment
The client and server establish a **direct TCP/IP connection** on a negotiated ephemeral port. From this point onward, **the Master is no longer in the data path** — all request/response traffic flows peer-to-peer.

### Phase 4 — Service Request
The client serializes its `M_{req}` message (according to the `.srv` definition) into a binary buffer using the **ROS Message Serialization Protocol** (a compact, version-stable binary format). It transmits the buffer to the server.

### Phase 5 — Server-Side Callback
The server deserializes the request, invokes a user-defined **callback function** $f_{cb}(req) \rightarrow res$, and computes the response.

### Phase 6 — Service Response
The server serializes the `M_{res}` and sends it back over the same TCP connection. The client's blocking call unblocks and returns the response object to the calling code.

## 2.2 The Blocking Call Equation

The blocking time experienced by the client can be modeled as:

$$T_{block} = T_{network} + T_{serialize} + T_{callback} + T_{deserialize} + T_{network}$$

Where:
- $T_{network}$ is the round-trip network latency
- $T_{serialize}$ is the request/response marshalling cost
- $T_{callback}$ is the server's actual processing time
- $T_{deserialize}$ is the unmarshalling cost

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because the client *blocks* during $T_{block}$, services should **never** be used for computationally heavy tasks within real-time control loops. The maximum recommended service latency for a 100 Hz control loop is **$T_{block} < 10$ ms**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Definition | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_{name}$ | Unique service name in the ROS graph | `/spawn`, `/get_loggers` | URI string |
| $N_{srv}$ | Number of concurrent service servers per name | **1** (strictly enforced) | count |
| $M_{req}$ | Request message schema | defined in `.srv` | typed fields |
| $M_{res}$ | Response message schema | defined in `.srv` | typed fields |
| $T_{conn}$ | TCP connection setup time | $\approx 1$ to $\vert 5 \vert$ | ms |
| $T_{block}$ | Total blocking duration on client | variable | ms |
| $P_{tcp}$ | Default ROS TCP port for Master | **11311** | port number |
| $L_{msg}$ | Maximum ROS message size | $\approx 256$ | MB |
| $C_{b}$ | Blocking flag (always 1 for services) | 1 | boolean |
| $\lambda_{req}$ | Service call rate (Poisson-distributed) | task-dependent | calls/s |

## 2.4 Standard ROS Service Commands (KTU Lab Focus)

The ROS command-line tooling provides direct access to services without writing code. These are **frequently tested in KTU viva/practical exams**:

| Command | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `rosservice list` | Lists **all active services** in the ROS graph |
| `rosservice type $\vert$ /service_name` | Returns the service type (e.g., `turtlesim/Spawn`) |
| `rosservice find $\vert$ type` | Finds services matching a type string |
| `rosservice call $\vert$ /service_name args` | Calls a service with arguments from CLI |
| `rosservice uri $\vert$ /service_name` | Returns the network URI of the service |
| `rosservice args $\vert$ /service_name` | Returns the request/response argument list |
| `rosservice info $\vert$ /service_name` | Returns Node, URI, and Type metadata |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Robotics (ABB, KUKA, Fanuc)**: Services are used for mode-switching (auto/manual), E-stop query, calibration trigger, and tool-changer actuation.
- **Autonomous Vehicles (Autoware, Apollo)**: Lane-change *authorization* requests, route re-planning triggers, and map-server tile fetch.
- **Service Robotics (Pepper, Fetch, TIAGo)**: Speech-recognition trigger, object database query, and arm-stow/unstow commands.
- **Drone Swarms**: Task assignment, leader election, and consensus queries.
