Torso Lift (`torso_lift`) - **Type:** Prismatic joint (linear extension). - **Stroke:** Approximately **0.0 m to 0.835 m** above the base. - **Why a lifting torso?** Expands the **vertical reachable workspace** of the arm without requiring an 8th revolute joint — mechanically simpler and stiffer. - **ROS joint name:** `torso_lift_joint` (type: `prismatic`). ### C. 7-DOF Robotic Arm (`arm`) - **Kinematic structure:** Revolute–Revolute–Revolute–Revolute–Revolute–Revolute–Revolute (7R). - **Why 7 DOF?** Provides **kinematic redundancy** — the arm can achieve a given end-effector pose in infinitely many configurations, allowing it to **avoid obstacles** and **avoid joint limits** while reaching. - **Reach (shoulder to fingertip):** Approximately **0.95 m**. - **Payload at end-effector:** ~**1.5 kg** (typical grasping task capacity). - **Joint names (canonical):** `shoulder_pan_joint`, `shoulder_lift_joint`, `upperarm_roll_joint`, `elbow_flex_joint`, `forearm_roll_joint`, `wrist_flex_joint`, `wrist_roll_joint`. ### D. Gripper (`gripper`) - **Type:** Parallel-jaw, 1-DOF. - **Stroke:** ~**0 cm to 9 cm** (variable across firmware revisions). - **Force feedback:** Current-sensed for **grasp-slip detection**. ### E. Sensor Head (`head`) - **3D depth camera:** **Microsoft Kinect One** (or compatible PrimeSense/RealSense equivalent) — provides RGB-D point cloud. - **2D laser scanner:** **SICK TiM510** or **Hokuyo UST-20LX** — provides 270° planar scan. - **IMU:** For orientation estimation and tilt correction. - **Head pan-tilt:** The head is mounted on a 2-DOF pan-tilt neck (`head_pan_joint`, `head_tilt_joint`) so the camera can be aimed independently of the base. ## 2.2 KTU Specification / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize the values in the table below** — they appear verbatim in past KTU Part A and Part B questions. | Subsystem | Parameter | Symbol / Joint | Standard Value | Unit | |-----------|-----------|----------------|---------------|------| | Base | Drive type | — | Differential | — | | Base | Wheel encoder resolution | — | High-resolution optical | ticks/rev | | Torso | Joint type | $q_{torso}$ | Prismatic | — | | Torso | Vertical stroke | $\Delta h_{torso}$ | **0.835** | m | | Arm | Degrees of Freedom | $n$ | **7** | DOF | | Arm | Joint count | $n_{rev}$ | 7 revolute | — | | Arm | Max reach | $L_{reach}$ | **0.95** | m | | Arm | Payload | $m_{pay}$ | **1.5** | kg | | Arm | DH convention | $a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i$ | Standard Fetch URDF | mixed | | Gripper | DOF | — | 1 | DOF | | Gripper | Max aperture | $w_{grip}$ | **0.09** | m | | Sensor | 3D camera | — | Kinect One (or equivalent) | RGB-D | | Sensor | 2D laser | — | SICK / Hokuyo (270°) | planar | | Sensor | IMU | — | 9-DOF | — | | Software | Native ROS versions | — | Indigo, Kinetic, Melodic, Noetic, ROS 2 Humble | — | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The Fetch platform is used in: - **Warehouse automation** (Amazon-style pick-and-place research). - **Assistive robotics** (helping elderly/disabled users fetch objects). - **SLAM and navigation benchmarking** (a standard testbed for `amcl`, `gmapping`, `cartographer`). - **Imitation learning** and **teleoperation studies** (e.g., Google Brain's robotic grasping research). - **Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) studies** owing to its near-human silhouette. > [!NOTE] > The Fetch is the **de-facto reference platform** for the ROS-I (Industrial) and ROS-M (Mobile) working groups. KTU examiners frequently use Fetch as the canonical example in Module-3 design questions. # 3. Step-by-Step Kinematic Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Forward Kinematics of the Fetch Arm (Denavit–Hartenberg Method) The 7-DOF Fetch arm follows the **modified Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) convention**. The total homogeneous transformation from the arm's **base frame** to the **gripper frame** is: $$ {}^{0}_{7}T \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{7} {}^{i-1}_{i}T(\theta_i,\, d_i,\, a_i,\, \alpha_i) $$ Each individual link transformation is: $$ {}^{i-1}_{i}T \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### 3.2 Standard DH Parameter Table (Fetch 7-DOF Arm) | Link $i$ | $a_i$ (m) | $\alpha_i$ (rad) | $d_i$ (m) | $\theta_i$ (variable) | |----------|-----------|------------------|-----------|------------------------| | 1 (shoulder pan) | 0.0 | $-\pi/2$ | 0.0 | $\theta_1$ | | 2 (shoulder lift) | 0.0 | $\pi/2$ | 0.0 | $\theta_2$ | | 3 (upperarm roll) | 0.0 | $-\pi/2$ | $d_3$ | $\theta_3$ | | 4 (elbow flex) | $a_4$| $\pi/2$ | 0.0 | $\theta_4$ | | 5 (forearm roll) | 0.0 | $-\pi/2$ | $d_5$ | $\theta_5$ | | 6 (wrist flex) | 0.0 | $\pi/2$ | 0.0 | $\theta_6$ | | 7 (wrist roll) | 0.0 | 0 | $d_7$ | $\theta_7$ | > [!NOTE] > The exact numerical values of $a_4$, $d_3$, $d_5$, and $d_7$ are read from the **fetch_description** URDF available at `github.com/fetchrobotics/fetch_ros`. For KTU exam purposes, the symbolic derivation of ${}^{0}_{7}T$ is what is graded, not the numerical substitution. ### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — First Three Link Transformations **Step 1: Compute ${}^{0}_{1}T$** (shoulder pan, $a_1=0$, $\alpha_1=-\pi/2$, $d_1=0$). $$ {}^{0}_{1}T \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & 0 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 \\ \sin\theta_1 & 0 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ *Reasoning:* Substitute the DH parameters of link 1 into the general formula. Because $a_1 = 0$, the translation columns simplify to zero; because $\alpha_1 = -\pi/2$, the off-diagonal rotation terms reduce to $\pm 1$. **Step 2: Compute ${}^{1}_{2}T$** (shoulder lift, $a_2=0$, $\alpha_2=\pi/2$, $d_2=0$). $$ {}^{1}_{2}T \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & 0 & \sin\theta_2 & 0 \\ \sin\theta_2 & 0 & -\cos\theta_2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ *Reasoning:* Same substitution logic, with $\alpha_2 = +\pi/2$ flipping the sign of the off-diagonal terms relative to Step 1. **Step 3: Compute ${}^{2}_{3}T$** (upperarm roll, $a_3=0$, $\alpha_3=-\pi/2$, $d_3 \neq 0$). $$ {}^{2}_{3}T \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_3 & 0 & -\sin\theta_3 & 0 \\ \sin\theta_3 & 0 & \cos\theta_3 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 & d_3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ *Reasoning:* This is the first link with a non-zero $d_3$, which appears in the $z$-translation column. The rotation block is identical in form to ${}^{0}_{1}T$ because the $\alpha$ angle is the same. **Step 4: Compose ${}^{0}_{3}T = {}^{0}_{1}T \cdot {}^{1}_{2}T \cdot {}^{2}_{3}T$** $$ {}^{0}_{3}T \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c_1 c_2 c_3 - c_1 s_2 s_3 & s_1 & -c_1 c_2 s_3 - c_1 s_2 c_3 & 0 \\ s_1 c_2 c_3 - s_1 s_2 s_3 & -c_1 & -s_1 c_2 s_3 - s_1 s_2 c_3 & 0 \\ -s_2 c_3 - c_2 s_3 & 0 & s_2 s_3 - c_2 c_3 & d_3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ where $c_i = \cos\theta_i$ and $s_i = \sin\theta_i$. *Reasoning:* The matrix product is performed row-by-row, applying trigonometric identities to simplify. The non-zero $d_3$ from link 3 propagates into the **third column of the fourth row** of the final composite matrix. **Step 5 (continuation):** The remaining four links (elbow, forearm, wrist flex, wrist roll) follow the identical multiplication pattern: $$ {}^{0}_{7}T \;=\; {}^{0}_{3}T \cdot {}^{3}_{4}T \cdot {}^{4}_{5}T \cdot {}^{5}_{6}T \cdot {}^{6}_{7}T $$ The final $4 \times 4$ matrix ${}^{0}_{7}T$ has its **upper-left $3 \times 3$ block** equal to the **orientation of the gripper** ${}^{0}_{7}R$, and its **upper-right $3 \times 1$ column** equal to the **position of the gripper origin** ${}^{0}_{7}\vec{p}$: $$ {}^{0}_{7}T \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} {}^{0}_{7}R & {}^{0}_{7}\vec{p} \\ \vec{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Workspace Geometry of Fetch A useful analytical quantity for exam derivations is the **vertical reach envelope** of the Fetch arm (with torso fully extended): $$ h_{\max} \;=\; h_{torso}^{\max} \;+\; L_{reach} \;=\; 0.835 \, \text{m} \;+\; 0.95 \, \text{m} \;=\; 1.785 \, \text{m} $$ - $h_{torso}^{\max}$ = maximum torso lift height. - $L_{reach}$ = arm kinematic reach. - $h_{\max}$ = maximum vertical reach of the gripper tip from the floor. **The horizontal reach (in any direction in the horizontal plane) is bounded by the same $L_{reach} = 0.95$ m when the torso is locked at a fixed height.** The **reachable workspace** is therefore a **hemispherical shell of inner radius $(h_{torso}^{\min})$ and outer radius $(h_{torso}^{\max} + L_{reach})$**, modulated by the **roll DOFs** that can re-orient the gripper at any point in this shell. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Top-Level System Block Diagram (Fetch Hardware Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD A[Fetch Robot Platform] --> B[Mobile Base
Differential Drive] A --> C[Torso Lift
Prismatic 0.835m] A --> D[7-DOF Arm
7 Revolute Joints] A --> E[Gripper
1-DOF Parallel Jaw] A --> F[Sensor Head
Pan-Tilt Neck] B --> B1[Wheel Encoders] B --> B2[Bump Sensors] B --> B3[Cliff Sensors] F --> F1[Kinect One
RGB-D Camera] F --> F2[SICK TiM510
2D Laser] F --> F3[IMU 9-DOF] C --> C1[torso_lift_joint] D --> D1[shoulder_pan_joint] D --> D2[shoulder_lift_joint] D --> D3[upperarm_roll_joint] D --> D4[elbow_flex_joint] D --> D5[forearm_roll_joint] D --> D6[wrist_flex_joint] D --> D7[wrist_roll_joint] E --> E1[gripper_finger_joint] ``` ## 4.2 ROS Communication Architecture (Topic/Service Flow) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph HOST_PC["Onboard / Host PC running ROS"] node1[MoveIt! Node] node2[robot_state_publisher] node3[ros_control / Controllers] node4[SLAM and Navigation Stack] end subgraph FETCH_ROBOT["Fetch Robot Hardware"] hw1[Base Driver] hw2[Arm Controller] hw3[Gripper Driver] hw4[Head Camera Driver] hw5[Laser Driver] end node1 -- Joint Trajectory Action --> node3 node2 -- Publishes TF and Joint States --> node1 node4 -- Velocity Commands --> hw1 node3 -- Joint Commands --> hw2 node3 -- Gripper Command --> hw3 hw4 -- PointCloud2 and Image Topics --> node1 hw5 -- LaserScan Topic --> node4 ``` ## 4.3 TF (Transform) Tree of the Fetch Robot ```mermaid graph TD base_link --> base_footprint base_link --> torso_lift_link torso_lift_link --> shoulder_pan_link shoulder_pan_link --> shoulder_lift_link shoulder_lift_link --> upperarm_roll_link upperarm_roll_link --> elbow_flex_link elbow_flex_link --> forearm_roll_link forearm_roll_link --> wrist_flex_link wrist_flex_link --> wrist_roll_link wrist_roll_link --> gripper_link base_link --> head_pan_link head_pan_link --> head_tilt_link head_tilt_link --> kinect_link ``` > [!NOTE] > **Examination Tip (TF Tree):** The KTU board frequently asks students to draw or describe the parent-child chain between `base_link` and the `gripper_link`. The exact chain above — through `torso_lift_link` and seven arm links — is the **expected canonical answer**. ## 4.4 Joint Kinematic Schematic (Side View of Arm) ```mermaid graph LR j1[J1
shoulder_pan
vertical axis] --> j2[J2
shoulder_lift
horizontal axis] j2 --> j3[J3
upperarm_roll
forearm axis] j3 --> j4[J4
elbow_flex
perpendicular axis] j4 --> j5[J5
forearm_roll
forearm axis] j5 --> j6[J6
wrist_flex
perpendicular axis] j6 --> j7[J7
wrist_roll
forearm axis] j7 --> gripper[Gripper
End Effector] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define the Fetch robot and list its five major physical subsystems.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Fetch** is a ROS-native mobile manipulation research robot developed by Fetch Robotics. It integrates a wheeled base with a high-DOF manipulator arm and rich sensor suite, making it suitable for HRI and pick-and-place research. The five major subsystems are: 1. **Mobile Base** — differential-drive wheeled platform. 2. **Torso Lift** — single prismatic joint providing vertical reach extension. 3. **Robotic Arm** — 7-DOF revolute-joint serial manipulator. 4. **Gripper** — 1-DOF parallel-jaw end effector. 5. **Sensor Head** — pan-tilt head with 3D depth camera and 2D laser scanner. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing 4 subsystems, 1 mark for complete listing and ROS context.)* ### **Q2. Why does the Fetch arm use 7 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) instead of the classical 6 DOF required for full spatial positioning?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** A classical 6-DOF manipulator can place its end effector at any (x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw) pose in 3D space. However, in cluttered real-world environments (e.g., a shelf with surrounding objects), the arm may need to **avoid obstacles** or **avoid singular configurations** while still reaching the target. A **7th revolute DOF** introduces **kinematic redundancy** — infinitely many joint configurations map to the same end-effector pose, allowing the controller to choose a "self-motion" that maximizes clearance from obstacles and stays away from joint limits. The 7th DOF is the **upperarm_roll_joint**, which rotates the entire forearm assembly. *(1 mark for identifying redundancy, 1 mark for obstacle-avoidance justification, 1 mark for naming the specific joint.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) follow a **Module Internal Choice** pattern: a single question is posed with two alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) from the same module, and the student answers **one**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** Describe in detail the hardware architecture of the Fetch mobile manipulator robot. Explain the role of each subsystem and the ROS joint names associated with them. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### **Part (a) [7 Marks] — Mobile Base, Torso Lift, and Gripper** **Model Solution:** **1. Mobile Base (`base_link` / `base_footprint`):** The Fetch mobile base is a **differential-drive** platform with two independently controlled drive wheels and supporting passive casters. It provides planar (x, y, yaw) mobility for the entire robot. ROS joint: there is **no active revolute joint** at the base level — the base pose is published via `odom` → `base_link` transform. Wheel odometry, IMU fusion, and 2D laser scan feed into standard ROS navigation packages such as `amcl`, `move_base`, and `gmapping`. **2. Torso Lift (`torso_lift_joint`):** This is a **prismatic joint** that linearly extends the arm assembly upward. The stroke is **0.0 m to 0.835 m**. ROS joint name: `torso_lift_joint` (type `prismatic`). It is published in the URDF and broadcast by `robot_state_publisher`. The lift expands the vertical reach envelope of the arm without adding a complex revolute joint. **3. Gripper (`gripper_finger_joint`):** A 1-DOF parallel-jaw gripper with maximum aperture of approximately **0.09 m**. ROS joint name: `gripper_finger_joint` (type `revolute` in the URDF approximation, but mechanically prismatic). Current-sensing provides **grasp-slip feedback** to the controller. *(1 mark for each subsystem description, 1 mark for joint names, 1 mark for ROS integration.)* #### **Part (b) [7 Marks] — 7-DOF Arm and Sensor Head** **Model Solution:** **1. 7-DOF Robotic Arm (Seven Revolute Joints):** | # | ROS Joint Name | Kinematic Role | Axis Direction | |---|----------------|----------------|----------------| | 1 | `shoulder_pan_joint` | Yaw rotation at the shoulder | Vertical (Z) | | 2 | `shoulder_lift_joint` | Pitch up/down at the shoulder | Horizontal | | 3 | `upperarm_roll_joint` | Roll of upper arm about its own axis | Forearm axis | | 4 | `elbow_flex_joint` | Pitch at the elbow | Horizontal | | 5 | `forearm_roll_joint` | Roll of forearm | Forearm axis | | 6 | `wrist_flex_joint` | Pitch at the wrist | Horizontal | | 7 | `wrist_roll_joint` | Final roll of gripper | Forearm axis | The 7th DOF (typically the elbow-flex or shoulder-pan redundancy) provides kinematic redundancy. All seven joints are exposed via the `joint_states` topic and are controllable through `FollowJointTrajectoryAction` servers. **2. Sensor Head (Pan-Tilt with 3D Camera + 2D Laser):** The head assembly is mounted on a 2-DOF neck — `head_pan_joint` and `head_tilt_joint`. It carries: - A **Kinect One (or RealSense)** RGB-D camera publishing `sensor_msgs/Image` and `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2`. - A **SICK TiM510** or Hokuyo 2D laser publishing `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` over 270°. - A **9-DOF IMU** publishing `sensor_msgs/Imu` for orientation. The head can be aimed independently of the base motion, decoupling perception from navigation. *(1 mark for naming all 7 joints, 1 mark for axis directions, 1 mark for redundancy, 1 mark for sensor head hardware, 1 mark for sensor topics, 1 mark for pan-tilt decoupling, 1 mark for integration narrative.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks):** Explain the Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) parameterization of the Fetch 7-DOF arm. Derive the forward kinematics transformation for at least the first three links. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### **Part (a) [7 Marks] — DH Convention and Fetch Arm Parameter Table** **Model Solution:** The **Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) convention** provides a minimal, systematic way to represent the kinematic chain of a serial manipulator. Each link $i$ is described by exactly four parameters: $a_i$ (link length), $\alpha_i$ (link twist), $d_i$ (link offset), and $\theta_i$ (joint angle). For a revolute joint, $\theta_i$ is the variable; for a prismatic joint, $d_i$ is the variable. The homogeneous transformation from frame $i-1$ to frame $i$ is: $$ {}^{i-1}_{i}T \;=\; R_z(\theta_i)\, T_z(d_i)\, T_x(a_i)\, R_x(\alpha_i) $$ This expands to the $4 \times 4$ matrix shown in Section 3.1. **Fetch 7-DOF Arm DH Parameter Table:** | Link $i$ | $a_i$ (m) | $\alpha_i$ (rad) | $d_i$ (m) | Variable | |----------|-----------|------------------|-----------|----------| | 1 | 0 | $-\pi/2$ | 0 | $\theta_1$ | | 2 | 0 | $\pi/2$ | 0 | $\theta_2$ | | 3 | 0 | $-\pi/2$ | $d_3$ | $\theta_3$ | | 4 | $a_4$ | $\pi/2$ | 0 | $\theta_4$ | | 5 | 0 | $-\pi/2$ | $d_5$ | $\theta_5$ | | 6 | 0 | $\pi/2$ | 0 | $\theta_6$ | | 7 | 0 | 0 | $d_7$ | $\theta_7$ | The alternating $\pm\pi/2$ twist angles reflect the **orthogonal axis arrangement** of consecutive revolute joints — a common design pattern in 7-DOF human-arm-like manipulators (e.g., the Barrett WAM, the Fetch arm, the Kinova Jaco). *(1 mark for explaining DH purpose, 1 mark for the matrix, 1 mark for the full table, 1 mark for explaining the pattern of alpha alternation.)* #### **Part (b) [7 Marks] — Forward Kinematics of the First Three Links** **Model Solution:** The exhaustive step-by-step derivation is given in Section 3.3 of these notes. The student is expected to reproduce the following final composite transformation: $$ {}^{0}_{3}T \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c_1 c_2 c_3 - c_1 s_2 s_3 & s_1 & -c_1 c_2 s_3 - c_1 s_2 c_3 & 0 \\ s_1 c_2 c_3 - s_1 s_2 s_3 & -c_1 & -s_1 c_2 s_3 - s_1 s_2 c_3 & 0 \\ -s_2 c_3 - c_2 s_3 & 0 & s_2 s_3 - c_2 c_3 & d_3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ The orientation of the third link frame relative to the base is the upper-left $3 \times 3$ block, and the position of the third link's origin is the upper-right $3 \times 1$ column — note that the $z$-component of this position is exactly $d_3$, confirming that the third link's origin lies at a height $d_3$ above the base when the first two joints are at zero. **Generalization to all 7 links:** The full transformation is ${}^{0}_{7}T = \prod_{i=1}^{7} {}^{i-1}_{i}T$. The position of the gripper origin in the base frame is ${}^{0}_{7}\vec{p}$, and its orientation is the rotation matrix ${}^{0}_{7}R$. These are the quantities that the ROS `robot_state_publisher` computes and broadcasts on the `/tf` topic. *(1 mark for matrix product, 1 mark for ${}^{0}_{3}T$ formula, 1 mark for interpreting upper-left block, 1 mark for upper-right block, 1 mark for ${}^{0}_{7}T$ generalization, 1 mark for ROS tf link.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Fetch with Freight:** Many students write answers for the *Freight* (base-only) robot instead of the *Fetch* (full manipulator). Always read the question carefully — if it asks about the "arm" or "manipulator," it is the Fetch, not the Freight. > 2. **Joint name typos:** The Fetch has **seven distinct arm joints** with non-obvious names (`upperarm_roll_joint` and `forearm_roll_joint` are *roll* joints, not flex). Spelling them as `shoulder_lift_roll` etc. costs marks. > 3. **Forgetting the Torso Lift:** Forgetting the `torso_lift_joint` prismatic joint in the DH table or TF tree is a common -2 mark deduction. > 4. **Using the obsolete URDF:** Fetch URDF names may differ between ROS 1 and ROS 2 distributions. State your distribution if relevant. > 5. **Sign convention mistakes in DH:** Forgetting the negative sign on $\alpha_1 = -\pi/2$ is the #1 error in kinematic derivations. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Fetch Robot (Module 3)** - 🔹 **Fetch = Mobile Manipulator**; **Freight = Base Only** (always remember the distinction). - 🔹 **Five Subsystems:** Base, Torso Lift, 7-DOF Arm, Gripper, Sensor Head. - 🔹 **Drive Type:** Differential drive (not omnidirectional). - 🔹 **Torso Lift:** **1 prismatic joint**, stroke = **0.835 m**, joint name = `torso_lift_joint`. - 🔹 **Arm DOF:** **7 revolute joints** (provides kinematic redundancy). - 🔹 **Arm Reach:** **0.95 m** from shoulder. - 🔹 **Max Vertical Reach:** **0.835 + 0.95 = 1.785 m**. - 🔹 **7 Arm Joint Names (in order):** `shoulder_pan`, `shoulder_lift`, `upperarm_roll`, `elbow_flex`, `forearm_roll`, `wrist_flex`, `wrist_roll`. - 🔹 **Gripper:** 1-DOF parallel-jaw, aperture ≈ **0.09 m**. - 🔹 **Sensors:** Kinect One (RGB-D) + SICK/Hokuyo (2D laser, 270°) + 9-DOF IMU. - 🔹 **Head:** Pan-Tilt 2-DOF neck. - 🔹 **TF Tree Root:** `base_link` → `torso_lift_link` → 7 arm links → `gripper_link`; and `base_link` → `head_pan_link` → `head_tilt_link` → `kinect_link`. - 🔹 **ROS Native Versions:** Indigo, Kinetic, Melodic, Noetic (ROS 1) and Humble (ROS 2). - 🔹 **Why 7 DOF?** Kinematic redundancy for obstacle avoidance and singularity avoidance. - 🔹 **DH Convention:** Modified DH; $\alpha_i$ alternates $\pm\pi/2$ between consecutive joints. - 🔹 **Forward Kinematics Equation:** ${}^{0}_{7}T = \prod_{i=1}^{7} {}^{i-1}_{i}T$. - 🔹 **Manufacturer:** Originally Fetch Robotics (2014, San Jose); now part of **Zebra Technologies (2021)**. - 🔹 **Primary Application Domains:** Warehouse logistics, HRI research, grasping research, assistive robotics, SLAM benchmarking. > **End of Notes — Fetch (Module 3)**

topics.title: Robonaut 2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robonaut 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robonaut 2 (R2)** is a dexterous, anthropomorphic (humanoid) robot jointly developed by **NASA's Lyndon B. Johnson Space Center (JSC)** and **General Motors (GM)** through a Space Act Agreement signed in **2007**. It is the second-generation successor to the Robonaut 1 prototype, engineered explicitly to function as an "astronaut's assistant" both inside spacecraft (Intra-Vehicular Activity – IVA) and outside (Extra-Vehicular Activity – EVA).

Formally, in the KTU 2024 syllabus context, Robonaut 2 is presented as a **flagship case study of a complex humanoid robotic platform fully integrated with the Robot Operating System (ROS)**, demonstrating how a distributed ROS node graph can be used to coordinate dozens of degrees of freedom (DoF), force-torque sensing, vision pipelines, and dexterous manipulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Fact for Examiners:** R2 is the first humanoid robot in space, delivered to the International Space Station (ISS) via Space Shuttle *Discovery* on mission **STS-133 in February 2011**. It was reactivated on the ISS in **December 2019** with upgraded climbing legs ("Robonaut 2 Climbing Legs" – 2014 upgrade).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the ROS syllabus, Robonaut 2 is treated as a *real-world industrial-strength deployment* of ROS (originally called *ROS Industrial / NASA JSC's OROCOS-based control stack*) and illustrates the use of **Gazebo simulation**, **ros_control**, **TF (transform) trees**, and **message-passing** on a 42+ DoF platform.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you hire a **new junior apprentice** in a busy mechanical workshop. This apprentice:
- has the **same size hands and arms as a human adult** (so it can hold the same screwdrivers, wrenches and toggle switches),
- can **see** with stereo cameras (like wearing bifocal glasses),
- can **feel** the force it exerts on a bolt (like having pressure-sensing fingertips),
- can be **teleoperated** by an expert sitting in another room who wears a 3D headset and gloves, and
- runs on a **software "shift supervisor"** called ROS that hands tasks to the appropriate sub-system (camera, arm, finger, etc.) the moment they are needed.

That apprentice is **Robonaut 2**. The "shift supervisor" handing out tasks is **ROS**, and the apprentice's "rule book" is a set of controllers running on real-time Linux.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Performance Metrics

| Metric | Value (Stock R2) | Value (R2 with Climbing Legs) |
|---|---|---|
| Total Mass | **≈ 330 lbs (150 kg)** | ≈ 500 lbs (230 kg) |
| Degrees of Freedom (DoF) | **42 DoF** | 58 DoF |
| Head DoF | 4 (pan, tilt, roll, yaw) | 4 |
| Arm DoF | 7 per arm (14 total) | 7 per arm (14 total) |
| Hand DoF | 12 per hand (24 total) | 12 per hand (24 total) |
| Waist DoF | 3 | 3 |
| Leg DoF | 0 (climbs using arms + torso) | 5 per leg (10 total) |
| Finger Force Sensing | **> 2 lbf (≈ 9 N) per finger** | same |
| Vision System | Stereo colour cameras + depth | same |
| On-board Compute | Multiple **PowerPC** & Intel processors | upgraded with **Raspberry Pi / Intel NUC** payloads |
| Operating System Base | VxWorks + Linux (RFL: Robonaut Framework Layer) | same + ROS extensions |

> [!NOTE]
> In ROS terms, every joint is essentially a *position / velocity / effort controller* published via the `JointTrajectoryController` action interface, and the whole robot's pose is broadcast continuously on the `/tf` topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Robonaut 2's Design

### 2.1.1 Anthropomorphic Constraint
The defining design decision is **anthropomorphism** – the robot must match the human form factor. The "Why" is *task compatibility*: most spacecraft, tools, and EVA hardware are designed for human geometry. A wheeled rover cannot turn an IVA toggle; a humanoid arm can.

### 2.1.2 Force-Controlled (Not Position-Controlled) Manipulation
Human hands do not move like a CNC mill – they *yield* when they hit an object. R2's fingers and wrists use **series-elastic / impedance-controlled** actuators. The reference position $q_{ref}$ is the *desired* joint angle, but the **measured torque** $\tau_m$ is fed back so the joint behaves as a virtual spring-damper:

$$
\tau_m = K_p (q_{ref} - q) + K_d (\dot{q}_{ref} - \dot{q})
$$

This is the **"How"** behind R2's ability to grasp a soft object (e.g., a fabric handrail) without crushing it, while still being able to tighten a bolt firmly.

### 2.1.3 Telepresence Architecture
An operator wears a **3D stereo headset + haptic gloves**. Head pose maps to R2's head joints, hand pose maps to R2's fingers. The "How" is a low-latency **ROS topic pair**:
- `/r2/head/cmd` (operator → robot)
- `/r2/head/state` (robot → operator, includes camera images + joint states)

Latency budget is **< 100 ms** round-trip for stable teleoperation.

## 2.2 ROS Software Stack of Robonaut 2

R2's software was originally NASA-proprietary, but the project was later **migrated / bridged to ROS (Hydro/Indigo era)** for the open-source research community. The stack has four tiers:

| Tier | Component | ROS Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **T1 – Hardware Abstraction** | EtherCAT joint motor controllers (Elmo / Copley) | `hardware_interface` from `ros_control` |
| **T2 – Real-Time Control** | Orocos / RTT real-time loops @ 1 kHz | `controller_manager`, `effort_controllers/JointTrajectoryController` |
| **T3 – Behaviour & Planning** | MoveIt! motion planning, IK solvers (KDL, Trac-IK) | `move_group` node, `compute_ik` service |
| **T4 – Perception & Cognition** | OpenCV / PCL pipelines, gesture recognition | `image_transport`, `pcl_ros` |

## 2.3 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Robonaut 2 is not a heavily mathematical topic, but the following formulas appear in viva/ESE questions on ROS humanoid platforms.

| # | Concept | Equation / Term | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Joint Impedance | $\tau = K_p (q_r - q) + K_d (\dot{q}_r - \dot{q})$ | Virtual spring-damper torque |
| 2 | Forward Kinematics (Arm) | $T_{end}^{base} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i(q_i)$ | DH chain from shoulder to fingertip |
| 3 | Inverse Kinematics | $\mathbf{x}_{desired} = f(\mathbf{q}) \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{q} = f^{-1}(\mathbf{x})$ | Solved numerically by KDL/Trac-IK |
| 4 | TF Tree Update Rate | $f_{tf} \geq 100 \text{ Hz}$ | Required for smooth teleop |
| 5 | Control Loop Period | $T_c = 1 \text{ ms}$ (1 kHz real-time) | EtherCAT cycle time |
| 6 | Hand DoF | $n_{hand} = 5 \text{ fingers} \times 3 + 2 \text{ palm} = 17$ (per hand, in newer design) | Original R2 used 12 per hand |
| 7 | Total DoF | $\sum DoF = 42 \text{ (no legs)} \;\vert\; 58 \text{ (with legs)}$ | Memorise both numbers |
| 8 | Workspace Reach | $r_{reach} \approx 0.95 \text{ m per arm}$ | Shoulder to fingertip |
| 9 | Force Threshold | $F_{touch} \approx 1 \text{ N}$ | Tactile detection threshold |
| 10 | Hand Pose Vocabulary | $N_{poses} \approx 26$ (ASL alphabet subset) | For gesture recognition |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Space Operations** – IVA tool use, EVA pre-cursor tasks on ISS.
2. **Automotive Manufacturing** – GM uses R2-derived dexterity research to programme assembly-line robots that *co-work* with humans (cobots) without cages.
3. **Disaster Response** – R2's climbing legs allow traversing rubble-strewn terrain.
4. **Research Testbed** – Open-source R2 simulations in **Gazebo** allow universities to test new ROS algorithms on a high-DoF humanoid without owning the hardware.
5. **Medical Robotics** – Lessons on impedance control migrated to surgical robots such as the da Vinci Research Kit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — How a ROS Command Reaches an R2 Joint

This is the **core analytical trace** examiners love: *"Trace a ROS message from a MoveIt! plan to a single finger joint."*

### Step 1 — User / Operator Issues Goal
The operator defines a Cartesian target in the *base_link* frame (e.g., "grasp the toggle at coordinates $(0.4,\;0.1,\;0.3)$ m").

### Step 2 — MoveIt! Planning Request
MoveIt's `move_group` node receives a `moveit_msgs/MoveGroupGoal` action goal. It samples collision-free trajectories in the configuration space.

### Step 3 — IK Solving
For every sampled end-effector pose $\mathbf{x}_{desired} = (x,y,z,\phi,\theta,\psi)$, MoveIt calls the IK plugin (KDL or Trac-IK) to obtain joint vectors $\mathbf{q} = (q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n)$.

The numerical IK iteration is essentially a Jacobian pseudo-inverse loop:

$$
\Delta \mathbf{q} = J^{\dagger}(\mathbf{q}) \, \Delta \mathbf{x}
$$

where $J^{\dagger} = J^{T}(JJ^{T})^{-1}$ is the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse, and $\Delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}_{desired} - f(\mathbf{q})$ is the Cartesian error. The loop is:

$$
\mathbf{q}_{k+1} = \mathbf{q}_k + \alpha \, J^{\dagger}(\mathbf{q}_k)\, \Delta \mathbf{x}
$$

with step size $0 < \alpha \leq 1$.

### Step 4 — Trajectory Smoothing
A quintic polynomial spline is fitted between waypoints:

$$
q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5
$$

This guarantees continuous position, velocity, and acceleration — essential because R2's fingers cannot tolerate jerk spikes that would cause grasped objects to slip.

### Step 5 — Trajectory Message Published
MoveIt publishes a `trajectory_msgs/JointTrajectory` message on the topic:

$$
\texttt{/r2/right\_arm\_controller/command}
$$

The message contains:

- `joint_names` — e.g., `["r_shoulder_yaw","r_shoulder_pitch", ..., "r_wrist_roll"]`
- `points[]` — array of `{positions, velocities, accelerations, time_from_start}`

### Step 6 — ros_control Action Server
The `JointTrajectoryController` action server (a *ros_control* component) receives the message and **samples** it at the controller's update rate (typically 1 kHz).

### Step 7 — Impedance / PID Law
For each joint $i$ the controller computes the effort command using the impedance law from Section 2.1.2:

$$
\tau_i = K_{p,i}(q_{i,\text{ref}} - q_i) + K_{d,i}(\dot{q}_{i,\text{ref}} - \dot{q}_i)
$$

### Step 8 — EtherCAT Bus to Motor Driver
The effort $\tau_i$ is sent over EtherCAT to the Elmo/Copley servo drive, which closes a current loop on the brushless DC motor. The cycle time is $T_c = 1 \text{ ms}$.

### Step 9 — Sensor Feedback Returns
The motor encoder reports $q_i$ and $\dot{q}_i$; the joint torque sensor reports $\tau_m$. These flow back up the stack and are also broadcast on:

- `/joint_states` (TF-compatible pose for every joint)
- `/tf` (full kinematic tree)

### Step 10 — Visualisation & Teleop Loop
RViz displays the live robot; the teleop operator's HMD receives updated stereo video at ≥ 30 Hz, completing the closed loop.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — R2 Specification vs. KTU Learning Outcomes

| KTU Course Outcome (CO) | R2 Topic Coverage | Mapping Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| CO1 – Understand ROS architecture | 4-tier ROS stack, nodes, topics, services | Table 2.2 |
| CO2 – Apply ros_control on real hardware | `controller_manager`, EtherCAT mapping | Steps 6–8 above |
| CO3 – Use TF & URDF for kinematics | 42-DoF URDF, TF tree at 100 Hz | Step 9, formula 4 |
| CO4 – Integrate perception with control | Stereo vision + force feedback fusion | T4 of stack |
| CO5 – Simulate in Gazebo | R2 models in `nasa_r2_sim` package | Mentioned below |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — IK Step on a 2-Link Planar Arm Analogue

To make the pseudo-inverse loop concrete, consider a *simplified* 2-D analogue of an R2 arm (shoulder–elbow) of length $L_1 = L_2 = 0.35$ m. Target Cartesian point $\mathbf{x}^* = (0.5, \; 0.2)$ m.

The forward kinematics are:

$$
x = L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1 \sin q_1 + L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

The Jacobian is:

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \\ L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) & L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

Starting from $q_1^{(0)} = 0.4$ rad, $q_2^{(0)} = 0.8$ rad, with step $\alpha = 0.5$:

1. Compute $f(\mathbf{q}^{(0)})$:

   $x = 0.35 \cos(0.4) + 0.35 \cos(1.2) = 0.323 + 0.124 = 0.447$ m
   $y = 0.35 \sin(0.4) + 0.35 \sin(1.2) = 0.136 + 0.326 = 0.462$ m

2. Cartesian error $\Delta \mathbf{x} = (0.5 - 0.447,\; 0.2 - 0.462) = (0.053,\; -0.262)$ m.

3. Evaluate $J$ and compute $J^{\dagger} = J^{T}(JJ^{T})^{-1}$.

4. Update $\mathbf{q}^{(1)} = \mathbf{q}^{(0)} + \alpha J^{\dagger} \Delta \mathbf{x}$.

5. Iterate until $\vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \vert < 10^{-4}$ m — typically 5–10 iterations for 2-D.

> [!NOTE]
> R2's real arms have **7 DoF** which gives a 1-DOF self-motion manifold (redundancy) — the null-space projection term $\lambda (I - J^{\dagger} J)\mathbf{q}_0$ is added to the update to keep the elbow away from singularities.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Robonaut 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OP[Operator with HMD and Haptic Gloves] -->|head pose, hand pose| TELEM[Teleop ROS Topic Bridge]
    TELEM -->|std msgs and sensor msgs| MG[MoveIt move_group Node]
    MG -->|IK request via compute_ik service| IK[Trac IK Solver]
    IK -->|joint vector q| MG
    MG -->|trajectory msgs JointTrajectory| JTC[Joint Trajectory Controller]
    JTC -->|impedance law tau equals Kp error plus Kd derivative| ECAT[EtherCAT Master 1 kHz]
    ECAT -->|PWM and current setpoint| DRV[Elmo Servo Drives]
    DRV -->|three phase current| MTR[Brushless DC Joint Motors]
    MTR -->|encoder feedback q dot q| DRV
    DRV -->|torque sensor reading tau| ECAT
    ECAT -->|joint state and tf| JS[/joint_states topic]
    ECAT -->|tf transforms| TF[/tf topic]
    JS --> MG
    TF --> RVIZ[RViz Visualization]
    TF --> OP
    CAM[Stereo Cameras in Head] -->|image transport| PERC[Perception Node OpenCV PCL]
    PERC -->|detected objects and poses| MG
    FTS[Finger Tactile Sensors] -->|force array msg| JTC
```

> [!NOTE]
> Read this diagram **bottom-up for sensing** and **top-down for actuation**. The two streams meet at the `JointTrajectoryController`, which is the **single point of synchronisation** between MoveIt's 50–100 Hz planning world and EtherCAT's 1 kHz real-time world.

## 4.2 Robonaut 2 — ROS TF Tree (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BASE[base_link] --> PEL[pelvis]
    PEL --> TOR[torso]
    TOR --> CLAV_L[clavicle_left]
    TOR --> CLAV_R[clavicle_right]
    TOR --> NECK[neck]
    CLAV_L --> SHL[shoulder_left]
    CLAV_R --> SHR[shoulder_right]
    SHL --> UPA_L[upperarm_left]
    SHR --> UPA_R[upperarm_right]
    UPA_L --> FOA_L[forearm_left]
    UPA_R --> FOA_R[forearm_right]
    FOA_L --> HND_L[hand_left]
    FOA_R --> HND_R[hand_right]
    HND_L --> F1L[finger_1_left]
    HND_L --> F2L[finger_2_left]
    HND_L --> F3L[finger_3_left]
    HND_L --> F4L[finger_4_left]
    HND_L --> F5L[finger_5_left thumb]
    HND_R --> F1R[finger_1_right]
    HND_R --> F2R[finger_2_right]
    HND_R --> F3R[finger_3_right]
    HND_R --> F4R[finger_4_right]
    HND_R --> F5R[finger_5_right thumb]
    NECK --> HEAD[head]
    HEAD --> L_CAM[left_camera_frame]
    HEAD --> R_CAM[right_camera_frame]
    HEAD --> IMU[imu_link]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `tf` tree is published at 100 Hz by a dedicated broadcaster node. In R2's URDF, every joint has explicit `<limit>`, `<inertial>`, and `<visual>` tags so that Gazebo can load the model directly.

## 4.3 Upgrade Evolution Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Robonaut 1 1997 to 2006 Research] -->|lessons learned| R2A[Robonaut 2 Torso and Arms 2011 ISS]
    R2A -->|2014 upgrade| R2B[Robonaut 2 with Climbing Legs 58 DoF]
    R2B -->|2019 reactivation| R2C[ISS Robonaut 2 Operational Phase]
    R2C -->|future| R2D[Upper Body Mobility Platform for Lunar Gateway]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
**List the four tiers of Robonaut 2's software architecture and identify the ROS package that implements each tier.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Hardware Abstraction Tier** — Implemented by `ros_control`'s `hardware_interface` and EtherCAT drivers (Elmo / Copley). *(1 mark)*
2. **Real-Time Control Tier** — Implemented by `controller_manager` running `JointTrajectoryController` and `effort_controllers` on a real-time Linux (Xenomai / PREEMPT-RT) kernel at 1 kHz. *(1 mark)*
3. **Behaviour & Planning Tier** — Implemented by MoveIt! (`move_group`, `moveit_core`) for motion planning, IK, and collision checking. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Perception & Cognition Tier** — Implemented by `image_transport`, `pcl_ros`, OpenCV pipelines for stereo vision, gesture and object recognition. *(0.5 mark)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**Why does Robonaut 2 use impedance control instead of pure position control for its fingers? Justify with one engineering reason.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pure position control assumes the *environment will yield* to the joint command, which causes large internal forces when the finger contacts an object. Impedance control models the joint as a virtual spring-damper, so the *robot yields* to the environment instead. **Engineering reason:** It allows R2 to grasp both rigid tools (bolts, wrenches) and compliant objects (fabric handrails, soft cables on ISS) without damaging either, while remaining stable under contact — a hard requirement for IVA tasks where the robot shares the workspace with delicate crew equipment. *(3 marks — 1 for concept, 1 for equation $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot e$, 1 for engineering reason)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — ROS Software Architecture Focus

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, describe the **four-tier ROS software architecture of Robonaut 2**, clearly explaining the role of *ros_control*, *MoveIt!*, and the *tf* system. List any two ROS topics used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Trace a single Cartesian "grasp" command from the operator's haptic glove to the finger motor current, identifying **at least five ROS message / topic / service interactions** along the way. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

- (1 mark) Introduction: Robonaut 2 is a NASA/GM humanoid robot whose software is structured into four tiers: Hardware Abstraction, Real-Time Control, Behaviour & Planning, and Perception & Cognition.
- (2 marks) T1 & T2: *ros_control* provides the *hardware_interface* base class that abstracts the Elmo/Copley EtherCAT drives. The *controller_manager* loads *JointTrajectoryController* plugins, and the impedance law $\tau = K_p(q_r - q) + K_d(\dot{q}_r - \dot{q})$ is enforced at 1 kHz.
- (2 marks) T3: MoveIt!'s *move_group* node subscribes to the robot description on `/robot_description`, plans a collision-free path using OMPL, solves IK via KDL/Trac-IK, and publishes the result as a `trajectory_msgs/JointTrajectory`.
- (1 mark) T4: The *tf* system maintains a kinematic tree (`base_link → torso → shoulder → … → fingertip`) broadcast on `/tf` and `/tf_static` at 100 Hz.
- (1 mark) Two ROS topics: `/r2/<arm>/joint_trajectory_controller/command`, `/joint_states`, `/tf`, `/r2/head/camera/left/image_raw`. Any two valid topics earn the mark.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

- (1 mark) Step 1 — Haptic glove → Teleop node publishes `geometry_msgs/Pose` on `/r2/teleop/right_hand/cmd`.
- (1 mark) Step 2 — MoveIt! receives the Cartesian target via a `MoveGroupGoal` action.
- (1 mark) Step 3 — IK solver computes joint vector $\mathbf{q} = (q_1, \dots, q_n)$ (5–10 iterations of the Jacobian pseudo-inverse).
- (1 mark) Step 4 — Smoothed trajectory published on `/r2/right_arm_controller/command`.
- (1 mark) Step 5 — `JointTrajectoryController` samples and computes $\tau$ every 1 ms.
- (1 mark) Step 6 — Effort sent over EtherCAT; encoder and torque feedback come back on `/joint_states` and a custom `/r2/torque_sensors` topic.
- (1 mark) Step 7 — Diagram drawn or final summary of the closed loop with explicit topic names.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Hardware & Kinematics Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** Explain the **kinematic structure** of Robonaut 2's arm. State the total degrees of freedom, the joint layout (shoulder, elbow, wrist), and the practical significance of having 7 DoF per arm. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **2-link planar Jacobian** for a simplified R2 arm analogue and use it to perform **one iteration** of the resolved-rate IK update for a target point $\mathbf{x}^* = (0.5, 0.2)$ m, starting from $\mathbf{q}^{(0)} = (0.4,\; 0.8)$ rad and step $\alpha = 0.5$. Take $L_1 = L_2 = 0.35$ m. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

- (2 marks) DoF count: R2 (no legs) = 4 (head) + 14 (arms) + 24 (hands) + 3 (waist) = **42 DoF**; with legs = 58 DoF.
- (2 marks) Arm joint layout: shoulder yaw, shoulder pitch, shoulder roll, upper-arm roll, elbow, forearm roll, wrist pitch + roll = 7 DoF. Show a labelled joint sequence: `S_yaw → S_pitch → S_roll → UA_roll → E → FA_roll → W_pitch → W_roll`.
- (2 marks) Significance of 7 DoF: One DoF more than the 6 needed to specify 3D position + 3D orientation, giving a **redundant** arm. The extra DoF can be used to (i) avoid joint limits and singularities, (ii) optimise manipulability, (iii) keep the elbow clear of the workspace — a key requirement for cluttered ISS panels.
- (1 mark) Practical note: redundancy resolution uses the null-space projection $J^{\dagger} = J^{T}(JJ^{T})^{-1}$ in the IK update.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

- (2 marks) Forward kinematics (from Section 3.3):

   $x = L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)$
   $y = L_1 \sin q_1 + L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2)$

- (1 mark) Jacobian derivation (partial derivatives of $x$ and $y$ w.r.t. $q_1, q_2$):

   $J = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \\ L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) & L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$

- (1 mark) Numerical substitution at $q_1=0.4$, $q_2=0.8$ rad:

   $J \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.35(0.389) - 0.35(0.932) & -0.35(0.932) \\ 0.35(0.921) + 0.35(0.362) & 0.35(0.362) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.462 & -0.326 \\ 0.449 & 0.127 \end{bmatrix}$

- (1 mark) Forward kinematics at start: $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (0.447,\; 0.462)$ m (carried from Section 3.3).

- (1 mark) Error $\Delta \mathbf{x} = (0.5 - 0.447,\; 0.2 - 0.462) = (0.053,\; -0.262)$ m.

- (1 mark) Compute $J^{\dagger} = J^{T}(JJ^{T})^{-1}$ and apply $\mathbf{q}^{(1)} = \mathbf{q}^{(0)} + \alpha J^{\dagger} \Delta \mathbf{x}$. Numerically:

   $JJ^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.319 & -0.249 \\ -0.249 & 0.218 \end{bmatrix}$, $\det = 0.319 \times 0.218 - (-0.249)^2 \approx 0.0074$.
   $J^{\dagger} = \frac{1}{0.0074}\begin{bmatrix} 0.218 & 0.249 \\ 0.249 & 0.319 \end{bmatrix} J^{T} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -1.51 & 14.0 \\ 6.0 & 5.0 \end{bmatrix}$ (approx, exact value requires calculator).
   $J^{\dagger}\Delta \mathbf{x} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -3.75 \\ 0.23 \end{bmatrix}$.
   $\mathbf{q}^{(1)} = (0.4,\; 0.8) + 0.5 \times (-3.75,\; 0.23) = (-1.475,\; 0.915)$ rad.

   The large first-component jump indicates a configuration where a smaller step or damped least-squares variant would be preferred — a good observation for the viva.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often confuse **degrees of freedom (DoF)** with the **number of joints** — they are not always equal. In R2 each joint is revolute, so they happen to match, but in general DoF = (joint freedoms) − (constraints). Lose **0.5 mark** for sloppy phrasing.
> 2. Do **not** say "R2 has 42 joints". Say "R2 has 42 degrees of freedom spread across head (4), arms (14), hands (24), waist (3)". Examiners dock marks for wrong totals.
> 3. In the impedance equation, the symbol $q$ refers to the **measured** joint angle. Mixing up $q_{ref}$ (reference) and $q$ (actual) costs 1 full mark.
> 4. Many students forget to mention that MoveIt! uses an **action** interface (`MoveGroupAction`), not a simple topic. This is a frequent 1-mark deduction in the 7-marker sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Robonaut 2 (R2)** is a NASA + GM humanoid robot, first humanoid in space (ISS, **2011**).
- **42 DoF** without legs, **58 DoF** with the 2014 climbing-leg upgrade.
- **7 DoF per arm** — redundant, used for singularity / obstacle avoidance.
- **Head DoF: 4** (pan, tilt, roll, yaw). **Hands: 12 DoF each** in the original R2 design.
- **Software is structured in 4 ROS-style tiers:** Hardware Abstraction → Real-Time Control → Behaviour & Planning → Perception & Cognition.
- The real-time loop runs at **1 kHz** (1 ms period) over **EtherCAT** to Elmo / Copley servo drives.
- **ros_control** components: `controller_manager`, `JointTrajectoryController`, `effort_controllers`.
- **MoveIt!** is used for motion planning and IK (KDL / Trac-IK).
- **Impedance control law:** $\tau = K_p(q_r - q) + K_d(\dot{q}_r - \dot{q})$ — gives safe, compliant grasping.
- **TF tree** is published at ≥ 100 Hz; R2's URDF has 42–58 links and identical joint count.
- **Gazebo simulation** of R2 is available as the `nasa_r2_sim` package for ROS Hydro/Indigo and later.
- **Telepresence** uses a stereo HMD + haptic gloves with < 100 ms round-trip latency.
- R2 has stereo colour cameras, IMU, joint encoders, **joint torque sensors**, and **finger tactile arrays** — the multi-modal sensor suite that makes impedance control possible.
- Always quote exact numbers in the exam: **42 DoF, 150 kg, 1 kHz EtherCAT, 7 DoF per arm, 12 DoF per hand, 4 head DoF, 3 waist DoF**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TurtleBot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TurtleBot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **TurtleBot** is the official reference mobile robot platform of the **Robot Operating System (ROS / ROS 2)** ecosystem, developed originally by **Willow Garage** and currently maintained by **Open Robotics** in collaboration with industrial partners (Clearpath Robotics for TurtleBot 3 and 4). It is a **low-cost, open-source, modular, programmable autonomous mobile robot** designed specifically for education, research, prototyping service-robot applications, and benchmarking SLAM/Navigation algorithms.

In the KTU 2024 OBE framework, TurtleBot is treated as the **canonical physical instantiation** of the abstract ROS architecture covered in Module 2 — every ROS concept (nodes, topics, services, actions, tf, URDF, launch files) is mapped directly to a running subsystem on the TurtleBot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The KTU syllabus positions TurtleBot as the *integration test-bed* on which concepts like **URDF (Unified Robot Description Format)**, **tf transforms**, **Navigation stack (Nav2 / move_base)**, **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)**, and **teleoperation** are demonstrated end-to-end.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine **TurtleBot as a "smart, programmable pet dog on wheels"** 🐕🤖:

| Human Pet Dog 🐶 | TurtleBot 🤖 |
|------------------|----------------|
| Has 2/4 legs to walk | Has 2 driven wheels + 1 caster to move |
| Eyes (vision) | RGB-D Camera / LiDAR |
| Ears (hearing) | Microphone array |
| Brain | Onboard SBC (Raspberry Pi / Jetson) |
| Instincts (reflexes) | ROS driver nodes running real-time |
| Trained via commands | Programmed via ROS topics/services |
| Walks in a house | Navigates a 2D occupancy grid map |

Just as you teach a dog by giving it treats and commands, you "teach" TurtleBot by publishing messages to ROS topics, calling services, and sending goals to the action server. The dog perceives its environment with senses; TurtleBot perceives via sensors that publish on ROS topics.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line intuition:** *TurtleBot = A ROS-native wheeled robot that turns every abstract ROS concept (topic, service, tf, URDF, Nav stack) into a physically observable action in the real world.*

---

## 1.3 Generations of TurtleBot — Evolution Timeline

The TurtleBot lineage is critical because every generation introduced architectural lessons that mirror advances in ROS itself.

### 1.3.1 TurtleBot 1 (2011) — *The Pioneer*
- **Base:** iRobot Create (a Roomba stripped of its vacuum).
- **Sensor:** Microsoft Kinect (RGB-D camera).
- **Compute:** ASUS Eee PC netbook running Ubuntu + ROS Fuerte.
- **Role:** The *first* reference platform proving that ROS could leave the simulation world.

### 1.3.2 TurtleBot 2 (2013) — *The Workhorse*
- **Base:** Kobuki (Yujin Robot) — robust, with bumpers, cliff sensors, gyroscope.
- **Sensor:** Microsoft Kinect / ASUS Xtion (RGB-D).
- **Compute:** Laptop-class x86 onboard.
- **Role:** Became the *de-facto* ROS teaching platform worldwide.

### 1.3.3 TurtleBot 3 (2017) — *The Burger Era*
- **Bases:** Burger, Waffle, Waffle Pi (modular, 3D-printable, small form factor).
- **Sensors:** 360° LiDAR (LDS-01 / LDS-02) + Intel RealSense / Camera.
- **Compute:** Raspberry Pi 3/4 (or Intel Joule / Jetson).
- **Role:** Affordable, modular platform — fully migrated to **ROS 2** (Melodic/Noetic → Foxy/Humble).
- **Open-source firmware (OpenCR)** — Arduino-compatible control board.

### 1.3.4 TurtleBot 4 (2022) — *The Pro-Grade Platform*
- **Base:** iRobot Create 3 (a re-engineered Roomba base).
- **Sensors:** OAK-D Pro (RGB-D + AI inference) + 2D LiDAR (RPLIDAR A1).
- **Compute:** Raspberry Pi 4 (Standard) / Jetson Orin (Pro).
- **OS:** Native **ROS 2 Humble / Iron / Jazzy**.
- **Role:** Industry-ready research platform with Nav2 stack pre-integrated.

---

## 1.4 Key Metrics & Standard Specifications (TurtleBot 3 Burger — Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> These specifications are **examination-high-yield** — the KTU paper frequently asks for numerical comparison or hardware-mapping questions.

| Parameter | Value (TB3 Burger) | Units |
|------------|:-------------------:|:-------|
| Maximum translational velocity | **0.22 m/s** | m·s⁻¹ |
| Maximum rotational velocity | **2.75 rad/s** | rad·s⁻¹ |
| Wheel radius | **0.033 m** | m |
| Wheel separation (L) | **0.16 m** | m |
| Payload (max) | **15 kg** | kg |
| Operating time | **≈ 2.5 h** (battery) | h |
| LiDAR scan rate | **5 Hz** (300 samples/scan) | Hz |
| Degrees of Freedom (DOF) | **2 (planar)** + passive caster | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hardware Architecture — Layered View

The TurtleBot hardware can be decoupled into **five logical layers**, each mapped to ROS abstraction layers:

### Layer 1 — Mobile Base (Differential Drive)
- Two independently driven wheels + 1 (or 2) passive caster(s).
- **Differential-drive kinematics** is the foundational motion model.
- Wheel encoders provide **odometry** feedback.

### Layer 2 — Sensor Suite
- **2D LiDAR** → publishes `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` on `/scan`.
- **RGB-D Camera** → publishes `sensor_msgs/Image`, `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2`, `sensor_msgs/CameraInfo`.
- **IMU** (on TurtleBot 3 Burger: integrated in OpenCR) → publishes `sensor_msgs/Imu`.
- **Wheel encoders** → integrated into base odometry.

### Layer 3 — Compute Board
- SBC running Ubuntu + ROS 2 (e.g., Raspberry Pi 4 on TB3 Burger).
- Runs all ROS 2 nodes: driver nodes, localization, navigation, perception.

### Layer 4 — Communication Bus
- USB (camera, LiDAR) + Serial/UART (OpenCR board) + Wi-Fi/Ethernet (ROS 2 DDS).

### Layer 5 — Software Stack (ROS 2)
- `turtlebot3_node` (driver), `robot_state_publisher`, `cartographer` / `slam_toolbox`, `nav2_*`.

---

## 2.2 Software Architecture — ROS 2 Node Map

The TurtleBot software stack is a **canonical ROS 2 application**:

1. **`turtlebot3_node`** → core driver node.
   - Subscribes: `/cmd_vel` (geometry_msgs/Twist)
   - Publishes: `/odom`, `/imu`, `/joint_states`, `/battery_state`, `/sensor_state`.
2. **`robot_state_publisher`** → broadcasts the **TF tree** from URDF + joint_states.
3. **`ld08_driver` (or `rplidar_ros2`)** → publishes `LaserScan` to `/scan`.
4. **`depthimage_to_laserscan`** → converts depth image (from RealSense) to virtual 2D scan.
5. **SLAM node** (`slam_toolbox` / `cartographer`) → builds `/map` from `/scan` + `/odom` + `/tf`.
6. **Navigation2 stack** → consumes `/map`, `/scan`, `/odom`, `/tf` and produces `/cmd_vel`.
7. **RViz2** → visualises TF, map, scan, robot model, paths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Every ROS 2 architectural concept (topic, service, action, parameter, tf) has a *literal* implementation running on the TurtleBot. This is why TurtleBot is the syllabus' integration vehicle.

---

## 2.3 Differential Drive Kinematics — The Core Theory

A TurtleBot is a **non-holonomic, differentially driven** mobile robot. Its pose in the world frame is:

$$ x = (x, y, \theta)^T $$

where $\theta$ is the heading angle measured counter-clockwise from the +X axis.

Let:
- $r$ = wheel radius (m)
- $L$ = distance between the two wheel contact points (m), often called *wheelbase*.
- $\omega_L, \omega_R$ = angular velocities of the left and right wheels (rad·s⁻¹).
- $v_L = r\omega_L$, $v_R = r\omega_R$ = linear velocities of left/right wheels at contact point (m·s⁻¹).

### 2.3.1 Forward Kinematics (Compute body twist from wheel velocities)

$$ v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} $$

$$ \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} $$

These are the **body-frame linear and angular velocities** of the robot center.

### 2.3.2 World-Frame Twist (Pose Update / Discrete Integration)

Using the unicycle model:

$$ \dot{x} = v \cos(\theta) $$
$$ \dot{y} = v \sin(\theta) $$
$$ \dot{\theta} = \omega $$

Discretised with timestep $dt$ (Euler integration — the way real odometry works on TurtleBot):

$$ x_{t+1} = x_t + v_t \cos(\theta_t) \, dt $$
$$ y_{t+1} = y_t + v_t \sin(\theta_t) \, dt $$
$$ \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \omega_t \, dt $$

### 2.3.3 Inverse Kinematics (Compute wheel speeds from desired body twist)

Given desired $v$ and $\omega$:

$$ v_L = v - \frac{\omega L}{2} $$
$$ v_R = v + \frac{\omega L}{2} $$

Or in terms of wheel angular velocities:

$$ \omega_L = \frac{1}{r}\left(v - \frac{\omega L}{2}\right) $$
$$ \omega_R = \frac{1}{r}\left(v + \frac{\omega L}{2}\right) $$

### 2.3.4 Odometry from Wheel Encoders

Wheel encoders give $\Delta \phi_L$ and $\Delta \phi_R$ (radians rotated per timestep).

$$ \Delta s = \frac{r(\Delta\phi_R + \Delta\phi_L)}{2} \quad \text{(arc length travelled)} $$
$$ \Delta\theta = \frac{r(\Delta\phi_R - \Delta\phi_L)}{L} \quad \text{(heading change)} $$

These deltas are then added to the pose. **This is exactly what the `turtlebot3_node` does internally before publishing `/odom`.**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Differential-Drive Model

- **Industrial AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** Almost all warehouse robots use differential drive (e.g., Amazon Kiva).
- **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance):** Use rocker-bogie differential steering — same kinematic equations.
- **Vacuum-cleaning robots (Roomba):** Differential drive + SLAM → TurtleBot 4 literally uses a Roomba base.
- **Disability-assistive wheelchairs:** Differential drive + Nav2 stack → direct research translation.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise this table — KTU 14-mark questions often test these equations numerically.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | Body linear velocity | $v = \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$ | m·s⁻¹ |
| 2 | Body angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$ | rad·s⁻¹ |
| 3 | Inverse: left wheel speed | $v_L = v - \dfrac{\omega L}{2}$ | m·s⁻¹ |
| 4 | Inverse: right wheel speed | $v_R = v + \dfrac{\omega L}{2}$ | m·s⁻¹ |
| 5 | Pose update (x) | $x_{t+1} = x_t + v \cos\theta \, dt$ | m |
| 6 | Pose update (y) | $y_{t+1} = y_t + v \sin\theta \, dt$ | m |
| 7 | Pose update (heading) | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \omega \, dt$ | rad |
| 8 | Arc from encoders | $\Delta s = \dfrac{r(\Delta\phi_R + \Delta\phi_L)}{2}$ | m |
| 9 | Heading change from encoders | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{r(\Delta\phi_R - \Delta\phi_L)}{L}$ | rad |
| 10 | Turning radius (non-zero $\omega$) | $R = \dfrac{v}{\omega} = \dfrac{L(v_R+v_L)}{2(v_R-v_L)}$ | m |

---

## 2.6 Critical Constraint — Non-Holonomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A differential-drive TurtleBot **cannot translate sideways** instantaneously — this is the **non-holonomic constraint**: $\dot{y}\cos\theta - \dot{x}\sin\theta = 0$.
> Consequence: planning smooth paths requires the **Dubins / Reeds-Shepp curves** that Nav2 uses internally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Kinematics from Scratch

We start from the geometry of the two-wheel system. Each wheel touches the ground at distance $L/2$ from the robot's center on either side along the lateral axis.

### Step 1 — Define per-wheel ground-contact linear velocities

The left wheel contact point moves at speed $v_L$, the right at $v_R$, both tangential to their respective wheels and pointing in the robot's forward direction.

$$ v_L = r \omega_L, \quad v_R = r \omega_R $$

**Logic:** Linear speed = radius × angular speed (rolling without slipping assumption).

### Step 2 — Linear velocity of the robot's center (average)

By symmetry, the body's center moves at the arithmetic mean of the two wheel speeds.

$$ v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} $$

**Logic:** Two wheels straddling the center contribute equally; averaging gives the central body's forward speed.

### Step 3 — Angular velocity about the robot's center

The robot rotates about an instantaneous center of rotation (ICR). The two wheels lie on a circle of radius $R$ (turning radius). For each wheel:

$$ \omega = \frac{v_R}{R + L/2} = \frac{v_L}{R - L/2} $$

Solving for $\omega$ by eliminating $R$:

$$ \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} $$

Substituting $v_R = r\omega_R$ and $v_L = r\omega_L$:

$$ \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} $$

**Logic:** The difference in wheel speeds produces rotation; the greater the difference, the tighter the turn.

### Step 4 — Pose update in world frame

Using the world-frame unicycle model:

$$ \dot{x} = v \cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v \sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega $$

**Logic:** Velocity must be projected onto world axes using the heading $\theta$.

### Step 5 — Discrete-time integration (Euler method)

For a fixed timestep $dt$ (typical ROS update rate is 20–50 Hz):

$$ x_{t+1} = x_t + v \cos(\theta_t) \, dt $$
$$ y_{t+1} = y_t + v \sin(\theta_t) \, dt $$
$$ \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \omega \, dt $$

**Logic:** Standard numerical Euler integration — sufficient for slow-moving TurtleBot at $v \le 0.22$ m/s.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inverse Kinematics (Body Twist → Wheel Speeds)

### Step 1 — Write forward kinematic equations as a 2×2 linear system

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_L + v_R &= 2v \\
v_R - v_L &= \omega L
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $v_L$ and $v_R$

Adding the two equations:

$$ 2v_R = 2v + \omega L \;\Rightarrow\; v_R = v + \frac{\omega L}{2} $$

Subtracting (second from first, after rearrangement):

$$ 2v_L = 2v - \omega L \;\Rightarrow\; v_L = v - \frac{\omega L}{2} $$

### Step 3 — Convert to wheel angular velocities

$$ \omega_L = \frac{v_L}{r} = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(v - \frac{\omega L}{2}\right) $$
$$ \omega_R = \frac{v_R}{r} = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(v + \frac{\omega L}{2}\right) $$

**Logic:** The Nav2 stack outputs $(v,\omega)$ to `/cmd_vel`; the TurtleBot driver node executes exactly this inverse-kinematics conversion before commanding the wheel motors.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Odometry from Wheel Encoder Readings

Wheel encoders give **incremental ticks**, which are converted to wheel angle increments $\Delta\phi_L$ and $\Delta\phi_R$ using the encoder resolution (e.g., TurtleBot 3 Burger uses 4096 ticks/rev).

### Step 1 — Convert wheel angle increments to arc-length increments

$$ \Delta s_L = r \, \Delta\phi_L, \quad \Delta s_R = r \, \Delta\phi_R $$

**Logic:** Arc length = radius × angle (in radians).

### Step 2 — Compute body displacement and rotation

$$ \Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2} = \frac{r(\Delta\phi_R + \Delta\phi_L)}{2} $$
$$ \Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{L} = \frac{r(\Delta\phi_R - \Delta\phi_L)}{L} $$

**Logic:** Same averaging / differencing logic as forward kinematics, applied to small finite increments.

### Step 3 — Update pose estimate

$$ x_{t+1} = x_t + \Delta s \, \cos(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}) $$
$$ y_{t+1} = y_t + \Delta s \, \sin(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}) $$
$$ \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta\theta $$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the **mid-point heading correction** $(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2)$ in the cos/sin — this is the standard **exact odometry update** used by `ros2_control` and the `diff_drive_controller`. KTU examiners appreciate seeing this detail over plain Euler.

**Logic:** Because $\theta$ changes during the timestep, we evaluate at the midpoint to reduce integration error.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A TurtleBot 3 Burger ($r = 0.033$ m, $L = 0.16$ m) receives a `/cmd_vel` of $v = 0.2$ m/s, $\omega = 1.0$ rad/s. Compute the required wheel angular velocities and the turning radius.

### Step 1 — Inverse kinematics

$$ v_L = v - \frac{\omega L}{2} = 0.2 - \frac{1.0 \times 0.16}{2} = 0.2 - 0.08 = 0.12 \text{ m/s} $$
$$ v_R = v + \frac{\omega L}{2} = 0.2 + 0.08 = 0.28 \text{ m/s} $$

### Step 2 — Wheel angular velocities

$$ \omega_L = \frac{v_L}{r} = \frac{0.12}{0.033} \approx 3.64 \text{ rad/s} $$
$$ \omega_R = \frac{v_R}{r} = \frac{0.28}{0.033} \approx 8.48 \text{ rad/s} $$

### Step 3 — Turning radius

$$ R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.2}{1.0} = 0.20 \text{ m} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A small turning radius of 20 cm indicates the robot is executing a tight right turn — this exceeds the max rotational velocity (2.75 rad/s) of TB3 Burger? No — 1.0 rad/s is within limits. ✓

### Step 4 — Pose update over $dt = 0.1$ s

$$ \Delta x = v\cos\theta \, dt = 0.2 \times \cos(0) \times 0.1 = 0.020 \text{ m} $$
$$ \Delta y = v\sin\theta \, dt = 0.2 \times 0 \times 0.1 = 0 \text{ m} $$
$$ \Delta\theta = \omega \, dt = 1.0 \times 0.1 = 0.1 \text{ rad} \approx 5.73° $$

**Logic:** Each timestep, the robot advances 2 cm and turns 5.73°.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — TurtleBot Generations

| Feature | TB1 | TB2 | TB3 Burger | TB3 Waffle Pi | TB4 Standard |
|---------|-----|-----|------------|----------------|---------------|
| Mobile Base | iRobot Create | Kobuki | TB3 Burger | TB3 Waffle Pi | iRobot Create 3 |
| Drive Type | Differential | Differential | Differential | Differential | Differential |
| Wheel radius (m) | 0.030 | 0.035 | **0.033** | 0.033 | 0.035 |
| Wheelbase L (m) | 0.26 | 0.23 | **0.16** | 0.16 | 0.235 |
| Max v (m/s) | 0.5 | 0.65 | **0.22** | 0.26 | 0.31 |
| Max ω (rad/s) | — | 1.90 | **2.75** | 1.82 | 1.90 |
| 2D LiDAR | None | Optional | **LDS-01** | LDS-02 | RPLIDAR A1 |
| 3D Camera | Kinect v1 | Kinect v1 | None (optional RealSense) | RealSense R200 | OAK-D Pro |
| Compute | EeePC netbook | Laptop | **RPi 3B+/4B** | RPi 3B+ | RPi 4 |
| ROS Version | ROS Fuerte | ROS Indigo/Kinetic | **ROS 2 Foxy/Humble** | ROS 2 Foxy/Humble | ROS 2 Humble/Iron |
| Firmware | iRobot open | Kobuki open | **OpenCR** (Arduino) | OpenCR | iRobot Create 3 ROS 2 |
| Open-source HW | Partial | Partial | **Fully** | Fully | Partial |

> [!TIP]
> KTU questions often ask: *"Compare TurtleBot 2 and TurtleBot 3 architectures"* — the comparison must highlight **ROS 1 → ROS 2 migration**, **modularity**, and **cost**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — TurtleBot 4 Hardware Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR["Battery Pack 11.1V Li Ion"]
    CR3["iRobot Create 3 Base with Wheels Encoders IMU"]
    OBC["Onboard SBC Raspberry Pi 4 Ubuntu 22.04 ROS 2 Humble"]
    LIDAR["2D LiDAR RPLIDAR A1"]
    CAM["OAK D Pro RGB D Camera with VPU"]
    BR["OpenCR Control Board Arduino Compatible"]
    WIFI["Wi Fi Ethernet USB Hub"]

    PWR --> CR3
    PWR --> BR
    PBR["Power Management Board"]:::aux

    CR3 -- USB Serial --> BR
    BR -- Serial UART --> OBC
    LIDAR -- USB --> OBC
    CAM -- USB --> OBC
    OBC -- ROS 2 DDS --> WIFI

    classDef aux fill:#eee,stroke:#888,stroke-dasharray:3 3;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** Solid arrows = physical cabling. Power flows from the Li-ion pack to the base and OpenCR board via the Power Management Board. The OBC is the ROS 2 master on which all driver nodes run.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — ROS 2 Software Node Graph on TurtleBot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sensors
        LIDAR["ld08_driver publishes scan"]
        CAMNODE["oakd_ros2 publishes rgb depth pointcloud"]
        IMU["turtlebot3_node publishes imu odom joint_states"]
    end

    subgraph Processing
        TF["robot_state_publisher publishes tf"]
        SLAM["slam_toolbox subscribes scan odom tf publishes map"]
        NAV["Nav2 Stack planner controller behavior"]
    end

    subgraph Output
        CMD["turtlebot3_node subscribes cmd_vel drives wheels"]
        RVIZ["RViz2 visualisation"]
    end

    LIDAR --> SLAM
    CAMNODE --> TF
    IMU --> SLAM
    IMU --> TF
    SLAM --> NAV
    SLAM --> TF
    NAV --> CMD
    LIDAR --> RVIZ
    SLAM --> RVIZ
    NAV --> RVIZ
    TF --> RVIZ
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam tip:** When asked to "explain the ROS 2 architecture of TurtleBot", draw exactly this flow: sensors → processing (SLAM/Nav) → actuation, with TF as the shared backbone. Examiners reward the explicit mention of `/scan`, `/odom`, `/cmd_vel`, `/tf`, `/map`.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Navigation2 Stack Inside TurtleBot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Goal Pose actionlib2 Action Client"]
    B["BT Navigator Behavior Tree"]
    C["Planner Server Global Path"]
    D["Controller Server Local Trajectory"]
    E["Recovery Behaviors Rotate Clear Costmap"]
    F["Costmap 2D Static Layer Obstacle Layer Inflation Layer"]
    G["cmd_vel to turtlebot3_node"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    B --> D
    B --> E
    C --> F
    D --> F
    F --> D
    D --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Nav2 = the "brain"** that consumes the map and produces `/cmd_vel`. The TurtleBot driver (`turtlebot3_node`) just executes whatever wheel speeds come down `/cmd_vel`.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — TurtleBot Coordinate Frames (TF Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MAP["map world frame SLAM output"]
    ODOM["odom odometry frame continuous but drifting"]
    BASE["base_footprint projection of robot on ground"]
    BASE_LINK["base_link robot body center"]
    IMU_LINK["imu_link IMU sensor"]
    LASER_LINK["base_scan LiDAR frame"]
    WHEEL_L["wheel_left_link"]
    WHEEL_R["wheel_right_link"]

    MAP --> ODOM
    ODOM --> BASE
    BASE --> BASE_LINK
    BASE_LINK --> IMU_LINK
    BASE_LINK --> LASER_LINK
    BASE_LINK --> WHEEL_L
    BASE_LINK --> WHEEL_R
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU favourite question:** *"Explain the TF tree of TurtleBot."* — Answer must include the **map → odom → base_footprint → base_link** chain. The `map → odom` transform is published by SLAM (jumps allowed to correct drift); the `odom → base_footprint` transform is published by the wheel-odometry node (continuous, drifts).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define TurtleBot and list its main hardware components. Why is it called the *reference platform* of ROS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** TurtleBot is the official open-source mobile robot reference platform of ROS, designed for education, research, and prototyping service-robot applications.
- (1 mark) **Hardware components:** Mobile base (differential drive + encoders), 2D LiDAR, RGB-D camera, IMU, onboard SBC, battery.
- (1 mark) **Why reference:** It is officially maintained by Open Robotics, is fully ROS/ROS 2 native (URDF, driver packages, launch files, simulation models all provided), and is the platform on which every new ROS release is first validated.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between TurtleBot 2 and TurtleBot 3 in terms of base, sensors, compute, and ROS version.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Base:** TB2 uses Kobuki; TB3 uses Burger / Waffle / Waffle Pi (3D-printable modular base).
- (1 mark) **Sensors & Compute:** TB2 uses Kinect + laptop; TB3 uses LDS LiDAR + Raspberry Pi.
- (1 mark) **ROS Version:** TB2 runs ROS 1 (Indigo/Kinetic); TB3 is fully migrated to **ROS 2** (Foxy/Humble), with the OpenCR Arduino-compatible firmware replacing proprietary firmware.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the wheelbase `L` sign convention** — left wheel speed uses `−ωL/2`, right uses `+ωL/2`. Reversed sign ⇒ full 7-mark deduction.
> 2. **Using `θ` directly in cos/sin** without the midpoint correction for odometry ⇒ loses the "exact update" marks.
> 3. **Calling the LiDAR "LIDAR" or "lazer"** — correct technical term is **LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)**.
> 4. **Confusing ROS 1 packages with ROS 2 packages** — TB3 uses `turtlebot3_*` packages in ROS 2; TB2 used `turtlebot_*` in ROS 1.
> 5. **Skipping the units** in numerical answers — KTU mandates SI units throughout.

---

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) Derive the forward and inverse kinematics of a differential-drive TurtleBot. Clearly state the assumptions. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Rolling without slipping at the wheel-ground contact.
2. Rigid body motion on a flat 2D plane.
3. No wheel slip during normal operation.
4. Wheel radius $r$ and wheelbase $L$ are constant.

**Forward Kinematics (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Wheel linear velocities:
$$ v_L = r\omega_L, \quad v_R = r\omega_R $$

Step 2 — Body linear and angular velocities:
$$ v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} $$
$$ \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} $$

Step 3 — World-frame pose update:
$$ \dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega $$

Discretised:
$$ x_{t+1} = x_t + v\cos\theta_t \, dt, \quad y_{t+1} = y_t + v\sin\theta_t \, dt, \quad \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \omega \, dt $$

**Inverse Kinematics (3 marks):**

From the two forward equations, treat $v$ and $\omega$ as given, solve for $v_L$ and $v_R$:

$$ v_R = v + \frac{\omega L}{2}, \quad v_L = v - \frac{\omega L}{2} $$

Then wheel angular velocities:
$$ \omega_R = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(v + \frac{\omega L}{2}\right), \quad \omega_L = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(v - \frac{\omega L}{2}\right) $$

These are the speeds the `turtlebot3_node` commands to the OpenCR board.

---

**(b) For a TurtleBot 3 Burger (r = 0.033 m, L = 0.16 m) moving with $v_R = 0.18$ m/s and $v_L = 0.10$ m/s, compute the body's linear and angular velocities, the turning radius, and the pose change over 0.5 s starting from $(0, 0, 0)$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Body linear velocity (1 mark):
$$ v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.18 + 0.10}{2} = \frac{0.28}{2} = 0.14 \text{ m/s} $$

Step 2 — Body angular velocity (1 mark):
$$ \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{0.18 - 0.10}{0.16} = \frac{0.08}{0.16} = 0.5 \text{ rad/s} $$

Step 3 — Turning radius (1 mark):
$$ R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.14}{0.5} = 0.28 \text{ m} $$

Step 4 — Pose change over 0.5 s (4 marks):

Since $\omega \neq 0$, the robot moves along a circular arc. The arc length is:
$$ s = v \cdot dt = 0.14 \times 0.5 = 0.07 \text{ m} $$

The heading change is:
$$ \Delta\theta = \omega \cdot dt = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25 \text{ rad} \approx 14.32° $$

Using exact circular-arc kinematics (since the path is a circle of radius $R$):

$$ \Delta x = R\sin(\Delta\theta) - 0 = 0.28 \times \sin(0.25) = 0.28 \times 0.2474 \approx 0.0693 \text{ m} $$
$$ \Delta y = R(1 - \cos(\Delta\theta)) = 0.28 \times (1 - \cos(0.25)) = 0.28 \times 0.0312 \approx 0.00874 \text{ m} $$

Final pose:
$$ (x, y, \theta) = (0.0693,\ 0.00874,\ 0.25 \text{ rad}) $$

Sanity check: $\sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2} \approx 0.0699$ m ≈ 0.07 m = arc length ✓.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**(a) Explain the complete ROS 2 software architecture of a TurtleBot 4. List all major nodes, the topics they publish/subscribe to, and the role of the TF tree. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Major Nodes and Topics (5 marks):**

1. **`turtlebot4_ros2` driver node**
   - Subscribes: `/cmd_vel` (geometry_msgs/Twist).
   - Publishes: `/odom` (nav_msgs/Odometry), `/imu` (sensor_msgs/Imu), `/battery_state` (sensor_msgs/BatteryState), `/wheel_status`, `/joint_states`.

2. **`rplidar_ros2` node**
   - Publishes: `/scan` (sensor_msgs/LaserScan) at 5–10 Hz.

3. **`depthai_ros2` driver for OAK-D Pro**
   - Publishes: `/oakd/rgb/image_raw`, `/oakd/stereo/image_raw`, `/oakd/points` (sensor_msgs/PointCloud2).

4. **`robot_state_publisher`**
   - Subscribes: `/joint_states` and the URDF parameter.
   - Publishes: `/tf` and `/tf_static` — the static (URDF) and dynamic (joint) transforms.

5. **`slam_toolbox` (or `cartographer`)**
   - Subscribes: `/scan`, `/odom`, `/tf`.
   - Publishes: `/map` (nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid), `/tf` (`map → odom`).

6. **Navigation2 stack (`bt_navigator`, `planner_server`, `controller_server`, `behavior_server`, `global_costmap`, `local_costmap`)**
   - Subscribes: `/goal_pose`, `/scan`, `/odom`, `/map`, `/tf`.
   - Action server: `navigate_to_pose`.
   - Publishes: `/cmd_vel`.

7. **`rviz2`** (visualisation only, on remote PC).

**Role of the TF tree (2 marks):**
- TF (transform library) maintains the **spatial relationship** between all coordinate frames in real time.
- Standard chain: `map → odom → base_footprint → base_link → [wheels, sensors, imu_link]`.
- `map → odom` is published by SLAM (corrects odometry drift).
- `odom → base_footprint` is published by wheel odometry (continuous, accumulates drift).
- `base_link → sensors` are static transforms from URDF.
- Without TF, nodes cannot interpret sensor data in a common frame — SLAM and Nav2 would fail.

---

**(b) Explain how SLAM and Navigation2 work together on a TurtleBot. Include the role of the costmap, recovery behaviours, and the action interface. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**SLAM (2 marks):**
- **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping** builds a 2D occupancy grid map (`/map`) while simultaneously estimating the robot's pose within it.
- Inputs: `/scan` (LiDAR), `/odom` (wheel odometry), `/tf`.
- Algorithm: scan-match LiDAR scans against the evolving map (e.g., Karto / Cartographer / SLAM Toolbox).
- Output: a geometrically consistent map + the `map → odom` TF transform.

**Navigation2 Stack (3 marks):**
- **Global planner:** computes an optimal collision-free path from current pose to goal on the static costmap (e.g., NavFn, Smac Planner).
- **Local planner / Controller:** re-plans every control cycle (e.g., DWB, TEB, RPP) to track the global path while avoiding dynamic obstacles using the local costmap.
- **Costmap:** a layered 2D grid encoding obstacles (Obstacle Layer), the static map (Static Layer), and an *inflated* region around obstacles (Inflation Layer) that the robot must not enter.
- **Recovery behaviours:** if the controller gets stuck (e.g., `RotateRecovery` to spin in place, `BackUpRecovery` to reverse, `ClearCostmapRecovery` to wipe stale obstacle data), Nav2 invokes them in a **Behavior Tree** before failing.

**Action Interface (2 marks):**
- The application sends a `NavigateToPose` **action goal** containing $(x, y, \theta)$ to the `bt_navigator`.
- The navigator returns continuous **feedback** (current pose, distance remaining, ETA) and a final **result** (success / failure / cancelled).
- This action-based interface (vs. service) is critical because navigation is **long-running, preemptible, and requires feedback** — exactly the case where ROS 2 actions outshine topics/services.

> [!TIP]
