> **KTU closing remark** students should memorise: *"SLAM gives you the map + localisation; Nav2 gives you the motion plan + actuation on that map. TurtleBot is the platform where these two stacks converge into physical motion."*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final rapid-revision checklist for TurtleBot (Module 3):**

- **What is TurtleBot?** The official ROS / ROS 2 reference mobile robot platform — low-cost, open-source, modular.
- **Generations:** TB1 (Create+Kinect) → TB2 (Kobuki+Kinect) → TB3 (Burger/Waffle, ROS 2, OpenCR) → TB4 (Create 3 + OAK-D, ROS 2 Humble).
- **Drive type:** Differential drive (non-holonomic) — two independently driven wheels + caster(s).
- **Wheelbase `L`** and **wheel radius `r`** are the two key kinematic parameters.
- **Forward kinematics:** $v = (v_R+v_L)/2$, $\omega = (v_R-v_L)/L$.
- **Inverse kinematics:** $v_L = v - \omega L/2$, $v_R = v + \omega L/2$.
- **Pose update (Euler):** $x_{t+1} = x_t + v\cos\theta \, dt$, $y_{t+1} = y_t + v\sin\theta \, dt$, $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \omega \, dt$.
- **Odometry (exact, midpoint):** use $(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2)$ inside $\cos/\sin$ — preferred in `ros2_control`.
- **Turning radius:** $R = v/\omega$ — must be $\ge L/2$ or the ICR lies *between* the wheels (zero-radius turn).
- **Sensors on TB4:** RPLIDAR A1 (2D LiDAR) + OAK-D Pro (RGB-D + on-device AI) + wheel encoders + IMU.
- **Compute:** Raspberry Pi 4 (TB4 Standard) running Ubuntu 22.04 + ROS 2 Humble.
- **TF tree chain:** `map → odom → base_footprint → base_link → sensor/wheel links`.
- **ROS 2 topics on TurtleBot:** `/cmd_vel`, `/odom`, `/scan`, `/imu`, `/map`, `/tf`, `/joint_states`, `/battery_state`.
- **SLAM** = `/scan + /odom + /tf → /map + (map→odom)`.
- **Nav2 =** `/map + /scan + /odom + /tf + goal → /cmd_vel` (via Behavior Tree + Planner + Controller + Recovery).
- **Action interface** used for navigation (`navigate_to_pose`): long-running, preemptible, with feedback.
- **Open-source firmware:** OpenCR (Arduino-compatible) on TB3; Create 3 ROS 2 native on TB4.
- **Non-holonomic constraint:** $\dot{y}\cos\theta - \dot{x}\sin\theta = 0$ — no sideways motion.
- **KTU numerical favs:** given $(v, \omega)$, find $(v_L, v_R, R, \Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta\theta)$ over $dt$; or given $(\Delta\phi_L, \Delta\phi_R)$, find odometry update.
- **Always state SI units** in numerical answers — lose marks otherwise.
- **Always mention ROS version explicitly** (ROS 1 for TB1/TB2, ROS 2 for TB3/TB4) — examiners deduct for ambiguity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulators - Stage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simulators - Stage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage** is an **open-source, 2D (two-dimensional) multi-robot simulator** that simulates a population of mobile robots, sensors, and environmental objects within a planar (flat, 2D) world. It was originally developed as part of the **Player/Stage (Player/Stage/Gazebo) Project** by researchers at the University of Southern California (USC) and has historically been one of the primary simulation backends used with the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** for prototyping, multi-agent research, and large-scale swarm experiments.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context for the course *OERAT722 – Introduction to Robot Operating System*, Stage is positioned as a **lightweight, high-fidelity 2D simulator** that prioritizes *simulation speed* and *multi-robot scalability* over photorealistic 3D rendering. It is functionally distinct from **Gazebo** (which is a 3D physics-based simulator) and **RViz** (which is a ROS visualization tool, not a simulator).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identifier for Exams:** Stage is a **2D simulator** belonging to the **Player/Stage Project** family. Students must not confuse it with Gazebo (3D) or RViz (visualizer). This distinction is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU ESE.

### 1.2 Key Characteristics of Stage

- **Dimensionality:** Strictly **2D (top-down planar view)**. All robots, obstacles, and sensors exist on the X-Y plane.
- **Simulation Scale:** Can simulate **hundreds to thousands of robots** simultaneously on commodity hardware due to its lightweight physics model.
- **Physics Engine:** Uses a simplified **2D kinematic/dynamic model** (no full rigid-body 3D physics). Collisions are computed via 2D ray-tracing and polygon intersection.
- **Sensors Simulated:** 2D **Laser Range Finders (LRF/LIDAR)**, **sonar arrays**, **odometry**, **cameras (synthesized blob detection)**, and **bumpers**.
- **Driver Architecture:** Stage acts as a **server** to the **Player** robot device server. In ROS, the equivalent is the **`stage_ros`** package.
- **File Format:** Configured via **`.world` files** that define the environment, robots, sensors, and their initial poses.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "Stage as a Strategy Board Game":**
>
> Imagine you are a **chess coach** who wants to simulate 50 matches simultaneously to test a new opening strategy. Instead of building a full 3D wooden chess set for every table (which would be **Gazebo** — high-fidelity, slow, expensive), you use a **giant flat paper grid with cardboard pieces** that you can slide around quickly. You draw walls with marker, and place "laser tripwires" (laser range finders) along the edges. You can run all 50 matches in parallel because each match only exists as a **2D top-down abstraction**.
>
> **Stage IS that paper grid.** It does not care about the visual realism of a robot — it only cares about:
> 1. **Where** the robot is on the X-Y plane,
> 2. **What shape** the robot has (footprint polygon),
> 3. **What sensors** the robot carries (laser rays),
> 4. **What obstacles** exist in the world.
>
> This makes Stage **blazingly fast** for testing algorithms (SLAM, path planning, swarm behavior) on **large robot populations** before deploying them on real hardware.

### 1.4 Position in the ROS Simulation Stack

| Simulator | Dimension | Primary Use Case | KTU Exam Keyword |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Stage** | **2D** | Multi-robot swarms, fast algorithm prototyping | *"Lightweight 2D simulator"* |
| **Gazebo** | 3D | Photorealistic single/multi-robot with physics | *"3D physics-based simulator"* |
| **RViz** | 3D (Visualization only) | Visualizing ROS topic data | *"Visualizer, NOT a simulator"* |
| **MATLAB/Simulink** | 2D/3D | Control system co-simulation | *"Mathematical model-based"* |

### 1.5 Historical Lineage — The Player/Stage Project

The **Player/Stage Project** was an open-source robotics research initiative launched in the late 1990s. It consisted of three coupled components:

1. **Player** — A *network robot device server* that abstracted hardware drivers into a TCP socket protocol.
2. **Stage** — A *2D multi-robot simulator* that provided a virtual Player server backend.
3. **Gazebo** — A *3D multi-robot simulator* (later split into a standalone project, now maintained by Open Robotics).

When ROS (Robot Operating System) emerged in 2007, it **reused the Player/Stage architecture philosophy** but replaced Player's TCP protocol with ROS topics/services. Stage's role remained as a **2D simulation backend** accessible via the **`stage_ros`** node, which publishes simulated sensor data as standard ROS topics (`/scan`, `/odom`, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of Stage

Stage operates on a **client-server model** layered over ROS. Understanding this layered architecture is critical for KTU exam questions on ROS simulation.

### 2.1.1 The Three-Tier Architecture

**Tier 1 — Configuration Layer (`.world` file)**
- The user writes a human-readable **world file** describing the environment.
- Format is **declarative** (you describe *what exists*, not *how to render it*).
- Defines: floorplan, obstacles, robot models, sensor models, initial poses.

**Tier 2 — Simulation Kernel (libstage)**
- The C++ simulation engine `libstage` parses the world file and instantiates in-memory representations of all entities.
- Runs the **discrete-time update loop** at a configurable rate (default **100 Hz**, i.e., **dt = 10 ms**).
- Performs **2D collision detection** using **ray-tracing** against polygon obstacles.

**Tier 3 — ROS Interface Layer (`stage_ros` node)**
- Bridges the simulator's internal data to **ROS topics**.
- Publishes simulated sensor readings as **standard ROS message types**:
  - `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` → simulated LIDAR
  - `nav_msgs/Odometry` → simulated wheel odometry
  - `geometry_msgs/Twist` → accepts velocity commands (subscribed)

### 2.2 The `.world` File — The Heart of Stage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Exams:** The `.world` file is the **configuration cornerstone** of Stage. Questions frequently ask students to identify the *type* of file used to define a Stage world or to interpret sample world file syntax.

A Stage `.world` file is structured into the following logical blocks:

```
1. include       → Import other world files (reusable models)
2. units         → Set measurement units (default: meters)
3. bitmaps       → Load floorplan images as background maps
4. resolution    → Map resolution in meters per pixel
5. interval_sim  → Simulation timestep (e.g., 100 ms)
6. interval_real → Real-time factor (1.0 = real-time, 0 = as-fast-as-possible)
7. model blocks  → Define obstacles, robots, sensors
```

### 2.3 The Robot Model in Stage — A 2D Polygon Entity

In Stage, every robot is described by:

| Property | Description | KTU Keyword |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **pose** `(x, y, theta)` | Position + heading in the world frame | *2D planar pose* |
| **size** `(length, width)` | Footprint dimensions (rectangular, axis-aligned before rotation) | *Bounding box* |
| **drive** type | `diff`, `omni`, or `car` — kinematic model | *Kinematic model* |
| **velocity** `(max_speed, max_turn_rate)` | Actuator saturation limits | *Velocity bounds* |
| **sensors** | Child entities: laser, sonar, camera, bumper | *Sensor mounts* |

### 2.4 The 2D Kinematic Models in Stage

Stage supports **three canonical drive models**, each mapped to a specific kinematic equation set. These are *examinable* in KTU derivations.

#### Model 1: `diff` — Differential Drive

A two-wheeled robot where linear velocity *v* is split between the two wheels.

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$$

where:
- $v_R$ = right wheel linear velocity (m/s)
- $v_L$ = left wheel linear velocity (m/s)
- $L$ = wheelbase (distance between wheels, meters)
- $v$ = forward linear velocity of robot (m/s)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)

#### Model 2: `omni` — Omnidirectional (Holonomic)

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

where $v_x$, $v_y$ are body-frame velocity components and $\omega$ is the angular velocity.

#### Model 3: `car` — Car-Like (Ackermann Steering)

$$\dot{x} = v \cos(\theta)$$

$$\dot{y} = v \sin(\theta)$$

$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L_{axle}} \tan(\delta)$$

where $\delta$ is the steering angle and $L_{axle}$ is the wheelbase.

### 2.5 The 2D Laser Range Finder (LRF) Model

The simulated laser in Stage performs **2D ray-casting** from a specified origin point on the robot, in a fan-shaped angular range.

**Sensor Equation:**

$$r_k = \min_{i} \left( d(P, Q_i) \right) \quad \text{for} \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

where:
- $r_k$ = range reading for the $k$-th laser beam
- $P$ = laser origin point on the robot
- $Q_i$ = $i$-th intersection point of the ray with any world obstacle
- $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ = Euclidean distance
- $N$ = number of laser samples per scan (e.g., 180, 360, 720)
- The "min" function selects the **closest** obstacle intersection (first hit).

**Angular Resolution of the Scan:**

$$\Delta\alpha = \frac{\alpha_{max} - \alpha_{min}}{N - 1}$$

where $\alpha_{min}$, $\alpha_{max}$ define the laser's field of view (e.g., $\alpha_{min} = -\pi$, $\alpha_{max} = +\pi$ for a 360° scan).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print This Table Before Your Exam.** These are the most testable equations for the "Simulators — Stage" topic.

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Differential drive linear velocity | $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$ | m/s |
| 2 | Differential drive angular velocity | $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$ | rad/s |
| 3 | Omnidirectional body→world transform | $SE(2)$ rotation matrix on $(v_x, v_y, \omega)$ | — |
| 4 | Ackermann steering rate | $\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan(\delta)$ | rad/s |
| 5 | Laser range (closest hit) | $r_k = \min_i d(P, Q_i)$ | meters |
| 6 | Laser angular resolution | $\Delta\alpha = (\alpha_{max} - \alpha_{min})/(N-1)$ | radians |
| 7 | World file timestep | `interval_sim 100` (ms) | milliseconds |
| 8 | Default real-time factor | `interval_real 100` (ms) = real-time | — |
| 9 | Pose representation | $(x, y, \theta) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ | x,y in m; θ in rad |
| 10 | Standard ROS message types | `LaserScan`, `Odometry`, `Twist` | from `sensor_msgs`, `nav_msgs` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the equation $r_k = \min_i d(P, Q_i)$, the *min* operator is a *functional* notation — it is **NOT a row separator** and therefore **does NOT contain a vertical pipe `|` character**. In markdown tables, write $r_k = \min_i d(P, Q_i)$ using `\min_i` and **never** as `r_k = min |d(P, Q_i)|`. The use of `|` inside a markdown table row would prematurely terminate the table cell.

### 2.7 Engineering Utility — Why Use Stage in Real Research?

- **Swarm Robotics:** A 2015 study at the University of Sheffield simulated **1,000 robots** in Stage to test decentralized flocking — a task impossible in Gazebo at the time.
- **SLAM Algorithm Benchmarking:** Stage's deterministic noise model makes it ideal for repeatable benchmarking of SLAM algorithms in 2D.
- **Education:** Stage is widely used in university robotics courses (including KTU-affiliated programs) because it requires **no GPU**, runs on low-end laptops, and has a **simple text-based world file**.
- **ROS Bridge:** Because Stage publishes standard ROS messages, the same `teleop_twist_keyboard` or `move_base` node that drives a real robot **drives a Stage robot unmodified** — a huge pedagogical advantage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** All algebraic steps are written out in full. No shortcuts. No "...". No "similarly". Every transformation is justified with a textual explanation row.

## 3.1 Derivation: Differential-Drive Odometry Update from Stage's Internal State

**Problem Setup (typical 7-mark KTU question):**
*A Stage robot of wheelbase $L = 0.4$ m has left and right wheel velocities $v_L = 0.3$ m/s and $v_R = 0.5$ m/s over a time interval $\Delta t = 0.1$ s. The robot starts at pose $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$. Compute the new pose.*

### Step 1 — Compute the Instantaneous Body-Frame Velocities

From the Stage drive model for a `diff` robot:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.5 + 0.3}{2} = \frac{0.8}{2} = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{0.5 - 0.3}{0.4} = \frac{0.2}{0.4} = 0.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

> **Explanation:** The linear velocity of the robot's center of mass is the **arithmetic mean** of the two wheel velocities (because both wheels contribute equally when both push forward). The angular velocity is **proportional to the velocity difference** divided by the wheelbase — this is the *nonholonomic constraint* that makes differential drive turn by skidding.

### Step 2 — Discretize the Continuous-Time Unicycle Model

The unicycle kinematic model in continuous time is:

$$\dot{x} = v \cos(\theta)$$

$$\dot{y} = v \sin(\theta)$$

$$\dot{\theta} = \omega$$

Apply **forward Euler discretization** with step $\Delta t$ (Stage's default integration scheme for short timesteps):

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v \cos(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v \sin(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega \cdot \Delta t$$

> **Explanation:** Euler integration is **first-order accurate**. Stage uses it because its simulation timestep is small (default **100 Hz**), and the resulting error per step is acceptable for visualization and algorithm testing. For higher accuracy, a Runge-Kutta 4 (RK4) integrator could be used, but the overhead is rarely justified in 2D simulation.

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$x_1 = 0 + 0.4 \cdot \cos(0) \cdot 0.1 = 0.4 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.1 = 0.04 \text{ m}$$

$$y_1 = 0 + 0.4 \cdot \sin(0) \cdot 0.1 = 0.4 \cdot 0 \cdot 0.1 = 0 \text{ m}$$

$$\theta_1 = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 0.1 = 0.05 \text{ rad}$$

> **Explanation:** With $\theta_0 = 0$, the robot is initially aligned with the global X-axis. The forward motion $(v \cos 0 = 0.4)$ produces displacement in the X direction only, and the angular velocity causes a small positive rotation. The robot has barely turned, so it has essentially moved 4 cm forward.

### Step 4 — Final Answer (Pose After One Timestep)

$$\boxed{(x_1, y_1, \theta_1) = (0.04 \text{ m}, \ 0.00 \text{ m}, \ 0.05 \text{ rad})}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: 2D Laser Ray-Cast Range for a Single Beam

**Problem Setup (typical 7-mark KTU question):**
*Consider a Stage robot at position $P = (2.0, 3.0)$ m. A single laser beam is emitted at angle $\alpha = \pi/4$ rad (relative to the robot's heading). The world contains a single rectangular obstacle with corners at $(3.0, 2.0)$, $(5.0, 2.0)$, $(5.0, 4.0)$, $(3.0, 4.0)$. Compute the range reading $r$.*

### Step 1 — Parametric Equation of the Laser Ray

The laser ray is a half-line (ray) starting at $P$ in the direction of angle $\alpha$:

$$R(t) = P + t \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha \\ \sin\alpha \end{bmatrix}, \quad t \geq 0$$

Substituting $P = (2.0, 3.0)$ and $\alpha = \pi/4$:

$$R(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 + t \cos(\pi/4) \\ 3.0 + t \sin(\pi/4) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 + 0.7071 \, t \\ 3.0 + 0.7071 \, t \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Explanation:** A ray is a *half-line*, so we restrict $t \geq 0$ to avoid "looking behind" the laser source. The direction vector is normalized to unit length, which is why $\cos(\pi/4) = \sin(\pi/4) = 0.7071$.

### Step 2 — Equation of the Nearest Obstacle Edge

The obstacle has edges connecting consecutive corners. The edge closest to the ray and oriented to be *hit first* is the **left vertical edge** at $x = 3.0$ m, spanning $y \in [2.0, 4.0]$.

The edge is the line $x = 3.0$. Solve for the intersection with the ray:

$$2.0 + 0.7071 \, t = 3.0$$

$$0.7071 \, t = 1.0$$

$$t = 1.0 / 0.7071 = 1.4142 \text{ m}$$

> **Explanation:** We solved the *X-component* of the ray equation for when it crosses $x = 3.0$. The result $t$ is the **distance along the ray** to the intersection, in meters, because the direction vector is a unit vector.

### Step 3 — Verify the Y-Coordinate is Within the Edge

$$y = 3.0 + 0.7071 \times 1.4142 = 3.0 + 1.0 = 4.0 \text{ m}$$

Check: Is $4.0 \in [2.0, 4.0]$? **Yes** (at the boundary, the upper-right corner of the obstacle).

> **Explanation:** Even though $y = 4.0$ lies exactly on the corner, the intersection is **valid** because the obstacle's vertical edge includes this endpoint. Stage's ray-tracer accepts this as a valid hit.

### Step 4 — Final Range Reading

$$\boxed{r = 1.4142 \text{ m} \approx \sqrt{2} \text{ m}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Stage Against Other Simulators

| Evaluation Criterion | Stage (2D) | Gazebo (3D) | RViz | MATLAB/Simulink |
|----------------------|------------|-------------|------|-----------------|
| Dimensionality | 2D (planar) | 3D (full 3D physics) | 3D (visualization only) | 2D/3D (model-based) |
| Physics Fidelity | Low (kinematic + 2D collision) | High (ODE, Bullet, DART) | None | Depends on user model |
| Multi-Robot Scalability | High (100s–1000s) | Moderate (10s) | N/A | Low |
| GPU Required | **No** | Yes (recommended) | Yes | No |
| Sensor Simulation | LRF, sonar, blob camera | LRF, RGB-D, IMU, force-torque | None (visualizes topics) | Custom blocks |
| File Format | `.world` | `.sdf` / `.urdf` | N/A | `.slx` / `.m` |
| ROS Integration | `stage_ros` node | `gazebo_ros_pkgs` | Native | Robotics Toolbox |
| KTU 1-Line Tag | *"Fast 2D multi-robot sim"* | *"Realistic 3D physics sim"* | *"ROS visualizer only"* | *"Numerical control sim"* |

> **Mnemonic for Exams:** **"Stage = Speed"** — when you need to simulate *many* robots *quickly* in 2D, choose Stage. **"Gazebo = Gravity"** — when you need *realistic physics* in 3D, choose Gazebo.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safety Rules Enforced:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and letter-prefixed (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`).
> - Node labels are **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** — no bold `**`, no italics `*`, no HTML tables, no special characters that break Mermaid parsing.
> - Reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style` are **NEVER** used as standalone node names.

## 4.2 Architecture Diagram — Stage's Three-Tier Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["USER LAYER: WORLD FILE"] --> nodeB["DECLARATIVE WORLD DESCRIPTION"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["OBSTACLES BITMAPS ROBOTS SENSORS"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["SIMULATION KERNEL LIBSTAGE"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["2D COLLISION ENGINE"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["KINEMATIC SOLVER"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["SENSOR RAY CASTER"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["ROS INTERFACE STAGE ROS NODE"]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["PUBLISH LASERSCAN"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["PUBLISH ODOMETRY"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["SUBSCRIBE TWIST"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["ROS TOPIC NETWORK"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM["USER NODES MOVE BASE TELEOP SLAM"]
```

> **Reading Guide:** The flow is **downward**: a user writes a `.world` file → the simulation kernel parses and simulates → the ROS interface publishes/subscribes standard ROS topics → user ROS nodes consume the data as if it were a real robot.

## 4.3 Data Flow Diagram — One Simulation Cycle (Tick)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant nodeUserCmd as USER CMD NODE
    participant nodeRosBridge as STAGE ROS BRIDGE
    participant nodeSimCore as SIMULATION CORE LIBSTAGE
    participant nodeSensor as SENSOR MODEL LRF
    participant nodeWorld as WORLD OBSTACLES

    nodeUserCmd->>nodeRosBridge: PUBLISH GEOMETRY MSGS TWIST ON CMD VEL
    nodeRosBridge->>nodeSimCore: INJECT VELOCITY INTO ROBOT MODEL
    nodeSimCore->>nodeSimCore: COMPUTE NEW POSE USING DIFF OR OMNI OR CAR MODEL
    nodeSimCore->>nodeSensor: TRIGGER LASER RAY CAST
    nodeSensor->>nodeWorld: QUERY RAY OBSTACLE INTERSECTIONS
    nodeWorld->>nodeSensor: RETURN NEAREST HIT DISTANCES
    nodeSensor->>nodeSimCore: RETURN RANGE ARRAY
    nodeSimCore->>nodeRosBridge: EMIT NEW ODOMETRY AND LASER SCAN
    nodeRosBridge->>nodeUserCmd: PUBLISH NAV MSGS ODOMETRY AND SENSOR MSGS LASERSCAN
```

> **Reading Guide:** This sequence diagram shows what happens in **one simulation tick** (default every **100 ms**). A user command enters, the simulator advances physics, sensors are updated, and results are published back to ROS.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Stage's Relationship to the Broader ROS Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSim["STAGE SIMULATOR 2D WORLD"] --> nodeRosBridge["STAGE ROS NODE"]
    nodeRosBridge --> nodeScan["TOPIC SCAN LASERSCAN"]
    nodeRosBridge --> nodeOdom["TOPIC ODOM ODOMETRY"]
    nodeRosBridge --> nodeCmd["TOPIC CMD VEL TWIST"]
    nodeScan --> nodeAlgo["ALGORITHM NODES SLAM NAV PLANNING"]
    nodeOdom --> nodeAlgo
    nodeAlgo --> nodeCmd
    nodeAlgo --> nodeViz["RVIZ VISUALIZATION"]
    nodeViz --> nodeHuman["HUMAN OBSERVER"]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Stage is a **drop-in replacement for real hardware** in the ROS computation graph. Any algorithm node (e.g., a SLAM node) is **unaware** whether `/scan` comes from a Stage simulator or a real Hokuyo LIDAR — the abstraction is preserved by ROS topics.

## 4.5 Hierarchical Model Block Diagram — A Stage Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeRoot["ROBOT ENTITY POSE X Y THETA"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeBody["BODY SIZE LENGTH WIDTH"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeDrive["DRIVE TYPE DIFF OR OMNI OR CAR"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeSensor1["SENSOR LASER RANGE FINDER"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeSensor2["SENSOR SONAR ARRAY"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeSensor3["SENSOR BUMPER ARRAY"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeSensor4["SENSOR ODOMETRY"]
    nodeDrive --> nodeParam1["MAX SPEED MS"]
    nodeDrive --> nodeParam2["MAX TURN RATE RAD PER S"]
    nodeSensor1 --> nodeL1["SAMPLES 180"]
    nodeSensor1 --> nodeL2["RANGE MAX 30 METERS"]
    nodeSensor1 --> nodeL3["FOV MINUS PI TO PI"]
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is a **functional decomposition** of a Stage robot entity. The `body` is the geometric footprint, the `drive` is the kinematic model, and the `sensors` are child entities attached to the body.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in the "Simulators — Stage" Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Stage with Gazebo:** Writing "Stage is a 3D physics simulator" in a 2-mark definition question can cost **full marks**. Stage is **strictly 2D**; Gazebo is **3D**. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 2. **Confusing Stage with RViz:** Students often write "Stage is a ROS visualization tool". **RViz** is the visualizer; **Stage is a simulator that can publish data INTO RViz**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the "Player/Stage" lineage:** KTU questions frequently ask *"Stage is part of which project?"* The answer is the **Player/Stage Project (or Player/Stage/Gazebo)** — not "Player Project" alone, not "Gazebo Project".
>
> 4. **Using wrong ROS message type names:** When asked "What is the message type of the simulated laser?", students write `LaserData` or `LaserReading`. The **correct** ROS-standard type is **`sensor_msgs/LaserScan`** (with capital S in `LaserScan`).
>
> 5. **Mixing units in derivations:** If the question gives $v_R$ in cm/s and $L$ in m without conversion, students often forget to convert. Always normalize units to **SI (meters, seconds, radians)** before substituting.

## 5.2 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define the term "Stage" as used in the Robot Operating System. List any three of its key features.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Stage is an **open-source 2D multi-robot simulator** originally developed as part of the **Player/Stage Project** and used in ROS to simulate mobile robots, sensors, and environmental objects in a planar (X-Y) world.

**Three Key Features:**
1. **2D simulation** — operates strictly on a top-down planar view; no 3D physics.
2. **High scalability** — can simulate hundreds to thousands of robots simultaneously on commodity hardware.
3. **Standard ROS integration** — publishes simulated sensor data as standard ROS message types (`sensor_msgs/LaserScan`, `nav_msgs/Odometry`), allowing the same algorithmic nodes to be tested in simulation and on real hardware without code changes.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for the correct definition mentioning "2D" and "Player/Stage". Award 0.5 marks per correctly identified feature. Full marks require at least 3 features.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between Stage and Gazebo simulators in ROS.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Stage | Gazebo |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| **Dimensionality** | 2D (planar, top-down) | 3D (full 3D environment) |
| **Physics Engine** | Simplified 2D kinematic collision | Full rigid-body physics (ODE, Bullet, DART) |
| **Primary Use Case** | Large-scale multi-robot swarm simulation, fast algorithm prototyping | Realistic single/multi-robot simulation, vision-based perception, manipulation |
| **File Format** | `.world` file | `.sdf` (Simulation Description Format) or `.urdf` |
| **Performance on Low-End Hardware** | Excellent (no GPU needed) | Requires a dedicated GPU for real-time performance |
| **KTU Keyword** | *"Lightweight 2D simulator"* | *"Photorealistic 3D simulator"* |

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark per correctly identified dimensional/physics/use-case distinction. Maximum 3 marks for 3+ valid points.)*

---

## 5.3 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Part B — Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-tier architecture of the Stage simulator** in ROS. Describe the role of each tier. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A differential-drive robot in Stage has a wheelbase $L = 0.5$ m. The right and left wheel velocities are $v_R = 0.6$ m/s and $v_L = 0.2$ m/s, respectively, for a duration of $\Delta t = 0.2$ s. The initial pose is $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$. Compute the new pose $(x_1, y_1, \theta_1)$ using the **forward Euler integration** method. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — Three-Tier Architecture

The Stage simulator is organized into **three logically distinct layers**:

**Tier 1 — Configuration Layer (`.world` file):**
- This is the **user-facing declarative interface**.
- The user describes the environment in a plain-text `.world` file, including floorplan, obstacles, robot entities, sensor models, and initial poses.
- Example directive: `interval_sim 100` sets the simulation timestep to 100 ms.
- **No code is written** at this tier — only configuration.

**Tier 2 — Simulation Kernel (`libstage`):**
- The C++ simulation engine that **parses** the world file and instantiates in-memory objects.
- Runs the **discrete-time update loop** at the configured `interval_sim` rate.
- Performs **2D collision detection** (via polygon-vs-ray intersection), **kinematic integration** (diff/omni/car), and **sensor simulation** (ray-casting for LIDAR/sonar).
- This tier is **hardware-agnostic** — it has no knowledge of ROS.

**Tier 3 — ROS Interface Layer (`stage_ros` node):**
- Bridges the simulator's internal data to the **ROS computation graph**.
- **Publishes:** `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` (simulated LIDAR), `nav_msgs/Odometry` (simulated wheel odometry).
- **Subscribes:** `geometry_msgs/Twist` (velocity commands from teleop or planning nodes).
- This tier makes the simulator **indistinguishable from a real robot** at the ROS topic level.

> **Block Diagram (as drawn in exam):**
> ```
> [ .world file ]  →  [ libstage kernel ]  →  [ stage_ros bridge ]  →  [ ROS topics ]
>     (Tier 1)            (Tier 2)               (Tier 3)            (ROS graph)
> ```

**Mark distribution for (a):** 2 marks for naming all three tiers, 3 marks for role description, 2 marks for the diagram.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Pose Update Derivation

**Given:** $L = 0.5$ m, $v_R = 0.6$ m/s, $v_L = 0.2$ m/s, $\Delta t = 0.2$ s, $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$.

**Step 1 — Compute linear velocity $v$:**

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.6 + 0.2}{2} = \frac{0.8}{2} = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute angular velocity $\omega$:**

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{0.6 - 0.2}{0.5} = \frac{0.4}{0.5} = 0.8 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply forward Euler update:**

$$x_1 = x_0 + v \cos(\theta_0) \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 0.4 \cdot \cos(0) \cdot 0.2 = 0.4 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.2 = 0.08 \text{ m}$$

$$y_1 = y_0 + v \sin(\theta_0) \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 0.4 \cdot \sin(0) \cdot 0.2 = 0.4 \cdot 0 \cdot 0.2 = 0 \text{ m}$$

$$\theta_1 = \theta_0 + \omega \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 0.8 \cdot 0.2 = 0.16 \text{ rad}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{(x_1, y_1, \theta_1) = (0.08 \text{ m}, \ 0 \text{ m}, \ 0.16 \text{ rad})}$$

**Mark distribution for (b):** 1 mark each for $v$ and $\omega$ (2 marks), 1 mark for the Euler equations, 1 mark each for the three final values (3 marks), 1 mark for the boxed final answer.

---

### Part B — Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of **2D Laser Range Finder (LRF) simulation** in Stage. Derive the equation for a single beam's range reading and the **angular resolution** of the scan. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Stage robot is located at position $P = (1.0, 2.0)$ m. A laser beam is emitted at heading angle $\alpha = 0$ rad. The world contains a vertical wall at $x = 4.0$ m spanning $y \in [0, 5.0]$ m. Compute the **range reading** $r$ for this single beam. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — LRF Simulation Concept

**Concept:**
In Stage, a **2D Laser Range Finder (LRF)** is a child sensor entity attached to a robot body. It simulates a planar LIDAR by performing **2D ray-casting** in a fan-shaped angular sweep around the robot. Each ray in the scan is a **half-line (ray)** originating from the laser mount point. When a ray intersects an obstacle (polygon edge or bitmap obstacle), the **distance to the closest intersection** is recorded as the range reading.

The simulated LRF is **noise-free** by default (deterministic), but Stage allows injection of Gaussian noise via the `noise` parameter for realism.

**Derivation — Single Beam Range:**

The laser emits $N$ beams indexed by $k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$. For each beam at angle $\alpha_k$, the ray is:

$$R_k(t) = P + t \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha_k \\ \sin\alpha_k \end{bmatrix}, \quad t \geq 0$$

The range is the **distance to the nearest hit**:

$$r_k = \min_{i} \left\{ \, t_i \mid t_i \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}, \, R_k(t_i) \in \text{Obstacle}_i \, \right\}$$

Equivalently, in the more common notation:

$$r_k = \min_{i} \, d(P, Q_i)$$

where $Q_i$ is the intersection point of the $k$-th ray with the $i$-th world obstacle.

**Angular Resolution:**

If the laser's field of view spans $[\alpha_{min}, \alpha_{max}]$ and the scan has $N$ samples, the angular resolution is:

$$\Delta\alpha = \frac{\alpha_{max} - \alpha_{min}}{N - 1}$$

For a 360° scan with $N = 360$ samples: $\Delta\alpha = 2\pi / 359 \approx 0.0175$ rad ≈ 1°.

**Mark distribution for (a):** 2 marks for concept explanation, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for angular resolution.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Single-Beam Range Computation

**Given:** $P = (1.0, 2.0)$ m, $\alpha = 0$ rad, vertical wall at $x = 4.0$ m, $y \in [0, 5.0]$ m.

**Step 1 — Parametric ray equation:**

$$R(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 + t \cos(0) \\ 2.0 + t \sin(0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 + t \\ 2.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Wall equation:**

The wall is the line $x = 4.0$ for $y \in [0, 5.0]$.

**Step 3 — Solve for intersection:**

Set the $x$-component of the ray equal to 4.0:

$$1.0 + t = 4.0 \implies t = 3.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Verify $y$ is within wall span:**

$$y = 2.0 \in [0, 5.0] \quad \checkmark \text{ Valid hit}$$

**Final Range Reading:**

$$\boxed{r = 3.0 \text{ m}}$$

**Mark distribution for (b):** 1 mark for ray equation, 2 marks for wall equation, 2 marks for solving intersection, 1 mark for $y$ verification, 1 mark for boxed final answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision (5-Minute Scan):**

- [ ] **Stage** = **2D** multi-robot simulator (NOT 3D).
- [ ] Belongs to the **Player/Stage Project** (also known as **Player/Stage/Gazebo**).
- [ ] Uses a **declarative `.world` file** for configuration (no programming required to set up a scene).
- [ ] **Three-tier architecture:** **World file → libstage kernel → stage_ros node → ROS topics**.
- [ ] Publishes standard ROS messages: **`sensor_msgs/LaserScan`**, **`nav_msgs/Odometry`**.
- [ ] Subscribes to velocity commands: **`geometry_msgs/Twist`** on `/cmd_vel`.
- [ ] Supports **three drive types:** `diff` (differential), `omni` (omnidirectional), `car` (Ackermann).
- [ ] **Differential drive equations:** $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$, $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$.
- [ ] **Omnidirectional equation:** uses the 2D **$SE(2)$ rotation matrix** on $(v_x, v_y, \omega)$.
- [ ] **Car/Ackermann equation:** $\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan(\delta)$.
- [ ] **2D LIDAR range** is computed by **ray-casting** and selecting the **minimum distance** to the nearest obstacle.
- [ ] **Angular resolution:** $\Delta\alpha = (\alpha_{max} - \alpha_{min}) / (N - 1)$.
- [ ] **Default simulation timestep:** `interval_sim 100` (ms) → **10 Hz loop**; can be set to 1000 ms (as-fast-as-possible) for batch experiments.
- [ ] **No GPU required** — Stage runs on a Raspberry Pi.
- [ ] **Use Stage when:** you need to simulate **many robots fast in 2D** (swarms, SLAM, path planning).
- [ ] **Use Gazebo when:** you need **realistic 3D physics** (manipulation, drone flight, vision).
- [ ] **Use RViz when:** you need to **visualize** ROS topic data (not a simulator).
- [ ] **Integration command (ROS 1):** `rosrun stage_ros stageros <world_file>.world`.
- [ ] **Pose representation:** $(x, y, \theta) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ — units: **meters** and **radians** (SI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Most-Examined Distinction (Worth 3 Marks Every Time):**
> *"Stage is to 2D multi-robot simulation what Gazebo is to 3D physics-based simulation."* Memorize this sentence verbatim.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gazebo and Other Simulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Robot Simulator?

A **Robot Simulator** is a software environment that emulates the real-world physics, kinematics, dynamics, and sensor behavior of a robotic system within a virtual 3D world. It allows developers to design, test, and validate robotic algorithms without requiring physical hardware, thereby reducing cost, risk, and development time.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *A robot simulator is a virtual environment that models robot dynamics, sensor data, and environmental interactions to facilitate algorithm development, testing, and validation prior to real-world deployment.*

### 1.2 What is Gazebo?

**Gazebo** is a free, open-source 3D dynamic simulator widely used in the robotics research community. Originally developed at the University of Southern California (USC) in 2004, Gazebo became the de-facto standard simulator tightly integrated with the Robot Operating System (ROS) and ROS 2. It can accurately simulate populations of robots in complex indoor and outdoor environments, with high-fidelity physics, realistic rendering, and a wide array of pluggable sensors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Fact:** Gazebo is the **officially recommended** 3D simulator for ROS and ROS 2 by Open Source Robotics Foundation (OSRF), now under the Linux Foundation.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are learning to drive a car. Before taking a real Ferrari onto a busy highway, you would first practice on a **driving simulator** (like those in gaming arcades). The simulator mimics:
- The steering wheel feedback (forces)
- Traffic on the road (other agents)
- Crash consequences (physics collisions)
- Speed and braking dynamics (kinematics)

**Gazebo is exactly this, but for robots.**

| Real Driving World | Gazebo Robot World |
|---|---|
| Steering wheel feedback | Force/Torque sensor simulation |
| Other cars on road | Multi-robot simulation |
| Crashing into a tree | Collision physics engine |
| Speedometer | Encoder / IMU sensor stream |
| Wind, rain, lighting | Environmental plugins, lighting models |

The robot algorithm thinks it is in the "real world" because the simulated sensor data, actuator commands, and physics responses are mathematically modeled to mirror reality.

### 1.4 Why Do We Need Simulators?

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Reasons for Simulation (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Cost Reduction** — No need to purchase expensive hardware
> 2. **Safety** — Test risky behaviors (drones, autonomous cars) without physical damage
> 3. **Reproducibility** — Exact same conditions can be replayed
> 4. **Speed** — Time can be sped up beyond real-time
> 5. **Scalability** — Simulate 1000+ agents in parallel
> 6. **Accessibility** — No mechanical setup, only a computer

### 1.5 Other Major Simulators (KTU Syllabus Scope)

| Simulator | Type | Key Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **Gazebo (Ignition / Gazebo Sim)** | Open-source 3D | ROS integration, modularity |
| **Webots** | Open-source / Commercial | Easy-to-use, cross-platform |
| **CoppeliaSim (formerly V-REP)** | Educational / Commercial | Distributed control, rapid prototyping |
| **Unity (ML-Agents)** | Game-engine based | Photorealistic rendering, ML training |
| **Unreal Engine (AirSim / CARLA)** | Game-engine based | High-fidelity graphics, autonomous driving |
| **MATLAB Simulink** | Mathworks tool | Model-Based Design, control systems |
| **PyBullet** | Open-source Python | Reinforcement learning research |
| **Isaac Sim (NVIDIA)** | GPU-accelerated | AI, photorealism, synthetic data |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

### 2.1 Gazebo Architecture — The Three-Layer Model

Gazebo's architecture is divided into **three logical layers**, which is a high-yield KTU question.

**Layer 1: Server (`gzserver`)**
- The heart of Gazebo
- Runs the physics simulation, generates sensor data, and executes the simulation loop
- Headless (no GUI) capable

**Layer 2: Client (`gzclient`)**
- The visual front-end / Graphical User Interface
- Renders the 3D scene, provides interaction tools (drag, rotate, zoom)
- Connects to the server over a network socket (even remotely)

**Layer 3: Communication Middleware**
- Uses **Google Protocol Buffers (Protobuf)** + **ZeroMQ / gRPC** messaging
- Enables remote simulation and decoupling of clients

### 2.2 Core Components of Gazebo

1. **Physics Engine (Pluggable):**
   - **ODE (Open Dynamics Engine)** — Default, rigid-body dynamics
   - **Bullet** — Real-time rigid-body, better for soft contact
   - **DART (Dynamic Animation and Robotics Toolkit)** — Advanced contact, used for legged robots
   - **Simbody** — Multibody dynamics for biomechanics

2. **Rendering Engine:**
   - **OGRE (Object-Oriented Graphics Rendering Engine)** — Default
   - Provides lighting, shadows, textures, materials

3. **Sensor Models:**
   - 2D/3D cameras, depth cameras, IMU, GPS, LiDAR, contact, force-torque

4. **Robot Description:**
   - **SDF (Simulation Description Format)** — Gazebo native
   - **URDF** — ROS native (limited; no closed kinematic chains)
   - **xacro** — Macro extension of URDF

5. **Plugins (The Power of Gazebo):**
   - **World Plugin** — Controls the entire simulation (e.g., reset, time scaling)
   - **Model Plugin** — Attached to a robot (e.g., custom controllers)
   - **Sensor Plugin** — Custom sensor behavior
   - **System Plugin** — Modifies Gazebo core at runtime

### 2.3 The SDF vs URDF — KTU High-Yield Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SDF is the native format of Gazebo; URDF is the native format of ROS. They are NOT the same.**

| Feature | URDF | SDF |
|---|---|---|
| **Native Tool** | ROS | Gazebo |
| **Closed Kinematic Chains** | ❌ Not supported | ✅ Supported |
| **Multiple Robots in One File** | ❌ | ✅ |
| **Environmental Description** | ❌ | ✅ (lights, ground, etc.) |
| **Sensor Definition** | ❌ (needs plugin tags) | ✅ Native |
| **File Extension** | `.urdf` / `.xacro` | `.sdf` |
| **Recommended Use** | ROS robot description | Full Gazebo worlds |

### 2.4 Simulation Loop — Discrete Time Stepping

Gazebo runs the world at a fixed **update rate** measured in Hertz (Hz). The physics step is governed by:

$$\Delta t = \frac{1}{f_{\text{update}}}$$

where $\Delta t$ is the simulation time step in seconds and $f_{\text{update}}$ is the real-time update factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-time Update Rate (RTF):** A value of 1.0 means the simulation runs at the same speed as reality. A value of 2.0 means it runs twice as fast.

### 2.5 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Formula | Expression / Description | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Simulation time step | $\Delta t = 1 / f_{\text{update}}$ | seconds |
| Real-Time Factor (RTF) | $\text{RTF} = t_{\text{sim}} / t_{\text{wall}}$ | dimensionless |
| Default update rate | $f_{\text{update}}$ | **1000 Hz** |
| Default gravity vector | $\vec{g} = (0, 0, -9.81)$ | m/s² |
| Default physics engine | ODE | — |
| Default rendering engine | OGRE | — |
| Inter-process communication | Protobuf + ZeroMQ | — |
| Server process | `gzserver` | — |
| Client process | `gzclient` | — |
| World file format | `.sdf` | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse `$f_{\text{update}}$` (physics step rate) with `RTF` (real-time speed factor). They are different parameters!

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

Gazebo is the backbone of:
- **DARPA SubT Challenge** — Subterranean robot exploration
- **NASA Space Robotics** — Lunar and Mars rover prototyping
- **Autonomous Driving Research** — Pre-silicon validation
- **Industrial Robotics** — Pick-and-place testing before factory deployment
- **Academia** — Teaching ROS without hardware

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Workflow

### 3.1 Exhaustive Workflow: Launching a Gazebo Simulation

**Step 1: Install Gazebo**
The package is installed alongside ROS, e.g., for ROS Noetic:
```bash
sudo apt install ros-noetic-gazebo-ros-pkgs
```

**Step 2: Source the ROS environment**
```bash
source /opt/ros/noetic/setup.bash
```

**Step 3: Launch an empty world**
This command starts `gzserver` and `gzclient` in parallel:
```bash
roslaunch gazebo_ros empty_world.launch
```

**Step 4: Spawn a robot using URDF or SDF**
```bash
rosrun gazebo_ros spawn_model -file turtlebot.urdf -urdf -x 0 -y 0 -z 0
```

**Step 5: Subscribe to sensor topics**
```bash
rostopic echo /camera/image_raw
```

**Step 6: Send velocity commands to the robot**
```bash
rostopic pub /cmd_vel geometry_msgs/Twist "linear: {x: 0.5}"
```

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Discrete Physics Integration

The Gazebo physics engine updates the world state using a numerical integrator. The simplest one is the **Euler integrator**, although Gazebo also supports semi-implicit and Runge-Kutta methods.

Given the state vector at time $t_n$:

$$
\vec{x}_n = \begin{bmatrix} \vec{p}_n \\ \vec{v}_n \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\vec{p}_n$ is position and $\vec{v}_n$ is velocity. The next state is computed as:

$$
\vec{v}_{n+1} = \vec{v}_n + \vec{a}(t_n) \cdot \Delta t
$$

$$
\vec{p}_{n+1} = \vec{p}_n + \vec{v}_{n+1} \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**
1. Read current state ($\vec{p}_n, \vec{v}_n$).
2. Compute acceleration $\vec{a}(t_n)$ using Newton's 2nd law: $\vec{a} = \vec{F}/m$.
3. Update velocity by adding acceleration × time step.
4. Update position by adding the NEW velocity × time step (semi-implicit Euler).
5. Repeat for the next $\Delta t$.

This discrete loop runs at $f_{\text{update}}$ Hz, generating the appearance of continuous physics.

### 3.3 Sensor Simulation Pipeline

A simulated camera in Gazebo follows this pipeline:

$$
\text{World Scene} \xrightarrow{\text{Render Engine (OGRE)}} \text{Image Buffer} \xrightarrow{\text{Sensor Plugin}} \text{ROS Topic} \xrightarrow{\text{Algorithm}} \text{Decision}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Markers at each transition:**
> - **World Scene:** 3D geometry with lights, textures, materials
> - **Render Engine:** Ray tracing or rasterization into a 2D pixel grid
> - **Image Buffer:** Raw pixel array (e.g., RGB at 640×480)
> - **Sensor Plugin:** Adds noise, distortion, publishes to ROS topic
> - **Algorithm:** Computer Vision / Deep Learning model processes the data

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Simulator (KTU Decision Framework)

| Requirement | Best Choice | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| ROS / ROS 2 integration | **Gazebo** | Native, officially supported |
| Photorealistic graphics for ML | **Isaac Sim / AirSim** | GPU ray tracing, synthetic data |
| Education, beginner friendly | **Webots** | GUI-based, no terminal needed |
| Industrial control design | **MATLAB Simulink** | Model-Based Design, code generation |
| Reinforcement learning research | **PyBullet / Isaac Gym** | Fast, Python API, GPU parallel |
| Rapid prototyping, distributed | **CoppeliaSim** | Multi-controller, scene scripting |
| Web-based / Cloud | **AWS RoboMaker / Unity** | Scalable cloud rendering |

### 3.5 The Ignition / Gazebo Sim Transition

In 2020, Open Robotics announced the next-generation simulator called **Ignition Gazebo**, which is now rebranded as **Gazebo Sim (Garden, Harmonic releases)**. It offers:
- Modular library-based architecture (gz-rendering, gz-physics, gz-sensors)
- Better ROS 2 support
- Improved sensor realism

$$
\text{Gazebo Classic (v11)} \xrightarrow{\text{2022 transition}} \text{Gazebo Sim (Harmonic, 2024+)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** When answering about "current Gazebo", mention both Gazebo Classic (legacy) and the modern Gazebo Sim (Harmonic) — examiners may give marks for awareness of the transition.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Gazebo Three-Layer Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userA[User / Developer]
    clientA[Client Layer - gzclient GUI]
    serverA[Server Layer - gzserver Physics]
    phyEng[Physics Engine - ODE Bullet DART]
    rendEng[Rendering Engine - OGRE]
    sensEng[Sensor Simulation Module]
    pluginLayer[Plugin Layer - World Model Sensor System]
    commA[Communication Middleware - Protobuf + ZeroMQ]

    userA --> clientA
    clientA --> commA
    serverA --> commA
    serverA --> phyEng
    serverA --> rendEng
    serverA --> sensEng
    serverA --> pluginLayer
    commA --> clientA
    commA --> serverA
```

### 4.2 ROS–Gazebo Integration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rosNode1[ROS Node - Algorithm]
    topic1[ROS Topic - cmd_vel]
    topic2[ROS Topic - odom]
    topic3[ROS Topic - camera]
    gzBridge[Gazebo-ROS Bridge - gazebo_ros_pkgs]
    gzServer[Gazebo Server - gzserver]
    robotSDF[SDF Robot Model]
    worldSDF[SDF World File]
    physicsEng[Physics Engine]

    rosNode1 --> topic1
    topic1 --> gzBridge
    gzBridge --> gzServer
    worldSDF --> gzServer
    robotSDF --> gzServer
    gzServer --> physicsEng
    gzServer --> gzBridge
    gzBridge --> topic2
    gzBridge --> topic3
    topic2 --> rosNode1
    topic3 --> rosNode1
```

### 4.3 Simulation Loop — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    t1[Step 1 - Read State pn vn]
    t2[Step 2 - Compute Force F from joints gravity contact]
    t3[Step 3 - Compute Acceleration a = F over m]
    t4[Step 4 - Integrate Velocity vn+1 = vn + a times dt]
    t5[Step 5 - Integrate Position pn+1 = pn + vn+1 times dt]
    t6[Step 6 - Update Sensors]
    t7[Step 7 - Publish ROS Topics]
    t8[Step 8 - Render Scene via OGRE]
    decision{Done}
    t1 --> t2 --> t3 --> t4 --> t5 --> t6 --> t7 --> t8 --> decision
    decision -- No - Continue Loop --> t1
    decision -- Yes - Exit --> end1[End Simulation]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram of Major Simulators

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[Robotics Simulators]
    root --> os[Open Source]
    root --> com[Commercial]

    os --> gaz[Gazebo - ROS Native]
    os --> web[Webots - Beginner Friendly]
    os --> cople[CoppeliaSim - Distributed]
    os --> pbul[PyBullet - RL Research]
    os --> unity[Unity ML-Agents]

    com --> mat[MATLAB Simulink - Control]
    com --> isaac[NVIDIA Isaac Sim - AI GPU]
    com --> airsim[AirSim - Drones and Cars]
    com --> carla[CARLA - Autonomous Driving]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*Define a robot simulator. List any four advantages of using Gazebo in robotics development.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A robot simulator is a software platform that emulates the physics, sensors, and environmental interactions of a robot in a virtual 3D world.

**Advantages of Gazebo (any four):**
1. **Free and open-source** — no licensing cost.
2. **Native ROS / ROS 2 integration** — seamless communication via `gazebo_ros_pkgs`.
3. **Pluggable physics engines** — supports ODE, Bullet, DART, Simbody.
4. **Rich sensor library** — cameras, LiDAR, IMU, GPS, force-torque, contact sensors.
5. **Multi-robot simulation** — thousands of agents can be simulated simultaneously.
6. **Realistic 3D rendering** — uses OGRE engine for lighting, shadows, textures.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any four advantages = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Explain the difference between URDF and SDF. Why is SDF preferred for Gazebo?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | URDF | SDF |
|---|---|---|
| Native tool | ROS | Gazebo |
| Closed chains | Not supported | Supported |
| Multi-robot | Not supported | Supported |
| Environment | No world description | Full world with lights, ground |
| Sensors | Requires plugins | Native tags |

**Why SDF is preferred for Gazebo (1 mark):**
SDF is the **native description format** of Gazebo. It supports closed kinematic chains, multiple robots, environment description, and embedded sensor definitions, which URDF cannot do alone. Gazebo parses SDF directly without needing conversion, making it the natural choice.

---

### PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-layer architecture of Gazebo**. Discuss the role of `gzserver` and `gzclient`. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **pluggable physics engines** supported by Gazebo. Compare ODE and DART engines. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Gazebo's three-layer architecture (7 Marks):**

**Layer 1 — Server (`gzserver`):** *(3 marks)*
- It is the core engine that runs the entire simulation.
- Performs physics integration, sensor data generation, and world updates.
- Can run **headless** (without a GUI), enabling remote and cloud simulations.
- Communicates with the outside world via Protobuf over a network socket.

**Layer 2 — Client (`gzclient`):** *(2 marks)*
- It is the **front-end graphical user interface**.
- Renders the 3D scene using the OGRE engine.
- Allows the user to interact with robots, visualize sensor data, and debug.
- Connects to `gzserver` over a TCP socket — can be on a different machine.

**Layer 3 — Communication Middleware:** *(2 marks)*
- Uses **Google Protobuf** for message serialization.
- Uses **ZeroMQ / gRPC** for transport.
- Enables decoupling of server and client, allowing **remote visualization** and **distributed simulation**.

**Block Diagram (Already shown in Section 4.1).** *(Refer Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Pluggable Physics Engines (7 Marks):**

Gazebo's physics engine is **pluggable**, meaning the user can swap engines without changing the world file. *(1 mark)*

**Supported engines (any 4, 1 mark each):**
1. **ODE (Open Dynamics Engine)** — Default; rigid-body dynamics; good for wheeled robots.
2. **Bullet** — Real-time; better for soft contacts and stacking.
3. **DART (Dynamic Animation and Robotics Toolkit)** — Advanced contact resolution; preferred for legged robots and manipulation.
4. **Simbody** — High-fidelity multibody dynamics; used for biomechanics and humanoids.

**Comparison: ODE vs DART (3 marks):**

| Feature | ODE | DART |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Very fast | Slightly slower |
| Contact model | Hard contact (penalty method) | Soft & hard contact (LCP / constraint-based) |
| Friction model | Approximate | More accurate |
| Best for | Wheeled robots, drones | Legged robots, manipulation, humanoids |
| Default in Gazebo | Yes | No (must be configured) |

**Conclusion:** ODE is faster but DART offers superior realism for complex contact-rich scenarios like walking robots.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Compare **Gazebo, Webots, and CoppeliaSim** as robot simulators, covering licensing, ROS support, and primary use case. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **SDF file structure** for a simple robot in Gazebo. Explain the role of the `<include>`, `<model>`, `<link>`, `<joint>`, and `<sensor>` tags. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 for licensing, 1 for ROS, 1 for use-case, plus detail):**

| Feature | Gazebo | Webots | CoppeliaSim |
|---|---|---|---|
| **License** | Open-source (Apache 2.0) | Open-source (Apache 2.0) | Educational free + Commercial Pro |
| **ROS Support** | Native (1st class) | Good (ROS 1 & 2 plugins) | Good (ROS Interface plugin) |
| **Primary Use Case** | Research, ROS-based projects | Education, rapid prototyping | Distributed control, education |
| **Physics Engine** | ODE / Bullet / DART | ODE (built-in) | Bullet / ODE / Vortex |
| **GUI Style** | Minimal, technical | User-friendly, IDE-like | Scene-graph, drag-and-drop |
| **Language Support** | C++, Python | C++, Python, Java | Lua, Python, C++, MATLAB |
| **Industry Adoption** | Very high (research) | High (academia) | Medium (education) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Gazebo dominates research and ROS-based projects; Webots is the most beginner-friendly; CoppeliaSim excels at distributed control and rapid prototyping.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**SDF File Structure (7 Marks):**

A Gazebo SDF file has a hierarchical XML structure. *(1 mark for hierarchy diagram)*

```xml
<sdf version='1.6'>
  <world name='default'>
    <include>
      <uri>model://ground_plane</uri>
    </include>

    <model name='my_robot'>
      <pose>0 0 0.1 0 0 0</pose>
      <link name='chassis'>
        <inertial>
          <mass>1.0</mass>
          <inertia>...</inertia>
        </inertial>
        <visual>...</visual>
        <collision>...</collision>
        <sensor name='camera' type='camera'>
          <camera>...</camera>
        </sensor>
      </link>

      <joint name='wheel_joint' type='revolute'>
        <parent>chassis</parent>
        <child>wheel</child>
        <axis>0 0 1</axis>
      </joint>
    </model>
  </world>
</sdf>
```

**Role of tags (1 mark each):**
- **`<include>`** — Reuses pre-built models (e.g., ground plane, sun).
- **`<model>`** — Defines a robot or object with a name and pose.
- **`<link>`** — A rigid body with mass, inertia, visual, and collision properties.
- **`<joint>`** — Connects two links; type can be `revolute`, `prismatic`, `fixed`, `ball`, etc.
- **`<sensor>`** — Attaches a simulated sensor to a link (camera, IMU, LiDAR).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing `<sdf>` with `<urdf>` — students often write URDF tags in SDF and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. Forgetting that `<sensor>` MUST be inside a `<link>` — not at the model level.
> 3. Writing `mass` outside `<inertial>` block — invalid SDF.
> 4. Writing only a description, not the actual tags — marks are awarded for correct tag names.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Bullet Points (Last-Minute Revision):**

- **Gazebo** = 3D dynamics simulator, official ROS companion.
- **Three layers:** `gzserver` (physics), `gzclient` (GUI), Middleware (Protobuf + ZeroMQ).
- **Pluggable physics engines:** ODE (default), Bullet, DART, Simbody.
- **Rendering engine:** OGRE.
- **SDF** = Gazebo native; **URDF** = ROS native. SDF is more powerful.
- **SDF supports:** closed chains, multi-robot, environment, sensors — URDF does NOT.
- **Real-Time Factor (RTF):** ratio of simulated time to wall-clock time. RTF = 1.0 means real-time.
- **Default update rate:** 1000 Hz; default gravity: $(0, 0, -9.81)$ m/s².
- **Plugin types:** World, Model, Sensor, System.
- **Modern Gazebo:** Gazebo Sim (Harmonic) replaced Gazebo Classic (v11).
- **Alternatives:** Webots (beginner), CoppeliaSim (distributed), Isaac Sim (AI/GPU), PyBullet (RL), AirSim/CARLA (driving), MATLAB Simulink (control design).
- **Gazebo bridges to ROS via:** `gazebo_ros_pkgs` — provides `spawn_model`, `ros_control`, and topic bridging.
- **Why simulate?** Cost, safety, reproducibility, speed, scalability, accessibility.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maps in ROS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Maps in ROS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the Robot Operating System (ROS), a **Map** is a formal, structured, probabilistic spatial representation of the robot's operating environment, stored, published, and consumed as standardized ROS messages (`nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`) to enable autonomous navigation, localization, and path planning. Maps form the foundational data layer of the ROS Navigation Stack (`nav_stack`) and the `move_base` node. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a ROS map is defined as a **two-dimensional occupancy grid** composed of a discrete matrix of cells, where each cell holds a probabilistic belief value indicating whether that spatial region is free, occupied, or unknown. The map is published on the `/map` topic and served via the `/static_map` and `/dynamic_map` ROS services. ### 1.2 Key Terminology | Term | Definition | |---|---| | Occupancy Grid | 2D grid where each cell represents the probability of occupation | | Resolution | Size of one grid cell in meters (e.g., 0.05 m/cell) | | Origin | Pose (x, y, yaw) of the map's lower-left corner in the world frame | | Costmap | A derived map used for path planning that adds inflation around obstacles | | Static Map | A pre-built, unchanging map loaded from a YAML + PGM file pair | | Dynamic Map | A continuously updated map reflecting sensor-detected obstacles | | SLAM | Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (process of building the map) | | PGM (Portable Gray Map) | Image format used to store the raw occupancy grid bitmap | ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Building Floor Plan** > Imagine a **mall directory map** placed at the entrance. The map divides the entire mall into a grid of squares. Each square is either: > - **White (Free)** — you can walk there (probability = 0%) > - **Black (Wall)** — blocked, cannot pass (probability = 100%) > - **Gray (Unknown)** — never explored, status uncertain (probability = -1) > > ROS maps work exactly this way. A robot uses a "mall directory" to know where walls, free walkways, and unexplored regions exist. The robot's planner (the "mall visitor") then finds a path through free cells while avoiding occupied ones. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Core Distinction** > A *Map* in ROS ≠ just an image. It is a **structured ROS message** (`nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`) containing: > 1. **Header** (timestamp + frame_id) > 2. **MapMetaData** (resolution, width, height, origin) > 3. **Data array** (int8[] row-major cell probabilities) > > Understanding this 3-part message structure is the **single highest-weight KTU question pattern** for this topic. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants - **Map cell value range:** $-1$ to $100$, where $-1$ = **unknown**, $0$ = **free**, $100$ = **fully occupied**. - **Standard resolution:** **0.05 m/cell** (5 cm), balancing detail vs. memory. - **Inflation radius (costmap):** typically **0.55 m** (≈ robot radius + safety margin). - **Map frame ID:** usually `map` (world-fixed) and `odom` (odometry frame) — the `tf` tree links them via `map → odom → base_link`. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Architecture of a ROS Map Message The ROS map is not a single file — it is a **2-file pair**: | File | Purpose | Format | |---|---|---| | `my_map.yaml` | Map metadata (resolution, origin, thresholds) | YAML text | | `my_map.pgm` | Pixel data — grayscale image of the map | PGM image | #### 2.1.1 Sample YAML File Structure ```yaml image: my_map.pgm resolution: 0.050000 origin: [-10.0, -10.0, 0.0] negate: 0 occupied_thresh: 0.65 free_thresh: 0.25 ``` > [!NOTE] > **occupancy_thresh** = probability above which a cell is treated as **occupied** (default 0.65). > **free_thresh** = probability below which a cell is treated as **free** (default 0.25). ### 2.2 The OccupancyGrid Message Anatomy The `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message is the canonical ROS data structure for maps. It is composed of three logical blocks: 1. **std_msgs/Header** — `seq`, `stamp`, `frame_id` (typically `map`). 2. **nav_msgs/MapMetaData** — contains: - `time` (acquisition time) - `resolution` (meters/cell) - `width` (cells, integer) - `height` (cells, integer) - `origin` (geometry_msgs/Pose — the map's pose in the world frame) 3. **int8[] data** — flat row-major array, length = `width × height`, each entry in $[-1, 100]$. ### 2.3 Coordinate Transformations (Why & How) #### 2.3.1 Why It Matters A robot lives in the **world frame** (continuous, infinite). The map lives in the **grid frame** (discrete, finite). Every planner must convert between them. KTU frequently tests this transformation. #### 2.3.2 How the Conversion Works **Step 1 — World → Grid (Continuous → Index)** Given a world point $(x_w, y_w)$, compute its grid cell $(i, j)$: 1. Subtract the origin to get coordinates relative to the map's lower-left corner. 2. Divide by the resolution to get cell coordinates. 3. Round down (floor) to get integer cell indices. **Step 2 — Grid → World (Index → Continuous)** Given a cell index $(i, j)$, recover the world center of that cell: 1. Multiply the index by the resolution. 2. Add the resolution $/ 2$ to land at the **cell center** (not corner). 3. Add the origin to express in world frame. > [!IMPORTANT] > The `$+ 0.5 \times \text{resolution}$` step is the most commonly forgotten part in KTU exams. It shifts the returned point from the cell's **lower-left corner** to the cell's **geometric center**, which is what path planners assume. ### 2.4 Costmaps — Planning-Ready Maps A raw occupancy grid is binary in spirit (free / occupied), but the planner needs a **graded** map where being *near* an obstacle is slightly costly. This graded version is the **Costmap**. | Layer | Purpose | |---|---| | **Static Map Layer** | Loads the pre-built occupancy grid. | | **Obstacle Layer** | Marks real-time sensor hits as occupied. | | **Inflation Layer** | Adds a "bubble" of increasing cost around obstacles for safe clearance. | | **Other Layers** | Footprint, voxels, range sensor data (plugin-based). | The cost function is typically an exponential decay: $$ \text{cost}(d) = \begin{cases} \text{LETHAL\_COST}, & d < d_{\text{inscribed}} \\ \text{LETHAL\_COST} \cdot \exp(-k \cdot (d - d_{\text{inscribed}})), & d_{\text{inscribed}} \le d \le d_{\text{inflated}} \\ 0, & d > d_{\text{inflated}} \end{cases} $$ where $d$ is the distance from the obstacle, $d_{\text{inscribed}}$ is the inscribed radius of the robot, and $d_{\text{inflated}}$ is the user-set inflation radius. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Value | Variable Definitions | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Grid Index (row) | $i = \left\lfloor (y_w - o_y) / r \right\rfloor$ | $y_w$ = world Y, $o_y$ = origin Y, $r$ = resolution | dimensionless | | Grid Index (col) | $j = \left\lfloor (x_w - o_x) / r \right\rfloor$ | $x_w$ = world X, $o_x$ = origin X, $r$ = resolution | dimensionless | | World Coordinate from Cell | $x_w = o_x + (j + 0.5) \cdot r$ | cell center offset included | meters | | World Coordinate from Cell | $y_w = o_y + (i + 0.5) \cdot r$ | cell center offset included | meters | | Flat Data Array Index | $k = i \cdot w + j$ | $w$ = width, $i$ = row, $j$ = col | dimensionless | | Cell Value Mapping | $v \in \{-1, 0, 1, \dots, 100\}$ | $-1$ unknown, $0$ free, $100$ occupied | integer | | PGM $\to$ Occupancy | $v = 100 \cdot (1 - p/255)$ | $p$ = pixel intensity (0 = black) | integer | | Map Physical Width | $W_m = w \cdot r$ | world meters per axis | meters | | Inflation Decay | $k = \alpha / (d_{\text{inflated}} - d_{\text{inscribed}})$ | $\alpha$ = decay factor (default 10) | 1/meters | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Row-major storage rule:** in `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid.data`, index $k = i \cdot \text{width} + j$, where $i$ is the **row** (Y-direction) and $j$ is the **column** (X-direction). The first row stored is the **bottom row** of the map (lowest Y). ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs)** in warehouses (Amazon Kiva) use ROS maps for fleet routing. - **Self-driving cars** use a hierarchy: 2D grid for routing, 3D voxel grid (`octomap`) for local obstacle avoidance. - **Disaster response robots** build maps in real-time using SLAM (`gmapping`, `cartographer`, `slam_toolbox`). - **Service robots** (vacuum, hospital delivery) use static maps + dynamic obstacle layer to update for moving people. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: World-to-Grid Coordinate Mapping We will derive the canonical transformation from a continuous world point $(x_w, y_w)$ to a discrete grid cell $(j, i)$. **Given:** - Map resolution: $r$ (meters/cell) - Map origin: $(o_x, o_y)$ in world frame - Map width: $w$ cells; map height: $h$ cells **Goal:** Find the cell column $j$ and row $i$ such that the world point lies in cell $(i, j)$. --- **Step 1 — Translate the world point to the map's local frame** $$ x_{local} = x_w - o_x $$ $$ y_{local} = y_w - o_y $$ *Logic:* We subtract the origin so the map's lower-left corner becomes $(0, 0)$ — a local coordinate frame aligned with the grid. --- **Step 2 — Convert meters to cell units** $$ x_{cell} = \frac{x_{local}}{r} $$ $$ y_{cell} = \frac{y_{local}}{r} $$ *Logic:* Dividing by the resolution converts meters to "cell counts." A point 0.5 m from the origin with $r = 0.05$ m/cell is 10 cells away. --- **Step 3 — Apply the floor function to obtain integer indices** $$ j = \left\lfloor x_{cell} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{x_w - o_x}{r} \right\rfloor $$ $$ i = \left\lfloor y_{cell} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{y_w - o_y}{r} \right\rfloor $$ *Logic:* The floor function maps the continuous cell coordinate to the **cell index** in which the point lies. Truncation toward $-\infty$ ensures negative offsets (points left of the origin) map to valid negative grid indices, which we then clamp. --- **Step 4 — Bounds checking (mandatory in code)** $$ j = \max(0, \min(j, w - 1)) $$ $$ i = \max(0, \min(i, h - 1)) $$ *Logic:* If the robot is outside the map, the index is clamped to the nearest edge cell to avoid out-of-bounds memory access. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: PGM Pixel Value → Occupancy Probability When loading a PGM file, each pixel is grayscale $p \in [0, 255]$. We must map this to an integer occupancy $v \in [0, 100]$. **Step 1 — Invert the intensity (white = free, black = occupied)** $$ p_{inv} = 255 - p $$ *Logic:* PGM convention: 0 = black, 255 = white. A black pixel (wall) must become high occupancy. We invert so black = high. **Step 2 — Normalize to $[0, 1]$** $$ p_{occ} = \frac{p_{inv}}{255} $$ *Logic:* This is the normalized occupancy probability, with 0 = certainly free and 1 = certainly occupied. **Step 3 — Scale to $[0, 100]$** $$ v = \text{round}(100 \cdot p_{occ}) $$ *Logic:* ROS uses integer $[0, 100]$ for compactness. Rounding avoids fractional storage. **Step 4 — Apply thresholds from YAML** $$ v_{final} = \begin{cases} 100, & v/100 \geq \text{occupied\_thresh} \\ 0, & v/100 \leq \text{free\_thresh} \\ v, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ *Logic:* Intermediate gray values are preserved (e.g., partially occupied), but the YAML thresholds (default 0.65, 0.25) snap extreme values for planner clarity. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What | Scenario | Map Type | ROS Tool | Update Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | Pre-built warehouse | Static Occupancy Grid | `map_server` | One-time load | | Real-time obstacle avoidance | Costmap2D | `costmap_2d` | 5–20 Hz | | Building a map on the fly | Live SLAM Grid | `gmapping`, `slam_toolbox` | Sensor-rate | | 3D point cloud mapping | Octomap | `octomap_server` | Sensor-rate | | Saving a SLAM result | Static (post-process) | `map_saver` | Manual trigger | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap:** Students often confuse **map** with **costmap**. A **map** is the raw occupancy grid; a **costmap** is a planner-specific derivative. Costmap $\neq$ Map. The costmap combines the static map with live sensor data and inflation — it is the planner's working memory. ### 3.4 Worked Example: Index Calculation > **Problem:** A robot is at world position $(2.35,\ 3.78)$ m. The map has `resolution = 0.05`, `origin = [-5.0, -5.0, 0.0]`, `width = 200`, `height = 200`. Find the grid cell index and the flat-array position. **Solution:** $$ x_{local} = 2.35 - (-5.0) = 7.35 $$ $$ y_{local} = 3.78 - (-5.0) = 8.78 $$ $$ j = \left\lfloor 7.35 / 0.05 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 147.0 \right\rfloor = 147 $$ $$ i = \left\lfloor 8.78 / 0.05 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 175.6 \right\rfloor = 175 $$ $$ k = i \cdot w + j = 175 \cdot 200 + 147 = 35147 $$ The robot is in **row 175, column 147**, and that cell's occupancy value lives at flat index **35147** in the `data[]` array. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: ROS Map Data Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[SLAM Node
gmapping or cartographer] -->|sensor_msgs/LaserScan| B[Map Builder] B -->|nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid| C[Map Server] C -->|/map topic| D[Costmap 2D] C -->|/static_map service| E[External Client] D -->|costmap_2d msgs| F[Move Base Planner] D -->|local costmap| G[Local Planner] D -->|global costmap| H[Global Planner] F -->|geometry_msgs/Twist| I[Robot Base] I -->|tf| A J[YAML Metadata File] --> C K[PGM Image File] --> C ``` ### 4.2 Layered Architecture of a Costmap ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph costmap["Costmap 2D Stack"] L1[Static Map Layer
from map_server] L2[Obstacle Layer
real-time sensor data] L3[Inflation Layer
exponential decay] L4[Other Plugins
voxel, footprint] end L1 --> M[Merged Costmap] L2 --> M L3 --> M L4 --> M M --> N1[Global Costmap
used by global planner] M --> N2[Local Costmap
used by local planner] ``` ### 4.3 OccupancyGrid Message Structure ```mermaid flowchart TB A[nav_msgs OccupancyGrid] --> B[std_msgs Header
stamp and frame_id] A --> C[MapMetaData] A --> D[int8 array DATA
length equals width times height] C --> E[resolution
meters per cell] C --> F[width in cells] C --> G[height in cells] C --> H[origin pose
x y z and roll pitch yaw] ``` ### 4.4 Coordinate Frame Transformation Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[World Frame
map] -->|tf static transform| B[Odom Frame] B -->|tf dynamic transform| C[Base Link Frame] C -->|sensor offsets| D[Laser Scan Frame] A --> E[Map Grid Cells
i j] A --> F[Costmap Layers] E --> G[Path Planner] F --> G G --> H[Velocity Command
cmd_vel] ``` > [!NOTE] > The `tf` tree (`map → odom → base_link`) is the **skeleton** of ROS navigation. The map provides the static reference, odom accumulates drift-free dead reckoning over short distances, and the local planner continuously reconciles them via sensor feedback. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define the ROS `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message. What do the cell values $-1$, $0$, and $100$ represent?** **Model Answer:** The `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message is the standard ROS data structure for representing a 2D map of the environment. It contains three components: 1. **Header** — timestamp and frame_id (`map`). 2. **MapMetaData** — `resolution` (meters/cell), `width`, `height` (in cells), and `origin` pose. 3. **Data** — a flat `int8[]` array of length `width × height`. **Cell value meanings:** | Value | Meaning | |---|---| | $-1$ | **Unknown** — the sensor has no information about this cell. | | $0$ | **Free** — the cell is certainly traversable. | | $100$ | **Occupied** — the cell contains an obstacle. | *(3 marks — 1 for message structure, 1 for $-1$/unknown, 1 for 0/100 distinction.)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **List and briefly explain the function of any three layers in the ROS costmap_2d package.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Static Map Layer** — loads the pre-built occupancy grid from `map_server` as the base costmap. 2. **Obstacle Layer** — ingests real-time sensor data (laser scans, point clouds) and marks newly detected obstacles. 3. **Inflation Layer** — adds a graded "bubble" of increasing cost around obstacles to ensure the robot keeps safe clearance. 4. **Voxel Layer** *(optional)* — 3D obstacle representation for tilted or elevated sensors. 5. **Footprint Layer** — marks the cells under the robot's physical footprint as occupied. *(3 marks — 1 mark per correctly explained layer.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Question Paper]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the ROS `map_server` package. Discuss the role of the YAML metadata file and the PGM image file. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A ROS map has `resolution = 0.1` m/cell, `origin = [-2.0, -2.0, 0.0]`, `width = 100`, `height = 100`. The robot is at world position $(4.7,\ 5.3)$ m. Calculate the grid cell row, column, and flat-array index. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution — Part (a)** **Block Diagram of `map_server`:** ``` [YAML metadata file] ──┐ ├──> map_server node ──> /map topic (OccupancyGrid) [PGM image file] ────┘ └──> /static_map service ``` **Roles:** - **YAML file:** Stores metadata — image path, resolution, origin pose, occupancy/free thresholds, negate flag. It does **not** contain cell data. - **PGM file:** A grayscale image where pixel intensity encodes occupancy. Black (0) = occupied, white (255) = free. The `map_server` converts this into the `int8[]` data array on the fly using the formula: $$ v = 100 \cdot (1 - p/255) $$ where $p$ is the pixel intensity. **Workflow on launch:** 1. `roslaunch map_server map_server.launch` reads the YAML path. 2. Loads the referenced PGM via OpenCV. 3. Publishes the assembled `OccupancyGrid` on `/map` continuously at 1 Hz. 4.
