Offers the `static_map` service for one-shot retrieval by clients like `amcl` or `rviz`. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for YAML role, 2 for PGM role, 1 for service/topic distinction.)* --- ##### **Model Solution — Part (b)** **Given:** - $r = 0.1$ m/cell - $(o_x, o_y) = (-2.0, -2.0)$ - $(x_w, y_w) = (4.7, 5.3)$ - $w = h = 100$ **Step 1 — Compute local coordinates:** $$ x_{local} = 4.7 - (-2.0) = 6.7 $$ $$ y_{local} = 5.3 - (-2.0) = 7.3 $$ **Step 2 — Convert to cell coordinates:** $$ x_{cell} = 6.7 / 0.1 = 67.0 $$ $$ y_{cell} = 7.3 / 0.1 = 73.0 $$ **Step 3 — Apply floor function:** $$ j = \left\lfloor 67.0 \right\rfloor = 67 \quad \text{(column)} $$ $$ i = \left\lfloor 73.0 \right\rfloor = 73 \quad \text{(row)} $$ **Step 4 — Verify bounds:** $0 \le 67, 73 \le 99$ — both valid. **Step 5 — Flat-array index:** $$ k = i \cdot w + j = 73 \cdot 100 + 67 = 7367 $$ **Final Answer:** The robot occupies **row 73, column 67**, and the corresponding cell value is stored at **flat index 7367** in the `data[]` array. *(7 marks: 1 setup, 2 local, 2 cell, 1 flat index, 1 unit/consistency check.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]** **(a)** Differentiate between a **static map** and a **costmap** in ROS. Why is the costmap essential for the `move_base` local planner? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the inflation layer of a ROS costmap with the standard cost decay formula. How is the inscribed radius computed for a circular robot of radius $0.3$ m with an inflation radius of $0.55$ m? **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution — Part (a)** | Aspect | Static Map | Costmap | |---|---|---| | Source | Pre-built YAML + PGM | Derived from static + live sensors | | Updates | One-time at load | Continuous (typically 5–10 Hz) | | Data Type | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` plus layer plugins | | Values | $\{-1, 0, 100\}$ | $[0, 255]$ graded | | Use | Global reference | Path planning buffer | **Why costmap is essential for local planner:** The local planner (`base_local_planner` / `dwa_local_planner` / `teb_local_planner`) needs to: 1. React to **dynamic obstacles** (people walking, chairs moved) not present in the static map. 2. Enforce a **safety margin** around obstacles — the static map is binary and would let the planner graze walls. 3. Use **graded cost gradients** for sampling-based planners like DWA, which search over velocity space and need smooth cost fields to score trajectories. Without the costmap, the local planner would either collide (no safety margin) or freeze (no smooth gradient to score trajectories). *(7 marks: 3 for table, 4 for necessity argument.)* --- ##### **Model Solution — Part (b)** **Inflation Layer — Concept:** The inflation layer takes the binary obstacle map and converts it into a **smooth, monotonically decreasing cost field** as you move away from obstacles. This is what allows planners to find paths that keep safe distance. **Standard Cost Decay Formula:** $$ \text{cost}(d) = \begin{cases} \text{LETHAL\_COST} = 254, & d < d_{\text{inscribed}} \\ \text{LETHAL\_COST} \cdot \exp(-k \cdot (d - d_{\text{inscribed}})), & d_{\text{inscribed}} \le d \le d_{\text{inflated}} \\ 0, & d > d_{\text{inflated}} \end{cases} $$ where the decay constant $k$ is computed as: $$ k = \frac{\alpha}{d_{\text{inflated}} - d_{\text{inscribed}}} $$ with $\alpha$ being a configurable scaling factor (default $\alpha = 10$). **Numerical Computation for the given robot:** - Robot radius: $r_{robot} = 0.3$ m - Inflation radius: $d_{\text{inflated}} = 0.55$ m - Inscribed radius for a circular robot: $d_{\text{inscribed}} = r_{robot} = 0.3$ m **Compute the decay constant:** $$ k = \frac{10}{0.55 - 0.3} = \frac{10}{0.25} = 40 \ \text{m}^{-1} $$ **Cost at $d = 0.4$ m (a point inside the inflation ring):** $$ \text{cost}(0.4) = 254 \cdot \exp(-40 \cdot (0.4 - 0.3)) = 254 \cdot e^{-4} \approx 254 \cdot 0.0183 \approx 4.65 $$ This cell would carry a cost of **5** (rounded), which the planner treats as traversable but undesirable — pushing the robot back toward the free-space centerline. *(7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for explanation, 3 for numerical computation.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting the $+0.5$ in world-from-cell conversion.** Marks deducted if the cell *corner* is returned instead of the cell *center*. Always include the half-cell offset. > 2. **Confusing row and column.** In ROS, $i$ is **row (Y-direction)** and $j$ is **column (X-direction)**. A common slip-up is to write $k = j \cdot h + i$ — this is **wrong**; the correct formula is $k = i \cdot w + j$. > 3. **Ignoring the `-1` (unknown) cell value.** Students often say the range is $0$ to $100$. KTU explicitly tests the unknown state — losing 1 mark per omission. > 4. **Map vs Costmap confusion.** A **map** is a *static snapshot*; a **costmap** is the *planner's working buffer*. Mixing their purposes costs 2–3 marks. > 5. **Writing the cost formula without the LETHAL region.** A piecewise definition with the $d < d_{\text{inscribed}}$ case is **required**, not optional. Examiners deduct full marks if only the exponential part is written. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Maps in ROS** - **Message type:** `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` — the canonical ROS map structure. - **Three components:** Header, MapMetaData, int8[] data. - **Cell values:** $-1$ unknown, $0$ free, $100$ occupied. - **File pair:** YAML (metadata) + PGM (pixel data) loaded by `map_server`. - **Topics & services:** - Publishes: `/map` (topic) - Serves: `/static_map` (service), `/dynamic_map` (service) - Saves: via `map_saver` from `map_server` package. - **Resolution:** meters per cell; typical $= 0.05$ m. - **Origin:** pose of the map's lower-left corner in the world frame. - **Flat-array index:** $k = i \cdot w + j$ (row-major, bottom-row first). - **World-to-cell:** $j = \lfloor (x_w - o_x) / r \rfloor$, $i = \lfloor (y_w - o_y) / r \rfloor$. - **Cell-to-world:** $x_w = o_x + (j + 0.5) r$, $y_w = o_y + (i + 0.5) r$. - **Costmap layers:** Static Map, Obstacle, Inflation, Footprint, Voxel. - **Inflation cost:** piecewise exponential decay with $k = \alpha / (d_{\text{inflated}} - d_{\text{inscribed}})$. - **Cost values:** range $[0, 254]$; $254$ = LETHAL, $253$ = INSCRIBED, $1$–$252$ = graded, $0$ = FREE. - **Static vs Dynamic:** Static loaded once from file; costmap is dynamic and updated from sensors. - **Common packages:** `map_server`, `map_saver`, `costmap_2d`, `navfn` (global planner), `amcl` (localization on a static map). - **Key ROS commands:** - `rosrun map_server map_server my_map.yaml` - `rosrun map_server map_saver -f my_map` - `rosmsg show nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` - `rviz` → add "Map" display, subscribe to `/map`. - **TF chain for navigation:** `map → odom → base_link → sensors`. - **Industrial relevance:** Used in AMRs, AGVs, autonomous cars, service robots, and drones.

topics.title: Recording Data with rosbag
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recording Data with rosbag

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **rosbag** (also called *rosbag 2* in ROS 2) is a command-line tool in the Robot Operating System (ROS) ecosystem used to **record, store, and play back** message data published on ROS topics. It captures the stream of serialized messages exchanged between nodes and persists them in a **bag file** (typically a `.db3` file in ROS 2 or a `.bag` file in ROS 1) for offline analysis, debugging, simulation, and dataset creation.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context for **OERAT722**, rosbag is positioned as a critical **data acquisition and logging utility** that allows engineers to:
- Decouple **real-time execution** from **post-hoc analysis**.
- Reproduce deterministic experimental conditions.
- Build reproducible datasets for machine-learning pipelines in robotics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of OERAT722 (Introduction to Robot Operating System) introduces *Recording Data with rosbag* as part of the **TF, URDF, and Launch Files** cluster. Mastery of rosbag is mandatory for completing laboratory assignments in *Robot Kinematics & Dynamics simulation* and *Autonomous Navigation*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are watching a **live cricket match** on television. You cannot replay or analyze a specific delivery later unless you **record** the match on a DVR. rosbag works exactly like a **DVR for ROS**:

| Real-World Analogy | rosbag Equivalent |
|---|---|
| DVR recording a TV broadcast | `ros2 bag record` capturing topics |
| DVR playback of a match | `ros2 bag play` replaying topics |
| Match statistics (runs, overs) | `ros2 bag info` showing topic counts |
| Trimming a recording | Filtering by topic or time slice |
| Compressed recording file | `-q` / `--compression-mode` option |

When robots operate in the real world, sensors (LIDAR, cameras, IMUs) publish thousands of messages per second. Without rosbag, you would have to **re-run the entire experiment** every time you want to debug a node — which is expensive, non-deterministic, and sometimes impossible. rosbag solves this by **persisting the message stream**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Constants & Metrics)

- **Message**: The atomic data unit on a ROS topic (e.g., `sensor_msgs/msg/LaserScan`).
- **Topic**: The named bus over which messages flow (e.g., `/scan`, `/odom`).
- **Bag File**: The serialized database file (`.db3` for ROS 2, `.bag` for ROS 1) holding recorded messages.
- **Serialization Format**: Default is **CDR (Common Data Representation)** in ROS 2, equivalent to **MD5-summed binary records** in ROS 1.
- **Metadata File**: `metadata.yaml` inside the bag directory that stores topic names, message types, counts, and time ranges.

> [!TIP]
> A single 10-minute recording of a Velodyne VLP-16 LIDAR at 10 Hz easily exceeds **1.2 GB**. Always plan your storage accordingly and prefer **compression** (`-q` in ros2 bag) for field deployments.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of rosbag

The rosbag workflow consists of **four canonical operations**:

1. **Record** – Subscribes to topics and writes messages to a bag file.
2. **Play** – Reads a bag file and republishes messages on the same topic names.
3. **Info** – Reports metadata (number of messages, duration, topic types) without playback.
4. **Filter** – Extracts a subset (by topic, time, or regex) into a new bag.

### 2.1.1 Recording Pipeline (How it Works)

The rosbag recording process establishes a **subscriber node** internally. For every topic the user requests:

```
Publisher (Sensor) ──► ROS DDS Middleware ──► /scan, /odom, /imu
                                                  │
                                                  ▼
                                       rosbag (Subscriber)
                                                  │
                                                  ▼
                                       SQLite3 Database (.db3)
```

Key internal mechanics:
- rosbag registers a **subscription** with the same QoS profile as the publisher (or a compatible one).
- Each incoming message is **serialized** to CDR and inserted into a **SQLite3 table**.
- A separate **metadata.yaml** file is updated continuously to keep the index consistent.

### 2.1.2 Playback Pipeline

During playback, rosbag becomes a **publisher node**:
- Reads rows from the SQLite3 database.
- Deserializes each message back to its ROS type.
- Publishes the message to the corresponding topic at the recorded timestamp.
- Default playback speed is **1.0×**; can be throttled with `--rate`.

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Command / Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Record all topics | `ros2 bag record -a` | Subscribes to every active topic |
| Record specific topics | `ros2 bag record /scan /odom` | Only the listed topics |
| Set output directory | `-o <name>` | Names the bag folder |
| Maximum duration | `-d <seconds>` | Stops after N seconds |
| Message count limit | `--max-messages <N>` | Stops after N messages per topic |
| File size limit | `--max-bag-size <bytes>` | Splits when file exceeds N bytes |
| Compression mode | `--compression-mode file` (or `message`) | Uses zstd compression |
| Compression format | `--compression-format zstd` | Default algorithm |
| Playback rate | `ros2 bag play --rate 2.0` | Play at 2× speed |
| Pause on start | `--start-paused` | Begin in paused state |
| Loop playback | `--loop` | Infinite replay |
| Information | `ros2 bag info <bag>` | Show topic, count, duration |
| YAML info | `ros2 bag info -y <bag>` | Machine-readable output |
| Filter | `ros2 bag filter` (ROS 1) / `rosbag2 filter` | Extract subset |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In ROS 2, the package is `rosbag2` (not `rosbag`). Students often mistakenly use `ros2 bag record /scan` when they should be invoking the correct sub-command. The tool group is `ros2 bag` — not `ros2 rosbag`.

## 2.3 Why rosbag Matters in Robotics Engineering

- **Reproducibility**: Guarantees that an algorithm is tested on the *exact* sensor stream captured.
- **Dataset Generation**: Foundation for creating ML datasets (e.g., for SLAM, object detection, autonomous driving).
- **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) Replay**: Validates controller code on recorded trajectories.
- **Log Mining**: Forensic analysis of robot behavior during field failures.
- **Simulation Bridge**: Tools like `rosbag2_to_ros1` and `ros2_bag_to_bag` allow cross-version dataset migration.

> [!NOTE]
> A **bag file** is a *lossless* capture — every byte of every message is preserved. This is fundamentally different from logging to CSV, which typically samples and drops information.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Worked Example: Recording and Replaying a LIDAR Stream

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A mobile robot publishes LIDAR scans on topic `/scan` at 10 Hz and odometry on `/odom` at 50 Hz. Demonstrate the *complete workflow* to record **30 seconds** of this data, inspect the bag, and replay it at **0.5× speed**.

### Step 1 — Verify Topics are Active

```bash
ros2 topic list
```

**Expected output:**
```
/scan
/odom
/parameter_events
/rosout
```

### Step 2 — Initiate Recording

```bash
ros2 bag record -o my_robot_run -d 30 /scan /odom
```

**Logic of each flag:**
- `-o my_robot_run` → Creates directory `my_robot_run/`.
- `-d 30` → Stops recording after 30 seconds automatically.
- `/scan /odom` → Only these two topics are captured.

### Step 3 — Inspect the Bag

```bash
ros2 bag info my_robot_run
```

**Expected output (illustrative):**
```
Files:             my_robot_run_0.db3
Bag size:          24.7 MiB
Storage id:        sqlite3
Duration:          30.012s
Start:             Jan 15 2025 10:23:14.221
End:               Jan 15 2025 10:23:44.233
Messages:          1812
Topic information:
  Topic: /scan | Type: sensor_msgs/msg/LaserScan | Count: 300 | Serialization: cdr
  Topic: /odom | Type: nav_msgs/msg/Odometry | Count: 1500 | Serialization: cdr
```

**Analytical Reasoning:**

$$
\text{Expected Scan Count} = 10 \text{ Hz} \times 30 \text{ s} = 300 \text{ messages}
$$

$$
\text{Expected Odom Count} = 50 \text{ Hz} \times 30 \text{ s} = 1500 \text{ messages}
$$

$$
\text{Total} = 300 + 1500 + (\text{/tf and /parameter_events if any}) = 1812 \text{ messages}
$$

### Step 4 — Replay at Half Speed

```bash
ros2 bag play my_robot_run --rate 0.5
```

**Logic:** The `--rate 0.5` flag multiplies wall-clock time by 2 — i.e., a 30-second bag takes 60 seconds to replay. This is useful for **slow-motion debugging** of high-frequency controllers.

### Step 5 — Visualize in RViz (Optional)

```bash
rviz2
```
Set the **Fixed Frame** to `odom` and add a **LaserScan** display subscribed to `/scan`. The replayed scan will render in real time as the bag plays.

## 3.2 Splitting Large Recordings (Analytical Logic)

For long-duration field tests, a single `.db3` file becomes unwieldy. The `--max-bag-size` flag enforces file splitting.

```bash
ros2 bag record -a -o field_test --max-bag-size 1073741824
```

**Math:**

$$
1 \text{ GiB} = 1024^3 \text{ bytes} = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \text{ bytes}
$$

When the active file reaches this threshold, rosbag closes it and opens a new sequential file (`field_test_0.db3`, `field_test_1.db3`, ...).

## 3.3 Compression Trade-Off Analysis

| Mode | Command Fragment | Compression Ratio | CPU Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uncompressed | (default) | 1.0× | Negligible |
| File mode | `--compression-mode file` | ~3–5× | Low (per file) |
| Message mode | `--compression-mode message` | ~2–3× | Higher (per message) |

**Engineering Decision Rule:**

$$
\text{Choose} = \begin{cases} \text{Message mode} & \text{if CPU is idle and disk is critical} \\ \text{File mode} & \text{if recording on embedded boards (e.g., Raspberry Pi)} \\ \text{Uncompressed} & \text{if real-time write throughput is paramount} \end{cases}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: rosbag Recording & Playback Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Sensors] -->|Publish| B[ROS 2 DDS Middleware]
    B -->|Topic /scan| C[ros2 bag record]
    B -->|Topic /odom| C
    B -->|Topic /imu| C
    C -->|Serialize to CDR| D[SQLite3 .db3 File]
    D -->|Updates| E[metadata.yaml]
    E -->|Index Info| F[ros2 bag info]
    D -->|Read & Deserialize| G[ros2 bag play]
    G -->|Republish| H[Consumer Nodes RViz, ML Pipeline]
    H -->|Verified Results| I[Engineering Insight]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: File-System Layout of a Bag

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[my_robot_run] --> B[my_robot_run_0.db3]
    A --> C[metadata.yaml]
    B --> D[Table: messages]
    B --> E[Table: topics]
    B --> F[Table: schema]
    C --> G[rosbag2_bagfile_information]
    C --> H[duration]
    C --> I[start_time]
    C --> J[end_time]
    C --> K[message_count]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Workflow Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1[Phase 1 Acquisition]
        P1[Start ROS 2 daemon] --> P2[Launch sensor drivers]
        P2 --> P3[ros2 topic list verification]
        P3 --> P4[ros2 bag record -d 30 /scan /odom]
    end

    subgraph PHASE2[Phase 2 Inspection]
        Q1[ros2 bag info my_robot_run] --> Q2[Verify count and duration]
        Q2 --> Q3[Validate topic types]
    end

    subgraph PHASE3[Phase 3 Replay]
        R1[ros2 bag play --rate 1.0] --> R2[Visualize in RViz]
        R2 --> R3[Run offline analysis node]
    end

    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3
```

> [!TIP]
> When explaining rosbag in KTU viva voce, sketch the **publisher → middleware → subscriber (rosbag)** chain on paper. Examiners award 2 marks specifically for this conceptual clarity.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
> Define *rosbag* in the context of ROS 2. List any **two** primary use cases in robotics.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
rosbag is a command-line tool in ROS 2 that records messages published on topics and stores them in a bag file (a SQLite3 database with a `.db3` extension) along with a `metadata.yaml` file.
**Use cases (any two):**
1. Recording sensor data (LIDAR, camera, IMU) for offline replay and debugging.
2. Building reproducible datasets for machine learning models (e.g., SLAM, object detection).
3. Sharing experimental data between research groups without re-running hardware.
*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 2 for use cases + ½ mark for terminology accuracy)*

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
> Differentiate between `ros2 bag record -a` and `ros2 bag record /scan /odom`.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- `ros2 bag record -a` subscribes to **all** currently active topics on the ROS graph and records every message.
- `ros2 bag record /scan /odom` records **only** the explicitly named topics, ignoring all others.
- Use `-a` for **complete log capture** (e.g., full system debugging); use selective recording for **focused experiments** to save disk space and reduce CPU.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
> **(a)** Explain the **internal architecture** of the rosbag recording process. How are messages serialized and persisted to disk? Include a labeled diagram. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A robot publishes wheel odometry on `/odom` at 100 Hz. You are tasked to record exactly **15 minutes** of data, with a maximum bag file size of **500 MB**. Write the **complete ros2 command** and derive the **expected message count**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Internal Architecture (7 Marks):**
1. rosbag instantiates an internal **subscriber node** and registers with the DDS middleware for each requested topic. (1.5 marks)
2. For every incoming message, rosbag acquires the **CDR (Common Data Representation) serialized buffer** directly from the middleware — avoiding a deserialization round-trip. (1.5 marks)
3. The serialized bytes, along with timestamp, topic name, and type hash, are inserted as a **row** in the SQLite3 `messages` table. (1.5 marks)
4. A parallel `metadata.yaml` file is updated incrementally to track the **start time, end time, message count, and topic-type map**. (1.5 marks)
5. The recording terminates when `--duration`, `--max-messages`, or Ctrl-C is reached. (1 mark)

**Diagram (any labeled flowchart earns 1 mark — use the Mermaid block from Section 4.1 as reference):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensor Publisher] --> B[DDS Layer]
    B --> C[rosbag Subscriber]
    C --> D[CDR Serialize]
    D --> E[SQLite3 Write]
    E --> F[metadata.yaml Update]
```

**(b) Command and Message Count Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Command (3 marks):**

```bash
ros2 bag record -o wheel_odom_session \
  -d 900 \
  --max-bag-size 524288000 \
  /odom
```

**Derivation (4 marks):**

$$
\text{Duration} = 15 \text{ min} = 15 \times 60 = 900 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\text{Frequency} = 100 \text{ Hz} = 100 \text{ messages/s}
$$

$$
\text{Total Messages} = 100 \text{ Hz} \times 900 \text{ s} = 90{,}000 \text{ messages}
$$

$$
\text{Max Bag Size} = 500 \text{ MB} = 500 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 524{,}288{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

If the average `Odometry` message is ~250 bytes, the estimated bag size is:

$$
90{,}000 \times 250 \text{ bytes} = 22{,}500{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 21.5 \text{ MiB}
$$

Hence, the bag will **not split** during this session, and the `--max-bag-size` limit is not triggered.

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Expected Style, Alternative Module Question]**
> **(a)** Compare the **file mode** and **message mode** compression strategies in `ros2 bag`. When would you choose each? Provide a numerical example where compression reduces a 2 GB bag to under 600 MB. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **replay mechanism** of rosbag. How is QoS (Quality of Service) handled during playback, and what happens if a subscriber expects `RELIABLE` delivery but the bag contains only `BEST_EFFORT`? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Compression Comparison (7 Marks):**

| Aspect | File Mode | Message Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **When compressed** | After recording ends | During recording (per message) |
| **CPU Load** | Low (post-hoc) | Higher (real-time) |
| **Disk I/O** | Burst at end | Distributed |
| **Best For** | Embedded boards, post-processing | Servers with idle CPU |

**Numerical Example (4 marks):**

Original bag size: $2 \text{ GB} = 2048 \text{ MiB}$

With zstd file mode achieving a typical 3.5× ratio:

$$
\text{Compressed Size} = \frac{2048 \text{ MiB}}{3.5} = 585 \text{ MiB} < 600 \text{ MiB} \quad \checkmark
$$

Therefore the compression target is met.

**(b) Replay Mechanism & QoS (7 Marks):**

1. **Mechanism (3 marks):** `ros2 bag play` reads the `metadata.yaml` to enumerate topics, opens the SQLite3 database, and for each message: deserializes the CDR payload back to the typed ROS message, then publishes it on the corresponding topic using an internal publisher node.

2. **QoS Handling (2 marks):** rosbag publishes using the **default QoS** profile. If a consumer subscribes with a *stricter* profile than the bag's recorded profile, the subscription may **fail to connect**, leading to a "no messages received" scenario.

3. **RELIABLE vs BEST_EFFORT (2 marks):** A subscriber expecting `RELIABLE` QoS will **not receive** messages published with `BEST_EFFORT` because the DDS layer rejects the QoS mismatch. Workaround: explicitly remap QoS with `--qos-profile-overrides-path` or pre-process the bag.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing ROS 1 and ROS 2 syntax** — ROS 1 uses `rosbag record`, ROS 2 uses `ros2 bag record`. Mixing them costs 1 mark per error.
> 2. **Forgetting the `-o` flag** — Without it, rosbag creates a timestamp-named folder that examiners may mark as "non-reproducible".
> 3. **Not converting units** — Questions asking for "15 minutes" expect `-d 900`, not `-d 15`. The same applies to MB vs MiB conversions.
> 4. **Ignoring QoS** — Playback fails silently if QoS is mismatched. Always mention QoS in viva explanations.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **rosbag** = DVR for ROS topics; captures every message on a topic.
- ✅ **Storage format**: SQLite3 `.db3` + `metadata.yaml`.
- ✅ **Record all**: `ros2 bag record -a`; **Selective**: `ros2 bag record /topic1 /topic2`.
- ✅ **Stop automatically**: `-d <seconds>` or `--max-messages <N>`.
- ✅ **Inspect**: `ros2 bag info <bag_folder>`.
- ✅ **Replay**: `ros2 bag play <bag_folder> --rate <N>`.
- ✅ **Compression**: `--compression-mode file|message` with `--compression-format zstd`.
- ✅ **Splitting**: `--max-bag-size <bytes>`.
- ✅ **Message Count Formula**: $\text{Count} = \text{Frequency (Hz)} \times \text{Duration (s)}$.
- ✅ **Use Cases**: Debugging, dataset creation, HIL replay, log mining.
- ⚠️ **QoS Mismatch** is the #1 reason replay "doesn't work" — always verify.
- ⚠️ **Disk space**: 10 minutes of LIDAR ≈ 1+ GB; plan accordingly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building Maps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Building Maps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Mapping** is the process of creating a **spatial representation** (a *map*) of an unknown environment using sensor measurements obtained while a robot navigates through that environment. In the **ROS (Robot Operating System)** context, a *map* in ROS is a **2D occupancy grid** (a `.pgm` image plus a `.yaml` metadata file) that represents the world as a discrete grid of cells, where each cell holds a probabilistic value indicating whether it is **free, occupied, or unknown**.

A *map* in ROS is a **nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid** message consisting of:
- **Header** (frame_id, stamp)
- **Map metadata** (resolution, width, height, origin)
- **Data array** (int8 values, one per cell: 0 = free, 100 = occupied, -1 = unknown)

> [!NOTE]
> **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** is the broader, coupled problem: building a map **while** simultaneously estimating the robot's pose within that map. *Mapping alone* assumes the robot's pose is known (e.g., from wheel odometry); SLAM jointly solves both. Most KTU questions refer to *map building* under the SLAM umbrella.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "A person in a dark warehouse with a flashlight":**
> Imagine being blindfolded inside a large, unfamiliar warehouse. You hold a flashlight (your **sensor**) and walk around. Each time the flashlight beam hits a wall, you mentally mark that location as a barrier. As you move, you keep a *mental sketch* (the **map**) of where obstacles are. Occasionally, you return to a previously visited area and realize, *"Ah, this corridor connects to where I was earlier!"* — this recognition is called **loop closure**, a critical event in map building. Your *body's sense of movement* is analogous to **odometry**, and your flashlight reflections are analogous to **laser scans** or **depth camera readings**. The ROS *mapping pipeline* is essentially a robot doing the same thing — sensing, integrating, and correcting — at thousands of measurements per second.

## 1.3 Key Terms and Constants

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Occupancy Grid** | A 2D grid where each cell holds a probability of being occupied |
| **Resolution** | Meters per cell (typically **0.05 m** = 5 cm in ROS default) |
| **Odometry** | Robot's estimated motion from wheel encoders / IMU |
| **Sensor scan** | Set of range readings (e.g., 360 beams from a 2D LIDAR) |
| **TF tree** | ROS coordinate-frame hierarchy (`map → odom → base_link`) |
| **ICP** | Iterative Closest Point (scan-matching algorithm) |
| **Loop closure** | Recognizing a previously visited location to correct drift |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard ROS map file pair:** `my_map.pgm` (Portable Gray Map image, 8-bit) + `my_map.yaml` (metadata: resolution, origin, thresholds).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Build Maps? — The Underlying Need

A robot operating in a real environment must answer three questions continuously:
1. **Where am I?** (Localization)
2. **What does the world look like around me?** (Mapping / Perception)
3. **How do I get from A to B safely?** (Navigation — *requires* a map)

Maps are the foundational *world model* that enables **path planning**, **obstacle avoidance**, and **autonomous navigation** in ROS using the `move_base` and `amcl` packages.

## 2.2 Map Representations in Robotics

| Representation | Data structure | Best for | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Metric / Occupancy grid** | 2D array of probabilities | Indoor, static env. | ROS `map_server`, `gmapping` |
| **Feature-based** | List of landmarks + descriptors | Sparse, structured env. | Visual SLAM with ORB-SLAM |
| **Topological** | Graph of nodes (places) + edges (paths) | Large-scale, corridor-like env. | Hybrid topological-metric |
| **3D voxel / Octomap** | 3D probabilistic grid | Aerial, manipulator, outdoor | `octomap` ROS package |
| **Point cloud** | Unordered 3D points | Raw 3D reconstruction | `pcl_ros`, RTAB-Map |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU's Module 4 emphasizes the **2D Occupancy Grid** because it is what the standard ROS navigation stack (`navigation` meta-package) consumes.

## 2.3 The Occupancy Grid Mapping Algorithm — Logical Steps

The **occupancy grid mapping** algorithm independently estimates, for every cell $m_i$ in a discretized grid, the posterior probability that it is occupied, given all sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and robot poses $x_{1:t}$ up to time $t$.

**Step 1 — Discretize the world** into a uniform grid of cells of size $r \times r$ (e.g., $r = 0.05$ m).

**Step 2 — Initialize** every cell with the prior probability $P(m_i) = 0.5$ (i.e., *unknown*).

**Step 3 — For each incoming sensor scan** $z_t$ at pose $x_t$:
- For every grid cell $m_i$ that lies *along* a sensor beam, compute the **inverse sensor model** $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$.
- For every cell *not* along a beam, apply $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) = P(m_i)$ (no information gained).

**Step 4 — Apply the recursive Bayesian update** to fuse the new measurement with the existing belief.

**Step 5 — Threshold** the final probability to classify each cell as **free** ($< p_{free}$), **occupied** ($> p_{occ}$), or **unknown** (in between).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | LaTeX | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inverse sensor model (cell on beam, before hit) | $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) = 0.4$ | Free-space model |
| 2 | Inverse sensor model (cell at hit) | $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) = 0.6$ | Occupied model |
| 3 | Inverse sensor model (cell beyond hit) | $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) = 0.5$ | No info |
| 4 | Recursive Bayesian posterior | $P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) \, P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) \, P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$ | Exact Bayes update |
| 5 | Log-odds form | $l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) - l_0$ | Additive update |
| 6 | Log-odds of probability | $l = \log \frac{p}{1 - p}$ | Maps $p \in [0,1]$ to $\mathbb{R}$ |
| 7 | Probability from log-odds | $p = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{l}}$ | Inverse transform |
| 8 | Map cell classification | $m_i = \begin{cases} \text{free} & p < p_{free} \\ \text{occupied} & p > p_{occ} \\ \text{unknown} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Output labeling |
| 9 | Cell index from world coords | $i = \lfloor (x - x_0) / r \rfloor, \quad j = \lfloor (y - y_0) / r \rfloor$ | Grid addressing |
| 10 | Typical ROS thresholds | $p_{free} = 0.196$ (log-odds $= -1.4$), $p_{occ} = 0.65$ (log-odds $= 0.65$) | From `map_server` defaults |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU theory questions, students often confuse $P(\text{occupied} \mid \text{data})$ with $P(\text{data} \mid \text{occupied})$. The mapping algorithm uses the **inverse sensor model** $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$, **not** the sensor likelihood $P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)$ — these are reciprocals only under uniform priors, which is why the log-odds update is preferred in practice.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse robotics (Amazon Kiva, Fetch):** Build maps of fulfillment centers to plan optimal picking routes.
- **Autonomous vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Continuously build and update HD maps.
- **Domestic vacuum robots (Roomba):** Use low-resolution occupancy grids for room coverage.
- **Disaster response & search-and-rescue:** Build maps of collapsed/rubble environments for teleoperation.
- **Agricultural robots:** Build orchard/vineyard maps for precision spraying.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Rule to the Log-Odds Update

### 3.1.1 Start: The Posterior for a Single Cell

The probability that cell $m_i$ is occupied, given all measurements up to time $t$ and all robot poses up to time $t$, is:

$$
P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t, z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})
$$

### 3.1.2 Apply Bayes' Rule

$$
P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid x_t)}
$$

### 3.1.3 Substitute for the Complementary Hypothesis

We define the complement $\neg m_i$ (cell is free) and apply Bayes' to the denominator:

$$
P(z_t \mid x_t) = P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1}) + P(z_t \mid \neg m_i, x_t) \, P(\neg m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})
$$

### 3.1.4 The Canonical Bayes' Filter Form

Dividing numerator and denominator by $P(z_t \mid \neg m_i, x_t) \, P(\neg m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$:

$$
P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{\frac{P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} \cdot \frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}}{\frac{P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} \cdot \frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})} + 1}
$$

(Conversion logic: each Bayes ratio is rewritten in terms of the *inverse sensor model* on the latest measurement and the *prior posterior* on the history.)

### 3.1.5 Define the Log-Odds Function

Define the **log-odds** function $l(x) = \log \frac{x}{1 - x}$. This maps probabilities in $(0,1)$ to real numbers $\mathbb{R}$, with $l(0.5) = 0$. Let $l_t \equiv l\bigl(P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})\bigr)$ and $l_{inv} \equiv l\bigl(P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)\bigr)$ and $l_0 = l(0.5) = 0$.

### 3.1.6 Take Log-Odds of the Bayes Form

Because the log of a fraction becomes a difference, the recursive update **simplifies dramatically**:

$$
l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l_{inv}(m_i, z_t, x_t) - l_0
$$

With $l_0 = 0$:

$$
\boxed{\,l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** the log-odds form is **additive** — you simply *add* a constant measurement term to the existing log-odds. This is computationally cheap (one floating-point addition per cell per scan) and avoids numerical underflow, which is why ROS packages like `gmapping` use it.

### 3.1.7 Recover Probability from Log-Odds

Given accumulated $l_t$, the occupancy probability is:

$$
P(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{l_t}}
$$

(Conversion logic: invert $l = \log \frac{p}{1-p}$ to get $p = \frac{e^{l}}{1+e^{l}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1+e^{l}}$.)

### 3.1.8 Clamp to Prevent Saturation

To prevent $l_t \to \pm \infty$ in repeatedly-observed cells, ROS clamps the log-odds to a bounded range, typically $l_t \in [l_{min}, l_{max}] = [-2.0, \; 3.5]$ (corresponding to $p \in [0.119, \; 0.971]$). These are the `map_save_min_thresh` and `map_save_max_thresh` values exposed by `map_server`.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample problem (K TU board-exam style):**
> A 2D LIDAR returns a range $z_t = 2.0$ m at pose $x_t = (0, 0, 0)$. The cell $m_i$ at world coordinate $(1.5, 0)$ lies on the beam, *before* the hit. The prior $P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1}) = 0.5$. The inverse sensor model returns $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) = 0.4$ (free space). Compute the updated log-odds and the new occupancy probability.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute prior log-odds**
$$
l_{t-1} = \log \frac{0.5}{1 - 0.5} = \log 1 = 0
$$

**Solution — Step 2: Compute measurement log-odds**
$$
l_{inv} = \log \frac{0.4}{1 - 0.4} = \log \frac{0.4}{0.6} = \log(0.6667) \approx -0.4055
$$

**Solution — Step 3: Add (with $l_0 = 0$)**
$$
l_t = 0 + (-0.4055) - 0 = -0.4055
$$

**Solution — Step 4: Convert back to probability**
$$
P(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.4055}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 0.6667} = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4
$$

**Interpretation:** The cell is now believed to be **free** with 40% occupancy probability — a single free-space observation reduced the cell's *occupied* belief from 50% to 40%. Multiple consistent observations would drive this further down, eventually crossing the free-threshold $p_{free} = 0.196$.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ROS Mapping Packages

| Package | Algorithm | Loop closure | Required odometry | Computational cost | Best use case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `gmapping` | Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter | Weak (poor) | Yes (mandatory) | Low | Indoor, small-medium |
| `karto_slam` | Graph-SLAM with pose optimization | Strong (SPA) | Optional | Medium | Indoor, medium |
| `hector_slam` | Scan-matching (Gauss-Newton) | None | **No** (odom-free) | Low | UAVs, lightweight robots |
| `cartographer` | Graph-SLAM + submap + CSM | **Very strong** (branch-and-bound) | Optional (IMU helps) | High (Google-optimized) | Large, multi-floor |
| `rtabmap` | Appearance-based loop closure | Strong (visual) | Optional (visual works) | High | RGB-D, stereo |
| `slam_toolbox` | Graph-SLAM, lifelong mapping | Strong (Karto derivative) | Optional | Medium | Modern ROS 2 default |

> [!NOTE]
> **Map → Odom → Base_link TF tree** (the standard mapping stack hierarchy):
>
> `map` (world-fixed, built by SLAM) → `odom` (continuous, drifts over time) → `base_link` (rigidly attached to robot). The SLAM node publishes the **map → odom** transform; the robot driver / EKF publishes **odom → base_link**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the ROS Mapping Pipeline

This diagram captures the data-flow between ROS nodes, topics, and transforms during 2D map building using `gmapping` (or any similar SLAM package).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Sensors] --> B[Sensor Drivers ROS Nodes]
    B -- /scan LaserScan --> C[SLAM Node gmapping]
    B -- /imu Imu --> C
    B -- /odom Odometry --> C
    C -- /map OccupancyGrid --> D[Map Server Node]
    C -- /tf map to odom --> E[TF Static and Dynamic Tree]
    C -- /map_metadata --> D
    D -- map.pgm and map.yaml --> F[Persistent Storage on Disk]
    G[Navigation Stack move_base] --> D
    G --> E
    E --> H[Path Planner and Costmap]
    H --> I[Velocity Commands to base]
    C --> J[Rviz Visualization]
    D --> J
    E --> J

    classDef sensor fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#8a6d00,color:#000
    classDef slam fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#005f73,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#cdb4db,stroke:#3a0ca3,color:#000
    classDef nav fill:#b5e48c,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000
    classDef viz fill:#f4a261,stroke:#9d0208,color:#000

    class A,B sensor
    class C,H slam
    class D,F storage
    class G,I nav
    class E,J viz
```

## 4.2 The Occupancy-Grid Update Loop (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitGrid
    InitGrid: Initialize all cells to p = 0.5
    InitGrid --> WaitForScan
    WaitForScan: Subscribe to /scan and /odom
    WaitForScan --> ProcessScan: New scan received
    ProcessScan: For each cell on beam, compute inverse sensor model
    ProcessScan --> UpdateLogOdds
    UpdateLogOdds: l_t = l_tminus1 + l_inv
    UpdateLogOdds --> Clamp
    Clamp: Clamp l_t to l_min, l_max
    Clamp --> PublishMap
    PublishMap: Publish nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid on /map
    PublishMap --> WaitForScan
    WaitForScan --> SaveMap: map_saver request
    SaveMap: Write map.pgm and map.yaml
    SaveMap --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map Representations Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Occupancy Grid 2D array of probabilities] --> A1[Free Occupied Unknown]
    B[Feature Map list of landmarks] --> B1[Lines Corners Distinctive points]
    C[Topological Map graph of places] --> C1[Nodes places Edges paths]
    D[Point Cloud 3D unorganized points] --> D1[x y z intensity per point]
    E[Octomap 3D probabilistic voxels] --> E1[Adaptive resolution octree]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define an *occupancy grid* map. Mention any two fields present in the `.yaml` metadata file of a ROS map.
> `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An *occupancy grid map* is a 2D discretization of the environment into a uniform grid of cells, where each cell stores a probabilistic estimate of whether it is occupied by an obstacle. In ROS, it is published as a `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message and saved as a `.pgm` image plus a `.yaml` metadata file.
Two fields in the `.yaml` file (1 mark each):
- **`resolution:`** — size of one cell in meters (e.g., 0.05).
- **`origin:`** — the 2D pose `(x, y, yaw)` of the cell `(0,0)` in the world frame.
*(Acceptable alternatives: `occupied_thresh`, `free_thresh`, `negate`, `image`)*

### Q2. What is **loop closure** in the context of map building? Why is it important?
> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Loop closure** (2 marks) is the process of recognizing that the robot has returned to a previously visited location and using this recognition to correct accumulated drift in the map and pose estimates. It is the event that triggers global optimization (e.g., graph relaxation) of the entire trajectory.
**Importance** (1 mark): Without loop closure, odometry errors accumulate unboundedly, producing inconsistent or distorted maps. Loop closure enforces global consistency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam – model question, Module 4]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **ROS 2D mapping pipeline** using the `gmapping` package. Identify all the major topics, messages, and the role of the `TF` tree. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **recursive Bayesian update** for a single cell of an occupancy grid. Show how the **log-odds** transformation simplifies the update into an additive rule. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) ROS 2D Mapping Pipeline (7 marks)

1. **Inputs to `gmapping` node** (1 mark):
   - `/scan` (`sensor_msgs/LaserScan`) — 2D LIDAR data.
   - `/tf` — transforms, especially `odom → base_link` (from wheel odometry) and (optionally) `base_link → laser_frame`.
   - (Optionally) `/imu` for better pose prediction.

2. **Outputs from `gmapping`** (1 mark):
   - `/map` (`nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`) — the generated 2D probabilistic map.
   - `/map_metadata` — accompanying metadata.
   - `/tf`: a dynamic transform **`map → odom`**, correcting odometric drift.

3. **Block diagram — narrative** (3 marks, see Section 4.1 for figure):
   - Sensor drivers (e.g., `urg_node`, `ydlidar_ros2_driver`) publish `/scan`.
   - Robot driver publishes `odom → base_link` based on wheel encoders + (optional) IMU fusion via `robot_localization`.
   - The `slam_gmapping` node fuses laser scans with odometry using a **Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter**, maintaining a particle cloud where each particle carries its own map hypothesis.
   - On resampling, particles that better match the laser scan (via scan-matching) gain weight; the best particle's map is published on `/map`.
   - The map is consumed by `map_server` (for saving) and `move_base` (for navigation).

4. **Role of TF tree** (2 marks):
   - Provides the spatial relationship between all coordinate frames at any time.
   - Specifically, `map → odom` (published by SLAM) and `odom → base_link → laser` (published by the robot stack).
   - Without a consistent TF tree, the scan cannot be placed correctly into the map.

#### Model Solution — (b) Derivation of the Log-Odds Update (7 marks)

Step 1 (1 mark): State the goal — compute $P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ recursively for each cell $m_i$.

Step 2 (1 mark): Apply Bayes' rule to the latest measurement $z_t$:

$$
P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid x_t)}
$$

Step 3 (1 mark): Substitute the complement form to express the result purely in terms of inverse-sensor-model ratios (see Section 3.1.4).

Step 4 (1 mark): Define the **log-odds** $l(x) = \log \frac{x}{1-x}$ and identify $l_0 = l(0.5) = 0$.

Step 5 (2 marks): Take log-odds of the Bayes expression; products become sums:

$$
\boxed{\,l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)\,}
$$

Step 6 (1 mark): State the practical significance — each new measurement *adds* a constant to the cell's log-odds. This is what ROS's `gmapping` and `cartographer` use internally for efficiency and numerical stability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q.A-b):**
> Students often (i) forget to *normalize* the Bayes expression, leaving an unsimplified ratio; (ii) confuse the **inverse sensor model** $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ with the **forward sensor model** $P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)$; (iii) drop the term $l_0$ when in fact $l_0 = 0$ only because the prior is 0.5 — a careless statement that loses a mark. Write out the inverse-sensor-model explicitly with the cell *before / at / beyond* the hit.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

> `[KTU University Exam – model question, Module 4]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the four major **map representations** in robotics: occupancy grid, feature-based, topological, and point cloud. For each, give one ROS package and one typical use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot's 2D LIDAR returns a measurement such that a particular cell $m_i$ lying *along* the beam, *before* the laser hit, has an **inverse sensor model** value $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) = 0.3$. The cell's prior occupancy probability from previous observations is $P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1}) = 0.7$. Using the log-odds formulation, compute the **updated log-odds** $l_t(m_i)$ and the **new occupancy probability**. Briefly comment on whether the cell is more likely to be free or occupied after this update. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Map Representation Comparison (7 marks)

| Representation | Data | ROS Package | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Occupancy grid** (1.5 m) | 2D grid, $p(\text{occ})$ per cell | `gmapping`, `map_server` | Indoor floor, navigation |
| **Feature-based** (1.5 m) | Landmarks + descriptors | ORB-SLAM2 ROS wrapper | Sparse, structured rooms |
| **Topological** (1.5 m) | Graph of places | `topological_navigation` | Warehouse corridors, multi-floor |
| **Point cloud** (1.5 m) | 3D $(x,y,z,\text{intensity})$ | `pcl_ros`, RTAB-Map | 3D reconstruction, outdoor terrain |

*(For 7 marks, present a short prose comparison highlighting: storage cost, ease of path planning, suitability to environment scale, and computational cost. Award 1 mark per correctly matched (package + use case) pair, plus 0.5 mark for the comparison prose.)*

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical Log-Odds Update (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Prior log-odds** (1 mark):
$$
l_{t-1} = \log \frac{0.7}{1 - 0.7} = \log \frac{0.7}{0.3} = \log(2.3333) \approx 0.8473
$$

**Step 2 — Inverse sensor model log-odds** (1 mark):
$$
l_{inv} = \log \frac{0.3}{1 - 0.3} = \log \frac{0.3}{0.7} = \log(0.4286) \approx -0.8473
$$

**Step 3 — Add (with $l_0 = 0$)** (1 mark):
$$
l_t = 0.8473 + (-0.8473) = 0.0
$$

**Step 4 — Convert back to probability** (1 mark):
$$
P(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{0.0}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2} = 0.5
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation** (1 mark): The cell's occupancy probability has dropped from 0.7 (believed occupied) to 0.5 (now *unknown*). The single free-space observation was strong enough to fully cancel one prior "occupied" belief, but the cell has not yet crossed the free-threshold $p_{free} = 0.196$; more consistent free observations are required to declare it definitively free.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination tip:** KTU's valuation scheme often awards partial credit (1–2 marks) for *correctly setting up* the log-odds formula even if the arithmetic is slightly off. So always state the formula in symbolic form first.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q.B-b):**
> Three common errors cost marks:
> 1. **Forgetting the $l_0$ term** — even though it is zero for a uniform prior, writing $l_t = l_{t-1} + l_{inv}$ without acknowledging the implicit $l_0 = 0$ assumption loses 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Mixing the cell-position cases** — the inverse sensor model value $0.3$ (or $0.4$) applies *only* to cells on the beam *before* the hit. If the problem said "cell at the hit", you would use $0.6$ instead.
> 3. **Unit confusion** — log-odds is dimensionless, but the probability must remain in $[0,1]$; check that your final answer satisfies $0 \le P \le 1$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Building Maps":**

- [ ] **Map = `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`** in ROS; saved as `.pgm` + `.yaml`.
- [ ] **Three cell states:** Free (0), Occupied (100), Unknown (-1).
- [ ] **Bayes' update** is exact but involves a division and possible underflow.
- [ ] **Log-odds** transform converts Bayes' update into an **additive** rule: $l_t = l_{t-1} + l_{inv}$.
- [ ] **Inverse sensor model** uses three cases: *before hit* (free), *at hit* (occupied), *beyond hit* (no info).
- [ ] **Clamping** prevents saturation: typical range $l \in [-2.0, 3.5]$.
- [ ] **TF tree** during mapping: `map → odom → base_link → laser_frame`.
- [ ] **`gmapping`** is the entry-level SLAM package; **`cartographer`** is the modern high-quality choice; **`slam_toolbox`** is the ROS 2 default.
- [ ] **Map resolution** is meters/cell; ROS default = **0.05 m**; tradeoff: finer → more memory + slower update.
- [ ] **Loop closure** is what makes the map globally consistent; without it, maps drift.
- [ ] **Navigation stack** (`move_base`) requires a *static* map; for dynamic environments, a *local costmap* is layered on top.
- [ ] **Coordinate frame** for the map is `map`; data flows as `OccupancyGrid.data[]` indexed as `data[j * width + i]`.
- [ ] **Saving a map:** `ros2 run nav2_map_server map_saver_cli -f my_map` (ROS 2) or `rosrun map_server map_saver -f my_map` (ROS 1).
- [ ] **Loading a map:** `nav2_map_server` reads `my_map.yaml` and publishes it on `/map`.

> **End of Module 4 Topic: Building Maps — KTU 2024 Scheme notes.**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🗺️ Module 4 — Starting a Map Server and Looking at a Map

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Map Server** in ROS (Robot Operating System) is a ROS node provided by the `map_server` package that serves a **2D Occupancy Grid Map** to the rest of the ROS ecosystem. It reads a map from a pair of files (a **PGM image** and a corresponding **YAML metadata file**) and publishes it as a `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message over the `/map` topic. The companion `map_saver` tool dumps an in-memory map (produced by SLAM algorithms such as `gmapping` or `cartographer`) back to disk in the same format.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must understand how to **launch**, **configure**, and **visualize** a map server. Map Server is the cornerstone of the ROS Navigation Stack — without a map, the global planner cannot perform path planning.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Library of Floor Plans"

Imagine you walk into a huge shopping mall. Before you can find the food court, you need a **floor plan** displayed on a digital kiosk. The kiosk is the **Map Server** — it holds the official layout and shows it to anyone who asks (other ROS nodes).

- The **PGM image** = the actual drawn floor plan (black = walls, white = open space).
- The **YAML file** = the "label" on the kiosk that tells you the scale, orientation, and origin of the drawing.
- **RViz** = the screen where you view the floor plan.
- **Other ROS nodes** (e.g., the robot's planner) = visitors who come and ask the kiosk for directions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric (KTU Expected Value):** Default map resolution commonly used in ROS 1 = **0.05 m/pixel** (i.e., **5 cm**). Probability thresholds: **occupied_thresh = 0.65**, **free_thresh = 0.196**.

### 1.3 Prerequisites Before Launching

| Requirement | Command / Action |
|---|---|
| ROS Noetic/Melodic installed | `source /opt/ros/<distro>/setup.bash` |
| `map_server` package | `sudo apt install ros-<distro>-map-server` |
| A workspace with `rospack` profile | `source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash` |
| Map files (`map.pgm`, `map.yaml`) | Placed in a known directory |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Map Files

### 2.1.1 The YAML Metadata File
The YAML file is the **"decoder ring"** that tells the Map Server how to interpret the pixels of the PGM image in real-world meters.

```yaml
image: map.pgm
resolution: 0.050000
origin: [-10.000000, -10.000000, 0.000000]
negate: 0
occupied_thresh: 0.65
free_thresh: 0.196
```

**Field-by-field meaning:**

| Field | Type | Meaning | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| `image` | string | Relative path to the PGM file | Points Map Server to the raw bitmap data |
| `resolution` | float (m/pixel) | Ground sampling distance | Converts pixel grid → metric grid |
| `origin` | $[x, y, yaw]$ | 2D pose of pixel $(0,0)$ in world frame | Aligns the map with `map` TF frame |
| `negate` | 0 / 1 | Whether to invert white↔black semantics | Used when satellite imagery treats white as occupied |
| `occupied_thresh` | float | Pixels above this are **100 (occupied)** | Cell classification threshold |
| `free_thresh` | float | Pixels below this are **0 (free)** | Cell classification threshold |
| (between thresholds) | — | **−1 (unknown)** | Cells in the "no man's land" |

### 2.1.2 The PGM Image File
- **PGM = Portable Grey Map** (lossless format).
- Each pixel has an intensity value in $[0, 255]$.
- Map Server linearly maps pixel intensity → occupancy probability using the formula in §3.
- White pixel (255) = free; Black pixel (0) = occupied; Grey = unknown.

### 2.2 The `OccupancyGrid` Message Structure

The `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message published on `/map` contains:

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `header` | Standard ROS header (`frame_id = "map"`, timestamp) |
| `info.width` | Number of columns in the grid |
| `info.height` | Number of rows in the grid |
| `info.resolution` | Meters per cell |
| `info.origin` | Pose of cell $(0,0)$ in the `map` frame |
| `data` | `int8[]` array of size `width × height`, row-major |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p_{occ} = 1 - \dfrac{I}{255}$ | Pixel intensity $I \in [0,255]$ → occupancy probability |
| 2 | $W_{world} = W_{pixels} \times R$ | Map width in meters = pixel width × resolution |
| 3 | $X_{world} = X_{origin} + c_x \cdot R$ | World X of cell column $c_x$ |
| 4 | $Y_{world} = Y_{origin} + c_y \cdot R$ | World Y of cell row $c_y$ |
| 5 | $c_{cell} = 100$ if $p_{occ} \geq T_{occ}$ | Occupied cell rule |
| 6 | $c_{cell} = 0$ if $p_{occ} \leq T_{free}$ | Free cell rule |
| 7 | $c_{cell} = -1$ otherwise | Unknown cell rule |

> [!NOTE]
> In every row above, $R$ = map resolution (m/pixel), $T_{occ}$ = `occupied_thresh`, $T_{free}$ = `free_thresh`.

## 2.4 The Map Server Node Lifecycle

1. **Initialization** — Reads YAML, loads PGM into memory.
2. **Service Registration** — Advertises `/static_map` (type `nav_msgs/GetMap`).
3. **Topic Publishing** — Publishes the `OccupancyGrid` message on `/map` (latched topic in ROS 1).
4. **Request Fulfillment** — Responds to one-shot service calls from `move_base`, `amcl`, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In industrial AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), warehouse robots, and autonomous cars, Map Server provides the static reference map for localization against a known environment — enabling global path planning without re-mapping every cycle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pixel-to-World Coordinate Conversion

The Map Server must convert pixel grid coordinates $(c_x, c_y)$ into real-world metric coordinates $(X_w, Y_w)$ inside the `map` frame so that downstream nodes (planners, localization) can reason geometrically.

**Given:**
- Origin of the map in world frame: $(X_0, Y_0, \theta_0)$
- Map resolution: $R$ (meters/pixel)
- Pixels are indexed **column-first**, with $(0,0)$ at the **bottom-left** of the image (ROS convention).

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Translate by resolution without rotation.**
Start by treating the grid as axis-aligned with the world frame:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{world}^{\prime} &= X_{0} + c_{x} \cdot R \\
Y_{world}^{\prime} &= Y_{0} + c_{y} \cdot R
\end{aligned}
$$

Here each unit step in pixel grid is scaled by $R$ meters, and the origin offsets $X_0$, $Y_0$ place pixel $(0,0)$ at the desired world location.

**Step 2 — Apply rotation by $\theta_0$ (yaw).**
If the origin has a non-zero yaw, the grid is rotated with respect to the world. Using the 2D rotation matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{world} &= X_{0} + (c_{x} \cdot R)\cos\theta_{0} - (c_{y} \cdot R)\sin\theta_{0} \\
Y_{world} &= Y_{0} + (c_{x} \cdot R)\sin\theta_{0} + (c_{y} \cdot R)\cos\theta_{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the **vast majority of KTU lab problems**, $\theta_0 = 0$, so the rotation collapses back to Step 1.

**Step 3 — World ↔ Cell inverse.**
To find the cell containing a world point $(X_w, Y_w)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_{x} &= \dfrac{X_{w} - X_{0}}{R} \\
c_{y} &= \dfrac{Y_{w} - Y_{0}}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The floor operator `⌊·⌋` is then applied to obtain the integer cell index.

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A map has `resolution = 0.05` m/pixel, `origin = [-5.0, -5.0, 0.0]`. Find the world coordinate of pixel $(40, 60)$.

**Step 1 — Substitute into Step 1 equations** (since $\theta_0 = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{world} &= X_{0} + c_{x} \cdot R \\
          &= -5.0 + 40 \times 0.05 \\
          &= -5.0 + 2.0 = -3.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{world} &= Y_{0} + c_{y} \cdot R \\
          &= -5.0 + 60 \times 0.05 \\
          &= -5.0 + 3.0 = -2.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The world coordinate of pixel $(40, 60)$ is $\boxed{(-3.0 \text{ m}, \, -2.0 \text{ m})}$ in the `map` frame.

## 3.3 Cell Classification Derivation

Given a raw pixel intensity $I \in [0, 255]$, Map Server maps it to occupancy using:

$$
p = 1 - \dfrac{I}{255}
$$

The cell value $c$ assigned in the `data[]` array is:

$$
c = \begin{cases} 100, & p \geq T_{occ} \\ 0, & p \leq T_{free} \\ -1, & T_{free} < p < T_{occ} \end{cases}
$$

### Quick Worked Example
For $I = 25$, $T_{occ} = 0.65$, $T_{free} = 0.196$:

$$
p = 1 - \dfrac{25}{255} \approx 0.902
$$

Since $0.902 \geq 0.65$, the cell is **occupied** ($c = 100$).

For $I = 220$:

$$
p = 1 - \dfrac{220}{255} \approx 0.137 \leq 0.196 \implies c = 0 \text{ (free)}
$$

For $I = 150$:

$$
p = 1 - \dfrac{150}{255} \approx 0.412 \implies c = -1 \text{ (unknown)}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
