# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`**
> Define `rospack find` and explain its role in ROS filesystem navigation. State one situation where it is preferable over `roscd`.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`rospack find <package_name>` is a command-line utility from the `rosbash` metapackage that **searches every directory** listed in the `$ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` environment variable and **returns the absolute filesystem path** of the first matching ROS package (validated by the presence of a `package.xml` file).

**Role:** It is the foundational lookup primitive; both `roscd` and `rosls` internally invoke `rospack find` before performing their own operations.

**When preferable over `roscd`:** When the user only needs the path string (e.g., embedding it in a script via `$(rospack find my_pkg)/config/params.yaml`) and does **not** want to change the current shell directory. Using `roscd` would alter the working directory as a side effect, which is undesirable in automation scripts.

**Mark Split:** 1 mark for definition · 1 mark for role · 1 mark for situational preference.

---

> **`Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
> What is the significance of the `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` environment variable? List the two commands used to inspect it from a terminal.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` environment variable is a **colon-separated list of directory roots** in which the ROS filesystem tools search for packages. It is the **single source of truth** for package discovery and its **order defines search priority** — the first matching package found along the path wins.

**Commands to inspect:**
1. `echo $ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` — quick direct view.
2. `printenv \\vert grep ROS` — view all ROS-related environment variables in one pass.

**Mark Split:** 1 mark for significance · 2 marks (1 each) for the commands.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **`Q3. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, ESE Module-4 Pattern]`**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the `rosbash` metapackage. List and briefly describe the **three primary navigation commands** it provides. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A student is working in `/home/student/catkin_ws/src/beginner_tutorials/`. The student opens a new terminal and runs `roscd beginner_tutorials` but gets the error `[rospack] Error: package 'beginner_tutorials' not found`. Diagnose the issue and provide a step-by-step resolution. **(7 marks)**

---

### 🟢 Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — The `rosbash` Metapackage (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
`rosbash` is a ROS metapackage that provides **bash shell extensions** to make ROS filesystem navigation faster. It installs scripts that **shadow standard Unix commands** with ROS-aware versions that consult the `$ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` and `package.xml` files.

**Three Primary Navigation Commands (5 marks = 1 + 2 + 2):**

1. **`rospack find <pkg>`** — Locates a package and prints its absolute path by walking `$ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`. *Example:* `rospack find rospy` → `/opt/ros/noetic/share/rospy`.

2. **`roscd <pkg>`** — Combines `rospack find` with the bash `cd` builtin to teleport the shell into a package's root directory. *Example:* `roscd turtlesim` opens `/opt/ros/noetic/share/turtlesim`.

3. **`rosls <pkg>`** — Combines `rospack find` with the bash `ls` builtin to list the contents of a package from any current directory. *Example:* `rosls turtlesim` shows `cmake images msg package.xml srv srv src` (content varies by distro).

**Bonus mention (1 mark extra credit if asked):** Tab-completion via bash-completion, and supplementary commands `rosed` (edit) and `roscp` (copy).

#### Part (b) — Diagnostic Walkthrough (7 marks)

**Diagnosis (2 marks):**
The `beginner_tutorials` package exists in the student's **private workspace** (`/home/student/catkin_ws/src`), but a new terminal session starts with **only the system ROS environment** sourced (i.e., `/opt/ros/noetic/setup.bash`). The system install has **no knowledge** of the user's workspace, so `$ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` does not include `~/catkin_ws/src`.

**Step-by-Step Resolution (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Open a terminal and confirm the error reproduces:
```
$ rospack find beginner_tutorials
[rospack] Error: package 'beginner_tutorials' not found
```

**Step 2:** Inspect the current `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`:
```
$ echo $ROS_PACKAGE_PATH
/opt/ros/noetic/share
```
*(Missing the user's `src` directory — confirmed.)*

**Step 3:** Ensure the package was actually built. Navigate to the workspace root and run `catkin_make`:
```
$ cd ~/catkin_ws
$ catkin_make
```

**Step 4:** Source the workspace's `setup.bash` to overlay the workspace on top of the system path:
```
$ source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash
```

**Step 5:** Re-verify the path and retry the navigation:
```
$ echo $ROS_PACKAGE_PATH
/opt/ros/noetic/share:/home/student/catkin_ws/src

$ rospack find beginner_tutorials
/home/student/catkin_ws/src/beginner_tutorials

$ roscd beginner_tutorials
$ pwd
/home/student/catkin_ws/src/beginner_tutorials
```

**Step 6 (Optional, for permanence):** Append the source line to `~/.bashrc` so every new terminal inherits it:
```
$ echo "source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash" >> ~/.bashrc
$ source ~/.bashrc
```

---

### ⭐ Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)

**(a)** Differentiate between `roscd` and `rosls` with suitable examples. Why can't `roscd` be used directly inside a non-interactive shell script? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the structure of a typical ROS **catkin workspace**. Illustrate with a block diagram the four standard sub-directories and their purpose. **(7 marks)**

---

### 🟢 Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — `roscd` vs `rosls` (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | `roscd <pkg>` | `rosls <pkg>` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Underlying action | `cd` (changes PWD) | `ls` (lists files) |
| Side effect on shell | **Mutates** the current directory | **No side effect** on PWD |
| Use case | Enter package to edit/build | Peek at package contents |
| Example | `roscd turtlesim` → opens `/opt/ros/.../turtlesim` | `rosls turtlesim` → lists files from any PWD |
| Required for editing | Yes (e.g., to `vim CMakeLists.txt`) | No |

**Why `roscd` cannot be used in a non-interactive script (3 marks):**
A non-interactive shell (e.g., one invoked via `bash -c` from a Makefile, CI runner, or `crontab`) is **stateless** — its working directory is discarded the moment the sub-shell exits. `roscd` mutates the *current* shell's PWD; in a non-interactive one-shot sub-shell, the PWD mutation is never observed by the calling environment. Therefore, scripts should use `$(rospack find <pkg>)` to **capture the path as a string variable** without side effects.

**Example in a script (1 mark extra):**
```bash
PKG_PATH=$(rospack find my_pkg)
source $PKG_PATH/setup.bash
```

#### Part (b) — Catkin Workspace Structure (7 marks)

A **catkin workspace** is a top-level directory in which you build, store, and manage ROS packages. It has **four standard sub-directories** (only `src` is mandatory for an out-of-the-box workspace):

| Directory | Mandatory? | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `src/` | **Yes** | Source space. Contains package directories, each with `package.xml` and `CMakeLists.txt`. You clone and create packages here. |
| `build/` | Generated | Build space. Intermediate cache, CMake/Make invocation artifacts. **Do not edit by hand.** |
| `devel/` | Generated | Development space. Contains the workspace's `setup.bash` and generated headers, libs, and executables for the current overlay. |
| `install/` | Optional | Install space. Populated when using `catkin_make install`. Mirror of `/opt/ros/...` for a portable, relocatable build. |

**Workflow sequence (2 marks extra credit):**
$$\text{create src} \rightarrow \text{add packages} \rightarrow \text{catkin\_make} \rightarrow \text{source devel/setup.bash} \rightarrow \text{use packages}$$

**Block diagram is provided in SECTION 4.2 above** — students may reproduce it verbatim for full marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to `source` the workspace** — 1 mark lost if the student does not mention the `source devel/setup.bash` step in any troubleshooting question.
> 2. **Conflating `roscd` with `rosls`** — They share the same syntax but produce different side effects. Examiners look for the *side-effect* distinction in 7-mark comparisons.
> 3. **Misnaming `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`** — A common typo is `ROSPACKAGEPATH` or `ROS_PACKAGE` (singular). Always use the exact form.
> 4. **Stating `roscd` works in scripts** — Incorrect. Use `$(rospack find …)` in scripts. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this misconception.
> 5. **Skipping Tab-completion** — Worth a quick mention for full-marks answers on workflow/efficiency questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- 📌 **Navigating in Code** in ROS = using `rosbash` shell extensions to move through the package filesystem.
- 📌 Three primary commands: **`rospack find`**, **`roscd`**, **`rosls`**.
- 📌 All three rely on the **`ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`** environment variable for discovery.
- 📌 A **catkin workspace** has four sub-directories: `src`, `build`, `devel`, `install`.
- 📌 `src` is the only mandatory one — it stores package source trees.
- 📌 `catkin_make` must be run before sourcing, otherwise new packages are invisible to `rospack`.
- 📌 Always `source devel/setup.bash` after every build; append to `~/.bashrc` for permanence.
- 📌 **Tab-completion** dramatically speeds up package-name entry and prevents typos.
- 📌 **In scripts:** use `$(rospack find <pkg>)` (string capture), never `roscd` (shell state mutation).
- 📌 `roscd` with **no argument** returns to `$ROS_WORKSPACE` (if set).
- 📌 `echo $ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` is the single most useful debug command.
- 📌 `rospack depends1` vs `rospack depends`: direct vs transitive dependency listing.
- 📌 `rosed` and `roscp` are bonus helpers for editing and copying within packages.
